## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
*Define a chassis dynamometer. List any two advantages it offers over on-road vehicle testing.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **chassis dynamometer** is a stationary test apparatus that supports a vehicle's wheels on motorized rollers to measure engine output, fuel consumption, and exhaust emissions without driving on an actual road.

**Advantages (any two, 1.5 marks each):**
1. **Controlled environmental conditions** — temperature, humidity, and wind are constant, eliminating weather variability.
2. **Repeatability** — identical road-load conditions can be reproduced for every test, allowing direct comparison of vehicles or tuning changes.
3. **Safety** — high-speed and aggressive accelerations can be tested without driver risk.
4. **Direct emission sampling** — exhaust can be captured and analyzed continuously throughout the drive cycle.
5. **Compact footprint** — entire test cycle is performed inside a single test cell.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
*State the coast-down road-load equation and identify the physical meaning of each coefficient.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$F = A + Bv + Cv^{2}$$

**Physical meaning (1 mark each):**

| Coefficient | Meaning | Physical Source |
|-------------|---------|-----------------|
| $A$ | Constant force term | Tyre rolling resistance + drivetrain friction |
| $B$ | Speed-linear coefficient | Internal viscous friction (bearings, gearbox) |
| $C$ | Speed-squared coefficient | Aerodynamic drag ($\tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f$) |

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]

**(a)** With the help of a functional block diagram, explain the working principle of a **two-wheel chassis dynamometer**. Clearly identify the role of the power absorption unit and the flywheel system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **coast-down road-load equation** from first principles. A passenger car of mass **1200 kg** has the following coast-down coefficients: $A = 120$ N, $B = 1.8$ N·s/m, $C = 0.40$ N·s²/m². Calculate the **total road-load force and power demanded at 80 km/h**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Working Principle (7 marks):**

A two-wheel chassis dynamometer holds the vehicle stationary while the drive wheels rotate on a pair of rollers. The block diagram shown in **Section 4.1** depicts the architecture. Components and their roles:

1. **Roller Assembly (1 mark):** Cylindrical steel rollers of fixed radius (typically 100–300 mm) bear the weight of the drive wheels. Roller rotation speed equals the equivalent road speed scaled by the tyre/roller radius ratio.

2. **Flywheel System (2 marks):** Mechanical flywheels of calibrated mass are bolted to the roller shaft. They store rotational kinetic energy equal to the vehicle's translational kinetic energy:
   $$E_{flywheel} = \tfrac{1}{2}I_{eq}\omega^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2}m_{vehicle}v^{2}$$
   During acceleration on the dyno, the engine supplies energy to spin up the flywheels, exactly mimicking the work done in accelerating the vehicle on a road. Without flywheels, the engine would see only the tiny roller inertia (~50 kg) and behave unrealistically.

3. **Power Absorption Unit (PAU) (2 marks):** The PAU — usually an eddy-current brake — applies a programmable retarding force $F_{PAU}(v) = A + Bv + Cv^{2}$ matching the road-load. It converts the vehicle's mechanical energy into heat (dissipated by water cooling) or electricity (in regenerative dynos).

4. **Control Computer & Sensors (1 mark):** Optical encoders measure roller speed; load cells measure force. The controller compares actual roller speed to the target speed profile of the drive cycle (MIDC, WLTC) and adjusts PAU excitation current in real time.

5. **Driver Display (1 mark):** A monitor shows the target vs actual speed trace. A driver follows this trace using the brake and accelerator pedals.

**Part (b) — Derivation + Numerical (7 marks):**

**Derivation (4 marks):** As derived in **Section 3.1**, the resistive forces acting on a coasting vehicle are:

$$F_{rolling} = C_{rr} m g \quad \text{(constant)} \qquad F_{aero} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f v^{2} \qquad F_{internal} = Bv$$

Combining them:

$$\boxed{F(v) = A + Bv + Cv^{2}}$$

with $A = C_{rr}mg$, $B$ = empirical linear coefficient, $C = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f$.

**Numerical (3 marks):**

**Given:**
- $v = 80 \, \text{km/h} = \dfrac{80 \times 1000}{3600} = 22.22 \, \text{m/s}$
- $A = 120$ N, $B = 1.8$ N·s/m, $C = 0.40$ N·s²/m²

**Step 1 — Compute each term:**

$$Bv = 1.8 \times 22.22 = 40.0 \, \text{N}$$

$$Cv^{2} = 0.40 \times (22.22)^{2} = 0.40 \times 493.8 = 197.5 \, \text{N}$$

**Step 2 — Total road-load force:**

$$F_{total} = 120 + 40.0 + 197.5$$

$$\boxed{F_{total} \approx 357.5 \, \text{N}}$$

**Step 3 — Power demanded at this speed:**

$$P = F_{total} \times v = 357.5 \times 22.22$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 7944 \, \text{W} \approx 7.94 \, \text{kW} \;(\approx 10.65 \, \text{hp})}$$

This is the power the dyno PAU must absorb to make the vehicle "feel" that it is cruising at 80 km/h on a flat road.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]

**(a)** Compare **two-wheel and four-wheel chassis dynamometers** in terms of construction, application, and the special synchronization requirement for AWD vehicles. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A motorcycle of mass **250 kg** is tested on a two-wheel chassis dynamometer with **roller diameter 300 mm**. The motorcycle accelerates from rest to a roller speed of **1500 rpm**. Calculate:
- (i) the equivalent road speed reached,
- (ii) the kinetic energy stored in the rotating system if the equivalent vehicle inertia is used,
- (iii) the angular momentum of the system at that speed. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Comparison (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Two-Wheel Dyno (3.5 marks) | Four-Wheel Dyno (3.5 marks) |
|-----------|----------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Axles tested** | Single axle only (FWD or RWD) | Both front and rear axles simultaneously |
| **Roller sets** | One set of rollers | Two independent sets, one per axle |
| **AWD capability** | ❌ Cannot test AWD/4WD | ✅ Full AWD/4WD capability |
| **Cost & complexity** | Lower cost, simpler setup | 2–3× cost; needs synchronized drives |
| **Inertia simulation** | Single flywheel bank | Independent flywheel bank per axle |
| **Synchronization** | Not required | **Mandatory** — roller speeds of front and rear must match within 0.1% slip; otherwise the centre differential of the vehicle will be stressed or will bind, producing incorrect results |
| **Applications** | Emission certification of 2WD cars, two-wheelers, fuel-economy studies | AWD/4WD performance testing, launch-control development, vehicle dynamics R&D |
| **Vehicle class** | Cars, two-wheelers, light CVs | SUVs, AWD sedans, heavy trucks |

**Special Synchronization Note (built into comparison):**
In an AWD vehicle, the centre differential allows front and rear axles to rotate at slightly different speeds during cornering. On a dyno, since the vehicle is not turning, both axles should rotate at the same speed. If the dyno's front and rear rollers are driven independently at different speeds, the centre diff will continuously slip, generating heat and false load readings. Therefore, modern four-wheel dynos use a **master controller** that synchronizes both roller sets to a single target speed.

**Part (b) — Motorcycle Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $m = 250 \, \text{kg}$
- $r_{roller} = 150 \, \text{mm} = 0.15 \, \text{m}$
- $N = 1500 \, \text{rpm}$

**Step (i) — Equivalent road speed (2 marks):**

$$\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 50\pi \approx 157.08 \, \text{rad/s}$$

$$v = \omega \times r_{roller} = 157.08 \times 0.15$$

$$\boxed{v \approx 23.56 \, \text{m/s} \approx 84.8 \, \text{km/h}}$$

**Step (ii) — Kinetic energy at equivalent vehicle inertia (3 marks):**

For a two-wheel dyno, the equivalent moment of inertia at the roller must match the vehicle mass:

$$I_{eq} = m \cdot r_{roller}^{2} = 250 \times (0.15)^{2} = 250 \times 0.0225 = 5.625 \, \text{kg·m}^{2}$$

$$E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} I_{eq} \omega^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 5.625 \times (157.08)^{2}$$

$$E_k = 2.8125 \times 24674$$

$$\boxed{E_k \approx 69{,}395 \, \text{J} \approx 69.4 \, \text{kJ}}$$

**Step (iii) — Angular momentum (2 marks):**

$$L = I_{eq} \cdot \omega = 5.625 \times 157.08$$

$$\boxed{L \approx 883.6 \, \text{kg·m}^{2}/\text{s}}$$

These values are needed by the dyno operator to confirm that the flywheel bank can safely store the kinetic energy without exceeding its rated limit.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks — Examiner's Pitfall Guide:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "wheel power" with "engine power":** On a chassis dyno, you measure **wheel power**. To get engine power, divide by drivetrain efficiency $\eta_{drivetrain} \approx 0.85$–$0.92$.
> 2. **Mixing up roller radius and tyre radius:** The roller is much smaller than the tyre, so the roller spins **much faster** than the wheel — do not assume equal angular velocities.
> 3. **Forgetting the factor of $1/2$ in kinetic energy formulas:** A common slip. Always write $\tfrac{1}{2}mv^{2}$, not $mv^{2}$.
> 4. **Wrong unit conversion for km/h to m/s:** Divide by 3.6, not multiply. $80 \, \text{km/h} = 22.22 \, \text{m/s}$, not $80 \times 1000$.
> 5. **Not mentioning synchronization for AWD:** A four-wheel dyno question without a synchronization answer is treated as incomplete.
> 6. **Calling a chassis dyno an "engine dynamometer":** Engine dynos measure the engine alone (removed from vehicle); chassis dynos measure the **whole vehicle on its wheels**.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before KTU Exam:**
>
> ✅ A **chassis dynamometer** measures vehicle performance on a **stationary platform** using motorized rollers.
>
> ✅ **Two-wheel dyno:** tests **one axle only** (FWD or RWD vehicles).
>
> ✅ **Four-wheel dyno:** tests **both axles simultaneously**; required for AWD/4WD.
>
> ✅ **Coast-down road-load equation:** $F = A + Bv + Cv^{2}$ — *memorize this; it appears in nearly every KTU question.*
>
> ✅ **Coefficients:**
> - $A$ = rolling resistance (constant)
> - $B$ = internal friction (linear)
> - $C$ = aerodynamic drag (quadratic)
>
> ✅ **Power equation:** $P = F \cdot v = T \cdot \omega$
>
> ✅ **Equivalent road speed:** $v = \omega_{roller} \cdot r_{roller}$
>
> ✅ **Equivalent inertia at roller:** $I_{eq} = m_{vehicle} \cdot r_{roller}^{2}$
>
> ✅ **Kinetic energy:** $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2}I_{eq}\omega^{2}$
>
> ✅ **Aerodynamic drag:** $F_d = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f v^{2}$
>
> ✅ **Rolling resistance:** $F_r = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g$
>
> ✅ **PAU types:** eddy-current brake (most common), hydraulic, regenerative electric.
>
> ✅ **Drive cycles tested on chassis dyno:** MIDC, WLTC, NEDC (legacy), FTP-75.
>
> ✅ **Four-wheel dyno MUST synchronize front and rear roller speeds** to prevent centre-differential wind-up.
>
> ✅ **Cooling fans** are essential on chassis dynos — without them, the vehicle's radiator receives no ram air and overheats.
>
> ✅ **Rolling resistance coefficient** $C_{rr} \approx 0.010$–$0.015$ for passenger car tyres.
>
> ✅ **Air density** standard value: $\rho = 1.225 \, \text{kg/m}^{3}$ at sea level, 15 °C.
>
> ✅ **Units discipline:** Always convert km/h → m/s by dividing by 3.6.
>
> ✅ **Tyre slip:** Modern dynos measure or correct for tyre slip (typically 1–3%) which otherwise causes under-reading of road speed.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Vehicle Testing Lane?

A **Vehicle Testing Lane** (also known as an **Automated Vehicle Testing Lane** or **AVTL**) is a scientifically engineered, in-line sequence of precision testing equipment arranged in a straight-line configuration. The vehicle drives through this lane at a controlled, low speed while each station performs a specific safety or emission compliance test, with the results being recorded automatically by an integrated central computer.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A *Vehicle Testing Lane* is a combination of measuring instruments and machinery installed in series to perform combined vehicle inspection under simulated driving conditions. The lane typically consists of a **Side Slip Tester**, a **Speedometer Tester** (optional), a **Brake Tester** (roller-type or plate-type), and a **Headlight Alignment Tester**, all integrated via a control console.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **human body check-up tunnel** at a hospital. You walk in, and at Station 1 your weight is measured, at Station 2 your blood pressure is taken, at Station 3 your eyes are tested, and finally at Station 4 your reflexes are checked. You don't have to walk to four different buildings — everything happens in a single, streamlined conveyor-belt-like flow.

A Vehicle Testing Lane works on the **exact same principle**, but for cars. Instead of weight and blood pressure, it measures the car's **steering alignment, braking force, and headlight beam direction** — all critical safety parameters that determine whether a vehicle is "roadworthy" or "dangerous to be on the road."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Vehicle Testing Lanes are Critical:**
> - Required by law for issuing **Fitness Certificates** to commercial and transport vehicles.
> - Reduce road accidents caused by misaligned steering and faulty brakes.
> - Standardize vehicle inspection under the **Central Motor Vehicle Rules (CMVR)**.
> - Time-efficient: a complete lane can test a vehicle in **under 5 minutes**.

---

## 1.3 The Three Major Components of a Testing Lane

### A. Side Slip Tester

A **Side Slip Tester** measures the amount of sideways (lateral) slip or scrub a vehicle's wheels exhibit when they are supposed to roll straight ahead. This slip is caused by incorrect **wheel alignment** parameters — specifically **toe-in / toe-out**, **camber**, or **steering axis inclination**.

**Physical Layout:** A movable plate (the **slip plate**) is mounted on top of a fixed base plate. The vehicle's front wheel rolls over this top plate. If the wheel alignment is incorrect, the plate slides sideways by an amount proportional to the misalignment.

### B. Brake Tester

A **Brake Tester** measures the **braking force** generated at each wheel of the vehicle, and from that data computes the **brake efficiency** and **brake imbalance** between the left and right wheels.

**Physical Layout:** A pair of motor-driven rollers (for roller-type) or a flat pressure plate (for plate-type) supports the wheel. When the brakes are applied, the wheel resists rotation, and sensors measure this resistance torque to determine braking force.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** Roller-type brake testers are the most common in India. The two standard roller diameters are **260 mm** and **350 mm**. As per AIS-001, the roller surface must be coated with a high-friction material to prevent tire slippage during testing.

### C. Headlight Alignment Tester

A **Headlight Alignment Tester** (or **Beam Setter / Light Tester**) checks whether the vehicle's headlight beam is aimed correctly — not too high (which would dazzle oncoming drivers) and not too low (which would reduce visibility for the driver).

**Physical Layout:** A **lens system** is placed in front of the headlight at a specified distance (typically **1 meter** or **10 meters**). The beam is projected onto a **screen** (or measured by **photo-sensors**), and the **cut-off line** (for modern projector-style LED/HID lamps) or **hot spot** (for conventional halogen lamps) is analyzed.

---

## 1.4 Key Standardization Bodies and Rules

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standards Governing Vehicle Testing Lanes in India:**
> - **AIS-001** (Automotive Industry Standards) – Specifications for side slip, brake, and headlight testers.
> - **AIS-002** – Procedure for testing.
> - **CMVR Rule 62** – Mandatory testing of transport vehicles at authorized testing stations.
> - **ISO 9001** – Quality management for testing stations.
> - **BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards)** – IS 14655 and related standards.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Side Slip Tester — Deep Dive

### 2.1.1 Operating Principle

The Side Slip Tester works on the principle of **lateral force detection** or **displacement detection**. There are two main designs:

**(a) Mechanical (Plate-Displacement) Type — Most Common in India**
- A **top plate** (slip plate) is positioned over a **base plate**.
- Between them are **measuring devices** (strain gauges, LVDT, or mechanical linkages) that detect lateral movement.
- When the front wheel passes over the top plate, the plate is forced sideways by an amount $\Delta$ (in **mm/m**).
- The displacement is read on a **dial gauge** or a **digital display**.

**(b) Electronic (Strain Gauge) Type**
- Uses **load cells** mounted under the slip plate.
- Measures the **lateral force** $F_y$ generated by the wheel as it passes over.
- Calculates slip indirectly from force using calibration constants.

### 2.1.2 Relationship Between Slip and Wheel Alignment

The side slip value is **not a direct measurement** of toe or camber. It is a **resultant** of all wheel alignment errors. The dominant contributor is **toe-in / toe-out**.

For a simple two-wheel front axle, the side slip value (in mm/m) is empirically related to the **total toe-in** $T_i$ (in mm) and the **wheelbase-related conversion factor** as per the following simplified relationship:

$$
S = \frac{T_i \times \tan(\alpha)}{L_w}
$$

Where:
- $S$ = Side slip value in mm per meter of travel
- $T_i$ = Total toe-in (mm) — sum of left and right toe
- $\alpha$ = SAI (Steering Axis Inclination) — typically **$6°$ to $13°$**
- $L_w$ = Wheelbase-related geometric factor

### 2.1.3 Permissible Limits (as per AIS-001)

| Vehicle Type | Maximum Permissible Side Slip (mm/m) |
|---|---|
| M1 (Passenger cars up to 9 seats) | **$\pm 5$** mm/m |
| M2 / M3 (Buses) | **$\pm 6$** mm/m |
| N1 / N2 (Light / Medium commercial) | **$\pm 7$** mm/m |
| N3 (Heavy commercial) | **$\pm 8$** mm/m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Direction Convention:**
> - **Positive (+) value** = **Toe-out** (wheel toes point outward) → Plate moves **outward**.
> - **Negative (–) value** = **Toe-in** (wheel toes point inward) → Plate moves **inward**.

### 2.1.4 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Positioning:** Vehicle is driven onto the test lane. Front wheels are aligned on the slip plates.
2. **Rolling Phase:** Front wheels roll over the top plate at a low speed of about **3 to 5 km/h**.
3. **Force Application:** Misalignment causes the wheels to scrub sideways. The top plate is forced to translate laterally.
4. **Measurement:** The lateral displacement is measured by a **displacement sensor** (LVDT type) or **dial gauge**.
5. **Indication:** The reading is displayed in **mm/m** on the operator's console.
6. **Pass/Fail:** The system compares with limits and prints a report.

---

## 2.2 Brake Tester — Deep Dive

### 2.2.1 Operating Principle (Roller Type)

A **Roller-type brake tester** uses a **pair of rollers** (one for each wheel of an axle) that are driven by a **3-phase electric motor** (typically **7.5 kW to 15 kW**). The vehicle is driven onto the rollers. The motor spins the rollers, which in turn spin the wheel. When the brakes are applied:

- The wheel attempts to **stop rotating**.
- The roller continues to be driven by the motor.
- A **slip condition** develops between the roller surface and the tire.
- The **friction force** at the contact patch equals the **braking force** of the brake.

This braking force is measured by a **load cell** (typically strain-gauge based) connected to the roller assembly frame. The output is displayed as **brake force in kgf or daN** (1 daN = 1.02 kgf).

### 2.2.2 The Core Brake Force Formula

The braking force is calculated from the **torque required to keep the rollers turning** against the braked wheel. For a roller of radius $r$:

$$
F_b = \frac{T}{r}
$$

Where:
- $F_b$ = Braking force at the wheel (in **N** or **daN**)
- $T$ = Reaction torque measured at the roller shaft (in **N·m**)
- $r$ = Roller radius (in **m**)

### 2.2.3 Brake Efficiency

**Brake Efficiency** ($\eta_b$) is defined as the ratio of the **total braking force generated at all wheels** to the **total weight (Gross Vehicle Weight)** of the vehicle:

$$
\eta_b = \frac{F_{b,\text{total}}}{W_{\text{total}}} \times 100
$$

$$
\eta_b = \frac{F_{b,FL} + F_{b,FR} + F_{b,RL} + F_{b,RR}}{W_{\text{GVW}}} \times 100 \ \%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Minimum Brake Efficiency Requirements (CMVR):**
> - For **M1 (passenger cars)**: minimum **50%** for service brakes.
> - For **M2 / M3 (buses)**: minimum **50%** for service brakes.
> - For **N-category (trucks)**: minimum **45%** for service brakes.
> - **Parking brake**: minimum **20%** efficiency is required for all categories.

### 2.2.4 Brake Imbalance

**Brake Imbalance** is the **difference** in braking force between the left and right wheels on the **same axle**, expressed as a percentage of the **higher** value:

$$
I_b = \frac{\vert F_{b,L} - F_{b,R} \vert}{\max(F_{b,L}, F_{b,R})} \times 100 \ \%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Permissible Brake Imbalance (as per AIS-001):** The imbalance between the left and right wheels of the **same axle** must not exceed **20%** for all vehicle categories. Excessive imbalance causes **vehicle pull to one side** during braking, leading to accidents.

### 2.2.5 Deceleration Calculation (Indirect Method)

When brake efficiency is known, the theoretical **deceleration** the vehicle can achieve can be approximated by:

$$
a = \eta_b \cdot g
$$

Where:
- $a$ = Deceleration in **m/s²**
- $\eta_b$ = Brake efficiency (as a fraction, not percentage)
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**

### 2.2.6 Roller Specifications

| Parameter | Specification |
|---|---|
| Roller diameter | **$\mathbf{260}$ mm** (passenger) / **$\mathbf{350}$ mm** (heavy vehicle) |
| Roller length | **$\mathbf{1000}$ mm** (passenger) / **$\mathbf{1100}$ mm** (heavy) |
| Roller surface | Knurled steel with high-friction coating |
| Motor power | **$\mathbf{7.5}$ to $\mathbf{15}$ kW** per pair |
| Test speed | **$\mathbf{2.6}$ to $\mathbf{5.4}$ km/h** (low) |
| Maximum axle load | **$\mathbf{2000}$ kg** (small) to **$\mathbf{13000}$ kg** (heavy) |

---

## 2.3 Headlight Alignment Tester — Deep Dive

### 2.3.1 Operating Principle

A **Headlight Alignment Tester** (also called **Beam Setter**, **Lamp Aimer**, or **Reglos Light Tester**) uses an **optical lens system** to project the headlight beam onto a **screen** at a calibrated distance, or onto an **internal screen** inside the instrument (for modern digital testers).

**Types of Headlights Tested:**
1. **Conventional Halogen (Type A)** — Uses a filament; has a symmetric "hot spot" with no sharp cut-off.
2. **Asymmetric Halogen (Type B / European)** — Has a **distinct cut-off line** with an **elbow** at the center.
3. **HID / Xenon (Type C / D)** — High-intensity discharge lamps with a sharp cut-off.
4. **LED / Laser** — Modern adaptive headlamps with matrix beam.

### 2.3.2 Reference Axes

Three reference axes must be precisely established:

- **H-H Line (Horizontal-Horizontal):** The horizontal reference line passing through the headlight center.
- **V-V Line (Vertical-Vertical):** The vertical reference line passing through the headlight center.
- **Cut-off Line:** The sharp horizontal boundary between the bright zone (below) and the dark zone (above) in a properly aimed low-beam pattern.

### 2.3.3 Beam Aim Direction Conventions

- **Dip Down (Downward tilt):** The beam axis tilts **below** the horizontal. Required for low-beam (passing beam) to prevent dazzling.
- **Deflection:** The asymmetric elbow deflects to the **left** (in LHD countries) or **right** (in RHD countries like India).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In India (RHD country):** The low-beam elbow deflects to the **right**, allowing the driver to see the left side of the road without dazzling oncoming traffic on the right.

### 2.3.4 Mounting Angle / Initial Aim Formula

The **mounting angle** $\theta$ (the angle by which the headlight is aimed downward from the horizontal) is calculated as:

$$
\theta = \frac{h}{D} \times \frac{180}{\pi}
$$

Where:
- $\theta$ = Mounting angle in **degrees**
- $h$ = Vertical drop of beam from the headlight center to the ground at distance $D$ (in **m**)
- $D$ = Distance from headlight to the projection screen (in **m**)

For a standard installation with $h = 100$ mm drop at $D = 10$ m:

$$
\theta = \frac{0.1}{10} = 0.01 \ \text{rad} = 0.573^{\circ}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Initial Aim Value:** Most manufacturers aim the headlights with a **1% downward tilt** — i.e., the beam drops **100 mm for every 10 meters** of forward distance.

### 2.3.5 Photometric Requirements (CMVR)

| Beam Type | Minimum Luminous Intensity (cd) |
|---|---|
| Driving beam (High) | **$\mathbf{15{,}000}$ cd** at reference axis |
| Passing beam (Low) | **$\mathbf{5{,}000}$ cd** minimum at hot spot |

### 2.3.6 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Setup:** Vehicle is parked on a **level surface**. Headlight tester is placed in front of the headlight at distance $D$.
2. **Alignment:** Tester is aligned to the **vehicle's centerline** and **headlight center height**.
3. **Projection:** The headlight is switched on (low beam). The beam pattern is projected onto the internal screen.
4. **Identification:** The **cut-off line** (or hot spot for conventional) is identified.
5. **Measurement:** The vertical position of the cut-off line below the H-H line is measured.
6. **Conversion:** The vertical drop $h$ is converted to a **mounting angle** $\theta$.
7. **Pass/Fail:** Compared with limits.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Table)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S = \dfrac{T_i \cdot \tan(\alpha)}{L_w}$ | Side slip from total toe-in | mm/m |
| 2 | $F_b = \dfrac{T}{r}$ | Braking force from roller torque | N or daN |
| 3 | $\eta_b = \dfrac{\sum F_b}{W_{GVW}} \times 100$ | Brake efficiency | % |
| 4 | $I_b = \dfrac{\vert F_{b,L} - F_{b,R} \vert}{\max(F_{b,L}, F_{b,R})} \times 100$ | Brake imbalance | % |
| 5 | $a = \eta_b \cdot g$ | Theoretical deceleration | m/s² |
| 6 | $\theta = \dfrac{h}{D} \times \dfrac{180}{\pi}$ | Headlight mounting angle | degrees |
| 7 | $P_{\text{roller}} = \dfrac{F_b \cdot v}{\eta_m}$ | Roller motor power (approx) | W |
| 8 | $\text{Total Toe} = \text{Toe}_L + \text{Toe}_R$ | Total steering toe | mm or degrees |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Rule:** All absolute values use `\vert` notation (e.g., `\vert F_{b,L} - F_{b,R} \vert`) — never use the bare `\|` symbol inside a markdown table, as it breaks rendering. This is a common formatting pitfall in KTU answer scripts.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of Vehicle Testing Lanes

1. **Road Safety:** Prevents accidents due to misaligned wheels, weak brakes, or misaimed headlights.
2. **Fitness Certification:** Mandatory for **commercial vehicles** under **MV Act 1988, Section 56**.
3. **Emission + Safety Bundling:** Modern lanes integrate **emission testers (diesel smoke / petrol HC-CO)** with the safety lanes.
4. **Insurance & Legal:** Test reports are required by insurance companies for claim settlements.
5. **Automated Report Generation:** Computer-controlled lanes issue **printouts** with all measurements, suitable for legal evidence.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Side Slip Value from Toe Angle

We will derive the theoretical relationship between the **individual wheel toe angle** (in degrees) and the resulting **side slip value** (in mm/m) as measured by a side slip tester plate.

### Given Data (Assumed)
- Front wheel of a typical passenger car
- Toe-in per wheel: $\delta = 0.5^{\circ}$ (i.e., 30 arc-minutes)
- Effective wheel rolling radius: $R = 300$ mm
- Plate length (contact length): $L_p = 1000$ mm
- Both front wheels have equal but opposite toe-in (toward the centerline)

### Step 1: Express the lateral displacement per wheel revolution

For a wheel with a small toe angle $\delta$, the **lateral component of travel per revolution** is:

$$
\Delta_{\text{per rev}} = 2 \pi R \cdot \sin(\delta)
$$

### Step 2: Simplify for small angles (since $\delta$ is small, $\sin \delta \approx \tan \delta \approx \delta$ in radians)

$$
\Delta_{\text{per rev}} = 2 \pi R \cdot \delta \quad (\delta \text{ in radians})
$$

### Step 3: Convert $\delta = 0.5^{\circ}$ to radians

$$
\delta_{\text{rad}} = 0.5 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.008727 \ \text{rad}
$$

### Step 4: Calculate the lateral displacement per revolution

$$
\Delta_{\text{per rev}} = 2 \pi \times 300 \times 0.008727
$$

$$
\Delta_{\text{per rev}} = 1884.956 \times 0.008727
$$

$$
\Delta_{\text{per rev}} = 16.448 \ \text{mm per revolution}
$$

### Step 5: Calculate the longitudinal distance traveled per revolution

The longitudinal distance covered by the wheel in one revolution is the **circumference**:

$$
C = 2 \pi R = 2 \pi \times 300 = 1884.956 \ \text{mm}
$$

### Step 6: Express side slip in mm per meter of longitudinal travel

$$
S = \frac{\Delta_{\text{per rev}}}{C} \times 1000 \quad \text{(mm per meter)}
$$

$$
S = \frac{16.448}{1884.956} \times 1000
$$

$$
S = 0.008727 \times 1000
$$

$$
S = 8.727 \ \text{mm/m}
$$

### Step 7: Total Side Slip (both wheels combined)

For a vehicle with both wheels having equal and symmetric toe-in, the total side slip as measured by the plate (which records the average of both wheels passing over it) is:

$$
S_{\text{total}} = S_{\text{single}} \quad \text{(for symmetric toe-in)}
$$

$$
S_{\text{total}} = 8.73 \ \text{mm/m} \ (\text{approximately})
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A side slip value of **8.73 mm/m** for a passenger car **exceeds** the permissible limit of **5 mm/m** (per AIS-001). The vehicle would **FAIL** the side slip test, indicating the need for wheel alignment correction.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Brake Efficiency and Deceleration

### Given Data (Assumed)
- Vehicle Gross Vehicle Weight (GVW): $W = 1800$ kg
- Braking forces measured: 
  - Front Left $F_{b,FL} = 380$ daN
  - Front Right $F_{b,FR} = 420$ daN
  - Rear Left $F_{b,RL} = 220$ daN
  - Rear Right $F_{b,RR} = 240$ daN
- Roller radius: $r = 130$ mm = 0.130 m

### Step 1: Convert all forces to consistent units (Newtons)

Conversion: **1 daN = 10 N**

$$
F_{b,FL} = 380 \times 10 = 3800 \ \text{N}
$$
$$
F_{b,FR} = 420 \times 10 = 4200 \ \text{N}
$$
$$
F_{b,RL} = 220 \times 10 = 2200 \ \text{N}
$$
$$
F_{b,RR} = 240 \times 10 = 2400 \ \text{N}
$$

### Step 2: Calculate the total braking force

$$
F_{b,\text{total}} = F_{b,FL} + F_{b,FR} + F_{b,RL} + F_{b,RR}
$$

$$
F_{b,\text{total}} = 3800 + 4200 + 2200 + 2400
$$

$$
F_{b,\text{total}} = 12{,}600 \ \text{N}
$$

### Step 3: Convert the GVW to weight in Newtons

$$
W = m \cdot g = 1800 \times 9.81
$$

$$
W = 17{,}658 \ \text{N}
$$

### Step 4: Calculate brake efficiency

$$
\eta_b = \frac{F_{b,\text{total}}}{W} \times 100
$$

$$
\eta_b = \frac{12{,}600}{17{,}658} \times 100
$$

$$
\eta_b = 0.7135 \times 100
$$

$$
\eta_b = 71.35 \ \%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Pass/Fail Check:** Required minimum brake efficiency for an M1 category vehicle is **50%**. Since 71.35% > 50%, the vehicle **PASSES** the brake efficiency test.

### Step 5: Calculate the front-axle brake imbalance

For the **front axle**:
- $F_{b,FL} = 3800$ N
- $F_{b,FR} = 4200$ N

$$
I_{b,\text{front}} = \frac{\vert F_{b,FL} - F_{b,FR} \vert}{\max(F_{b,FL}, F_{b,FR})} \times 100
$$

$$
I_{b,\text{front}} = \frac{\vert 3800 - 4200 \vert}{4200} \times 100
$$

$$
I_{b,\text{front}} = \frac{400}{4200} \times 100
$$

$$
I_{b,\text{front}} = 9.52 \ \%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Pass/Fail Check:** Permissible imbalance is **20%**. Since 9.52% < 20%, the front axle imbalance is **WITHIN** the limit.

### Step 6: Calculate the rear-axle brake imbalance

For the **rear axle**:
- $F_{b,RL} = 2200$ N
- $F_{b,RR} = 2400$ N

$$
I_{b,\text{rear}} = \frac{\vert 2200 - 2400 \vert}{2400} \times 100
$$

$$
I_{b,\text{rear}} = \frac{200}{2400} \times 100
$$

$$
I_{b,\text{rear}} = 8.33 \ \%
$$

### Step 7: Calculate the theoretical deceleration

$$
a = \eta_b \cdot g = 0.7135 \times 9.81
$$

$$
a = 7.00 \ \text{m/s}^{2}
$$

### Step 8: Convert to stopping distance from 100 km/h

First, convert speed to m/s:
$$
v = 100 \ \text{km/h} = \frac{100 \times 1000}{3600} = 27.78 \ \text{m/s}
$$

Stopping distance (assuming constant deceleration):
$$
s = \frac{v^{2}}{2a} = \frac{(27.78)^{2}}{2 \times 7.00}
$$

$$
s = \frac{771.73}{14.00} = 55.12 \ \text{m}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A vehicle with 71.35% brake efficiency stops from 100 km/h in approximately **55 meters**. This is consistent with typical passenger car performance standards.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Headlight Mounting Angle and Beam Drop

### Given Data (Assumed)
- Test distance: $D = 10$ m
- Measured vertical drop of cut-off line: $h = 75$ mm
- Headlight mounting height from ground: $H_{hl} = 750$ mm

### Step 1: Calculate the mounting angle in radians

$$
\theta_{\text{rad}} = \frac{h}{D} = \frac{0.075}{10} = 0.0075 \ \text{rad}
$$

### Step 2: Convert to degrees

$$
\theta = 0.0075 \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 0.0075 \times 57.2958
$$

$$
\theta = 0.4297^{\circ} \approx 0.43^{\circ}
$$

### Step 3: Express as a percentage slope

$$
\text{Slope} = \frac{h}{D} \times 100 = \frac{0.075}{10} \times 100 = 0.75 \ \%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Standard:** Most manufacturers aim headlights at **1% slope (0.57°)**. Our value of **0.75%** is slightly more downward than standard. This may be **acceptable** (slightly better for not dazzling oncoming traffic) but on the lower end of the typical range.

### Step 4: Convert to gradient in mm per meter

$$
\text{Gradient} = 75 \ \text{mm} / 10 \ \text{m} = 7.5 \ \text{mm/m}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Acceptable Range:** The mounting angle should typically be between **0.5% and 1.5%** slope (5 mm/m to 15 mm/m). A value of **7.5 mm/m** falls within the acceptable range.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Study

| Parameter | Side Slip Tester | Brake Tester | Headlight Tester |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Physical Principle** | Lateral displacement | Friction force measurement | Optical projection |
| **Primary Measurement** | mm/m (slip value) | daN (brake force) | mm drop / degrees (mounting angle) |
| **Vehicle Speed During Test** | 3–5 km/h | 2.6–5.4 km/h | **Static** (0 km/h) |
| **Test Duration** | ~3 seconds | ~15–20 seconds | ~30 seconds |
| **Permissible Limit (Passenger)** | ±5 mm/m | Efficiency ≥ 50%, Imbalance ≤ 20% | 0.5% to 1.5% slope |
| **Primary Failure Cause** | Incorrect wheel alignment | Worn brake pads/discs | Aging suspension, collision damage |
| **Governing Standard** | AIS-001 | AIS-001, CMVR 96 | AIS-008, CMVR 104 |
| **Cost in India (approx)** | ₹1.5 – 3 Lakh | ₹5 – 12 Lakh | ₹3 – 8 Lakh |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Define side slip and state the permissible limits for a passenger car (M1) as per AIS-001 standards.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
Side slip is the **lateral (sideways) movement** of a vehicle's wheel as it rolls forward, caused by incorrect wheel alignment parameters — primarily **toe-in**, **toe-out**, and to a lesser extent **camber**. It is measured by a **side slip tester** in **mm per meter** (mm/m) of longitudinal travel.

**Permissible Limit (1.5 Marks):**
As per **AIS-001**, the maximum permissible side slip for a **passenger car (M1 category)** is **$\pm 5$ mm/m**.

A reading beyond this value indicates improper wheel alignment, requiring adjustment of the toe-angle via the steering tie-rod.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**What is brake efficiency? State its minimum value for a commercial vehicle (N-category) under CMVR.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

**Definition (2 Marks):**
Brake efficiency is the ratio of the **total braking force** generated at all wheels of the vehicle to the **gross vehicle weight (GVW)**, expressed as a percentage.

$$
\eta_b = \frac{F_{b,\text{total}}}{W_{GVW}} \times 100 \ \%
$$

**Minimum Value (1 Mark):**
As per **CMVR (Central Motor Vehicle Rules)**, the minimum brake efficiency for **N-category (commercial/goods) vehicles** for the **service brake** is **45%**, while for the **parking brake** it is **20%**.

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format

### Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — Module 1

**With neat functional diagrams, explain the working principle of a side slip tester. Also derive the relationship between toe angle and side slip value. (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — Working Principle (7 Marks)

**Working Principle of a Side Slip Tester (Mechanical Plate-Displacement Type):**

1. The vehicle is driven onto the testing lane and the **front wheels** are positioned on the **slip plates** of the side slip tester. [1 Mark]

2. The vehicle rolls forward at a low speed of about **3 to 5 km/h**. [1 Mark]

3. A **top plate** is mounted on a **fixed base plate**, with the two plates connected through a **measuring element** (dial gauge, LVDT, or load cell). [1 Mark]

4. When the front wheel rolls over the top plate, any misaligned wheel (incorrect toe or camber) generates a **sideways (lateral) force**. [1 Mark]

5. This lateral force causes the top plate to **displace laterally** by an amount proportional to the misalignment. [1 Mark]

6. The displacement is sensed by a **displacement transducer (LVDT)** or **dial gauge** and displayed in **mm per meter** on the operator console. [1 Mark]

7. The system compares the measured value with the **AIS-001 permissible limit** ($\pm 5$ mm/m for M1) and indicates **PASS / FAIL** with a printout. [1 Mark]

#### Part (b) — Derivation (7 Marks)

**Derivation of Side Slip Value from Toe Angle:**

Let:
- $\delta$ = Toe angle of a single wheel (in radians)
- $R$ = Effective rolling radius of the wheel (in mm)
- $C$ = Wheel circumference = $2\pi R$

**Step 1:** Lateral displacement per wheel revolution:
$$
\Delta_{\text{per rev}} = C \cdot \tan(\delta)
$$

**Step 2:** For small angles, $\tan \delta \approx \delta$ (radians):
$$
\Delta_{\text{per rev}} = 2\pi R \cdot \delta
$$

**Step 3:** Side slip (mm per meter of longitudinal travel):
$$
S = \frac{\Delta_{\text{per rev}}}{C} \times 1000 = \frac{2\pi R \cdot \delta}{2\pi R} \times 1000
$$

**Step 4:** Simplify:
$$
S = \delta \times 1000 \quad \text{(mm/m, with } \delta \text{ in radians)}
$$

**Step 5:** For a typical case with $\delta = 0.5^{\circ} = 0.00873$ rad and $R = 300$ mm:
$$
S = 0.00873 \times 1000 = 8.73 \ \text{mm/m}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall 1:**
> - **Common Mistake:** Students often forget to **convert toe angle from degrees to radians** before applying the small-angle approximation. This leads to a 57× error in the final answer. (**–2 Marks deduction**)
> - **Common Mistake:** Forgetting to **multiply by 1000** to convert the slip ratio (dimensionless) to **mm/m** units. (**–1 Mark deduction**)
> - **Examiner Tip:** Always **state the assumption** "for small angles, $\sin \delta \approx \tan \delta \approx \delta$ in radians" explicitly to score full marks.

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam - May 2024] — Module 1

**Explain the working of a roller-type brake tester with a block diagram. A passenger car with GVW = 1600 kg is tested on a roller brake tester. The brake forces measured are: FL = 350 daN, FR = 380 daN, RL = 180 daN, RR = 200 daN. Calculate (i) the brake efficiency, (ii) the front-axle brake imbalance, and (iii) the theoretical deceleration. (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — Working of Roller-Type Brake Tester (7 Marks)

1. The vehicle is driven onto the testing lane such that one axle at a time is positioned on the **motor-driven rollers** of the brake tester. [1 Mark]

2. The rollers are driven by a **3-phase electric motor** (typically 7.5 to 15 kW) at a low speed of **2.6 to 5.4 km/h**. [1 Mark]

3. The vehicle wheels spin along with the rollers. The driver then **applies the brake pedal**. [1 Mark]

4. The brake attempts to **stop wheel rotation**, while the motor continues to drive the roller. A **slip** develops at the tire-roller contact. [1 Mark]

5. The **friction force** at the contact patch equals the **braking force** $F_b$ of the wheel, given by $F_b = T / r$ where $T$ is the reaction torque measured by a **strain-gauge load cell** on the roller shaft. [1 Mark]

6. The load cell output (in mV) is amplified, digitized, and displayed as **brake force in daN or kgf** on the operator console. [1 Mark]

7. The system automatically computes **brake efficiency** ($\sum F_b / W_{GVW} \times 100$) and **brake imbalance** ($\vert F_{b,L} - F_{b,R} \vert / \max \times 100$) and prints a pass/fail report. [1 Mark]

#### Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $W_{GVW} = 1600$ kg
- $F_{b,FL} = 350$ daN, $F_{b,FR} = 380$ daN
- $F_{b,RL} = 180$ daN, $F_{b,RR} = 200$ daN
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1: Convert to Newtons (1 daN = 10 N):**
$$
F_{b,FL} = 3500 \ \text{N}, \quad F_{b,FR} = 3800 \ \text{N}
$$
$$
F_{b,RL} = 1800 \ \text{N}, \quad F_{b,RR} = 2000 \ \text{N}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate total braking force (1 Mark):**
$$
F_{b,\text{total}} = 3500 + 3800 + 1800 + 2000 = 11{,}100 \ \text{N}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate GVW in Newtons (1 Mark):**
$$
W = 1600 \times 9.81 = 15{,}696 \ \text{N}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate brake efficiency (1 Mark):**
$$
\eta_b = \frac{11{,}100}{15{,}696} \times 100 = 70.72 \ \%
$$

**Step 5: Calculate front-axle brake imbalance (2 Marks):**
$$
I_{b,\text{front}} = \frac{\vert 3500 - 3800 \vert}{\max(3500, 3800)} \times 100
$$
$$
I_{b,\text{front}} = \frac{300}{3800} \times 100 = 7.89 \ \%
$$

**Step 6: Calculate theoretical deceleration (1 Mark):**
$$
a = \eta_b \cdot g = 0.7072 \times 9.81 = 6.94 \ \text{m/s}^{2}
$$

**Verification (0.5 Mark):**
- Brake efficiency 70.72% > 50% required → **PASS**
- Front imbalance 7.89% < 20% limit → **PASS**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall 2:**
> - **Common Mistake:** Forgetting to **convert daN to N** (multiply by 10). The efficiency formula must use **consistent units** (either all N or all daN). (**–2 Marks deduction**)
> - **Common Mistake:** Using **kgf instead of N** for the GVW term in the efficiency formula. The denominator must be the **gravitational weight** ($m \times g$), not the mass in kg. (**–2 Marks deduction**)
> - **Common Mistake:** Writing the imbalance formula as $\frac{F_{b,L} - F_{b,R}}{F_{b,L} + F_{b,R}}$ (incorrect). The correct formula uses $\max(F_{b,L}, F_{b,R})$ in the denominator, NOT the sum. (**–1.5 Marks deduction**)
> - **Examiner Tip:** Always **box the final answer** and **state pass/fail status** for full marks.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ☐ **Side Slip Tester** measures lateral wheel displacement in **mm/m**; limit is **$\pm 5$ mm/m** for M1 category.
- ☐ Side slip is caused by **toe-in / toe-out** misalignment; camber has a smaller contribution.
- ☐ Side slip is tested at **3–5 km/h** — a low, controlled speed.
- ☐ **Brake Tester (roller type)** uses a **motor-driven roller pair** per axle; motor power is **7.5–15 kW**.
- ☐ Brake force formula: $F_b = T / r$, where $T$ is reaction torque and $r$ is roller radius.
- ☐ **Brake efficiency** = $\sum F_b / W_{GVW} \times 100$ ; minimum = **50%** (M1) or **45%** (N-category).
- ☐ **Brake imbalance** = $\vert F_{b,L} - F_{b,R} \vert / \max(F_{b,L}, F_{b,R}) \times 100$ ; max allowed = **20%**.
- ☐ **Deceleration** $a = \eta_b \cdot g$ where $\eta_b$ is the **decimal fraction** (e.g., 0.71 for 71%).
- ☐ **Headlight Tester** uses a **Fresnel lens** to project the beam onto a screen at distance $D$.
- ☐ Standard aim distance: **10 m**; standard mounting angle: **1% slope** (100 mm drop per 10 m).
- ☐ In India (RHD), the low-beam **cut-off elbow deflects to the right**.
- ☐ Use `\vert` for absolute value in LaTeX — never use `\|` in markdown tables.
- ☐ **Governing standards:** AIS-001 (lane equipment), CMVR 96 (brakes), CMVR 104 (headlights), AIS-008 (headlamps).
- ☐ Roller diameters: **260 mm** (passenger cars) and **350 mm** (heavy commercial vehicles).
- ☐ The vehicle testing lane is a **mandatory fitness test** for all commercial vehicles in India.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Engine Analysers for Petrol and Diesel Engines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT724 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Engine Analysers for Petrol and Diesel Engines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engine Analyser (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> An **engine analyser** is a comprehensive, integrated electronic diagnostic instrument system used to monitor, measure, and evaluate the performance, combustion quality, and emission characteristics of internal combustion (IC) engines. For **petrol (SI) engines**, the analyser primarily evaluates ignition timing, spark quality, cylinder balance, and exhaust gas composition. For **diesel (CI) engines**, it focuses on injection timing, compression health, smoke density, and combustion pressure.

These instruments consolidate multiple diagnostic functions (tachometer, dwell meter, oscilloscope, exhaust gas analyser, etc.) into a single chassis, enabling rapid condition assessment without disassembling the engine.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Think of an **engine analyser as a doctor's diagnostic kit for a car engine**:

| Human Body | Engine (Vehicle) |
|------------|------------------|
| Stethoscope | **Stethoscope probe** (mechanical noise/vibration) |
| Blood test report | **Exhaust gas analyser** (CO, HC, CO₂, O₂, NOx) |
| ECG/EKG | **Oscilloscope** (ignition waveform / injector pulse) |
| Thermometer | **Pyrometer** (exhaust gas temperature) |
| Heart rate monitor | **Tachometer** (engine RPM) |
| Spirometer (lung test) | **Smoke meter** (diesel opacity) |

Just as a doctor can tell a lot about a patient without surgery, an engine analyser reveals the "health" of an engine by reading its electrical, chemical, and mechanical signatures **non-invasively**.

## 1.3 Classification Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Broad Categories of Engine Analysers in KTU 2024 Module 1:**
> 1. **Petrol (SI) Engine Analysers** — focused on ignition, combustion smoothness, and tailpipe emissions.
> 2. **Diesel (CI) Engine Analysers** — focused on injection, compression, smoke, and combustion pressure.
> Both share some common modules (tachometer, temperature probe, pressure gauge) but differ sharply in emission and combustion sub-systems due to the fundamental difference in ignition mechanism (spark vs. compression).

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (Petrol):** **14.7 : 1** (mass basis)
- **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (Diesel):** **14.5 : 1** (mass basis)
- **Universal Gas Constant:** R = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- **Standard atmospheric pressure:** P₀ = **101.325 kPa**
- **Opacity measurement wavelength (smoke meter):** Typically **550 nm** (green light, per ISO 11614)
- **NOx formation threshold temperature:** **~1370 K** (Zeldovich mechanism)
- **Standard exhaust gas temperature (petrol):** **400 °C – 600 °C**
- **Standard exhaust gas temperature (diesel):** **300 °C – 500 °C**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Petrol (SI) Engine Analyser — Sub-Systems

A modern petrol engine analyser integrates the following sub-modules:

1. **Ignition Analyser (Cylinder-by-Cylinder Scope)**
   - Displays primary & secondary ignition waveforms.
   - Reveals: spark plug fouling, open/short circuits, coil condition.
2. **Exhaust Gas Analyser (4-Gas or 5-Gas)**
   - Measures CO, HC, CO₂, O₂ (4-gas) and additionally NOx (5-gas).
   - Uses **NDIR (Non-Dispersive Infrared)** for CO/CO₂/HC, **electrochemical cell** for O₂/NOx.
3. **Engine RPM / Dwell Tachometer**
   - Inductive pickup on HT lead or clamp on battery cable.
4. **Cylinder Power Balance Tester**
   - Sequentially disables each cylinder; measures RPM drop to detect weak cylinders.
5. **Vacuum / Pressure Gauge**
   - Manifold vacuum diagnostics.
6. **Coolant / Oil Temperature Probes**
7. **Battery / Charging System Tester**

## 2.2 Diesel (CI) Engine Analyser — Sub-Systems

1. **Diesel Smoke Meter (Opacimeter)**
   - Measures **opacity (%)** or **K (m⁻¹) coefficient** of exhaust smoke.
   - Types: **Full-flow** (Hartridge) and **Partial-flow** (Bosch).
2. **Compression Tester**
   - Reads peak cylinder pressure (typically **25–35 bar** for healthy diesel).
3. **Injection Timing Tester (Diesel Timing Light)**
   - Clamps on injector line; strobe light indicates timing advance/retard.
4. **Injector Nozzle Tester / Pop Tester**
   - Measures injection spray pattern and opening pressure.
5. **Diesel Exhaust Gas Analyser**
   - Measures **NOx, CO, CO₂, O₂, PM (particulate matter)**.
6. **Common-Rail Pressure Tester**
   - Monitors rail pressure (typically **200–2000 bar** for CRDI systems).
7. **Dynamometer Interface** (for loaded testing).

## 2.3 Working Principle — Logical Steps

### A. Petrol Engine — Ignition Waveform Analysis

1. **Step 1:** Inductive clamp on the HT (High Tension) lead picks up the **high-voltage pulse** (typically **10–35 kV**) at each spark event.
2. **Step 2:** The pulse is conditioned by an **attenuator** and fed to the analyser oscilloscope.
3. **Step 3:** A **healthy waveform** shows:
   - Firing voltage spike (peak)
   - Spark line (burn time: ~1.5 ms)
   - Dwell section
   - Oscillation tail.
4. **Step 4:** Pattern deviations (tall spike, no spark line, sloping spark line) indicate **plug wear, coil failure, or open circuits**.

### B. Diesel Engine — Smoke Opacity Measurement

1. **Step 1:** Exhaust gas sample is drawn into a measuring chamber.
2. **Step 2:** A collimated **light beam** (LED/laser at ~550 nm) passes through the sample.
3. **Step 3:** A photodetector on the opposite side measures **transmitted light intensity I**.
4. **Step 4:** **Opacity N(%)** is computed as the ratio of blocked light to incident light.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulae below are **board-exam essentials** for Module 1 of OEMUT724.

| Sl. No. | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|:-:|:--|:--|:-:|:--|
| 1 | Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR) | $AFR = \dfrac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}}$ | dimensionless | Stoich. petrol: 14.7; Diesel: 14.5 |
| 2 | Equivalence Ratio | $\lambda = \dfrac{AFR_{actual}}{AFR_{stoich}}$ | dimensionless | $\lambda \lt 1$ = rich; $\lambda \gt 1$ = lean |
| 3 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_{v} = \dfrac{m_{a,actual}}{m_{a,theoretical}} = \dfrac{m_{a,actual}}{\rho_{a} \, V_{d}}$ | % | Petrol: 80–95 %; Diesel: 85–92 % |
| 4 | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | $BSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{f}}{P_{b}} \times 3600$ | kg/kWh | Petrol: ~0.30; Diesel: ~0.25 |
| 5 | Brake Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{b,th} = \dfrac{P_{b}}{\dot{m}_{f} \times CV}$ | % | Petrol: 25–30 %; Diesel: 30–35 % |
| 6 | Smoke Opacity | $N(\%) = \left(1 - \dfrac{I}{I_{0}}\right) \times 100$ | % | $I_0$ = incident intensity, $I$ = transmitted |
| 7 | Smoke Absorption Coefficient | $K = -\dfrac{1}{L} \ln\left(\dfrac{I}{I_{0}}\right)$ | m⁻¹ | L = optical path length (typ. 0.3 m) |
| 8 | NOx Formation (Zeldovich) | Rate $\propto e^{\left(-E_{a}/RT\right)}$ | mol/s | $E_{a} \approx 251$ kJ/mol for O + N₂ |
| 9 | Lambda from O₂ reading | $\lambda \approx \dfrac{20.9}{20.9 - \%O_{2,exh}}$ | dimensionless | Approximation for petrol (SI) |
| 10 | Manifold Vacuum (petrol) | $P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{manifold}$ | kPa | Healthy idle: 50–65 kPa vacuum |
| 11 | Compression Pressure (diesel) | $P_{comp} = P_{1} \times r^{n_{1}}$ | bar | r = compression ratio, n₁ = polytropic index |
| 12 | Cylinder Pressure Indicated | $P_{ind} = \dfrac{W_{ind}}{V_{s}}$ | kPa | $W_{ind}$ from P-θ diagram |
| 13 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{P_{b}}{P_{i}}$ | % | Petrol: 85–90 %; Diesel: 80–85 % |
| 14 | Cylinder Imbalance | $\%\Delta RPM = \dfrac{RPM_{all} - RPM_{i}}{RPM_{all}} \times 100$ | % | < 5 % = healthy |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Substitution Rule:** Always use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of the vertical pipe `\vert x \vert` inside table cells — never use the raw `\vert` symbol which breaks markdown table parsing.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Service Workshops:** Engine analysers reduce diagnosis time from hours to minutes.
- **Emission Testing Centres:** Mandatory in most countries (e.g., India **BS-VI**, Euro 6).
- **R&D Departments:** Used on engine dynamometers for calibration of ECU maps.
- **Quality Control (OEM Plants):** Every engine off the production line is analyser-tested before shipping.
- **Emission Control Compliance:** Data logged for regulatory submission to pollution control boards (e.g., **CPCB**, **EPA**).
- **Telematics & Connected Vehicles:** Modern analysers are cloud-linked for **remote diagnostics**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio for Petrol (C₈H₁₈, Iso-Octane)

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is **frequently asked** as a Part-A or sub-part of Part-B questions.

### Combustion Equation

The complete combustion of iso-octane is:

$$ C_{8}H_{18} + a \left(O_{2} + 3.76 N_{2}\right) \rightarrow b \, CO_{2} + c \, H_{2}O + 3.76 a \, N_{2} $$

### Step 1 — Carbon Balance

Each C atom in fuel must form one CO₂:

$$ b = 8 \quad \text{(8 carbons yield 8 CO}_2\text{)} $$

### Step 2 — Hydrogen Balance

Each 2 H atoms form one H₂O:

$$ 2c = 18 \Rightarrow c = 9 \quad \text{(9 water molecules)} $$

### Step 3 — Oxygen Balance

Total O atoms in products = 2(8) + 9 = 25 (for CO₂ and H₂O).

$$ 2a = 25 \Rightarrow a = 12.5 \quad \text{(oxygen moles required)} $$

### Step 4 — Air Required (by mass)

Mass of fuel:
$$ m_{fuel} = 8(12) + 18(1) = 96 + 18 = 114 \text{ kg/kmol} $$

Mass of air (molecular weight ≈ 28.97 kg/kmol):
$$ m_{air} = 12.5 \times 32 + 12.5 \times 3.76 \times 28 = 400 + 1320.8 = 1720.8 \text{ kg} $$

Wait — re-do the air mass:

$$ m_{air} = a \times 32 + a \times 3.76 \times 28 = 12.5(32) + 12.5(3.76)(28) $$

$$ = 400 + 12.5 \times 105.28 = 400 + 1316 = 1716 \text{ kg} $$

### Step 5 — Stoichiometric AFR

$$
\begin{aligned}
AFR_{stoich} &= \dfrac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}} \\
&= \dfrac{1716}{114} \\
&\approx 15.05
\end{aligned}
$$

> For **real petrol** (a blend of paraffins, aromatics, naphthenes), the accepted value is **14.7 : 1**, while the *theoretical* iso-octane value is **~15.05 : 1**. Examiners accept **14.7** unless they specify the exact fuel.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Smoke Opacity ↔ Absorption Coefficient Relationship

### Step 1 — Define Opacity

By Beer-Lambert law, the transmitted intensity after passing through a smoke column of length $L$ is:

$$ I = I_{0} \, e^{-K L} $$

where $K$ = absorption coefficient (m⁻¹).

### Step 2 — Express Transmittance

$$ \dfrac{I}{I_{0}} = e^{-KL} $$

### Step 3 — Define Opacity N

Opacity is the **fraction of light blocked**:

$$ N = 1 - \dfrac{I}{I_{0}} = 1 - e^{-KL} $$

### Step 4 — Solve for K (Absorption Coefficient)

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$
\begin{aligned}
e^{-KL} &= 1 - N \\
-KL &= \ln(1 - N) \\
K &= -\dfrac{1}{L} \ln(1 - N)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Numerical Validation

Assume: $L = 0.3$ m, $N = 40\% = 0.40$ (typical for moderately loaded diesel).

$$
\begin{aligned}
K &= -\dfrac{1}{0.3} \ln(1 - 0.40) \\
&= -\dfrac{1}{0.3} \ln(0.60) \\
&= -\dfrac{1}{0.3} \times (-0.5108) \\
&= 1.703 \text{ m}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Indian BS-VI limit (diesel):** Smoke opacity $\le$ **0.7 m⁻¹ (K)** under free acceleration (ELR test) per AIS-137. KTU board questions often test this conversion.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Brake Power from Engine Analyser Data

### Step 1 — Energy Input Rate (Fuel)

$$ \dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{m}_{f} \times CV \quad \text{[kW]} $$

### Step 2 — Indicated Power (from P-θ Diagram)

The indicated work per cycle is area under the P-θ curve:

$$ W_{ind} = \oint P \, dV \quad \text{[J/cycle]} $$

For an engine running at $N$ rpm with $n_c$ cylinders:

$$ P_{i} = \dfrac{W_{ind} \times N \times n_c}{60 \times 2} \quad \text{[W]} $$

(The factor of 2 accounts for 4-stroke cycle — one working stroke per 2 revolutions.)

### Step 3 — Brake Power

$$ P_{b} = P_{i} - P_{f} $$

where $P_f$ = friction power (measured by motoring test).

### Step 4 — BSFC and Brake Thermal Efficiency

$$
\begin{aligned}
BSFC &= \dfrac{\dot{m}_{f}}{P_{b}} \quad \text{[kg/kWh]} \\
\eta_{b,th} &= \dfrac{P_{b}}{\dot{m}_{f} \times CV} \times 100 \quad [\%]
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Numerical Example

Given: $\dot{m}_{f} = 2$ kg/h, $P_b = 6$ kW, $CV = 43$ MJ/kg (diesel).

$$
\begin{aligned}
BSFC &= \dfrac{2}{6} = 0.333 \text{ kg/kWh} \\
\eta_{b,th} &= \dfrac{6}{2 \times 43/3600} \times 100 = \dfrac{6}{0.02389} \times 100 \approx 25.1 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Petrol vs Diesel Engine Analyser Comparison

| Feature | Petrol (SI) Engine Analyser | Diesel (CI) Engine Analyser |
|:--|:--|:--|
| **Primary Diagnostic Focus** | Ignition, spark quality | Injection, compression |
| **Key Waveform Tool** | Ignition oscilloscope (HT lead) | Injector pop tester, rail pressure |
| **Main Emission Target** | CO, HC, unburnt fuel | NOx, Particulate Matter (PM/soot) |
| **Smoke Measurement** | Not applicable (no visible smoke normally) | Opacimeter / Smoke meter (K in m⁻¹) |
| **Cylinder Pressure (Healthy)** | 8 – 12 bar | 25 – 35 bar |
| **Idle RPM** | 700 – 900 rpm | 600 – 800 rpm |
| **Stoichiometric AFR** | 14.7 : 1 | 14.5 : 1 |
| **Compression Ratio Range** | 8 : 1 to 12 : 1 | 14 : 1 to 22 : 1 |
| **Combustion Sensor** | Knock sensor (piezo) | Glow plug pressure transducer |
| **Common Emission Standard** | Euro 6 / BS-VI (Petrol) | Euro 6 / BS-VI (Diesel) |
| **Key Health Indicator** | Lambda ($\lambda$) from O₂ sensor | Smoke opacity (N) and rail pressure |

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Equivalence Ratio from Exhaust Oxygen (Petrol)

### Step 1 — Approximate Combustion Stoichiometry

For a rich mixture, excess fuel leaves unburnt HC and consumes O₂. For a lean mixture, O₂ remains.

### Step 2 — Empirical Relation

For petrol engines with no major exhaust leaks:

$$ \lambda \approx \dfrac{20.9}{20.9 - \%O_{2,exh}} $$

### Step 3 — Derivation Outline

Mass balance of O₂ in exhaust gives the relation. For a more accurate form using **Spindt's formula** (all 4 gases):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= \dfrac{\left[CO_{2}\right] + \dfrac{\left[CO\right]}{2} + \left[H_{2}O\right] + \left[NO\right] + \dots}{\left[CO_{2}\right] + \left[CO\right] + \left[H_{2}O\right]} \times \dfrac{1}{stoich}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Worked Example

Measured exhaust O₂ = **2 %**. Find $\lambda$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= \dfrac{20.9}{20.9 - 2} = \dfrac{20.9}{18.9} \approx 1.106
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\lambda > 1$, the mixture is **lean**. The engine analyser would flag a possible vacuum leak or faulty MAF sensor.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Architecture — Petrol Engine Analyser

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Engine Under Test SI] --> B[HT Pickup Inductive Clamp]
    A --> C[O2 Lambda Sensor Inbuilt]
    A --> D[Manifold Vacuum Tap]
    A --> E[Tach Pulse Injector]
    B --> F[Signal Conditioning and Attenuator]
    F --> G[Dual Channel Oscilloscope]
    G --> H[Mains Isolation and Microcontroller]
    C --> I[NDIR Exhaust Gas Analyser 4 Gas 5 Gas]
    I --> J[CO HC CO2 O2 NOx Display]
    D --> K[Vacuum Transducer]
    E --> L[RPM and Dwell Module]
    H --> M[Central LCD Display and Printer]
    I --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Data Output RS232 USB Ethernet]
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture — Diesel Engine Analyser

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Diesel Engine Under Test CI] --> B[Smoke Sampling Probe Partial Flow]
    A --> C[Compression Test Adapter Glow Plug Bore]
    A --> D[Injector Pulse Pickup Piezo Inductive]
    A --> E[Rail Pressure Sensor Tap]
    A --> F[Tach and Timing Light Strobe]
    B --> G[Optical Chamber 550nm LED and Photodiode]
    G --> H[Opacity and Absorption Coefficient Calculator]
    H --> I[Central Processor ARM Cortex]
    C --> J[Pressure Transducer 50 bar]
    J --> I
    D --> K[Injector Pulse Width and Frequency Analyser]
    K --> I
    E --> L[Rail Pressure Display 2000 bar]
    L --> I
    F --> M[Timing Advance and Retard Display]
    M --> I
    I --> N[TFT Display and Data Logger]
    N --> O[CAN Bus Output and PC Interface]
```

## 4.3 Decision Logic Flow — Diagnostic Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Diagnostic] --> B{Fuel Type}
    B -- Petrol SI --> C[Connect HT Clamp and O2 Sensor]
    C --> D[Idle and Capture Waveform]
    D --> E{Waveform Healthy}
    E -- Yes --> F[Capture 4 Gas Exhaust]
    E -- No --> G[Flag Misfire and Report]
    F --> H{CO percent in Range}
    H -- Yes --> I[Pass]
    H -- No --> J[Flag Catalyst or Lambda Fault]
    B -- Diesel CI --> K[Connect Smoke Probe and Compression Adapter]
    K --> L[Run Free Acceleration Test]
    L --> M{Opacity below Limit}
    M -- Yes --> N[Pass]
    M -- No --> O[Flag Injector or Air Filter Fault]
    I --> P[End Report]
    J --> P
    G --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
```

## 4.4 Ignition Waveform — Healthy vs Faulty (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HEALTHY[Healthy Waveform]
        H1[Firing Voltage Spike 12 kV] --> H2[Spark Line 1.5 ms]
        H2 --> H3[Dwell Charging Section]
        H3 --> H4[Oscillation Tail Damped]
    end
    subgraph FAULTY[Faulty Waveform - Fouled Plug]
        F1[Low Firing Spike 6 kV] --> F2[Distorted Spark Line]
        F2 --> F3[Erratic Dwell Section]
        F3 --> F4[High Frequency Oscillations]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> The waveform shapes are represented as logical flow nodes; in physical analyser screens they appear as voltage-time traces on the oscilloscope. The healthy waveform should always exhibit **a sharp rise, a flat spark line, and damped oscillations within 5 cycles** — anything else is diagnostic.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define an engine analyser. List its major sub-systems for a petrol engine. `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** An engine analyser is an integrated electronic diagnostic instrument used to monitor and evaluate the performance and emission health of an IC engine non-invasively.
>
> **Major Sub-Systems for Petrol (SI) Engine (2 marks):**
> 1. Ignition oscilloscope (primary/secondary waveform).
> 2. Exhaust gas analyser (CO, HC, CO₂, O₂, NOx via NDIR and electrochemical cells).
> 3. Tachometer with dwell meter.
> 4. Cylinder power balance module.
> 5. Manifold vacuum gauge.

---

### Question 2: What is a smoke meter? Name two types used in diesel engine analysis. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** A smoke meter (opacimeter) is a device that measures the opacity of diesel engine exhaust gases by passing a light beam through a sampled exhaust column and detecting the reduction in transmitted light intensity.
>
> **Two Types (1.5 marks):**
> 1. **Full-flow smoke meter** (e.g., **Hartridge opacimeter**) — entire exhaust passes through.
> 2. **Partial-flow smoke meter** (e.g., **Bosch smoke meter**) — sample of exhaust is drawn into the chamber.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall:**
> - Students often confuse **opacity N(%)** with **absorption coefficient K (m⁻¹)** — these are **not the same** and are related by Beer-Lambert. The conversion equation $K = -\frac{1}{L}\ln(1 - N)$ is **worth 2–3 marks** in board exams. Skipping this step is the #1 reason students lose marks on smoke meter questions.
> - Many students write "AFR of diesel = 14.7" — **wrong**. Petrol = 14.7, Diesel = **14.5** (mass basis).
> - Always state the **units** for K (m⁻¹) and N (%). Examiners explicitly deduct 0.5–1 mark for missing units.
> - In the ignition waveform question, students frequently forget to mention the **three main parts**: firing line, spark line, and dwell section.

### Question A: Petrol Engine Analyser — Sub-Systems and Exhaust Gas Analysis `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
With a neat block diagram, explain the various sub-systems of a **petrol engine analyser**. Discuss the working of the **exhaust gas analyser (4-gas)** in detail.

**Model Answer — Part (a):**

1. **Block Diagram (3 marks):** *(Refer to Section 4.1 architecture.)*
   - HT clamp → Oscilloscope
   - Lambda sensor → NDIR analyser
   - Vacuum tap → Pressure transducer
   - Tach pickup → RPM module
   - All → Central processor → Display

2. **Exhaust Gas Analyser (4-Gas) — Working (4 marks):**
   - **CO and CO₂** are measured using **NDIR (Non-Dispersive Infrared)** sensors: each gas absorbs IR at characteristic wavelengths.
   - **HC** is measured using **FID (Flame Ionization Detector)** in some analysers, or NDIR in 4-gas units.
   - **O₂** is measured using an **electrochemical cell** (zirconia or galvanic).
   - The sample is drawn from the tailpipe via a pump, filtered, and passed through the sensor chambers.
   - Output: digital readout in **% volume** (CO, CO₂, O₂) or **ppm** (HC).

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
A petrol engine running at 3000 rpm and 60 % load has the following exhaust gas composition: **CO = 4 %, CO₂ = 12 %, O₂ = 2 %, HC = 250 ppm**. Calculate the **equivalence ratio** and **air-fuel ratio** by mass. Comment on the mixture condition.

**Model Answer — Part (b):**

Given:
- CO = 4 %, CO₂ = 12 %, O₂ = 2 %, HC = 250 ppm
- Stoichiometric AFR for petrol = 14.7

**Step 1: Use Spindt's relation (simplified 4-gas form).**

$$ \lambda \approx \dfrac{20.9}{20.9 - \%O_{2}} = \dfrac{20.9}{20.9 - 2} = \dfrac{20.9}{18.9} \approx 1.106 $$

**Step 2: AFR by mass.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
AFR_{actual} &= \lambda \times AFR_{stoich} \\
&= 1.106 \times 14.7 \\
&\approx 16.26
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Comment.**

Since $\lambda \approx 1.11 > 1$, the mixture is **lean**. A **lean burn** of this magnitude (≈ 11 % excess air) at 60 % load suggests a possible **vacuum leak**, **faulty MAF sensor**, or **weak fuel pump**. A 4 % CO reading is on the **high side** for a lean mixture, which may indicate a **catalyst fault** (catalyst unable to oxidize CO efficiently).

> *(Cross-check using fuller Spindt: $\lambda = \frac{CO_2 + CO/2 + H_2O}{CO_2 + CO + H_2O} \cdot \frac{1}{stoich}$ gives similar order — examiners accept the O₂-approximation form.)*

---

### Question B: Diesel Engine Analyser — Smoke Meter and Compression Testing `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
Explain with a block diagram the working of a **diesel smoke meter (partial-flow type)**. Define **opacity** and **absorption coefficient** and derive the relation between them.

**Model Answer — Part (a):**

1. **Block Diagram (3 marks):** *(Refer to Section 4.2 — Smoke Probe → Optical Chamber → Photodiode → Processor.)*
   - Sample probe draws exhaust from tailpipe.
   - Optical chamber with 550 nm LED source and matched photodetector.
   - Signal conditioned → Microcontroller computes opacity (N) and absorption coefficient (K).
   - Display: % opacity and m⁻¹ K simultaneously.

2. **Definitions and Derivation (4 marks):**

> **Opacity N (%):** The percentage of incident light blocked by the smoke column.
> $$ N(\%) = \left(1 - \frac{I}{I_0}\right) \times 100 $$

> **Absorption Coefficient K (m⁻¹):** A measure of how strongly the smoke absorbs light per unit path length.

> **Derivation (Beer-Lambert Law):**
> Starting from $I = I_0 e^{-KL}$:
> $$ \frac{I}{I_0} = e^{-KL} $$
> $$ 1 - N = e^{-KL} $$
> Taking the natural log:
> $$ \ln(1 - N) = -KL $$
> $$ \boxed{K = -\frac{1}{L}\ln(1 - N)} $$

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
A diesel engine undergoing a **free acceleration test** records a smoke opacity of **65 %** through a **partial-flow meter** with optical path length **0.43 m**. Calculate the **absorption coefficient K** and state whether the engine passes the **BS-VI ELR (Effective Load Response) limit of K = 1.2 m⁻¹**.

**Model Answer — Part (b):**

Given:
- N = 65 % = 0.65
- L = 0.43 m
- Limit: K ≤ 1.2 m⁻¹ (BS-VI)

**Step 1: Apply the relation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K &= -\dfrac{1}{L}\ln(1 - N) \\
&= -\dfrac{1}{0.43}\ln(1 - 0.65) \\
&= -\dfrac{1}{0.43}\ln(0.35)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Evaluate the logarithm.**

$$ \ln(0.35) = -1.0498 $$

**Step 3: Compute K.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K &= -\dfrac{1}{0.43} \times (-1.0498) \\
&= \dfrac{1.0498}{0.43} \\
&\approx 2.44 \text{ m}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Verdict.**

Since **K = 2.44 m⁻¹ > 1.2 m⁻¹**, the engine **fails** the BS-VI ELR test. Probable causes: **clogged air filter**, **injector over-fuelling**, **worn piston rings** (blow-by), or **turbocharger failure**. The vehicle should be rejected for emission certification and sent for mechanical rectification.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Points to Remember for OEMUT724 Module 1:**

- **Engine analyser** = integrated electronic diagnostic instrument; non-invasive; multi-function (tach, scope, gas analyser, etc.).
- **Petrol analyser** focuses on **ignition waveform** and **tailpipe emissions** (CO, HC, CO₂, O₂, NOx).
- **Diesel analyser** focuses on **smoke opacity**, **injection timing**, **compression pressure**, and **rail pressure**.
- **Healthy ignition waveform** = sharp firing spike (~12 kV) + flat spark line (~1.5 ms) + damped oscillation tail.
- **Healthy cylinder pressure** = petrol: 8–12 bar; diesel: 25–35 bar.
- **Stoichiometric AFR:** Petrol = **14.7**, Diesel = **14.5**.
- **Equivalence ratio λ = 1** at stoich; **λ > 1 = lean**, **λ < 1 = rich**.
- **Lambda from O₂:** $\lambda \approx 20.9 / (20.9 - \%O_2)$.
- **Opacity N (%) = (1 - I/I₀) × 100.**
- **Absorption coefficient K (m⁻¹) = -(1/L) × ln(1 - N).**
- **BS-VI diesel smoke limit (ELR):** K ≤ 1.2 m⁻¹.
- **NDIR sensor** measures CO, CO₂, HC; **electrochemical cell** measures O₂; **Zirconia** for λ probe; **FID** for trace HC.
- **Smoke meter types:** Full-flow (Hartridge) and Partial-flow (Bosch).
- **BSFC** = fuel per unit brake energy; petrol ≈ 0.30, diesel ≈ 0.25 kg/kWh.
- **Brake thermal efficiency:** petrol 25–30 %, diesel 30–35 %.
- **Volumetric efficiency:** petrol 80–95 %, diesel 85–92 %.
- **Cylinder power balance test:** disables one cylinder at a time; > 5 % RPM drop = weak cylinder.
- **NOx formation threshold:** ~1370 K (Zeldovich mechanism).
- **Typical exhaust gas temperature:** petrol 400–600 °C, diesel 300–500 °C.
- **Common rail pressure (CRDI):** 200–2000 bar.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "DIP-CORN":** **D**iagnostic, **I**gnition, **P**ressure, **C**ombustion, **O**pacity, **R**PM, **N**Ox — the seven pillars of every engine analyser report.
