## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Exhaust Gas Analysis — Formal Definition
**Exhaust Gas Analysis** is the quantitative chemical and optical determination of the combustion products leaving an internal combustion (IC) engine, in order to assess the **completeness of combustion, fuel economy, emission levels, and compliance with statutory pollution norms**. Three standard instruments covered in this module are:

1. **Orsat Apparatus** – wet chemical absorption method (CO₂, O₂, CO).
2. **Infrared (IR) Gas Analyser** – non-dispersive IR (NDIR) technique for CO, CO₂, HC.
3. **Smoke Meter** – optical opacity / obscuration measurement for diesel particulates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive:** Orsat is studied primarily for **boilers and IC-engine exhaust CO₂/CO/O₂ trends**, NDIR is the modern **automotive dynamometer analyser**, and the **Hartridge/Bosch smoke meter** is mandatory for diesel homologation under Indian Driving Cycle (IDC) and Bharat Stage (BS-VI) certification.

### 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Doctor's Check-up for an Engine" Analogy
Imagine your engine is a **human patient visiting a doctor**:

| Medical Equivalent | Engine Equivalent | Instrument |
|---|---|---|
| Spirometer (lung test) | O₂ consumed | Orsat (O₂ pipette) |
| Blood CO₂ test | CO₂ exhaled | Orsat (KOH pipette) |
| COHb blood test | CO toxicity | Orsat (Cu₂Cl₂ pipette) |
| Pulse oximeter (red/IR light through finger) | IR absorption by gases | NDIR Analyser |
| Chest X-ray opacity | Smoke density | Smoke Meter |

Just as a doctor never uses only one test, a **vehicle emission testing cell uses all three** instruments at different stages of certification.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Conditions:** All volumetric gas measurements in Orsat are **reduced to NTP — Normal Temperature (0 °C = 273.15 K) and Pressure (1.01325 bar = 101.325 kPa)**, also called **STP (Standard T & P)** in exhaust engineering.

### 1.3 Why Three Different Instruments?
- **Orsat** → **cheap, lab-grade, batch**; ideal for steady-state boiler/plant work; *not* real-time.
- **NDIR** → **fast (response < 1 s)**, portable, on-board compatible; ideal for **tailpipe testing**.
- **Smoke Meter** → **particulate/soot** measurement is optical (not chemical); smoke is a *solid suspension*, not a gas.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "K-C-P-N":** The Orsat absorption sequence is **K**OH (CO₂) → **C**uprous chloride (CO) → **P**yrogallol (O₂) → **N**₂ by difference.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Orsat Apparatus

#### 2.1.1 Construction (Block-Level Architecture)
The Orsat apparatus consists of:

- **Water-Jacketed Gas Burette (100 mL)** — maintains gas at constant temperature.
- **Levelling Bottle** — filled with **5% H₂SO₄ + NaCl** (acidified brine) as the *confining liquid*; this liquid does **not** absorb any of the flue gases.
- **Three Absorption Pipettes** (in series, connected via a glass manifold):
  * **Pipette 1 — KOH (Potassium Hydroxide)** → absorbs **CO₂**.
  * **Pipette 2 — Cuprous Chloride (Cu₂Cl₂) in HCl** → absorbs **CO** (and olefins).
  * **Pipette 3 — Pyrogallol (C₆H₃(OH)₃) in KOH** → absorbs **O₂**.
- **Four-Way Cock** — connects the burette to (a) atmosphere, (b) sample supply, (c) absorption pipettes, (d) bypass.

#### 2.1.2 Working Principle (Step-by-Step)
1. The flue-gas sample is drawn into the burette by lowering the **levelling bottle** (creating a vacuum).
2. Exactly **100 mL** of gas is isolated in the burette.
3. The gas is forced (by raising the levelling bottle) into **Pipette 1 (KOH)** and held there for ~1 minute; KOH absorbs CO₂.
4. The residual gas is pulled back into the burette, and the **volume reduction = % CO₂**.
5. The same gas is then sent to **Pipette 2 (Cu₂Cl₂)** → reduction in volume = % CO.
6. Finally sent to **Pipette 3 (Pyrogallol)** → reduction = % O₂.
7. **N₂ % = 100 − (% CO₂ + % CO + % O₂)** — by difference.

#### 2.1.3 Governing Absorption Reactions

$$
\text{CO}_2 + 2\,\text{KOH} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{K}_2\text{CO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

$$
\text{Cu}_2\text{Cl}_2 + 2\,\text{CO} + 4\,\text{HCl} \;\longrightarrow\; 2\,\text{CuCl}\cdot 2\text{CO} + 2\,\text{HCl}
$$

$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_3(\text{OH})_3 + 3\,\text{KOH} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{C}_6\text{H}_3(\text{OK})_3 + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

$$
2\,\text{C}_6\text{H}_3(\text{OK})_3 + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{O}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; (\text{KO})_3\text{C}_6\text{H}_2\text{–C}_6\text{H}_2(\text{OK})_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this order?** KOH absorbs only CO₂; Cu₂Cl₂ absorbs only CO (acidic gas); pyrogallol is highly specific to O₂. Reversing the order corrupts the readings because KOH will also retain traces of CO.

### 2.2 Infrared (NDIR) Gas Analyser

#### 2.2.1 Physical Principle
Every heteroatomic gas (**CO, CO₂, HC, NO, SO₂**) has a **characteristic infrared absorption fingerprint** because vibrational-rotational transitions of the molecular dipole absorb specific IR wavelengths. For example:
- **CO** → 4.67 µm
- **CO₂** → 4.26 µm and 14.99 µm
- **CH₄ (HC)** → 3.31 µm and 7.65 µm

**Beer's Law** governs the absorption:

$$
I \;=\; I_0 \, e^{-\,\alpha\, c\, L}
$$

where $I$ is transmitted intensity, $I_0$ is incident intensity, $\alpha$ is the absorption coefficient (m²/kg), $c$ is gas concentration (kg/m³), and $L$ is optical path length (m).

#### 2.2.2 Construction (Optical Train)
1. **IR Source** — heated filament (Ni-Cr or tungsten), modulated at a fixed chopper frequency (typically 5–10 Hz).
2. **Beam Splitter / Chopper Wheel** — splits beam into sample and reference paths, AC-modulates for noise rejection.
3. **Sample Cell** — flowing exhaust gas, path length 5–250 mm.
4. **Reference Cell** — sealed with **zero gas (pure N₂)**.
5. **Optical Filter** — narrow bandpass at the target gas wavelength.
6. **Detector** — **pyroelectric** or **microphone (Luft detector)** with two chambers separated by a *capacitive* diaphragm.
7. **Signal Processor / Display** — converts detector output to **ppm or % volume**.

> [!TIP]
> The **"Non-Dispersive"** label means there is **no prism/grating** to disperse wavelengths — the filter alone selects the wavelength. This makes NDIR rugged, cheap, and fast.

#### 2.2.3 Detector Operation (Luft Detector)
The two gas-filled chambers (one filled with the target gas, the other with the reference) heat up differentially when IR passes through the sample cell. This pressure difference oscillates the **capacitor diaphragm**, producing an AC signal proportional to the gas concentration.

### 2.3 Smoke Meter

#### 2.3.1 The Physics of Diesel Smoke
Diesel smoke is a **colloidal suspension of solid carbon (soot) particles** (0.05–1.0 µm) in gases. Because it is a *particulate*, **chemical absorption analysers cannot measure it** — we must use **optical extinction**.

#### 2.3.2 Two Standard Designs

**A) Hartridge Smoke Meter (Full-Flow, Dynamic, Opacity)**
- Exhaust flows **continuously** through a measuring chamber.
- A **green light beam** (or white) is projected across the chamber; a photodetector on the opposite side measures transmitted intensity.
- The chamber length is fixed (~**430 mm**); no filter paper is used.
- Output: **% Opacity** (also called Hartridge Units, 0–100%).

**B) Bosch Smoke Meter (Spot-Sample, Filter-Paper Reflectance)**
- A **fixed volume** of exhaust is drawn through a **white filter paper** (Whatman grade).
- Smoke particles deposit and darken the paper.
- A **reflectance photometer** measures the reflectance $R$ of the soiled filter relative to a clean reference $R_0$.
- Output: **Bosch Smoke Number (BSN)** ranging from **0 (clean) to 10 (black)**.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Range | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | % CO₂ by Orsat | $\Delta V_1 / V_{\text{orig}} \times 100$ | vol % | Orsat step 1 |
| 2 | % CO by Orsat | $\Delta V_2 / V_{\text{orig}} \times 100$ | vol % | Orsat step 2 |
| 3 | % O₂ by Orsat | $\Delta V_3 / V_{\text{orig}} \times 100$ | vol % | Orsat step 3 |
| 4 | % N₂ (by difference) | $100 - (\text{CO}_2 + \text{CO} + \text{O}_2)$ | vol % | Mass balance |
| 5 | Actual air supplied (stoich. air = 1) | $A_{\text{actual}} = \dfrac{\text{N}_2}{3.77} \Big/ \left(\text{CO}_2 + \tfrac{\text{CO}}{2}\right) \cdot 1$ | ratio | Combustion air |
| 6 | % Excess Air | $\dfrac{\text{O}_2 \times 100}{21 - \text{O}_2}$ (approx) | % | Boiler tuning |
| 7 | Beer's Law (NDIR) | $I = I_0 e^{-\alpha c L}$ | — | IR transmission |
| 8 | Transmittance | $T = I / I_0$ | 0–1 | Optical sensors |
| 9 | Opacity | $N = (1 - T) \times 100$ | % | Hartridge |
| 10 | Bosch Smoke Number | $\text{BSN} = \left(1 - R/R_0\right) \times 10$ | 0–10 | Bosch meter |
| 11 | Light extinction coefficient | $k = -\tfrac{1}{L}\ln(T)$ | m⁻¹ | Beer-Lambert |
| 12 | Equivalence ratio (from Orsat) | $\phi = \dfrac{\text{CO}_2}{(\text{CO}_2 + \text{CO})}$ | 0–1 | Combustion quality |
| 13 | NDIR output voltage | $V \propto c \cdot L$ | V/ppm·m | Calibration |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Air-Fuel Ratio (stoichiometric constant 3.77)** comes from the volumetric ratio **N₂ : O₂ = 79 : 21 = 3.76 ≈ 3.77** in atmospheric air. Every mole of O₂ supplied by air drags 3.77 moles of N₂ into the flue gas. KTU examiners **love** testing this identity.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility
- **Orsat** is still used in **coal-fired boiler power stations** for daily efficiency audits.
- **NDIR** is the heart of every **petrol vehicle PUC (Pollution Under Control) certificate** device and of **OBD-II** evaporative-emission diagnostics.
- **Smoke Meters** are mandatory for **BS-VI diesel certification** in India under **CMVR (Central Motor Vehicle Rules) and AIS-137** standards.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Excess Air from Orsat Dry Flue Gas Analysis

**Given** (by Orsat):
- $\text{CO}_2 = a\,\%$, $\text{CO} = b\,\%$, $\text{O}_2 = c\,\%$, $\text{N}_2 = d\,\%$, with $a+b+c+d = 100$.

**Goal:** Derive an expression for **% Excess Air (EA)**.

#### Step 1 — Combustion Stoichiometry
For 1 kmol of dry flue gas, the carbon balance gives the carbon atoms supplied:
- Carbon leaves as CO₂ and CO: total C atoms = $a + b$ (kmol C per kmol dry flue gas).

For complete combustion, 1 C atom needs 1 O₂ atom:
$$
\text{O}_2 \text{ supplied for C burning} \;=\; a + \tfrac{b}{2}
$$

#### Step 2 — Air Supplied
Since every kmol of O₂ entering with air brings 3.77 kmol of N₂:
$$
\text{O}_2 \text{ supplied} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{N}_2}{3.77} \;=\; \dfrac{d}{3.77}
$$

#### Step 3 — Excess O₂
The **unused O₂** is simply the measured free O₂:
$$
\text{O}_2 \text{ excess} \;=\; c
$$

#### Step 4 — Stoichiometric O₂ (Theoretical)
$$
\text{O}_2 \text{ theoretical} \;=\; \text{O}_2 \text{ supplied} - \text{O}_2 \text{ excess} \;=\; \dfrac{d}{3.77} - c
$$

#### Step 5 — % Excess Air Definition
$$
\boxed{\;\text{EA}\,\% \;=\; \dfrac{\text{O}_2 \text{ excess}}{\text{O}_2 \text{ theoretical}} \times 100 \;=\; \dfrac{c}{\dfrac{d}{3.77} - c}\times 100\;}
$$

#### Step 6 — Approximate Shorthand
Since N₂ is computed by difference and assuming all O₂ in the supplied air is what reaches the flue, a common engineering approximation is:
$$
\text{EA}\,\% \;\approx\; \dfrac{c}{21 - c}\times 100
$$

where $c$ is the Orsat O₂ reading. This assumes atmospheric air has exactly 21% O₂, neglecting the small CO correction.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** An Orsat analysis of a boiler flue gas gives: CO₂ = 12%, CO = 2%, O₂ = 6%. Determine: (i) N₂ %, (ii) % Excess Air, (iii) actual Air–Fuel ratio, given that the fuel is C₁₀H₂₀ (decene, M = 140 kg/kmol, stoich AFR = 14.7).

#### Solution

**Part (i): N₂ by difference**
$$
\text{N}_2 \;=\; 100 - (12 + 2 + 6) \;=\; 80\,\%
$$

**Part (ii): % Excess Air (exact)**
$$
\text{EA}\,\% \;=\; \dfrac{6}{\tfrac{80}{3.77} - 6}\times 100
$$

Compute the denominator:
$$
\tfrac{80}{3.77} \;=\; 21.22 \quad\Rightarrow\quad 21.22 - 6 \;=\; 15.22
$$

$$
\text{EA}\,\% \;=\; \dfrac{6}{15.22}\times 100 \;=\; 39.42\,\%
$$

**Using the approximate formula:**
$$
\text{EA}\,\% \;\approx\; \dfrac{6}{21 - 6}\times 100 \;=\; 40\,\%
$$

(The 0.6% discrepancy comes from the CO correction.)

**Part (iii): Actual AFR**
$$
\text{AFR}_{\text{actual}} \;=\; \text{AFR}_{\text{stoich}} \times \left(1 + \dfrac{\text{EA}\,\%}{100}\right) \;=\; 14.7 \times 1.3942
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{AFR}_{\text{actual}} \;\approx\; 20.50\;}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Bosch Smoke Number (BSN) from Reflectance

**Given:** Reflectance of clean filter = $R_0$, reflectance of soiled filter = $R$.

#### Step 1 — Opacity from Reflectance
The fraction of light *not* reflected (i.e., absorbed/scattered by soot) is:
$$
\text{Opac} \;=\; 1 - \dfrac{R}{R_0}
$$

#### Step 2 — Bosch Number Definition
The smoke meter scales the fractional opacity to a 0–10 range by multiplying by 10:
$$
\boxed{\;\text{BSN} \;=\; 10 \left(1 - \dfrac{R}{R_0}\right)\;}
$$

#### Step 3 — Sanity Checks
- **Clean air** → $R = R_0$ → BSN = **0**.
- **Pitch-black deposit** → $R = 0$ → BSN = **10** (max).
- For a typical IDI diesel at full load: BSN ≈ **3–5**.
- For a healthy modern CRDi: BSN ≈ **0.5–1.5**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Instrument Selection Decision Tree

| Scenario | Best Instrument | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Steady-state boiler audit, 30-min run | Orsat | Cheap, accurate, lab conditions |
| Tailpipe CO/HC during transient drive cycle | NDIR | Response time < 1 s |
| Diesel particulate compliance (BS-VI) | Smoke Meter (Hartridge) | Real-time opacity |
| Indoor air quality | NDIR | Continuous monitoring |
| LPG/PNG leakage | NDIR (HC channel) | C-H bond at 3.31 µm |
| Combustion efficiency study | Orsat + calculated EA | Direct stoichiometry |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse **Opacity** with **Smoke Number**. **Opacity is in % (0–100)**, **Smoke Number is in Bosch units (0–10)**. They are linearly related only via the BSN definition.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Orsat Apparatus — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    flue[Flue Gas Source] --> fourCock[Four-Way Cock]
    fourCock --> burette[Water-Jacketed Burette 100 mL]
    burette <--> pipe1[Pipette 1 KOH Absorbs CO2]
    burette <--> pipe2[Pipette 2 Cu2Cl2 Absorbs CO]
    burette <--> pipe3[Pipette 3 Pyrogallol Absorbs O2]
    burette <--> levelBottle[Levelling Bottle Acidified Brine]
    levelBottle -. controls .-> burette
```

**Operating Logic:**
- Lower levelling bottle → gas in.
- Raise levelling bottle → gas pushed into a selected pipette.
- Each absorption step reduces burette volume by the % of that gas.

---

### 4.2 NDIR Analyser — Optical Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    irSource[IR Source Filament] --> chopper[Chopper Wheel 5 to 10 Hz]
    chopper --> beamSplitter[Beam Splitter]
    beamSplitter --> sampCell[Sample Cell Exhaust Flow]
    beamSplitter --> refCell[Reference Cell N2 Sealed]
    sampCell --> filterA[Narrow Bandpass Filter Target Wavelength]
    refCell --> filterA
    filterA --> detTop[Detector Chamber A Target Gas Filled]
    filterA --> detBot[Detector Chamber B Reference Gas]
    detTop --> diaphragm[Capacitor Diaphragm]
    detBot --> diaphragm
    diaphragm --> sigCond[Signal Conditioner AC Amplifier]
    sigCond --> disp[Display ppm or Percent Volume]
```

**Operating Logic:**
- Chopper makes the beam AC-modulated → rejects ambient IR noise.
- Sample cell absorbs some energy → differential heating between two detector chambers.
- Diaphragm displacement → AC voltage proportional to gas concentration.
- Single-beam (no reference cell) variants exist for cost-sensitive applications.

---

### 4.3 Smoke Meter Comparison Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    smoke[Smoke Measurement] --> type{Type}
    type -->|Full Flow Dynamic| hart[Hartridge Smoke Meter]
    type -->|Spot Sample Filter| bosch[Bosch Smoke Meter]

    hart --> hOp[Opacity 0 to 100 Percent]
    hart --> hUse[Real Time Transient Cycles]
    hart --> hComp[Compliance BS VI AIS 137]

    bosch --> bOp[Bosch Smoke Number 0 to 10]
    bosch --> bUse[Steady State Spot Checks]
    bosch --> bComp[Workshop Diagnosis]
```

---

### 4.4 Orsat Absorption Sequence — Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start([Start 100 mL Flue Gas in Burette]) --> s1[Send to KOH Pipette]
    s1 --> s1m[Measure Reduction vol percent CO2]
    s1m --> s2[Send to Cu2Cl2 Pipette]
    s2 --> s2m[Measure Reduction vol percent CO]
    s2m --> s3[Send to Pyrogallol Pipette]
    s3 --> s3m[Measure Reduction vol percent O2]
    s3m --> s4[N2 percent equals 100 minus sum]
    s4 --> end([Report Composition CO2 CO O2 N2])
```

> [!TIP]
> **Remember the "K-C-P-N" mnemonic** for the order: **K**OH → **C**u₂Cl₂ → **P**yrogallol → **N**₂. KTU questions often test the *order* of absorption.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List the three absorbing reagents used in an Orsat apparatus, the order in which they are used, and the gas each one absorbs.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The three reagents, in the order used, are:
1. **Potassium Hydroxide (KOH)** — absorbs **Carbon Dioxide (CO₂)**.
2. **Cuprous Chloride (Cu₂Cl₂) in HCl** — absorbs **Carbon Monoxide (CO)**.
3. **Pyrogallol (C₆H₃(OH)₃) in KOH** — absorbs **Oxygen (O₂)**.
The remaining gas is **Nitrogen**, computed by difference. (3 marks: 1 mark each reagent + 1 mark for the order rationale if mentioned.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the physical principle on which the Non-Dispersive Infrared (NDIR) gas analyser works. Name two gases it can detect.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The NDIR works on the principle of **selective absorption of infrared radiation by gas molecules** (specifically, vibrational-rotational transitions of heteroatomic molecules). Each gas absorbs at a unique IR wavelength — e.g., **CO at 4.67 µm, CO₂ at 4.26 µm, HC at 3.31 µm**. The transmitted intensity follows **Beer's Law** $I = I_0 e^{-\alpha c L}$, so the drop in intensity is proportional to gas concentration. **(1 mark principle, 1 mark Beer's law, 1 mark any two named gases.)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — Orsat & Combustion Analysis** [14 Marks]

**(a) [7 Marks]** *With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of the Orsat apparatus. Explain why the order of absorption matters.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *The Orsat analysis of a boiler flue gas yields: CO₂ = 10%, O₂ = 7%, CO = 1%, N₂ = 82% (by difference). Calculate: (i) the % excess air, (ii) the actual air–fuel ratio if the stoichiometric AFR for the fuel is 15.0.*

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Orsat Construction and Working (7 marks):**

| Component | Description | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| Burette | 100 mL, water-jacketed, calibrated 0.1 mL divisions | 1 |
| Levelling bottle | Holds acidified brine (5% H₂SO₄ + NaCl), adjusts pressure | 1 |
| Three pipettes | KOH, Cu₂Cl₂, Pyrogallol — sealed with reagent columns | 2 |
| Four-way cock | Routes gas to atmosphere, supply, pipettes, or bypass | 1 |
| Working steps | 100 mL drawn → pass to KOH (CO₂ absorbed) → Cu₂Cl₂ (CO absorbed) → Pyrogallol (O₂ absorbed) → N₂ by difference | 2 |

**Order matters because:**
- KOH is selective for CO₂.
- Cu₂Cl₂ must come before Pyrogallol because Pyrogallol's alkaline medium will *not* absorb CO if KOH remains in the gas path.
- Reversing the order gives incorrect (cumulative) readings.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

Given: $a=10$, $b=1$, $c=7$, $d=82$.

**(i) % Excess Air (3.5 marks):**

$$
\text{EA}\,\% \;=\; \dfrac{c}{\tfrac{d}{3.77} - c}\times 100
$$

$$
\dfrac{82}{3.77} \;=\; 21.75 \qquad 21.75 - 7 \;=\; 14.75
$$

$$
\text{EA}\,\% \;=\; \dfrac{7}{14.75}\times 100 \;=\; 47.46\,\%
$$

**Approximation (KTU often accepts):**

$$
\text{EA}\,\% \;\approx\; \dfrac{7}{21-7}\times 100 \;=\; 50\,\%
$$

**(ii) Actual AFR (3.5 marks):**

$$
\text{AFR}_{\text{actual}} \;=\; \text{AFR}_{\text{stoich}} \times \left(1 + \dfrac{\text{EA}}{100}\right)
$$

$$
\text{AFR}_{\text{actual}} \;=\; 15.0 \times 1.4746 \;=\; 22.12
$$

**Final Answers:** % Excess Air = **47.46 %**, Actual AFR = **22.12 : 1**.

---

#### **Question B — NDIR & Smoke Meter** [14 Marks]

**(a) [7 Marks]** *With the help of a labelled block diagram, explain the working of an NDIR (Non-Dispersive Infrared) gas analyser. Discuss the role of the chopper and the Luft detector.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Compare the Hartridge and Bosch smoke meters in terms of principle, range, sample handling, and typical application. A Hartridge meter measures 75% opacity on a 430 mm path length. Calculate the extinction coefficient of the smoke.*

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — NDIR Working (7 marks):**

| Block | Function | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| IR Source | Heated filament emits broadband IR | 1 |
| Chopper | Mechanical blade rotating at 5–10 Hz; converts DC IR to AC for noise rejection | 1.5 |
| Beam splitter / sample & reference cells | Differential absorption between sample and N₂ reference | 1.5 |
| Optical filter | Narrow bandpass at the target absorption wavelength | 1 |
| Luft detector (two-chamber microphone) | Diaphragm displacement ∝ gas concentration | 1.5 |
| Signal conditioner & display | AC voltage → ppm/% volume | 0.5 |

**Role of Chopper:** AC modulation enables **phase-locked detection**, eliminating drift and ambient IR. Without the chopper, the detector cannot distinguish sample signal from background.

**Role of Luft Detector:** The two gas-filled chambers heat up **differentially** based on how much IR is absorbed in the sample cell. The resulting **pressure wave** flexes a thin diaphragm, which is a **capacitor plate**; capacitance change → AC voltage → ppm reading.

**Part (b) — Smoke Meter Comparison (7 marks):**

| Feature | Hartridge | Bosch |
|---|---|---|
| Principle | Light transmission through flowing gas | Reflectance of soot on filter paper |
| Output | % Opacity (0–100) | Bosch Smoke Number (0–10) |
| Sample handling | Continuous full-flow | Spot sample, fixed volume |
| Path / cell | 430 mm fixed cell | Filter paper disc |
| Application | Real-time transient cycles | Workshop steady-state |
| Compliance | BS-VI / AIS-137 (India), ISO 11614 | Workshop diagnostics, AIS-137 |
| Response | Real-time | Delayed (sampling + filter) |

**Extinction coefficient (3 marks within Part b):**

Opacity $N = 75\,\% \Rightarrow T = 1 - 0.75 = 0.25$.

$$
k \;=\; -\dfrac{1}{L}\ln(T) \;=\; -\dfrac{1}{0.430}\ln(0.25)
$$

$$
\ln(0.25) \;=\; -1.3863
$$

$$
k \;=\; \dfrac{1.3863}{0.430} \;=\; 3.224\,\text{m}^{-1}
$$

**Final Answer:** $k = 3.224\,\text{m}^{-1}$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Writing N₂ by difference as 79% blindly** — this is wrong if CO and CO₂ are non-zero. Always *compute* N₂ = 100 − (CO₂ + CO + O₂).
> 2. **Using the approximate % EA formula without stating the assumption** — write it as "assuming O₂ alone accounts for excess air, neglecting CO."
> 3. **Confusing opacity and absorbance** — Opacity = $1 - T$, Absorbance = $-\log_{10} T$. Smoke meters use **linear** opacity, not logarithmic.
> 4. **In NDIR, forgetting to mention the chopper** — it is *the* defining element of "non-dispersive" AC operation.
> 5. **In Bosch number, mixing up $R/R_0$ with $R_0/R$** — it is **reflected / incident**, i.e., soiled / clean.
> 6. **Bosch number range 0–10, not 0–100.** If you write 75% Bosch number, you have already lost 1 mark.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Orsat** measures **CO₂, CO, O₂**; **N₂ by difference**. Reagents in order: **KOH → Cu₂Cl₂ → Pyrogallol**.
- **Confining liquid** in Orsat is **acidified brine (5% H₂SO₄ + NaCl)** — *not* water (CO₂ would dissolve in water).
- **Pyrogallol** is a strongly alkaline reducing agent; reacts with O₂ *only* in alkaline medium.
- **NDIR** is **non-dispersive** (no prism) and **AC-modulated** (chopper). The **Luft detector** is a *capacitive microphone* type.
- **Beer's Law** $I = I_0 e^{-\alpha c L}$ is the foundation of every optical gas analyser.
- **IR absorption wavelengths to memorize:** CO = **4.67 µm**, CO₂ = **4.26 µm**, CH₄ = **3.31 µm**.
- **Smoke Meter** units: **Hartridge = % Opacity (0–100)**; **Bosch = Smoke Number (0–10)**. Linear relation: $\text{BSN} = 10 \times (1 - R/R_0)$.
- **% Excess Air** (exact) $= \dfrac{c}{\tfrac{d}{3.77} - c}\times 100$; (approx) $= \dfrac{c}{21-c}\times 100$.
- **3.77** = volumetric N₂/O₂ ratio in atmospheric air (79/21).
- **Equivalence ratio** $\phi = \text{CO}_2 / (\text{CO}_2 + \text{CO})$ — quality indicator of combustion.
- **Opacity** = $1 - T$; **Extinction coefficient** $k = -\ln(T)/L$ — used to compare smoke independently of cell length.
- **Application priority:** Orsat → boilers; NDIR → petrol tailpipe; Smoke Meter → diesel particulates.
- **KTU 2024 OBE tag** — these instruments map to **CO1: *Understand emission measurement principles for vehicle certification and engine performance evaluation***.
- **Bloom's level** for this topic: **L2 (Understand)** and **L3 (Apply)** — expect numericals with formulas, not derivations from first principles.
- **Common unit trap:** 1 m³ of flue gas at NTP weighs ~1.293 kg; conversions between **vol %** and **mass %** use the molecular weight of each gas (CO₂ = 44, CO = 28, O₂ = 32, N₂ = 28).
- **Modern trend:** Four-gas NDIR analysers (HC, CO, CO₂, NO) plus Bosch-type smoke meter are mandated in Indian **PUC centres** under **BS-VI** norms. The Orsat is retained in KTU syllabus for *pedagogical* clarity of wet chemistry principles.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Memory Hook — The "4-3-2-1" of Emission Analysers:**
> **4** gases (CO₂, CO, O₂, N₂) — Orsat
> **3** wavelength regions (3.31, 4.26, 4.67 µm) — NDIR
> **2** smoke meter types (Hartridge, Bosch)
> **1** unifying physics law (Beer–Lambert / Beer's Law)
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Testing Methods and Procedures for Engine Cooling Fan, Air Conditioning System, and Brake Compressors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT724 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Testing Methods and Procedures for Engine Cooling Fan, Air Conditioning System, and Brake Compressors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Engine Cooling Fan — Testing Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Engine Cooling Fan Testing** is the systematic evaluation of an engine's cooling fan assembly to verify its **airflow delivery capacity (CFM / m³/h)**, **static pressure development**, **power consumption (W)**, **noise emission (dB(A))**, and **structural durability** under simulated thermal load conditions as per standards such as **SAE J1393**, **ISO 13400**, and **AIS-010**.

**Key Performance Parameters Under Test:**
- **Volumetric Airflow (Q)** — measured in CFM (Cubic Feet per Minute) or m³/h
- **Static Pressure (ΔP)** — measured in mm of water column (mmWC) or Pascal (Pa)
- **Fan Speed (N)** — measured in RPM
- **Specific Speed (Ns)** — dimensionless similarity parameter
- **Acoustic Emission (LwA)** — measured in dB(A)
- **Power Input (P)** — measured in Watts

### 1.2 Air Conditioning System — Testing Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Automotive Air Conditioning (A/C) System Testing** is a comprehensive evaluation of the refrigeration loop (compressor, condenser, expansion valve, evaporator) to validate the **Coefficient of Performance (COP)**, **cooling capacity (BTU/hr or kW)**, **refrigerant pressure-temperature (P-T) curves**, **superheat and subcooling values**, and **compressor performance** under standardized ambient conditions (e.g., **ASRAE 210/240**, **SAE J276**, **ISO 13073**).

### 1.3 Brake Compressor — Testing Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Brake Compressor Testing** involves the validation of an air-brake compressor (typically used in commercial vehicles, trucks, and buses) for its **displacement (CC/rev or CFM)**, **cut-in and cut-out pressures (bar/psi)**, **air delivery rate (L/min)**, **duty cycle ratio**, **lubrication integrity**, and **valve seating performance** as per **SAE J1343**, **ECE R13**, and **AIS-044** standards.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Three Heroes of Vehicle Performance"**
> - **Engine Cooling Fan** is like a **"personal trainer"** for the engine — it sweats (cools) the engine when it works out (combustion), preventing overheating.
> - **A/C System** is like a **"personal AC chamber"** for the passengers — it pulls heat from inside the cabin and dumps it outside, just like a refrigerator.
> - **Brake Compressor** is like a **"lung"** for the brake system — it breathes in atmospheric air, compresses it, and pushes it into brake actuators to stop a multi-tonne vehicle.
> 
> Testing these components is like conducting a **"medical checkup"** for the vehicle — ensuring each organ performs its duty at peak efficiency before being declared roadworthy.

**Standard Reference Conditions for Vehicle Component Testing:**
- **Ambient Temperature (T∞):** 25°C to 45°C (regional standard)
- **Atmospheric Pressure (Pₐₜₘ):** 101.325 kPa (sea level reference)
- **Relative Humidity (RH):** 50% ± 10%
- **Test Voltage (V):** 13.5 V ± 0.5 V (for 12V automotive electrical systems)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Engine Cooling Fan — Testing Methodology

**Step-by-Step Operational Logic:**

- **Step 1 — Test Bench Setup:** Mount the cooling fan on a calibrated test bench equipped with a **flow straightener**, **nozzle chamber**, and **tachometer coupling**.
- **Step 2 — Baseline Measurement:** Measure **no-tachometer-load static pressure (ΔP₀)** with the fan running at rated voltage.
- **Step 3 — Airflow Measurement:** Use a **flow nozzle** or **Pitot-static tube array** placed downstream. The pressure differential (Δh) is converted to airflow using:
  $$Q = C_d \cdot A_n \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot \Delta P_n}{\rho_a}}$$
- **Step 4 — Speed-Power Curve:** Vary fan voltage from **9V to 16V** to plot the **P-Q-N characteristic curve**.
- **Step 5 — Acoustic Test:** Conduct **sound power level** measurement in a semi-anechoic chamber at **1 meter** distance per **ISO 3744**.
- **Step 6 — Endurance Test:** Run continuously for **1000 hours** at rated duty cycle to evaluate bearing wear, blade erosion, and motor insulation.

**Why It Matters:** Engine cooling fans are the **first line of defense** against thermal runaway. A 5% drop in airflow can increase coolant temperatures by **8–12°C**, leading to detonation, oil degradation, and piston scuffing.

### 2.2 Air Conditioning System — Testing Methodology

**Step-by-Step Operational Logic:**

- **Step 1 — Static P-T Test (Compressor Off):** Measure **high-side and low-side static pressures** after 4 hours of equilibrium. Static high-side should be approximately equal to **saturation pressure of refrigerant at ambient temperature**.
- **Step 2 — Dynamic P-T Test (Compressor Running):** With engine at **1500 RPM**, doors open, blower on high, record pressures and temperatures at all four service ports.
- **Step 3 — Superheat Measurement:** Use the formula:
  $$SH = T_{evap-out} - T_{sat}(P_{evap-out})$$
- **Step 4 — Subcooling Measurement:**
  $$SC = T_{sat}(P_{cond-out}) - T_{liq-out}$$
- **Step 5 — Performance (Capacity) Test:** Measure cabin temperature drop over 30 minutes in a **solar-loaded chamber** at **1000 W/m²** to simulate real-world conditions.
- **Step 6 — Leak Test:** Conduct **electronic sniff (HFC-134a) or helium mass spectrometer** test for leak rate ≤ **15 g/year** (per SAE J1628).

**Why It Matters:** A poorly tuned A/C system can cause:
- **High superheat (>12°C):** Compressor starved → bearing seizure
- **Low superheat (<2°C):** Liquid slugging → catastrophic compressor failure
- **High subcooling loss:** Reduced cooling capacity (every 1°C subcooling loss ≈ 1% capacity loss)

### 2.3 Brake Compressor — Testing Methodology

**Step-by-Step Operational Logic:**

- **Step 1 — Pressure Build-Up Test:** From **0 bar**, measure time (t₁) to reach cut-out pressure (typically **8.0 to 10.0 bar**). Calculate build-up rate.
- **Step 2 — Compressor Performance Curve:** Plot **Pressure (bar) vs. Air Delivery (L/min)** at fixed engine RPM (typically **2000, 2500, 3000 RPM**).
- **Step 3 — Cut-In/Cut-Out Verification:** Cycle governor between **cut-in (≈ 7.0 bar)** and **cut-out (≈ 8.5 bar)** for 50 cycles — verify pressure differential stays within **±0.1 bar**.
- **Step 4 — Thermal Test:** Run at 25% duty cycle for 4 hours, monitor cylinder head temperature (limit: **180°C** for cast iron, **220°C** for aluminum).
- **Step 5 — Vibration Test:** Subject to **5g RMS** random vibration per **ISO 16750-3**.
- **Step 6 — Endurance Test:** **500 hours** or **100,000 km** equivalent, whichever comes first.

**Why It Matters:** A faulty brake compressor can cause **service brake failure** — the single most safety-critical component in commercial vehicles. **AIS-044 mandates strict compliance** with pressure build-up rates of **≥ 1.5 L/min at 2000 RPM**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Airflow Rate | $Q$ | $C_d \cdot A_n \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot \Delta P_n \vert \rho_a}$ | m³/s | Fan nozzle test |
| Air Density | $\rho_a$ | $P_{atm} \vert (R_{air} \cdot T_{amb})$ | kg/m³ | Altitude correction |
| Fan Power | $P_f$ | $\Delta P \cdot Q \vert \eta_{fan}$ | W | Fan efficiency |
| Fan Efficiency | $\eta_f$ | $P_{air} \vert P_{electrical}$ | — | Dimensionless |
| Specific Speed | $N_s$ | $N \cdot \sqrt{Q} \vert (gH)^{0.75}$ | — | Fan classification |
| COP (Refrigeration) | $COP$ | $Q_L \vert W_{comp}$ | — | A/C performance |
| Refrigeration Effect | $Q_L$ | $\dot{m}_r \cdot (h_1 - h_4)$ | kW | Evaporator capacity |
| Heat of Compression | $W_c$ | $\dot{m}_r \cdot (h_2 - h_1)$ | kW | Compressor work |
| Superheat | $SH$ | $T_{evap-out} - T_{sat}(P_{evap-out})$ | K | Expansion valve tuning |
| Subcooling | $SC$ | $T_{sat}(P_{cond-out}) - T_{liq-out}$ | K | Condenser performance |
| Pressure Build-up Rate | $\dot{V}_{br}$ | $V_{tank} \cdot (P_{cutout} - P_{cutin}) \vert t_{cycle}$ | L/min | Brake compressor |
| Compressor Sweep Volume | $V_d$ | $\pi \cdot (D^2 \vert 4) \cdot L \cdot n$ | L | Theoretical displacement |
| Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_v$ | $V_{actual} \vert V_{theoretical}$ | — | Compressor health |
| Noise Level | $L_{wA}$ | $20 \cdot \log_{10}(P \vert P_0)$ | dB(A) | Acoustic test |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions & Standards to Remember:**
> - **Standard Atmospheric Pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa
> - **Specific Gas Constant for Air:** $R_{air} = 287.05$ J/(kg·K)
> - **Speed of Sound (20°C):** $a = 343$ m/s
> - **Reference Sound Pressure:** $P_0 = 20 \;\mu Pa$
> - **Refrigerant Standard:** HFC-134a (R-134a) — GWP = 1430
> - **Brake System Test Pressure:** 8.0 bar (cut-out), 7.0 bar (cut-in) ± 0.1 bar

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Airflow Through Test Nozzle (Fan Testing)

**Problem Statement:** A cooling fan is tested on a bench with a single inlet flow nozzle of diameter **Dₙ = 250 mm**. The nozzle differential pressure is **ΔPₙ = 250 Pa**. The ambient conditions are **T = 35°C**, **Pₐₜₘ = 101.325 kPa**. The discharge coefficient is **C_d = 0.98**. Calculate the airflow rate in m³/h and CFM.

**Step 1: Calculate the air density at test conditions.**

The ideal gas law gives:

$$P_{atm} = \rho_a \cdot R_{air} \cdot T_{amb}$$

Solving for density:

$$\rho_a = \frac{P_{atm}}{R_{air} \cdot T_{amb}}$$

Convert temperature to Kelvin:

$$T_{amb} = 35 + 273.15 = 308.15 \; K$$

Substitute numerical values:

$$\rho_a = \frac{101325}{287.05 \times 308.15}$$

Compute denominator:

$$287.05 \times 308.15 = 88457.6 \; J/kg$$

Compute density:

$$\rho_a = \frac{101325}{88457.6} = 1.1455 \; kg/m^3$$

**Step 2: Calculate the nozzle throat area.**

$$A_n = \frac{\pi \cdot D_n^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.250)^2}{4} = 0.04909 \; m^2$$

**Step 3: Calculate the volumetric flow rate.**

$$Q = C_d \cdot A_n \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot \Delta P_n}{\rho_a}}$$

Substitute values:

$$Q = 0.98 \times 0.04909 \times \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 250}{1.1455}}$$

Inner fraction:

$$\frac{2 \times 250}{1.1455} = \frac{500}{1.1455} = 436.49 \; m^2/s^2$$

Square root:

$$\sqrt{436.49} = 20.89 \; m/s$$

Final flow rate:

$$Q = 0.98 \times 0.04909 \times 20.89 = 1.005 \; m^3/s$$

Convert to m³/h:

$$Q = 1.005 \times 3600 = 3618 \; m^3/h$$

Convert to CFM (1 m³/h = 0.5886 CFM):

$$Q = 3618 \times 0.5886 = 2129 \; CFM$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — A/C System Coefficient of Performance

**Problem Statement:** A vehicle A/C system uses R-134a. The mass flow rate of refrigerant is **ṁᵣ = 0.06 kg/s**. The evaporator enthalpy drop is **(h₁ - h₄) = 175 kJ/kg**. The compressor work input is **(h₂ - h₁) = 45 kJ/kg**. Calculate the cooling capacity in kW and the COP.

**Step 1: Calculate the cooling capacity (refrigeration effect).**

$$Q_L = \dot{m}_r \cdot (h_1 - h_4)$$

Substitute values:

$$Q_L = 0.06 \times 175 = 10.5 \; kW$$

**Step 2: Calculate the compressor work input.**

$$W_c = \dot{m}_r \cdot (h_2 - h_1) = 0.06 \times 45 = 2.7 \; kW$$

**Step 3: Calculate the COP.**

$$COP = \frac{Q_L}{W_c} = \frac{10.5}{2.7} = 3.89$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** A COP of 3.89 is considered **excellent** for an automotive A/C system. A typical healthy system has COP between **2.5 and 4.0**. Below 2.0 indicates a major issue (compressor wear, condenser blockage, or refrigerant overcharge).

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Brake Compressor Pressure Build-Up Time

**Problem Statement:** A truck brake system has a reservoir volume of **V = 60 L**. The cut-in pressure is **P_ci = 7.0 bar** and the cut-out pressure is **P_co = 8.5 bar**. The compressor delivers a free air delivery (FAD) of **Q_bc = 280 L/min** at engine idle (800 RPM). The system has a leakage rate of **Q_leak = 5 L/min** (gauge). Calculate the pressure build-up time from cut-in to cut-out.

**Step 1: Calculate the net air delivery.**

$$Q_{net} = Q_{bc} - Q_{leak} = 280 - 5 = 275 \; L/min$$

**Step 2: Calculate the volume of air needed to raise pressure from cut-in to cut-out.**

Using Boyle's Law (isothermal), the air volume required is:

$$\Delta V_{air} = V \cdot \left( \frac{P_{co} - P_{ci}}{P_{atm}} \right)$$

Convert reservoir volume to air volume at atmospheric pressure:

$$\Delta V_{air} = 60 \cdot \left( \frac{8.5 - 7.0}{1.01325} \right)$$

Calculate pressure ratio:

$$\frac{1.5}{1.01325} = 1.4802$$

Calculate required air volume:

$$\Delta V_{air} = 60 \times 1.4802 = 88.81 \; L$$

**Step 3: Calculate the build-up time.**

$$t = \frac{\Delta V_{air}}{Q_{net}} = \frac{88.81}{275} = 0.323 \; min = 19.4 \; s$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** Per **AIS-044** and **ECE R13**, the build-up time from 0 bar to 80% of cut-out pressure must not exceed **30 seconds** at engine idle. Our result of 19.4 s meets this standard. If build-up time exceeds 45 s, the compressor should be rebuilt or replaced.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Test Failure Modes

| **Component** | **Test Parameter** | **Acceptable Range** | **Failure Threshold** | **Probable Root Cause** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cooling Fan | Airflow | ±5% of spec | < -10% | Blade fouling, motor wear |
| Cooling Fan | Noise | ≤ 65 dB(A) | > 72 dB(A) | Bearing failure, imbalance |
| A/C System | High-side Pressure | 1.4 – 1.6 MPa | > 2.0 MPa | Overcharge, condenser blockage |
| A/C System | Low-side Pressure | 0.2 – 0.3 MPa | < 0.1 MPa | Refrigerant undercharge |
| A/C System | Superheat | 4 – 8°C | > 12°C | Low refrigerant, TXV stuck |
| Brake Compressor | Build-up Time | < 30 s | > 45 s | Worn rings, valve leakage |
| Brake Compressor | Cut-out Pressure | 8.0 – 8.5 bar | < 7.5 bar | Governor malfunction |
| Brake Compressor | Oil Carryover | < 50 mg/m³ | > 100 mg/m³ | Worn piston rings |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Engine Cooling Fan — Test Bench Architecture

```mermaid

    FAN1["FAN MOTOR UNIT"] --> N1["FLOW NOZZLE CHAMBER"]
    N1 --> FS1["FLOW STRAIGHTENER"]

    V1 --> FAN1

    MIC1 --> DAQ1
    AN1 --> FAN1
    DAQ1 --> PC1["PC WITH P-Q-N PLOTTER"]
```

### 4.4 Integrated Functional Block Diagram — Vehicle Subsystem Testing

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ENG_GROUP ["ENGINE COOLING SUBSYSTEM"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to Read These Diagrams in an Exam:**
> - Always trace **flow direction** (left-to-right in `LR` graphs, top-to-bottom in `TB` graphs).
> - Identify **sensors** (rectangular blocks feeding into the DAQ) and **actuators** (driven by external inputs).
> - Note the **feedback loop** — DAQ feeds PC which can adjust the test parameters, making this a **closed-loop test setup**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any three performance parameters tested in an engine cooling fan and state the standard used for the test.**

**Model Answer:**
The three primary performance parameters tested in an engine cooling fan are:
1. **Airflow delivery (Q)** — measured in CFM or m³/h using a flow nozzle per **SAE J1393**.
2. **Static pressure (ΔP)** — measured in mmWC or Pa at zero flow condition.
3. **Acoustic emission (LwA)** — measured in dB(A) at 1 m distance per **ISO 3744**.

Additional parameters include fan speed (RPM), power input (W), and endurance life (hours).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]**
**Define superheat and subcooling in the context of an A/C system. What are the typical acceptable values?**

**Model Answer:**
**Superheat (SH)** is the temperature difference between the refrigerant vapor leaving the evaporator and its saturation temperature at the evaporator outlet pressure.

$$SH = T_{evap-out} - T_{sat}(P_{evap-out})$$

Acceptable range: **4°C to 8°C**.

**Subcooling (SC)** is the temperature difference between the saturation temperature at the condenser outlet and the actual temperature of the liquid refrigerant leaving the condenser.

$$SC = T_{sat}(P_{cond-out}) - T_{liq-out}$$

Acceptable range: **3°C to 8°C**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat test setup diagram, explain the procedure for testing an **engine cooling fan** on a calibrated bench. List the standards and instruments used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A cooling fan delivers **2800 CFM** at **1500 RPM** against a static pressure of **12 mmWC**. Calculate the **air power output** in watts. If the electrical input is **85 W**, calculate the **fan efficiency**. Take air density = **1.2 kg/m³**. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) — Cooling Fan Test Procedure

**Test Setup Diagram (Conceptual):**

```
[Fan Motor] → [Flow Nozzle] → [Flow Straightener] → [Pitot Tube Array]
       ↓                  ↓                  ↓
[Tachometer]      [DP Transducer]    [Acoustic Sensor]
       ↓                  ↓                  ↓
       └──────→ [Data Acquisition System] ←──┘
                            ↓
                    [PC with P-Q-N Plotter]
```

**Step-by-Step Procedure:**

1. **Mount the fan** on the test bench with proper alignment to ensure no shaft eccentricity.
2. **Connect the airflow measurement system** — flow nozzle and Pitot-static tube are placed downstream of the fan.
3. **Connect the data acquisition system (DAQ)** to read differential pressure (ΔP), fan speed (N), voltage (V), current (I), and noise (dB).
4. **Apply rated voltage** (typically 13.5V for 12V automotive systems).
5. **Record the airflow** at no-load (free delivery) and various throttling conditions.
6. **Plot the P-Q curve** — pressure (Y-axis) vs. airflow (X-axis).
7. **Measure the sound level** in a semi-anechoic chamber at 1 m per **ISO 3744**.
8. **Run endurance test** for 1000 hours per **SAE J1393**.

**Standards Used:** SAE J1393, ISO 13400, AIS-010, ISO 3744.
**Instruments:** Pitot-static tube, flow nozzle, DP transducer, tachometer, sound level meter, power analyzer.

#### Part (b) — Air Power and Efficiency Calculation

**Given Data:**
- Q = 2800 CFM
- N = 1500 RPM
- ΔP = 12 mmWC
- Electrical input P_e = 85 W
- ρ_a = 1.2 kg/m³

**Step 1: Convert CFM to m³/s.**

$$1 \; CFM = 0.0004719 \; m^3/s$$

$$Q = 2800 \times 0.0004719 = 1.3215 \; m^3/s$$

**Step 2: Convert mmWC to Pa.**

$$1 \; mmWC = 9.80665 \; Pa$$

$$\Delta P = 12 \times 9.80665 = 117.68 \; Pa$$

**Step 3: Calculate the air power (output).**

$$P_{air} = \Delta P \cdot Q = 117.68 \times 1.3215 = 155.51 \; W$$

**Step 4: Calculate the fan efficiency.**

$$\eta_{fan} = \frac{P_{air}}{P_{electrical}} = \frac{155.51}{85} \times 100\% = 182.96\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **EXAMINER'S VALUATION PITFALL:**
> The efficiency **exceeding 100%** indicates either a **calculation error** or a **wrong input data assumption**. In a real scenario, fan efficiency is always less than 100% (typically 30–60% for axial fans). Re-verify the units of ΔP and Q before submission. In a board exam, **always show the unit conversion steps** to earn full marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Describe the **P-T (Pressure-Temperature) diagnostic procedure** for an automotive air conditioning system. Explain the significance of superheat and subcooling with typical values. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A truck's brake compressor has a **sweep volume of 320 cc/rev** and runs at **2000 RPM**. The **volumetric efficiency is 85%**. The **mechanical efficiency is 90%**. The reservoir volume is **50 L**, and the system pressure must rise from **0 to 8.0 bar (gauge)**. Calculate the **theoretical free air delivery (FAD)** in L/min and the **pressure build-up time** assuming **no leakage**. Take atmospheric pressure = **1.01325 bar**. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) — P-T Diagnostic Procedure for A/C System

**Procedure:**

1. **Connect manifold gauges** to high-side and low-side service ports of the A/C system.
2. **Set conditions:** Engine at 1500 RPM, A/C on maximum, blower on high, doors open, ambient temp 35°C.
3. **Record high-side pressure** (typically 1.4 – 1.6 MPa for R-134a).
4. **Record low-side pressure** (typically 0.2 – 0.3 MPa).
5. **Measure evaporator outlet temperature** using a thermocouple.
6. **Measure condenser outlet temperature** using a thermocouple.
7. **Compare with P-T chart** for R-134a to identify saturation temperatures.
8. **Calculate superheat and subcooling** as per the formulas below.

**Superheat (SH):** Temperature of vapor above its saturation point at the evaporator outlet. Acceptable: **4 – 8°C**.
- **High SH (>12°C):** Low refrigerant charge, TXV stuck closed, or evaporator restriction.
- **Low SH (<2°C):** Flooded evaporator, TXV stuck open, or refrigerant overcharge.

**Subcooling (SC):** Temperature of liquid below its saturation point at the condenser outlet. Acceptable: **3 – 8°C**.
- **High SC (>10°C):** Overcharged system, restricted receiver drier.
- **Low SC (<2°C):** Undercharged system, condenser fan failure.

#### Part (b) — Brake Compressor FAD and Build-Up Time

**Given Data:**
- V_d = 320 cc/rev
- N = 2000 RPM
- η_v = 85%
- η_m = 90% (not used in FAD)
- V_tank = 50 L
- P_final = 8.0 bar (gauge) = 9.01325 bar (absolute)
- P_atm = 1.01325 bar
- No leakage

**Step 1: Calculate the theoretical displacement per minute.**

$$V_{disp} = V_d \cdot N = 320 \times 2000 = 640000 \; cc/min = 640 \; L/min$$

**Step 2: Apply volumetric efficiency to get actual FAD.**

$$FAD = V_{disp} \cdot \eta_v = 640 \times 0.85 = 544 \; L/min$$

**Step 3: Calculate the air volume required at atmospheric pressure.**

$$\Delta V_{air} = V_{tank} \cdot \left( \frac{P_{final,abs} - P_{initial,abs}}{P_{atm}} \right)$$

$$= 50 \cdot \left( \frac{9.01325 - 1.01325}{1.01325} \right)$$

$$= 50 \cdot \left( \frac{8.0}{1.01325} \right) = 50 \times 7.8955 = 394.78 \; L$$

**Step 4: Calculate the build-up time.**

$$t = \frac{\Delta V_{air}}{FAD} = \frac{394.78}{544} = 0.7257 \; min = 43.5 \; s$$

> [!WARNING]
> **EXAMINER'S VALUATION PITFALL:**
> - A common mistake is using **gauge pressure** directly without converting to **absolute pressure** for the air volume calculation. The reservoir initially contains air at **atmospheric pressure (1.01325 bar)**, not at **0 bar**. Always subtract or add atmospheric pressure explicitly.
> - **Mechanical efficiency (η_m)** is irrelevant for FAD calculation — it only applies to power/shaft work. Including it will lead to **wrong answers and mark deduction**.
> - **Final answer must be in SI units** (seconds, m³/s) with **units written at every step** to avoid partial mark loss.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 2 Testing Methods**

- ✅ **Cooling Fan Tests:** Airflow (CFM/m³/h), static pressure (mmWC/Pa), noise (dB), and endurance (1000 hr).
- ✅ **Fan Test Standards:** SAE J1393, ISO 13400, AIS-010.
- ✅ **A/C Test Standards:** ASHRAE 210/240, SAE J276, ISO 13073.
- ✅ **A/C Acceptable Pressures (R-134a):** High = 1.4 – 1.6 MPa; Low = 0.2 – 0.3 MPa.
- ✅ **A/C Superheat:** 4 – 8°C | **Subcooling:** 3 – 8°C.
- ✅ **A/C Typical COP:** 2.5 – 4.0 (healthy system).
- ✅ **Brake Compressor Test Standards:** SAE J1343, ECE R13, AIS-044.
- ✅ **Brake Compressor Cut-in/Cut-out:** 7.0 / 8.5 bar ± 0.1 bar.
- ✅ **Brake Build-up Time Limit:** < 30 s from 0 to 80% cut-out (per AIS-044).
- ✅ **Key Conversion Factors:** 1 CFM = 0.0004719 m³/s | 1 mmWC = 9.80665 Pa.
- ✅ **Standard Test Conditions:** 25°C ambient, 50% RH, 101.325 kPa.
- ✅ **Always convert gauge pressure to absolute** when applying gas laws (P_abs = P_gauge + P_atm).
- ✅ **FAD formula:** FAD = V_d × N × η_v (volumetric efficiency, NOT mechanical).
- ✅ **Noise reference:** P₀ = 20 μPa; Sound power level = $20 \cdot \log_{10}(P/P_0)$.
- ✅ **Compressor sweep volume:** $V_d = \pi \cdot (D^2/4) \cdot L \cdot n$ (n = number of cylinders).

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Exam Strategy:**
> 1. **Always draw the test setup diagram** before solving numerical problems — it earns easy 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Mention the standard** (SAE/ISO/AIS) being followed — examiners award marks for this.
> 3. **Show unit conversions explicitly** — a 1-mark cushion for every conversion step.
> 4. **State assumptions** (adiabatic, no leakage, steady-state) at the start of derivations.
> 5. **For P-T questions**, always draw the **vapor compression cycle** on the P-h diagram and mark the four state points (1-2-3-4).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuel Consumption Tests, Test Route Selection, and Vehicle Test Speeds
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT724 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fuel Consumption Tests, Test Route Selection, and Vehicle Test Speeds

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Fuel Consumption Tests — Formal Definition

A **Fuel Consumption Test** is a standardized, controlled procedure used to quantify the rate at which a motor vehicle consumes fuel under precisely defined operating conditions, driving cycles, ambient parameters, and vehicle loading states. These tests are essential for **type approval**, **compliance certification**, **OEM benchmarking**, and **fleet efficiency management**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (OEMUT724 Module 2):**
> Fuel consumption tests evaluate the volume or mass of fuel consumed by a vehicle over a specified distance or under a prescribed driving cycle, expressed in **litres per 100 kilometres (L/100 km)**, **kilometres per litre (km/L)**, or **grams per kilometre (g/km)** of CO₂ emitted.

The test must be conducted on a chassis dynamometer (for laboratory cycles) or on a calibrated test track (for road tests), with controlled ambient temperature (**20°C to 30°C**), barometric pressure, and relative humidity.

### 1.2 Test Route Selection — Formal Definition

**Test Route Selection** is the engineering process of designing or choosing a representative driving route (either on-road or simulated as a driving cycle) that statistically replicates real-world driving conditions within a geographical region, including urban, suburban, and highway segments.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why routes matter:** A vehicle tested only on flat highways will report optimistic fuel economy figures that misrepresent actual customer experience. Route selection ensures the data is **representative**, **reproducible**, and **legally defensible**.

### 1.3 Vehicle Test Speeds — Formal Definition

**Vehicle Test Speeds** refer to the standardized, predefined velocities at which a vehicle is operated during fuel consumption and performance evaluations. These speeds are mandated by regulatory standards such as **IS 14664:1999**, **AIS-050**, **ECE R101**, and **EPA Federal Test Procedure (FTP-75)**.

The standard KTU-referenced test speeds are:
- **40 km/h** (typical urban cruise)
- **50 km/h** (suburban steady state)
- **60 km/h** (city-outskirt cruising)
- **90 km/h** (highway cruising)
- **120 km/h** (expressway / motorway speed)

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it like measuring your daily commute mileage:**
>
> Imagine you want to know how "thirsty" your motorcycle is. You wouldn't just ride it on a downhill slope and say "Wow, 100 km/L!" — that's misleading. Instead, you'd test it across **city traffic** (stop-and-go), **open roads** (steady cruise), and **highways** (high-speed sustained) and average the results. The standardized fuel consumption test does **exactly this**, but with controlled, repeatable, scientifically designed cycles — so the number on the spec sheet actually means something comparable across brands.
>
> The **test speed** is your chosen cruise velocity. The **test route** is your chosen mix of road types. The **fuel measurement** is your accurate fuel gauge reading before and after.

### 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Reference fuel density (Petrol) | **0.745** | g/cm³ at 15°C |
| Reference fuel density (Diesel) | **0.832** | g/cm³ at 15°C |
| Standard ambient temperature | **20–30** | °C |
| Standard atmospheric pressure | **99.325** | kPa (ISA Sea Level) |
| Test vehicle mass | Kerb mass + 75/100 kg driver | kg |
| Carbon content of petrol (C%) | **86.2** | % by mass |
| Carbon content of diesel (C%) | **86.4** | % by mass |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Methods of Fuel Consumption Measurement

#### A. Volumetric Method
Measures the **volume of fuel consumed** directly using a precision burette or flow meter connected to the engine fuel line, bypassing the main tank. The burette is graduated in mL, allowing direct L/100 km conversion.

#### B. Gravimetric Method
Measures the **mass of fuel consumed** by weighing a sealed auxiliary fuel reservoir before and after the test run on a precision balance (accuracy ±0.1 g). Converted to volume using fuel density:

$$V_{fuel} = \frac{m_{fuel}}{\rho_{fuel}}$$

#### C. Carbon Balance Method (Exhaust-Based)
Indirect measurement using exhaust gas composition (CO, CO₂, HC). Total carbon in exhaust must equal total carbon in fuel burned:

$$m_{fuel} = \frac{0.866 \times m_{CO_2} + 0.429 \times m_{CO} + 0.273 \times m_{HC}}{C_{\%}}$$

This is the method used by modern OBD-II systems and is the **indirect but most accurate** approach in OEM laboratories.

#### D. PEMS-Based Measurement (Portable Emissions Measurement System)
Real-world driving measurement using portable analyzers. Increasingly adopted under **Euro 6d / BS-VI Stage 2** for Real Driving Emissions (RDE).

### 2.2 Why Each Method Matters — The "Why & How"

| Method | Why Used | How It Works |
|---|---|---|
| Volumetric | Simple, cheap, real-time | Burette drop measured |
| Gravimetric | High accuracy, lab-grade | Mass delta / density |
| Carbon Balance | Non-intrusive, exhaustive | Exhaust analysis |
| PEMS | Real-world, in-service | Portable 5-gas analyzer |

### 2.3 Standardized Test Cycles (Reference Knowledge)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cycles You MUST memorize for KTU exams:**

- **Indian Driving Cycle (IDC)** — Modified Indian Driving Cycle (MIDC) since 2014
- **NEDC** — New European Driving Cycle (4 ECE-15 + 1 EUDC)
- **WLTP** — Worldwide harmonized Light vehicles Test Procedure (Class 1, 2, 3)
- **FTP-75** — US Federal Test Procedure
- **JC08** — Japanese Cycle
- **ECE R101** — UN Regulation governing M1 vehicles

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $$FC = \frac{V_{fuel}}{D} \times 100$$ | Fuel consumption in L/100 km | L/100 km |
| $$FE = \frac{D}{V_{fuel}}$$ | Fuel economy in km/L | km/L |
| $$FC_{combined} = \frac{0.55 \cdot FC_{urban} + 0.45 \cdot FC_{extra}}{1}$$ | Weighted combined fuel consumption | L/100 km |
| $$m_{fuel} = \rho_{fuel} \times V_{fuel}$$ | Mass of fuel from volume | kg |
| $$CO_2 = 3.16 \times FC_{petrol}$$ | Approximate CO₂ emission (petrol) | g/km |
| $$CO_2 = 3.17 \times FC_{diesel}$$ | Approximate CO₂ emission (diesel) | g/km |
| $$v_{avg} = \frac{D_{cycle}}{\Delta t_{cycle}}$$ | Average cycle speed | km/h |
| $$\eta_{cycle} = \frac{D_{cycle}}{V_{fuel-cycle}}$$ | Cycle fuel economy | km/L |

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

- **OEM R&D:** Benchmark competitors, validate calibration, perform trade-off studies between performance and economy.
- **Regulatory:** Type approval under AIS-050, BS-VI Phase 2, ECE R101.
- **Consumer Information:** Fuel economy labelling as mandated by **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE), India**.
- **Fleet Management:** TCO (Total Cost of Ownership) calculations.
- **Aftermarket Tuning:** Before/after validation of remaps and hardware upgrades.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Fuel Consumption from Volumetric Method

**Given:**
- Volume of fuel consumed in burette: $V_b = 85$ mL
- Distance covered: $D = 5.0$ km
- Fuel density: $\rho_{petrol} = 0.745$ g/cm³

**Step 1:** Convert burette reading to litres.
$$V_{fuel} = V_b \times 10^{-3} = 85 \times 10^{-3} = 0.085 \text{ L}$$

**Step 2:** Apply fuel consumption formula.
$$FC = \frac{V_{fuel}}{D} \times 100$$

**Step 3:** Substitute numerical values.
$$FC = \frac{0.085}{5.0} \times 100 = 1.7 \text{ L/100 km}$$

**Step 4:** Express fuel economy (inverse metric).
$$FE = \frac{D}{V_{fuel}} = \frac{5.0}{0.085} = 58.82 \text{ km/L}$$

**Step 5:** Verify dimensional consistency.
$$\left[FC\right] = \frac{L}{km} \times 100 = \frac{L}{100 \text{ km}} \quad \checkmark$$

### 3.2 Derivation — Carbon Balance Fuel Calculation

**Given exhaust measurements:**
- CO₂ flow rate: $m_{CO_2} = 150$ g/km
- CO flow rate: $m_{CO} = 0.5$ g/km
- HC flow rate: $m_{HC} = 0.05$ g/km
- Fuel carbon fraction: $C_\% = 0.862$

**Step 1:** Apply stoichiometric carbon balance.
$$m_{fuel} = \frac{0.866 \cdot m_{CO_2} + 0.429 \cdot m_{CO} + 0.273 \cdot m_{HC}}{C_\%}$$

**Step 2:** Compute numerator contributions.
$$0.866 \times 150 = 129.9$$
$$0.429 \times 0.5 = 0.2145$$
$$0.273 \times 0.05 = 0.01365$$

**Step 3:** Sum the terms.
$$\text{Numerator} = 129.9 + 0.2145 + 0.01365 = 130.128$$

**Step 4:** Divide by carbon fraction.
$$m_{fuel} = \frac{130.128}{0.862} = 150.96 \text{ g/km}$$

**Step 5:** Convert to volume using fuel density.
$$V_{fuel} = \frac{m_{fuel}}{\rho_{fuel}} = \frac{150.96}{745} = 0.2026 \text{ L/km}$$

**Step 6:** Express final fuel consumption.
$$FC = 0.2026 \times 100 = 20.26 \text{ L/100 km}$$

### 3.3 Derivation — Combined Cycle Weighted Fuel Consumption

**Given:** Urban cycle FC = 8.5 L/100 km, Extra-urban FC = 5.2 L/100 km.

**Step 1:** Apply weighted combination (MIDC uses 55% urban + 45% extra-urban).
$$FC_{combined} = 0.55 \cdot FC_{urban} + 0.45 \cdot FC_{extra}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values.
$$FC_{combined} = 0.55 \times 8.5 + 0.45 \times 5.2$$

**Step 3:** Multiply each term.
$$0.55 \times 8.5 = 4.675$$
$$0.45 \times 5.2 = 2.34$$

**Step 4:** Sum the contributions.
$$FC_{combined} = 4.675 + 2.34 = 7.015 \text{ L/100 km}$$

**Step 5:** Express fuel economy.
$$FE = \frac{100}{FC_{combined}} = \frac{100}{7.015} = 14.255 \text{ km/L}$$

### 3.4 Derivation — Average Speed over a Driving Cycle

**Given:** MIDC cycle distance = 10.16 km, cycle duration = 1224 s.

**Step 1:** Convert duration to hours.
$$t = \frac{1224}{3600} = 0.34 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 2:** Apply average speed formula.
$$v_{avg} = \frac{D}{t} = \frac{10.16}{0.34}$$

**Step 3:** Compute result.
$$v_{avg} = 29.88 \text{ km/h}$$

**Step 4:** Sanity check.
$$v_{avg} < 35 \text{ km/h} \implies \text{consistent with urban MIDC profile} \quad \checkmark$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Test Speed vs. Fuel Consumption Behavior

| Test Speed (km/h) | Driving Regime | Expected FC Trend | Dominant Loss Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| 20 | Crawl traffic | High | Idling losses |
| 40 | Urban cruise | Moderate | Acceleration events |
| 50 | Suburban steady | Optimal for many cars | Aerodynamic drag rises |
| 60 | Open road | Optimal for small cars | Drag quadratic rise |
| 90 | Highway | Moderate-high | Parasitic + Drag |
| 120 | Expressway | High | Drag dominates ($v^3$) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physics Insight:** Aerodynamic drag force scales as $F_d = 0.5 \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2$. Power required to overcome drag scales as $v^3$. Hence, doubling speed from 60 to 120 km/h theoretically increases fuel consumption by ~2.4× (assuming all other losses are constant).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Architecture — Fuel Consumption Test Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Fuel Tank Main] --> node2[Auxiliary Reservoir]
    node2 --> node3[Engine Fuel Rail]
    node3 --> node4[Engine Combustion]
    node4 --> node5[Exhaust System]

    node9 --> node10
    node10 --> node11[FC Calculator L per 100 km]
    node11 --> node12[Type Approval Report]
    node10 --> node13[Dynamometer Load Cell]
    node13 --> node11
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Q: Define fuel consumption test. List any two standard methods of fuel consumption measurement.**

**Model Answer:**

A **fuel consumption test** is a controlled, standardized procedure to quantify the volume or mass of fuel consumed by a vehicle over a specified distance or driving cycle, expressed in **L/100 km**, **km/L**, or **g/km CO₂**.

Two standard methods:
1. **Volumetric method** — Uses a precision burette to directly measure the volume of fuel drawn from the engine fuel line during the test run.
2. **Gravimetric method** — Measures the mass of fuel consumed by weighing a sealed auxiliary reservoir before and after the test, then converting mass to volume using fuel density.

---

#### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Q: Why is test route selection important in vehicle fuel consumption testing? State the typical Indian test speeds.**

**Model Answer:**

**Importance of test route selection:**
- Ensures the test results **represent real-world driving** in the target market (urban, suburban, highway mix).
- Guarantees **repeatability** — the same driver on the same route yields consistent results.
- Provides **regulatory comparability** — different OEMs tested under identical conditions can be benchmarked fairly.

**Standard Indian test speeds:**
- 40 km/h, 50 km/h, 60 km/h (urban-suburban)
- 90 km/h, 120 km/h (highway-expressway)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2 Choice A]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **volumetric and gravimetric methods** of fuel consumption measurement with neat sketches. Highlight their respective **advantages and limitations**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Volumetric Method:**

In this method, a precision burette (typically 100 mL capacity with 0.1 mL graduations) is connected in series with the engine fuel supply line, bypassing the main fuel tank. As the engine consumes fuel during the test run, the liquid level in the burette drops. The volume consumed ($V_b$ in mL) is read directly from the burette scale.

**Procedure:**
1. Fill burette to a known upper mark.
2. Record initial level and odometer reading.
3. Run the prescribed driving cycle / constant speed.
4. Record final burette level and odometer.
5. Compute:
$$FC = \frac{V_b \times 10^{-3}}{D} \times 100 \quad [\text{L/100 km}]$$

**Advantages:** Real-time data, simple, low cost, suitable for chassis dyno tests.
**Limitations:** Limited burette capacity, requires refill for long tests, affected by fuel slosh in the burette, fuel temperature sensitivity.

**(ii) Gravimetric Method:**

The vehicle's fuel supply is rerouted to a sealed auxiliary reservoir placed on a precision electronic balance (accuracy ±0.1 g). The mass of fuel is recorded before and after the test.

**Procedure:**
1. Weigh reservoir → $m_1$.
2. Run the test cycle.
3. Weigh reservoir → $m_2$.
4. Compute mass consumed: $\Delta m = m_1 - m_2$.
5. Convert to volume: $V = \Delta m / \rho_{fuel}$.
6. Compute $FC$ as before.

**Advantages:** Higher accuracy than volumetric, suitable for long test cycles (multi-hour), no interruption needed.
**Limitations:** Requires precision balance, sensitive to vehicle tilt, vibration, and ambient temperature fluctuations.

**(b)** During a chassis dynamometer fuel consumption test on a passenger car, the following data was obtained at a steady speed of **60 km/h**:
- Distance covered: **50 km**
- Mass of fuel consumed (gravimetric): **2.55 kg**
- Fuel type: Petrol with density $\rho = 0.745$ g/cm³
- CO₂ emission measured: **155 g/km**

Calculate:
(i) Volume of fuel consumed **(2 Marks)**
(ii) Fuel consumption in **L/100 km** and **km/L** **(3 Marks)**
(iii) Verify the CO₂ emission using the empirical relation and comment on the discrepancy **(2 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Volume of fuel consumed:**
$$V_{fuel} = \frac{m_{fuel}}{\rho_{fuel}} = \frac{2.55 \text{ kg}}{0.745 \text{ kg/L}}$$
$$V_{fuel} = \frac{2550}{745} = 3.423 \text{ L}$$

**(ii) Fuel consumption in L/100 km:**
$$FC = \frac{V_{fuel}}{D} \times 100 = \frac{3.423}{50} \times 100 = 6.846 \text{ L/100 km}$$

Fuel economy in km/L:
$$FE = \frac{D}{V_{fuel}} = \frac{50}{3.423} = 14.61 \text{ km/L}$$

**(iii) Empirical CO₂ check:**
$$CO_2 = 3.16 \times FC = 3.16 \times 6.846 = 21.63 \times 6.846 / 3.16 \quad \text{(recheck)}$$

$$CO_2 = 3.16 \times 6.846 = 21.63 \text{ × 10} = \text{Wait — recompute:}$$
$$CO_2 = 3.16 \times 6.846 = 21.634 \text{ g/km} \times 10$$

Recompute carefully:
$$3.16 \times 6.846 = 3 \times 6.846 + 0.16 \times 6.846 = 20.538 + 1.095 = 21.633$$

But the standard empirical formula is:
$$CO_2 \text{ (g/km)} = 23.2 \times FC \text{ (L/100 km)} \quad \text{for petrol}$$

Using standard formula:
$$CO_2 = 23.2 \times 6.846 = 158.83 \text{ g/km}$$

Measured value = 155 g/km. Discrepancy = $\frac{158.83 - 155}{158.83} \times 100 = 2.41\%$

**Comment:** The discrepancy of 2.41% is well within the typical measurement tolerance of ±5% for chassis dynamometer tests. It can be attributed to fuel composition variation (real fuel C% may differ slightly from the 86.2% baseline used in the empirical formula).

---

#### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2 Choice B]**

**(a)** Discuss the **criteria for test route selection** in road-based fuel consumption testing. Explain how **statistical representativeness** and **repeatability** are ensured. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Criteria for Test Route Selection:**

1. **Geographical Representativeness:** The route must include the typical road categories encountered by target customers — urban arterial, city centre, suburban connector, rural road, and highway. For Indian testing, the route typically passes through metro traffic, town outskirts, and national highway segments.

2. **Topographical Profile:** Altitude variation should be moderate (less than ±150 m over the route) to avoid excessive grade effects that bias fuel consumption. Standardized test routes avoid steep inclines.

3. **Traffic Density:** The route must allow the test driver to follow a representative speed profile without being held up by abnormal congestion. Average urban speeds of **15–30 km/h** must be achievable.

4. **Road Surface Quality:** Smooth, well-maintained asphalt without potholes, speed breakers (where avoidable), or loose gravel — to minimize ride-related calibration errors.

5. **Stopping Point Frequency:** Adequate traffic lights, intersections, and pedestrian crossings to reproduce the stop-go pattern of the target cycle.

6. **Length and Duration:** Sufficient length (typically **10–20 km** for one cycle) to capture meaningful fuel consumption data.

**Statistical Representativeness:**
- Multiple candidate routes are surveyed using GPS telemetry from real customer fleets (often 100+ vehicles).
- Trip data is clustered using statistical methods (k-means clustering) into representative patterns.
- The chosen route's micro-trip profile (acceleration, deceleration, idling) must match the statistical centroid of real-world data within ±10%.

**Repeatability:**
- The selected route is driven **5 times minimum** by the same test driver under controlled ambient conditions.
- Standard deviation of FC across runs must be **less than 2%** for the route to be certified.
- Driving style is dictated by a **road-test procedure checklist** (exact gear selection, acceleration pedal modulation).

---

**(b)** A vehicle is tested on a **chassis dynamometer** following the Modified Indian Driving Cycle (MIDC). The MIDC has:
- Total distance: **10.16 km**
- Total duration: **1224 seconds**
- Urban phase contribution: 55%
- Extra-urban phase contribution: 45%

Urban phase fuel consumption = **8.7 L/100 km**
Extra-urban phase fuel consumption = **5.4 L/100 km**

Calculate:
(i) Average speed of the complete MIDC cycle **(3 Marks)**
(ii) Combined fuel consumption in L/100 km **(3 Marks)**
(iii) Convert the combined FC to km/L and approximate CO₂ emission in g/km **(1 Mark)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Average speed of MIDC:**
$$v_{avg} = \frac{D_{cycle}}{\Delta t_{cycle}} = \frac{10.16 \text{ km}}{1224 \text{ s}}$$
$$v_{avg} = \frac{10.16}{1224/3600} = \frac{10.16}{0.34} = 29.88 \text{ km/h}$$

**(ii) Combined fuel consumption (weighted):**
$$FC_{combined} = 0.55 \cdot FC_{urban} + 0.45 \cdot FC_{extra}$$
$$FC_{combined} = 0.55 \times 8.7 + 0.45 \times 5.4$$
$$= 4.785 + 2.43 = 7.215 \text{ L/100 km}$$

**(iii) Fuel economy in km/L:**
$$FE = \frac{100}{7.215} = 13.86 \text{ km/L}$$

**Approximate CO₂ emission (using petrol coefficient 23.2):**
$$CO_2 \approx 23.2 \times 7.215 = 167.39 \text{ g/km}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Forgetting to convert mL to L in burette readings (×10⁻³) or seconds to hours for speed. This is the **#1 reason** students lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Wrong Fuel Density:** Using 0.74 for diesel or 0.83 for petrol. Memorize both.
> 3. **Combined Cycle Weighting:** Always use **0.55 urban + 0.45 extra-urban** for MIDC. ECE R101 uses different weights — verify the standard.
> 4. **Empirical CO₂ Coefficient:** Use **23.2 g/km per L/100 km** for petrol, **26.5** for diesel (some texts use 3.16/3.17 — these are kg CO₂ per L of fuel; divide by FC/100 to get g/km — they are equivalent but mark allocation expects proper dimensional handling).
> 5. **Forgetting to state units** in final answer — automatic 0.5 mark deduction in KTU valuation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Bullet Recap — Module 2 Fuel Consumption Testing:**

- **FC Formula:** $FC = \frac{V_{fuel}}{D} \times 100$ → units **L/100 km**
- **FE Formula:** $FE = \frac{D}{V_{fuel}}$ → units **km/L**
- **Conversion:** $FE = \frac{100}{FC}$ and $FC = \frac{100}{FE}$
- **Petrol density:** **0.745 g/cm³** | **Diesel density:** **0.832 g/cm³**
- **Carbon fraction petrol:** **86.2%** | **Diesel:** **86.4%**
- **MIDC weighting:** **55% urban + 45% extra-urban**
- **Standard test speeds:** **40, 50, 60, 90, 120 km/h**
- **CO₂ empirical (petrol):** **23.2 g/km per L/100 km**
- **CO₂ empirical (diesel):** **26.5 g/km per L/100 km**
- **Methods of FC measurement:** Volumetric, Gravimetric, Carbon Balance, PEMS
- **Test route criteria:** Urban + Suburban + Highway mix, topographically flat, repeatable, statistically representative
- **Cycle validation:** Standard deviation of FC across 5 runs **< 2%**
- **Drag physics:** Power to overcome aerodynamic drag $\propto v^3$ → doubling speed → ~2.4× fuel consumption increase (drag-limited regime)
- **Standards to remember:** **ECE R101, AIS-050, IS 14664, BS-VI Phase 2, WLTP**
- **Average MIDC speed:** ≈ **29.88 km/h**
- **Carbon balance numerator:** $0.866 \cdot m_{CO_2} + 0.429 \cdot m_{CO} + 0.273 \cdot m_{HC}$
- **Indian ambient test temp:** **20°C to 30°C**, **99.325 kPa** reference pressure
- **Carbon balance denominator:** Carbon fraction $C_\%$ of fuel (0.862 petrol, 0.864 diesel)

---

**End of Module 2 Topic Notes — Fuel Consumption Tests, Test Route Selection, and Vehicle Test Speeds**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Collision and Crash Testing: Human Testing and Dummies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT724 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Collision and Crash Testing: Human Testing and Dummies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Collision and Crash Testing

**Collision and Crash Testing** is the systematic experimental evaluation of a vehicle's structural integrity and occupant protection systems during and immediately after an impact event. It quantifies the *crashworthiness* of a vehicle — the ability of the vehicle's structure, restraint systems, and interior geometry to protect occupants from injury under defined impact conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Crash testing is categorized as a sub-domain of *Active and Passive Safety Evaluation*. The 2024 scheme specifically stresses understanding of **biomechanics**, **injury criteria**, and the role of **Anthropomorphic Test Devices (ATDs)** in lieu of human subjects.

### 1.2 Human Testing in Crash Research

**Human Testing** refers to the controlled exposure of live human volunteers, cadavers (post-mortem human subjects — PMHS), and animals to impact and deceleration environments for the purpose of collecting biomechanical data. Modern human testing is severely restricted by ethical and legal constraints; therefore, mechanical surrogates have largely replaced it.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine you are a baker trying to find out how much force it takes to crack an egg when it is dropped from different heights. You cannot drop every egg to check, so you build a *mechanical egg* made of rubber, plastic, and sensors. You drop this *dummy egg* and measure the impact. The crash test dummy plays the exact same role — it is a *biomechanical egg* built to mimic how a human body would respond in a crash.

### 1.3 Crash Test Dummies (Anthropomorphic Test Devices — ATDs)

An **Anthropomorphic Test Device (ATD)** is a calibrated, instrumented, human-shaped mechanical surrogate used to measure the loads, accelerations, and displacements experienced by vehicle occupants during a crash. ATDs are engineered to replicate the **kinematic** (motion) and **kinetic** (force-response) behaviour of a human of a given size, mass distribution, and gender.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> *"An Anthropomorphic Test Device (ATD) is a biofidelic mechanical surrogate instrumented to record occupant response during standardized crash tests, enabling objective evaluation of injury risk without endangering human subjects."*

The principal dummies in current regulatory use are:
- **Hybrid III 50th Percentile Male** — frontal impact testing
- **Hybrid III 5th Percentile Female** — frontal, small occupant
- **Hybrid III 95th Percentile Male** — out-of-position tests
- **THOR** — advanced frontal, modern NCAP
- **WorldSID / EuroSID 2** — side impact testing
- **Q-Series** (Q3, Q6, Q10) — child occupants

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metrics (KTU High-Yield):**
> - **HIC threshold (HIC₁₅) = 700** (FMVSS 208 limit, corresponds to ~16% risk of severe head injury)
> - **Nij (Neck Injury Criterion) ≤ 1.0** for low-risk
> - **Chest deflection limit ≈ 63 mm** (Hybrid III, 50th male)
> - **Femur compressive load limit = 10 kN**
> - **AIS 3+** is the standard severe-injury threshold

### 1.4 Why Dummies Instead of Humans?

| Constraint | Consequence |
|---|---|
| **Ethical / IRB restrictions** | No live human may be crashed; Nuremberg Code, Declaration of Helsinki prohibit non-therapeutic harm. |
| **Injury risk** | Even sub-injurious volunteer tests can cause whiplash, concussion, or internal trauma. |
| **Reproducibility** | A 75 kg human varies in bone density, muscle tone, age, posture — invalidates comparative tests. |
| **Cost & liability** | Cadaver studies require rare PMHS specimens costing $10,000–$50,000 USD per specimen. |
| **Regulatory mandate** | FMVSS, Euro NCAP, AIS, and GTR regulations explicitly require ATDs, not humans. |
> *Note: Vertical pipe characters in equations above are rendered as `\\vert` for markdown safety.*
