# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Biomechanics — The Foundation of Injury Criterion

**Biomechanics** is the application of mechanical principles (statics, dynamics, energy absorption) to the human body. The three pillars of crash biomechanics are:

1. **Force tolerance** of tissues (e.g., cortical bone tolerates ≈ 235 MPa compression).
2. **Acceleration tolerance** of organs (brain injury arises from Δv and Δa).
3. **Energy dissipation** capacity of restraint systems (seatbelts, airbags, crumple zones).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters (Engineering Utility):**
> In the automotive industry, biomechanical data is the *bridge* between a *physical crash test* and a *predicted injury outcome*. Without injury criteria, a crash test would simply record accelerations and displacements with no clinical meaning.

## 2.2 Injury Criteria — The Quantitative Language of Crash Safety

### 2.2.1 Head Injury Criterion (HIC)

HIC is the most widely used predictor of **traumatic brain injury (TBI)**. It is calculated from the resultant head acceleration $a(t)$ (in g's) over a specified time window.

$$
\text{HIC} = \max_{t_1, t_2} \left[ \left( \frac{1}{t_2 - t_1} \int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\, dt \right)^{2.5} (t_2 - t_1) \right]
$$

where the time interval $(t_2 - t_1)$ is capped at:
- **HIC₁₅** → 15 ms window
- **HIC₃₆** → 36 ms window (older FMVSS 208)

**Interpretation thresholds (KTU 2024 critical):**
- **HIC ≤ 240** → no/low risk
- **HIC ≤ 700** → AIS ≤ 2 (FMVSS 208 pass threshold)
- **HIC ≥ 1000** → high risk of fatal brain injury (AIS 4+)

### 2.2.2 Neck Injury Criterion (Nij)

$$
N_{ij} = \frac{F_{z}}{F_{z,c}} + \frac{M_{y}}{M_{y,c}}
$$

where:
- $F_z$ = axial (tension/compression) neck force
- $F_{z,c}$ = critical axial force limit (≈ **4.17 kN tension, 4.0 kN compression**)
- $M_y$ = flexion/extension moment
- $M_{y,c}$ = critical moment limit (≈ **57.83 Nm flexion, 42 Nm extension**)

**Nij ≤ 1.0** indicates a low probability of neck injury (AIS ≤ 2).

### 2.2.3 Chest Injury (Thoracic) Criterion

For Hybrid III, **chest deflection** measured by sternum-mounted potentiometer is the primary predictor. The limit is:
- **63 mm** (chest deflection peak) for 50th male → ~25% probability of AIS 3+ injury
- **52 mm** for 5th female

**Compression Criterion (CC)** for the thorax:
$$
CC = \frac{D_{max}}{D_{limit}}
$$
where $D_{max}$ is peak sternum deflection and $D_{limit}$ is the tolerance.

### 2.2.4 Femur Load Criterion

$$
F_{femur} \leq 10\,\text{kN} \quad \text{(axial compression)}
$$

Exceeding this load risks femur shaft fracture (AIS 2+).

### 2.2.5 Abbreviated Injury Scale (AIS)

A clinically validated 6-level ordinal scale (1 = minor, 6 = unsurvivable). It is the **lingua franca** linking biomechanical metrics to medical outcomes.

| AIS Level | Severity | Example |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | No injury | — |
| 1 | Minor | Bruise, superficial laceration |
| 2 | Moderate | Whiplash, simple fracture |
| 3 | Serious | Compound fracture, concussion |
| 4 | Severe | Ruptured liver, internal hemorrhage |
| 5 | Critical | Major vessel laceration |
| 6 | Unsurvivable | Decapitation, aortic rupture |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Point:** The threshold for "serious injury" in most modern NCAP star-rating systems is **MAIS 3+** (Maximum AIS ≥ 3).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

| # | Symbol | Formula | Meaning | Typical Limit | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | HIC | $\left( \frac{1}{t_2 - t_1} \int a\,dt \right)^{2.5} (t_2 - t_1)$ | Head injury | $\leq 700$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | GSI | $\int a^{2.5}\,dt$ (Gadd) | Pre-HIC index | — | dimensionless |
| 3 | Nij | $\frac{F_z}{F_{z,c}} + \frac{M_y}{M_{y,c}}$ | Neck injury | $\leq 1.0$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | CC | $D_{max} / D_{limit}$ | Chest compression | $\leq 1.0$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | $F_{femur}$ | Axial force | Femur load | $\leq 10$ | kN |
| 6 | $a_{3ms}$ | Peak 3 ms accel. | Spine injury | $\leq 60$ | g |
| 7 | VC | $0.5\, (a_x + a_z) \cdot V_{eff}$ | Viscous criterion (thorax/abdomen) | $\leq 1.0$ | m/s |
| 8 | TTI | $\sqrt{a_y^2 + a_z^2}$ | Thoracic trauma (DRI) | $\leq 85$ | g |
| 9 | Tibia Index | $(F_{tib} / F_c) + (M_{tib}/M_c)$ | Lower-leg injury | $\leq 1.0$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\Delta v$ | $v_0 - v_f$ | Velocity change | 50 km/h frontal | m/s |

> *Vertical bars in column entries (where required) are written as `\\vert` or `\\mid` to preserve table rendering.*

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Star Rating Determination (Euro NCAP / Global NCAP):** Injury criterion thresholds directly feed the 5-star occupant protection rating.
2. **Restraint System Tuning:** Airbag deployment pressure and seatbelt pretensioner force are calibrated using HIC and chest deflection targets.
3. **Vehicle Structure Iteration:** Engineers target *intrusion* vs. *ride-down* trade-off — structures must crush to absorb energy but resist hard intrusion into the occupant compartment.
4. **Insurance & Legal Compliance:** IIHS Top Safety Pick+ and GTR (Global Technical Regulation) require ATD-based validation.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation of the Head Injury Criterion (HIC)

### Step 1: Origin in the Gadd Severity Index (1961)

The U.S. Army commissioned Dr. Charles Gadd in 1961 to find a single index predicting brain injury from ejection-seat tests. He proposed the **Gadd Severity Index (GSI)**:

$$
\text{GSI} = \int_0^T a(t)^{2.5}\,dt
$$

where $a(t)$ is **instantaneous resultant head acceleration in g's** and $T$ is the total impact duration.

> *Logic:* Because animal experiments showed that acceleration magnitude raised to the **2.5 power** correlated best with concussion incidence, the integral aggregates this power over the impact duration.

### Step 2: Limitation of GSI

GSI included pre- and post-impact accelerations, inflating the index for non-injurious vibrations. **Dr. Lawrence Patrick** and the General Motors Biomechanics team refined it to consider only the *most injurious* time segment $(t_1, t_2)$ of duration at most 15 ms or 36 ms.

### Step 3: Windowed Average

Define the **effective average acceleration** over $(t_1, t_2)$:

$$
\bar{a} = \frac{1}{t_2 - t_1}\int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\,dt
$$

### Step 4: Form the HIC expression

$$
\text{HIC} = \max_{t_1, t_2} \left[ \bar{a}^{\,2.5} \cdot (t_2 - t_1) \right]
$$

subject to $0 \le t_1 < t_2 \le t_{\text{end}}$ and $t_2 - t_1 \le \Delta t_{\max}$, where $\Delta t_{\max} = 15\,\text{ms}$ for HIC₁₅ and $36\,\text{ms}$ for HIC₃₆.

### Step 5: Numerical Worked Example

Suppose an accelerometer trace yields (in g) over a 25 ms impact:

| Time t (ms) | a(t) (g) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 5 |
| 5 | 25 |
| 10 | 60 |
| 15 | 55 |
| 20 | 30 |
| 25 | 8 |

We search for the maximum over candidate intervals. Evaluate for the central, most-injurious window (8–20 ms):

$$
\bar{a} = \frac{(60+55)\,\Delta t/2 + (30)\,\Delta t}{2\,\Delta t/2 + 2\,\Delta t/2}
$$

Using trapezoidal integration over 8 to 20 ms (Δt = 12 ms):

$$
\int_{8}^{20} a(t)\,dt \approx \frac{12}{2}(60 + 30) = 540\, \text{g·ms}
$$

$$
\bar{a} = \frac{540}{12} = 45\,\text{g}
$$

$$
\text{HIC} = (45)^{2.5} \times 12\,\text{ms} = 13{,}500 \times 12 = 162{,}000
$$

Wait — this is over the regulatory 700 limit, suggesting severe head injury.

> **Numerical check (correct form):** GSI-like integrand uses $a^{2.5}$ integrated, but HIC uses $\bar{a}^{2.5}\cdot\Delta t$. For $\bar a = 45\,\text g$:
> $\quad 45^{2.5} = 45^2 \cdot 45^{0.5} = 2025 \cdot 6.708 \approx 13{,}585$
> $\quad \text{HIC} = 13{,}585 \times 0.012\,\text s = 163.02$

**Corrected: HIC ≈ 163** (well below 700 — no head injury risk). The first calculation confused units. The product $\bar a^{2.5}$ is dimensionless (accel expressed in g) and must be multiplied by time in **seconds**, not ms.

### Step 6: Clinical Interpretation

HIC ≈ 163 ⇒ AIS ≤ 1. If HIC > 1000, AIS ≥ 4 (severe, life-threatening).

| Body Region | Dummy Component | Sensors (Qty) | Injury Risk | Key Metric | Standard |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Head** | Aluminium skull + ballasted cavity | 3-axis accel. (3) | Brain injury, skull fracture | HIC₁₅ ≤ 700 | FMVSS 208 |
| **Neck** | Segmented rubber/aluminium discs | Load cell (Fz), moment (My) | Whiplash, basilar fracture | Nij ≤ 1.0 | FMVSS 208 |
| **Thorax** | Steel rib cage + damper spine | Chest defl. potentiometer | Rib fracture, lung contusion | Defl. ≤ 63 mm | Euro NCAP |
| **Lumbar Spine** | 4-joint polymer | — | Lumbar burst fracture | a_3ms ≤ 60 g | FMVSS 208 |
| **Pelvis** | PU foam + steel hip pins | Iliac load cell | Pelvic ring fracture | Pubic force ≤ 6 kN | Euro NCAP |
| **Femur** | Steel tube | 2-axis load cell | Femur shaft fracture | F ≤ 10 kN | FMVSS 208 |
| **Knee** | Aluminium + rubber | Knee slider | Patellar fracture | Knee disp. ≤ 15 mm | Euro NCAP |
| **Tibia** | Steel | Fz + My | Tibia fracture | TI ≤ 1.0 | Euro NCAP |

> *Note: All `≤` symbols above are rendered as `\\leq` for LaTeX clarity. Use `\\leq` (not `\\le`) to align with KTU valuation scripts.*

## 3.3 Derivation of Deceleration Pulse from Crash Test

Given a barrier impact at initial velocity $v_0$, the rigid barrier makes the vehicle decelerate over a wall-stop distance. The average deceleration is:

$$
\bar{a} = \frac{v_0^2}{2 d_{crush}}
$$

For a frontal impact at $v_0 = 56\,\text{km/h} = 15.56\,\text{m/s}$ with crush distance $d_{crush} = 0.5\,\text m$:

$$
\bar{a} = \frac{(15.56)^2}{2 \times 0.5} = \frac{242.1}{1.0} = 242.1\,\text{m/s}^2
$$

Converting to g ($1\,g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$):

$$
\bar{a} = \frac{242.1}{9.81} \approx 24.7\,g
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A real crash pulse is **not** a constant deceleration; it has a *peak* (typically 25–40 g) followed by *ride-down* (10–20 g) as the occupant compartment decelerates with the structure. The *ride-down* region is what determines occupant injury — peak alone is meaningless without pulse shape.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define Head Injury Criterion (HIC) and state its regulatory threshold.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Head Injury Criterion (HIC) is a biomechanical index that quantifies the likelihood of traumatic brain injury (TBI) resulting from head impact or acceleration. It is computed as the maximum of $\bar a^{2.5} \cdot \Delta t$ over any 15 ms window of the head acceleration trace. The **regulatory threshold for FMVSS 208 is HIC₁₅ ≤ 700**, corresponding to a probability of less than 16 % of AIS ≥ 3 head injury.

> *Mark split:* 1 mark definition, 1 mark formula mention, 1 mark threshold & interpretation.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**What is an Anthropomorphic Test Device (ATD)? List any four types of ATDs.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An **Anthropomorphic Test Device (ATD)** is an instrumented mechanical surrogate of a human occupant, used in crash tests to record forces, accelerations, and deflections that a human would experience, thereby allowing objective injury risk assessment.
Four common types: **(i) Hybrid III 50th percentile male, (ii) Hybrid III 5th percentile female, (iii) THOR, (iv) WorldSID / EuroSID 2 for side impact.**

> *Mark split:* 1 mark definition, 1 mark purpose, 1 mark listing four types.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Examination Note:** ESE Module questions in this course have an **internal choice**. Two alternatives are provided below; both have sub-parts (a) and (b) carrying **7 marks each**.

---

### QUESTION A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]

#### (a) Describe in detail the construction, instrumentation, and biofidelity requirements of the Hybrid III 50th percentile male dummy. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. Anthropometric Specs (1.5 marks):**
The Hybrid III 50th represents a 175 cm, 78 kg North American male. Stature, sitting height, hip breadth, chest depth, thigh length, and shoulder width are scaled to within ±5% of the **50th percentile SAE J963** anthropometric envelope.

**2. Construction by Module (3.5 marks):**
- **Head:** Aluminium skull with vinyl-coated polyurethane skin, instrumented with a tri-axial accelerometer cluster at the CG. Mass = 4.54 kg.
- **Neck:** A stack of 6 rubber-butadiene discs bonded to aluminium plates, with an occipital condyle load cell and an anterior shear load cell. Calibrated for flexion/extension pendulum impact.
- **Thorax:** 6 steel ribs, each urethane-padded, mounted on a thoracic spine made of steel cable and rubber. Sternum-mounted chest deflection potentiometer measures sternal travel relative to spine.
- **Lumbar spine:** Four-joint polymer block transmitting axial force.
- **Pelvis:** Rigid PU foam in a steel-reinforced shell, with iliac load cells behind each hip.
- **Lower limbs:** Steel femur (calibrated strain gauges) → knee slider potentiometer → steel tibia (Fz and My load cells) → ball-joint foot/ankle.

**3. Instrumentation & DAQ (1 mark):**
More than **40 individual channels** (3 head accel., 6 neck, chest defl., lumbar, 2 hip, 2 femur, 2 knee, 4 tibia, etc.) routed through a 1000 Hz low-pass filtered DAS.

**4. Biofidelity (1 mark):**
Biofidelity is the *degree to which a dummy's dynamic response matches that of a real human subject under the same load*. Hybrid III was validated against cadaver, animal, and volunteer tests in frontal, but is **less biofidelic for oblique and rear impacts** — THOR was developed to address this gap.

#### (b) Explain the various injury criteria used in crash testing, with regulatory limits. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

A summary table is requested by most board examiners. Provide definitions and limits:

**1. Head Injury Criterion (HIC) — 1.5 marks:**
$$
\text{HIC} = \max_{t_1,t_2} \left[ \bar{a}^{2.5}\,(t_2 - t_1) \right],\quad \bar{a} = \frac{1}{t_2 - t_1}\int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\,dt
$$
**Limit:** HIC₁₅ ≤ 700. Higher = more severe brain injury risk.

**2. Neck Injury Criterion (Nij) — 1.5 marks:**
$$
N_{ij} = \frac{F_z}{F_{z,c}} + \frac{M_y}{M_{y,c}}
$$
**Limit:** Nij ≤ 1.0 for low risk. $F_{z,c} \approx 4.17\,\text{kN}$ (tension), $4.0\,\text{kN}$ (compression); $M_{y,c} \approx 57.83\,\text{Nm}$ (flexion), $42\,\text{Nm}$ (extension).

**3. Chest Deflection / Compression Criterion (CC) — 1.5 marks:**
Hybrid III 50th — peak sternum deflection ≤ **63 mm**. Lower limit ~52 mm for 5th female.

**4. Femur Load — 1 mark:**
Axial compressive force ≤ **10 kN**. Beyond this, femur shaft fracture is likely (AIS 2+).

**5. Tibia Index (TI) — 0.75 mark:**
$$
\text{TI} = \frac{F_{tib}}{F_c} + \frac{M_{tib}}{M_c} \le 1.0
$$

**6. Viscous Criterion (VC) for side impact thorax — 0.75 mark:**
$$
\text{VC} = 0.5 \cdot (a_x + a_z) \cdot V_{eff} \le 1.0
$$

> *Examiner note:* Always quote **both** the formula and the numerical limit; students who only state the formula lose 0.5–1 mark per criterion.

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam - June 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]

#### (a) Discuss the various types of crash tests performed on automobiles and their significance. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. Frontal Crash Tests (2 marks):**
- **Full-Width Rigid Barrier (FWRB):** Vehicle impacts a flat rigid wall face-on. Standardized at 56 km/h in FMVSS 208. Tests restraint system + crumple zone.
- **Offset Deformable Barrier (ODB):** Vehicle strikes a deformable aluminium honeycomb barrier with 40 % overlap. Higher intrusion, higher HIC, used in Euro NCAP at 64 km/h.
- **Small Overlap Test (IIHS):** Only 25 % of the front engages the barrier, targeting the wheel and footwell intrusion path.

**2. Side Impact Tests (1.5 marks):**
- **Mobile Deformable Barrier (MDB):** A 950 kg trolley strikes the stationary car at 50 km/h, 90° to vehicle's longitudinal axis.
- **Pole Test:** Vehicle is propelled sideways into a rigid pole (32 km/h, Euro NCAP). Worst case for head, thorax, and pelvis of far-side occupant.

**3. Rear / Whiplash (1 mark):**
- Sled or barrier rig with 35 km/h rear impact; measures neck injury via NIC. Endorsed by Euro NCAP and Insurance Ratings.

**4. Rollover (1 mark):**
- Roof crush test (FMVSS 216) measures roof strength under a quasi-static load (≥ 3.0 × vehicle curb weight).

**5. Sled Tests and Sub-System Tests (1 mark):**
- Used in early R&D; instrumented sled with sub-system (seat + belt + airbag + dummy) accelerated by pneumatic piston. Cost-effective, repeatable, no need to destroy full vehicle.

**6. Pedestrian and Cyclist Tests (0.5 mark):**
- Impactor tests on bonnet, bumper, and windshield for head, leg, and pelvis injury (AIS limits for adult and child).

**Significance:** These tests allow manufacturers to demonstrate compliance with **FMVSS (USA)**, **ECE (EU)**, **AIS / GTR (UN)** and obtain consumer ratings from **Euro NCAP, IIHS, Global NCAP**.

#### (b) Explain human testing in crash research, with its historical evolution and ethical considerations. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. Historical Perspective (3 marks):**
- **1949–1952 — Sierra Sam (US Air Force):** First anthropomorphic dummy, used in ejection-seat tests. Basic shoulder-and-head shape, no instrumentation.
- **1954 — Colonel John Paul Stapp's rocket-sled experiments:** Stapp, on a rocket-propelled sled at Holloman AFB, personally endured decelerations up to **46.2 g** in 1954, suffering temporary blindness, retinal hemorrhages, and broken ribs. This produced the first real human tolerance data.
- **1960s — Hybrid I, Hybrid II, Sierra Stan:** GM's Wayne State University team led by Dr. Lawrence Patrick developed increasingly biofidelic dummies.
- **1970s — Hybrid III (introduced 1976):** Became the global standard for FMVSS 208.
- **PMHS Cadaver Tests (1950s–present):** Used in WSU, Heidelberg, Innsbruck, Paris. Limited availability; published PMI (Post Mortem Interval) data.
- **Volunteer Tests:** Currently limited to low-speed whiplash and quasi-static tests. Volunteer must give informed consent, with IRB oversight.

**2. Types of Human Testing (2 marks):**
- **PMHS (Post-Mortem Human Subjects) tests:** Drop tower, pendulum, and sled impacts on cadaver specimens to obtain bone and tissue response.
- **Volunteer tests:** Limited to whiplash, sub-injury acceleration, and belt-fit studies.
- **Animal models:** Baboon, chimpanzee, pig, and dog models used historically (e.g., NCSU's NHP biofidelity data for THOR).

**3. Ethical Considerations (2 marks):**
- **Nuremberg Code, Declaration of Helsinki, Belmont Report:** Prohibit non-therapeutic harmful experiments on human subjects.
- **Institutional Review Boards (IRB):** Volunteer studies require ethics committee approval.
- **Informed consent:** Each volunteer must sign a detailed consent form disclosing risks.
- **Equivalence principle:** Animal models must follow the **3R rule** — **Replacement, Reduction, Refinement** — to minimize suffering.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Loss of Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing AIS with HIC:** Students often state "HIC ≤ 700 is fatal" — wrong. HIC 700 is a *regulatory pass limit*, not fatal. Fatal brain injury correlates with HIC > 1000 (AIS 4+).
> 2. **Missing numerical limit:** Stating Nij formula but not quoting Nij ≤ 1.0 loses 0.5–1 mark.
> 3. **Hybrid III confusion with Hybrid II:** Hybrid II is the older, less biofidelic version; current regulatory dummy is Hybrid III. Mixing them up is a 1-mark deduction.
> 4. **Stating "Human testing is widely done" in 2024 scheme answers:** Wrong. Modern human testing is restricted; dummies are the regulatory standard.
> 5. **Skipping units in formulas:** HIC is dimensionless but acceleration must be in **g** (not m/s²) for the 2.5 power interpretation. Examiner will deduct 0.5 mark.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Crash testing** evaluates **crashworthiness** — the structure's ability to protect occupants.
- **ATD (Anthropomorphic Test Device)** is a mechanical human surrogate; Hybrid III 50th is the most common regulatory dummy.
- **HIC₁₅ ≤ 700** is the FMVSS 208 frontal head injury threshold.
- **Nij ≤ 1.0** for low neck injury risk.
- **Chest deflection ≤ 63 mm** for 50th male Hybrid III.
- **Femur load ≤ 10 kN** is the lower-limb fracture limit.
- **AIS 3+** is the modern "serious injury" threshold for NCAP.
- **Human testing** is severely restricted; modern crash safety relies on **dummies + cadaver data + computational (FE) human body models**.
- **Test types:** Frontal (FWRB, ODB), Side (MDB, Pole), Rear (Whiplash), Rollover, Sled, Pedestrian.
- **Standards body hierarchy:** UNECE GTR → FMVSS (USA) / ECE (EU) → Euro NCAP / IIHS (consumer rating).
- **Biofidelity** measures how closely an ATD mimics a real human's dynamic response.
- **Ethical frameworks (3Rs, Helsinki, IRB)** govern any human/animal crash testing today.

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Mnemonic — "HEAD NECK CHEST LEGS AIS":**
> H(HIC)·E(Eye)·A(Anterior shear)
> N(Nij)·E(Ey)·C(Cervical)
> K(Knee)
> **C(Compression)**·H(HIC chest)·E(E)
> **S(Sternum)**
> **T(Tibia)**·I(Ilium)·B(Buckle)·A(Ankle)·L(Lumbar)
> **S(Spine a3ms)**
> Link each capital letter to a regulatory limit and a dummy sensor for **quick recall in the 3-hour KTU exam**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Crashworthiness, Pole Crash Testing, and Near Crash Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT724 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Crashworthiness, Pole Crash Testing, and Near Crash Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Crashworthiness — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crashworthiness** is the inherent ability of a vehicle's structural and restraint systems to **attenuate the kinetic energy of a crash**, preserve the integrity of the occupant survival space (the "survival cell"), and limit the biomechanical loads transmitted to the occupants — thereby minimizing the risk of fatal or serious injury.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, crashworthiness is quantified using three load-paths:

1. **Primary load path** — structural energy absorption through controlled plastic deformation of the front/rear crumple zone.
2. **Secondary load path** — interaction of the occupant with the restraint system (airbag, seatbelt pretensioner, knee bolster).
3. **Tertiary load path** — post-impact ejection prevention and interior subsystem padding (H-point, door trim, headliner).

**Key Metric:** Injury criteria such as **HIC (Head Injury Criterion ≤ 1000)**, **Chest deflection ≤ 75 mm**, and **Femur force ≤ 10 kN** must be satisfied for a 5-star Euro NCAP rating.

## 1.2 Pole Crash Testing — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> A **Pole Crash Test** is a side-impact regulatory and consumer-information test in which a stationary vehicle is propelled (or a moving pole strikes a stationary vehicle) at a rigid, vertical, cylindrical pole of **254 mm diameter**, oriented perpendicular to the vehicle's longitudinal axis. The test evaluates head, thorax, abdomen, and pelvis protection — especially when the impact occurs at the **A-pillar / roof rail** zone where airbags may not deploy optimally.

Common regulatory references for pole testing include **FMVSS 214E (US)**, **ECE R95 Supplement 1**, and **Euro NCAP Side Impact Pole Protocol**.

## 1.3 Near Crash Testing — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Near Crash Testing** (also called *near-miss testing* or *pre-crash scenario testing*) evaluates the effectiveness of active safety systems — particularly **Autonomous Emergency Braking (AEB)**, **Forward Collision Warning (FCW)**, and **Electronic Stability Control (ESC)** — in scenarios that approach but do not culminate in a collision. The system response is graded by **time-to-collision (TTC)**, **deceleration profile**, and **collision avoidance probability**.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Egg & Cushion" Model

Imagine you are throwing a raw egg against a brick wall:

| Element in Analogy | Real Vehicle Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Brick wall (rigid) | Rigid road barrier or pole |
| Egg (delicate occupant) | Human body inside the cabin |
| Air-tight container with bubble wrap | Crumple zone + airbag |
| Slow hand-catch (controlled stop) | Vehicle structural collapse |
| Time taken to stop | Crash pulse duration |

> **Key Insight:** A good crashworthy vehicle is *not* the one that does not deform — it is the one that **deforms strategically** to extend the deceleration pulse (Δt) and thereby **reduce peak acceleration (a_peak)** because impulse `F·Δt = m·Δv` is constant. A longer "ride-down" equals smaller forces on the chest and skull.

---

## 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Gravitational acceleration:** **g = 9.81 m/s²**
> - **Pole diameter (Euro NCAP / FMVSS 214E):** **254 mm (10 inches)**
> - **Crash test dummy mass (Hybrid III 50th percentile):** **78 kg** (full assembly)
> - **Equivalent test speed (front rigid barrier):** **64.4 km/h (40 mph)**
> - **Pole test impact speed (Euro NCAP):** **32 km/h**
> - **HIC threshold (fatal injury):** **1000**
> - **AIS 3+ (serious injury) femur load:** **10 kN**
> - **Euro NCAP chest deflection limit (Side Pole):** **42 mm** (for 50th percentile dummy)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Crashworthiness — Engineering Philosophy

A crashworthy vehicle is designed around the principle of **impulse-momentum management**:

$$F \cdot \Delta t = m \cdot \Delta v$$

Since the change in velocity (Δv) is fixed by the crash scenario, the designer has only two levers:

- **Maximize Δt** (longer deceleration pulse) → *achieved by crushable front structure*
- **Minimize peak F** for a given impulse → *achieved by progressive force-deflection curve*

### 2.1.1 Force-Deflection Characteristic (Crush Curve)

The structural member exhibits a non-linear crush behavior:

$$F(x) = F_0 + k \cdot x^n$$

Where:
- $F_0$ = initial peak force (kN) — represents the buckling/elastic threshold
- $k$ = crush stiffness (kN/m)
- $n$ = hardening exponent (typically 1.0 to 1.5 for modern ultra-high-strength-steel rails)
- $x$ = local crush displacement (m)

The **total energy absorbed** during crush from 0 to $d$:

$$E_{abs} = \int_0^d F(x) \, dx = F_0 d + \frac{k \cdot d^{n+1}}{n+1}$$

### 2.1.2 Average vs. Peak Deceleration

$$\bar{a} = \frac{v_i^2}{2 \cdot s}$$

$$\bar{F} = m \cdot \bar{a} = \frac{m \cdot v_i^2}{2 \cdot s}$$

A **longer crush stroke (s)** yields a lower average force on the occupant, even though the total impulse is the same.

---

## 2.2 Pole Crash Test — Physics of Oblique Loading

### 2.2.1 Energy Considerations

For a vehicle of mass *m* impacting a rigid pole at velocity $v_p$:

$$E_{kinetic} = \frac{1}{2} m v_p^2$$

This energy must be dissipated by:

1. **Local bending of the door / B-pillar** (the primary energy sink — must absorb ≥ 60% of E_kin)
2. **Lateral frame rail deformation**
3. **Floor pan buckling**
4. **Thorax-pad and door-trim energy absorption**

### 2.2.2 Intrusion Velocity Model

The occupant contact velocity is approximated by:

$$v_{occ} = v_p \cdot \sqrt{\frac{m_v}{m_v + m_{door-eff}}}$$

Where $m_{door-eff}$ is the effective mass of the deformable door structure resisting the pole.

### 2.2.3 HIC (Head Injury Criterion) — Full Equation

$$\text{HIC} = \left[ \frac{1}{t_2 - t_1} \int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t) \, dt \right]^{2.5} (t_2 - t_1)$$

Where $a(t)$ is the resultant head acceleration in **g** units, and $(t_2 - t_1)$ is bounded by the **HIC15** (15 ms) or **HIC36** (36 ms) window. The maximum HIC value is the lower of the two:

$$\text{HIC} = \max\left(\text{HIC}_{15}, \text{HIC}_{36}\right) \leq 1000 \text{ (FMVSS limit)}$$

### 2.2.4 Chest Severity Index (CSI) — Thoracic Load

For pole tests, the **Viscous Criterion (VC)** is also evaluated:

$$VC(t) = \frac{v(t) \cdot C(t)}{D_C}$$

Where:
- $v(t)$ = chest deflection velocity (m/s)
- $C(t)$ = chest deflection (m)
- $D_C$ = critical chest deflection constant (**0.25 m** for rib fracture)

Threshold: $VC_{max} \leq 1.0$ m/s for AIS ≤ 3 injury.

---

## 2.3 Near Crash Testing — Active Safety Metrics

### 2.3.1 Time-to-Collision (TTC)

$$TTC(t) = \frac{d_{rel}(t)}{v_{rel}(t)}$$

Where $d_{rel}$ and $v_{rel}$ are relative distance and velocity between host vehicle and target.

A typical AEB activation threshold is **TTC ≤ 2.5 s** (warning) and **TTC ≤ 1.4 s** (full braking).

### 2.3.2 Deceleration Required to Avoid Collision

$$a_{req} = \frac{v_{rel}^2}{2 \cdot d_{rel}}$$

If the AEB system delivers $a_{AEB} \geq a_{req}$, collision is avoided.

### 2.3.3 Subjective Rating (Euro NCAP AEB Protocol)

> Subjective performance is graded on a 4-point scale: **Poor, Marginal, Acceptable, Good**, with each subsystem (city, inter-urban, pedestrian) scored independently.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Threshold |
|---|---------|-------------|--------------------|
| 1 | $E_k = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$ | Kinetic energy at impact | Joules (J) |
| 2 | $F \cdot \Delta t = m \cdot \Delta v$ | Impulse-momentum theorem | N·s = kg·m/s |
| 3 | $F(x) = F_0 + k x^n$ | Crush force-displacement law | kN, m |
| 4 | $E_{abs} = F_0 d + \frac{k d^{n+1}}{n+1}$ | Energy absorbed in crush zone | J |
| 5 | $\bar{a} = \frac{v_i^2}{2 s}$ | Average deceleration | m/s² |
| 6 | $\bar{F} = \frac{m v_i^2}{2 s}$ | Average force on occupant | N |
| 7 | $\text{HIC} = \left[\frac{1}{t_2-t_1} \int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\,dt\right]^{2.5} (t_2-t_1)$ | Head Injury Criterion | dimensionless, ≤ 1000 |
| 8 | $VC = \frac{v_{rib} \cdot C_{rib}}{0.25}$ | Viscous Criterion (chest) | m/s, ≤ 1.0 |
| 9 | $TTC = \frac{d_{rel}}{v_{rel}}$ | Time to collision | seconds (s) |
| 10 | $a_{req} = \frac{v_{rel}^2}{2 d_{rel}}$ | Required braking deceleration | m/s² |
| 11 | $v_{occ} = v_p \sqrt{\frac{m_v}{m_v + m_{door}}}$ | Occupant contact velocity | m/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** These equations drive the **CAE (Computer-Aided Engineering) workflow** — explicit FE codes such as **LS-DYNA** and **RADIOSS** integrate Equations (3) and (4) over each shell element at every time-step, while Equation (7) is post-processed from dummy accelerometer data (channels HEAD_X, HEAD_Y, HEAD_Z) to compute injury risk before the physical prototype is even built. The Euro NCAP star rating of a vehicle is essentially a function of whether these formulas evaluate below their injury thresholds.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Energy Absorbed by a Linear Crush Member

### Statement
A 1.5 m long front rail has a linear crush characteristic given by $F(x) = 50 + 400x$ (kN), where *x* is in meters. Find the total energy absorbed when the rail crushes by **0.30 m**. Take $F_0 = 50$ kN, $k = 400$ kN/m, $n = 1$.

### Working

**Step 1 — Identify the functional form.**
The given force-displacement law is linear (n = 1):

$$F(x) = F_0 + k \cdot x = 50 + 400x \quad [\text{kN}]$$

**Step 2 — Express energy as work done (integral of force over displacement).**

$$E_{abs} = \int_0^d F(x) \, dx = \int_0^{0.30} (50 + 400x) \, dx$$

**Step 3 — Perform the integration term by term.**

$$\int_0^{0.30} 50 \, dx = 50 \cdot x \Big|_0^{0.30} = 50 \cdot 0.30 = 15.0 \quad [\text{kN·m}]$$

$$\int_0^{0.30} 400x \, dx = 400 \cdot \frac{x^2}{2} \Big|_0^{0.30} = 200 \cdot (0.30)^2 = 200 \cdot 0.09 = 18.0 \quad [\text{kN·m}]$$

**Step 4 — Add contributions.**

$$E_{abs} = 15.0 + 18.0 = 33.0 \quad [\text{kN·m}] = 33{,}000 \quad [\text{J}] = 33 \quad [\text{kJ}]$$

### Verification (using $F_0 d + k d^2 / 2$ form)

$$E_{abs} = F_0 d + \frac{k d^2}{2} = 50(0.30) + \frac{400 (0.30)^2}{2} = 15 + 18 = 33 \text{ kJ} \;\checkmark$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: HIC Calculation from a Triangular Pulse

### Statement
A dummy head accelerometer records a triangular acceleration pulse during a pole test: it rises linearly from 0 g at t = 0 to 60 g at t = 10 ms, then falls linearly to 0 g at t = 20 ms. Compute the **HIC15** (15 ms window centered to maximize the value).

### Working

**Step 1 — Express a(t) in two intervals.**

For $0 \leq t \leq 10$ ms:

$$a(t) = \frac{60}{10} t = 6t \quad [\text{g, with t in ms}]$$

For $10 \leq t \leq 20$ ms:

$$a(t) = 60 - 6(t - 10) = 120 - 6t \quad [\text{g}]$$

**Step 2 — Compute average acceleration over the 15 ms window.**
Choose the window that maximizes HIC — by symmetry of the triangular pulse, the maximum-average window is roughly centered on the peak. Take $[t_1, t_2] = [2.5, 17.5]$ ms (a 15 ms window centered at 10 ms).

**Step 3 — Integrate a(t) over the window.**

$$\int_{2.5}^{10} 6t \, dt = 3t^2 \Big|_{2.5}^{10} = 3(100) - 3(6.25) = 300 - 18.75 = 281.25$$

$$\int_{10}^{17.5} (120 - 6t) \, dt = 120t - 3t^2 \Big|_{10}^{17.5} = (2100 - 918.75) - (1200 - 300) = 1181.25 - 900 = 281.25$$

Total integral:

$$I = 281.25 + 281.25 = 562.5 \quad [\text{g·ms}]$$

**Step 4 — Compute average acceleration.**

$$\bar{a} = \frac{I}{t_2 - t_1} = \frac{562.5}{15} = 37.5 \quad [\text{g}]$$

**Step 5 — Compute HIC15.**

$$\text{HIC}_{15} = (\bar{a})^{2.5} \cdot (t_2 - t_1)$$

$$(37.5)^{2.5} = 37.5^2 \cdot \sqrt{37.5} = 1406.25 \cdot 6.1240 \approx 8611.9$$

$$\text{HIC}_{15} = 8611.9 \cdot 15 \text{ ms} = 129{,}178$$

> [!WARNING]
> Wait — HIC units are *implicit*. The HIC formula uses acceleration in **g** and time in **seconds**. Re-doing step 5 with time in **seconds**:

$$(t_2 - t_1) = 0.015 \text{ s}$$

$$\text{HIC}_{15} = 8611.9 \cdot 0.015 = 129.2$$

**Corrected HIC15 ≈ 129** (well below the 1000 threshold — survivable head impact).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Crash Pulse Duration and Survivable Acceleration

### Statement
A 1500 kg vehicle impacts a rigid wall at 50 km/h. The average occupant compartment deceleration must be limited to **20 g**. Determine the required crush distance.

### Working

**Step 1 — Convert velocity to m/s.**

$$v_i = 50 \times \frac{1000}{3600} = 13.89 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Use the average deceleration relation.**

$$s = \frac{v_i^2}{2 \bar{a}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $\bar{a} = 20 \times 9.81 = 196.2$ m/s².**

$$s = \frac{(13.89)^2}{2 \times 196.2} = \frac{192.9}{392.4} = 0.492 \text{ m}$$

### Interpretation

A crush stroke of **~492 mm** is required to keep the cabin deceleration within the 20 g survivability envelope. This is why modern vehicles allocate **450–600 mm** of front crumple length.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Time-to-Collision and Required AEB Braking

### Statement
A host vehicle traveling at 60 km/h approaches a stationary target at a relative distance of 35 m. The AEB system can deliver a maximum deceleration of 0.6 g. Will the system avoid collision?

### Working

**Step 1 — Convert velocity.**

$$v_{host} = 60 \times \frac{1000}{3600} = 16.67 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute TTC.**

$$TTC = \frac{d_{rel}}{v_{rel}} = \frac{35}{16.67} = 2.10 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute required deceleration.**

$$a_{req} = \frac{v_{rel}^2}{2 d_{rel}} = \frac{(16.67)^2}{2 \times 35} = \frac{277.9}{70} = 3.97 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 4 — Compare with available deceleration.**

$$a_{AEB} = 0.6 \times 9.81 = 5.89 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 5 — Decision.**

Since $a_{AEB} = 5.89$ m/s² $> a_{req} = 3.97$ m/s², the AEB **avoids** the collision. ✓

**Stopping distance under AEB:**

$$s_{stop} = \frac{v_{host}^2}{2 a_{AEB}} = \frac{277.9}{11.78} = 23.6 \text{ m} < 35 \text{ m} \;\checkmark$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix — Crashworthiness vs. Pole vs. Near-Crash

| Parameter | Crashworthiness (Frontal) | Pole Test (Side) | Near-Crash (AEB) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Direction of force** | Longitudinal (x) | Lateral (y), often oblique | Longitudinal (x) |
| **Test mass considered** | Full vehicle (m_v) | Vehicle + dummy (m_v + m_d) | Subject (host) vehicle |
| **Primary energy sink** | Front rail, sub-frame | Door, B-pillar, roof rail | Brake system thermal capacity |
| **Dominant injury metric** | HIC, Chest G, Femur force | HIC, Viscous Criterion, Pelvis | TTC, $a_{req}$ vs $a_{AEB}$ |
| **Crash severity metric** | Crush depth (s) | Intrusion velocity | Required vs. available deceleration |
| **Standard velocity** | 64.4 km/h (40 mph) | 32 km/h | Variable (12–80 km/h) |
| **Test configuration** | Full-frontal or ODB | Moving deformable barrier or pole | Moving target / balloon car |
| **Primary regulatory body** | FMVSS 208, Euro NCAP | FMVSS 214E, ECE R95 | Euro NCAP AEB, IIHS |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Crash Test Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vehicle Crash Test]:::root --> B[Passive Safety Tests]
    A --> C[Active Safety Tests]
    A --> D[Hybrid Pre-Crash Tests]

    B --> B1[Frontal Impact - Full Width]
    B --> B2[Frontal Impact - Offset 40%]
    B --> B3[Side Impact - MDB]
    B --> B4[Pole Side Impact]
    B --> B5[Rear Impact]
    B --> B6[Roof Crush Test]

    C --> C1[AEB - City]
    C --> C2[AEB - Inter Urban]
    C --> C3[AEB - Pedestrian]
    C --> C4[Lane Support Systems]
    C --> C5[Speed Assist Systems]

    D --> D1[Near-Crash Scenarios]
    D --> D2[Pre-Crash Restraint Activation]

    B4 --> B4A[Pole Diameter 254 mm]
    B4 --> B4B[Impact Speed 32 km/h]
    B4 --> B4C[Oblique Loading 75 deg]

    D1 --> D2A[TTC Threshold 1.4 s]
    D1 --> D2B[Activation Timing 250 ms]
    D1 --> D2C[Reversible Pretensioner]

    classDef root fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0a1a30,stroke-width:2px
    classDef passive fill:#2a9d8f,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1a6f63
    classDef active fill:#e76f51,color:#ffffff,stroke:#a8442d
    classDef hybrid fill:#f4a261,color:#000000,stroke:#b87530
```

## 4.2 Pole Crash Test — Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Vehicle_Under_Test [Vehicle Under Test - VUT]
        VUT1[Powertrain Mount]
        VUT2[Front Rail Crumple Zone]
        VUT3[B Pillar Reinforcement]
        VUT4[Side Door Intrusion Beam]
        VUT5[Roof Rail]
        VUT6[Floor Pan Cross Member]
    end

    subgraph Pole_Interface [Rigid Pole 254 mm]
        POL1[Pole Material - Steel 1010]
        POL2[Vertical Cylinder]
    end

    subgraph Occupant_Compartment [Occupant Compartment]
        OC1[Side Airbag - Thorax]
        OC2[Curtain Airbag - Head]
        OC3[Door Trim Pad]
        OC4[Hybrid III Dummy 50th]
        OC5[H Point Sensor]
    end

    subgraph Data_Acquisition [Data Acquisition System]
        DAQ1[Head Accelerometer XYZ]
        DAQ2[Chest Deflection Pot]
        DAQ3[Femur Load Cell]
        DAQ4[Pelvis Accelerometer]
        DAQ5[High Speed Camera 1000 fps]
    end

    POL2 --> VUT4
    VUT4 --> VUT3
    VUT3 --> VUT5
    POL2 --> OC3
    OC3 --> OC4
    OC1 --> OC4
    OC2 --> OC4
    OC4 --> OC5
    OC4 --> DAQ1
    OC4 --> DAQ2
    OC4 --> DAQ3
    OC4 --> DAQ4
    VUT4 --> DAQ5
```

## 4.3 Near-Crash Test — Functional Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Radar as Radar / Lidar Sensor
    participant ECU as ADAS Control Unit
    participant Brake as Brake Actuator
    participant Belt as Pretensioner
    participant Airbag as Airbag Controller
    participant Dummy as Test Subject
    participant Log as Data Logger

    Radar->>ECU: Relative distance 35 m
    Radar->>ECU: Relative velocity 16.67 m/s
    ECU->>ECU: Compute TTC = 2.10 s
    ECU->>Log: Log TTC and time stamp
    ECU->>Brake: Pre-fill brake request (TTC = 2.0 s)
    ECU->>Dummy: Audio-visual warning (TTC = 1.8 s)
    ECU->>Belt: Reversible pretension (TTC = 1.4 s)
    ECU->>Brake: Full AEB 0.6 g (TTC = 1.0 s)
    ECU->>Airbag: Pre-arm squib (TTC = 0.3 s)
    Brake->>Dummy: Decelerate vehicle
    Brake->>Log: Store deceleration profile
    Log->>Log: Compute stopping distance
    Log-->>ECU: Collision avoided flag TRUE
```

## 4.4 Energy Management Hierarchy During Crash

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K[Kinetic Energy at Impact]:::energy -->|50 percent| A1[Front Crumple Zone]
    K -->|20 percent| A2[Sub-frame Buckling]
    K -->|10 percent| A3[Powertrain Mounting]
    K -->|15 percent| A4[Restraint Airbag]
    K -->|5 percent| A5[Interior Trim]

    A1 --> A1a[Progressive Collapse - 400 mm]
    A1 --> A1b[Intumescent Bulkhead]

    A4 --> A4a[Airbag Venting 80 percent]
    A4 --> A4b[Belt Pretensioner - 4 kN]

    A1a -->|Convert to plastic work| H[Heat and Sound 95 percent]
    A1b --> H
    A2 --> H
    A3 --> H
    A4a --> H
    A4b --> H
    A5 --> H

    H --> OC[Occupant Safe - HIC less than 1000]

    classDef energy fill:#264653,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0d1f24,stroke-width:2px
    classDef oc fill:#06d6a0,color:#000000,stroke:#04a87f
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — Define crashworthiness and list its three load paths. (3 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
Crashworthiness is the inherent capability of a vehicle's structure and restraint systems to attenuate crash-induced kinetic energy, preserve occupant survival space, and limit transmitted biomechanical loads.

The three load paths are:
1. **Primary** — structural energy absorption via plastic deformation of crumple zones.
2. **Secondary** — restraint system interaction (seatbelt, airbag, knee bolster).
3. **Tertiary** — post-impact ejection control and interior subsystem padding (H-point, headliner).

---

### Question 2 — What is a pole crash test? State its standard impact speed and pole diameter. (3 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
A pole crash test is a side-impact test in which a vehicle (or moving deformable barrier) strikes a vertical rigid cylindrical pole, typically 254 mm in diameter. The standard impact speed per Euro NCAP / FMVSS 214E is **32 km/h**, with the pole oriented to strike the vehicle at the most demanding point (often 75° to the vehicle longitudinal axis, near the A-pillar or roof rail). It evaluates head, thorax, abdomen, and pelvis protection in oblique side collisions.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided)

> **KTU 2024 Internal Choice Rule:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**.

---

### Question A — Crashworthiness Analysis (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A(a)** Define crashworthiness. With a neat sketch, explain the function of a **crumple zone** and a **survival cell** in a passenger car. (7 Marks)

**A(b)** A 1200 kg vehicle traveling at 54 km/h strikes a rigid barrier. The front rail exhibits a linear crush characteristic $F(x) = 40 + 350x$ kN. If the maximum permitted cabin deceleration is **25 g**, determine the **minimum crush distance** required to limit occupant loading. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution — A(a)

Crashworthiness is the ability of the vehicle to protect occupants through controlled energy management. The **crumple zone** is the engineered front (or rear) section designed to deform plastically in a controlled manner, absorbing kinetic energy through progressive buckling of beams. The **survival cell** (or safety cell) is the reinforced passenger compartment that must remain intact during the crash event.

A schematic:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Impact Force] --> B[Front Crumple Zone 400 to 600 mm]
    B --> C[Engine Sub-frame Folding]
    C --> D[Survival Cell - Reinforced]
    D --> E[Occupant]
    D --> F[Side Impact Beams]
    D --> G[Reinforced A B C Pillars]
    D --> H[Sill Reinforcement]
```

The crumple zone is built from **press-hardened boron steel** and **aluminum extrusions** that buckle in a calibrated sequence. The survival cell uses **hot-formed ultra-high-strength steel (UHSS, 1500 MPa)** to maintain cabin integrity.

#### Model Solution — A(b)

**Step 1 — Convert impact velocity.**

$$v_i = 54 \times \frac{1000}{3600} = 15.0 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute initial kinetic energy.**

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2} m v_i^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 1200 \times (15.0)^2 = 135{,}000 \text{ J} = 135 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 3 — Convert the deceleration limit.**

$$\bar{a}_{max} = 25 \times 9.81 = 245.25 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 4 — Compute the minimum crush distance using the kinematic relation.**

$$s_{min} = \frac{v_i^2}{2 \bar{a}_{max}} = \frac{(15.0)^2}{2 \times 245.25} = \frac{225}{490.5} = 0.459 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Verification using energy method.**

Energy absorbed by the rail (with $F_0 = 40$ kN, $k = 350$ kN/m):

$$E_{abs}(d) = 40 d + \frac{350 d^2}{2} = 40d + 175 d^2 \quad [\text{kJ, with d in m}]$$

Set $E_{abs} = 135$ kJ and solve:

$$175 d^2 + 40 d - 135 = 0$$

Using the quadratic formula:

$$d = \frac{-40 + \sqrt{40^2 + 4(175)(135)}}{2(175)} = \frac{-40 + \sqrt{1600 + 94500}}{350} = \frac{-40 + \sqrt{96100}}{350} = \frac{-40 + 310}{350} = 0.771 \text{ m}$$

> **Critical discussion:** The kinematic and energy methods give **different results (0.459 m vs. 0.771 m)** because the energy method accounts for the *initial peak force* of 40 kN that must be overcome before progressive crushing begins. The **larger value (0.771 m) is the correct design target**, ensuring the rail can absorb the full kinetic energy without exceeding the 25 g envelope. The kinematic method underestimates the stroke because it assumes an idealized constant force.

---

### Question B — Pole Crash Test and Injury Criteria (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**B(a)** Explain the **Euro NCAP side impact pole test** procedure. List the dummies used and the injury metrics recorded. (7 Marks)

**B(b)** A head accelerometer records the following triangular pulse: 0 g at t = 0, peak of **80 g at t = 8 ms**, returning to 0 g at t = 16 ms. Compute the **HIC15**. Comment on the injury risk. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution — B(a)

**Euro NCAP Side Pole Test Procedure:**

1. **Pole specification:** Rigid vertical steel cylinder, **254 mm diameter**, with its longitudinal axis vertical.
2. **Test vehicle (VUT):** Stationary; propelled by a trolley at impact.
3. **Impact velocity:** **32 km/h (±0.5 km/h)**.
4. **Impact angle:** Pole strikes the driver's side, perpendicular to the vehicle longitudinal axis, at the most demanding lateral position (typically aligned with the dummy's head).
5. **Dummies installed:**
   - WorldSID 50th percentile (driver and rear passenger)
   - Q-Series child dummies (5th percentile in rear)
6. **Injury metrics recorded:**

| Channel | Location | Metric | Threshold |
|---|---|---|---|
| HEAD_X, Y, Z | Head CG | HIC15 | ≤ 1000 |
| RIB_1 to RIB_6 | Thorax | Deflection | ≤ 42 mm |
| PUB_F | Pubic symphysis | Force | ≤ 6 kN |
| ABD | Abdomen | Force | ≤ 2.5 kN (internal) |
| PELVIS_Y | Pelvis | Iliac load | ≤ 5.95 kN |
| FEMUR_L, R | Femur | Axial force | ≤ 10 kN |

#### Model Solution — B(b)

**Step 1 — Express a(t).**

For $0 \leq t \leq 8$ ms:

$$a(t) = \frac{80}{8} t = 10t \quad [\text{g}]$$

For $8 \leq t \leq 16$ ms:

$$a(t) = 160 - 10t \quad [\text{g}]$$

**Step 2 — Choose the optimal 15 ms window.**

For a 15 ms window on a 16 ms pulse, the window must fit inside the pulse. Choose $[t_1, t_2] = [0.5, 15.5]$ ms (centered as best as possible).

**Step 3 — Integrate over the window.**

$$\int_{0.5}^{8} 10t \, dt = 5t^2 \Big|_{0.5}^{8} = 5(64) - 5(0.25) = 320 - 1.25 = 318.75$$

$$\int_{8}^{15.5} (160 - 10t) \, dt = 160t - 5t^2 \Big|_{8}^{15.5} = (2480 - 1201.25) - (1280 - 320) = 1278.75 - 960 = 318.75$$

Total integral:

$$I = 318.75 + 318.75 = 637.5 \quad [\text{g·ms}]$$

**Step 4 — Compute average acceleration.**

$$\bar{a} = \frac{637.5}{15} = 42.5 \text{ g}$$

**Step 5 — Compute HIC15 with time in seconds.**

$$(t_2 - t_1) = 0.015 \text{ s}$$

$$(42.5)^{2.5} = 42.5^2 \cdot \sqrt{42.5} = 1806.25 \cdot 6.519 = 11{,}776$$

$$\text{HIC}_{15} = 11{,}776 \times 0.015 = 176.6$$

**Step 6 — Injury risk comment.**

Since $\text{HIC}_{15} = 176.6 \ll 1000$, the head injury risk is **low (AIS ≤ 1)** — survivable with no permanent brain damage. The accident victim would likely sustain only minor concussion symptoms.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit error in HIC:** HIC formula uses acceleration in **g** and time in **seconds** — many students erroneously keep time in ms, producing HIC values off by a factor of 1000. **Always convert $(t_2 - t_1)$ to seconds.**
> 2. **Pole diameter confusion:** Students often write 250 mm or 200 mm — the standard is **254 mm** (exactly 10 inches, the imperial derivation).
> 3. **TTC vs. stopping distance:** Near-crash AEB questions must distinguish between *time-to-collision* and *distance-to-stop* — examiners specifically check that you compute both.
> 4. **Energy vs. kinematic methods:** When a crush curve $F(x)$ is given, the **energy method (integration of F over d)** is *always* preferred over the kinematic method, because the kinematic method ignores the initial peak force $F_0$.
> 5. **Miscounting load paths:** Crashworthiness has *three* load paths (primary, secondary, tertiary), not two. Missing the tertiary path costs 1 mark.
> 6. **HIC15 vs. HIC36 confusion:** HIC15 is more conservative; the **final published HIC** is the **max of HIC15 and HIC36**, not their sum.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

- ✅ **Crashworthiness** is a *system property* — it is the integrated result of structural design, restraint system, and interior padding.
- ✅ The **impulse-momentum equation** $F \Delta t = m \Delta v$ is the foundational principle; longer $\Delta t$ = smaller peak $F$.
- ✅ The **average deceleration** $\bar{a} = v^2 / (2s)$ links vehicle speed to crush stroke — modern cars need **~450–600 mm** of front crumple to survive a 64 km/h barrier at ≤ 20 g.
- ✅ **HIC ≤ 1000** is the survivability threshold; HIC > 2400 is generally unsurvivable.
- ✅ The **Viscous Criterion (VC)** governs chest injury in side impacts — it is *velocity-dependent* and peaks at intermediate deflection rates.
- ✅ **Pole test** is the most demanding side impact — pole diameter **254 mm**, speed **32 km/h**, oblique loading at the A-pillar.
- ✅ **Near-crash testing** evaluates *active* systems (AEB, FCW) and uses **TTC** as the key trigger metric. Typical activation: warning at TTC = 2.5 s, full brake at TTC = 1.0 s.
- ✅ **Euro NCAP AEB** scores are based on (a) collision avoidance, (b) speed reduction, and (c) false-positive suppression.
- ✅ The **three load paths** (primary, secondary, tertiary) must be quoted as a triplet — examiners specifically test for all three.
- ✅ **Restraint system** must work in concert with structure: pretensioner fires at ~5 ms, airbag deploys at ~20–30 ms, airbag vents at ~80–100 ms to prevent rebound injury.
- ✅ **A_peak** is not the only design variable — *pulse shape* matters equally; a square pulse is more injurious than a half-sine of equal peak.
- ✅ **A-pillar / B-pillar / roof rail** are the structural members most critical in pole tests — they must resist intrusion into the head strike zone.
- ✅ **CAE tools** (LS-DYNA, PAM-CRASH) solve the full set of equations every time step; crashworthiness is now predominantly a *virtual* discipline before physical testing.
- ✅ **FMVSS 208** (US) and **ECE R94 / R95** (Europe) are the two key regulatory frameworks — know the *speed* and *barrier type* of each.
- ✅ The **Barrier Equivalent Velocity (BEV)** is the velocity of an equivalent rigid barrier impact that produces the same damage — useful for accident reconstruction.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vehicle-to-Vehicle Impact and Side Impact Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT724 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Vehicle-to-Vehicle Impact and Side Impact Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
**Vehicle-to-Vehicle (V2V) Impact** is a category of vehicle crashworthiness evaluation in which two moving vehicles, or a moving vehicle and a stationary test fixture, undergo a controlled collision to quantify structural energy absorption, occupant compartment integrity, and restraint-system effectiveness. It is the cornerstone of **Real-World Crash Compatibility** assessment, distinguishing the laboratory measurement of *self-protection* from *partner-protection*.

**Side Impact Testing** is a specialized vehicle crashworthiness procedure where the lateral (perpendicular) surface of a stationary or moving test vehicle is struck either by a **Moving Deformable Barrier (MDB)** or by a rigid pole, simulating T-bone (90°), oblique-side, or pole-strike impacts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** Module 2 demands a strong comparative understanding of *frontal offset* and *side MDB* tests, the associated **biofidelic dummies**, **injury criteria (HIC, NIC, ThCC, V\*C)**, and **regulatory frameworks (Euro NCAP, IIHS, AIS, FMVSS 208/214, ECE R94/R95)**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Two-Way Punch":**
> Imagine two people standing in a room who suddenly push each other. The *self-protection* problem asks: *"How badly is the person who pushed hurt?"* The *partner-protection* problem asks: *"How badly is the person being pushed hurt by my elbow, fist, or torso?"* Vehicle-to-vehicle impact testing reproduces this exact physics — the front of *Car A* is matched against the side (or front) of *Car B*. A tall, rigid SUV "punching" a small low-to-the-ground sedan is the classic incompatibility problem that V2V testing is designed to expose.

### 1.3 Key Metrics and Standardized Parameters
- **Test mass of MDB:** **1400 kg** (Euro NCAP / ECE R95).
- **MDB striking velocity:** **50 km/h** (Euro NCAP), **62 km/h** (IIHS).
- **Barrier overlap in offset frontal:** **40% of vehicle width** (driver-side overlap, ODB).
- **Pole impact velocity:** **32 km/h** at **75°** angle (Euro NCAP).
- **HIC15 limit:** **≤ 700** for an adult occupant (Euro NCAP, FMVSS 208).
- **HIC36 limit:** **≤ 1000** (FMVSS 208 — long-duration head injury metric).
- **Chest deflection limit:** **≤ 50 mm** for the 50th-percentile Hybrid III dummy (Euro NCAP).
- **Femur compressive load limit:** **≤ 10 kN**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Two Test Families? — The Physics of a Crash
A real-world collision exchanges **momentum** and **kinetic energy**. The two principal test families exist because the engineering and human-tolerance responses are *fundamentally different* in these two loading directions:

| Loading Axis | Energy Density | Restraint System | Key Vulnerable Body Region |
|---|---|---|---|
| Frontal V2V | High (large crumple zone, hood, bumper) | Multi-stage airbags, pretensioners, knee bolster | Lower extremities, chest, head |
| Side V2V | Very High (only the door between occupant and intruding mass) | Side airbag, curtain airbag, door-beam | Thorax, pelvis, head (lateral), cervical spine |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why side impact is more severe per kJ:** In a frontal crash, the engine bay provides up to **800–1000 mm** of crush space. In a side impact, the door intrudes directly into the occupant with a crush stroke of typically only **200–300 mm**, and there is **no airbag pre-impact cushioning** from the fascia.

### 2.2 Vehicle-to-Vehicle (V2V) Impact — Operational Steps
1. **Compatibility Matching:** Vehicles are selected across mass and stiffness classes (light–light, light–heavy, heavy–heavy) to evaluate *geometric and stiffness compatibility*.
2. **Barrier Selection:** Either full-width rigid barrier (FWB), 40% offset deformable barrier (ODB), or a real *moving vehicle* (full-V2V test).
3. **Propulsion:** The test vehicle (or barrier) is accelerated to a calibrated impact velocity, typically **56–64 km/h**.
4. **Synchronized Data Acquisition:** High-speed cameras (≥ 1000 fps), accelerometers on the B-pillar, sills, and dummy head, chest, and pelvis.
5. **Post-Test Analysis:** Crush profile of the front structure, intrusion into the occupant cell, dummy injury values.

### 2.3 Side Impact Testing — Operational Steps
1. **Dummies Installed:** A **WorldSID 50th** in the struck-side front seat and a **SID-IIs 5th-percentile female** in the struck-side rear seat.
2. **MDB Alignment:** MDB strikes the **driver-side** at 90° (or oblique 75° in pole test) with the **barrier face centred on the vehicle's R-point**.
3. **Impact Velocity:** **50 km/h** (Euro NCAP) or **62 km/h** (IIHS side moderate overlap).
4. **Data Acquisition:** Triaxial accelerometers measure HIC, Chest Deflection (ThCC), Pelvis Acceleration, Abdominal Peak Force.
5. **Injury Score Computation:** Per Euro NCAP's whole-body injury assessment.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation / Rule | Units / Limit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Head Injury Criterion (HIC) | $HIC = \left[\frac{1}{t_2 - t_1}\int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\,dt\right]^{2.5}(t_2 - t_1)$ | HIC15 ≤ 700; HIC36 ≤ 1000 |
| 2 | 3-ms Head Cumulative (HPC) | $HPC = \left[\frac{1}{t_2 - t_1}\int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\,dt\right]^{2.5}(t_2 - t_1)$ | Same form, sliding 36 ms window |
| 3 | Neck Injury Criterion (NIC) | $NIC = 0.2\,a_{rel} + v_{rel}^2$ | $\leq 15 \,\text{m}^2/\text{s}^2$ |
| 4 | Chest Compression (ThCC) | $D = \frac{\text{Deflection}_{\text{max}}}{r_0}\times 100$ | $\leq 42\,\text{mm}$ (ES-2); $\leq 50\,\text{mm}$ (HIII) |
| 5 | Viscous Criterion (V\*C) | $V \cdot C = v(t)\cdot C(t) = v(t)\cdot \frac{D(t)}{r_0}$ | $\leq 1.0 \,\text{m/s}$ |
| 6 | Femur Load | $F_{femur}$ (direct) | $\leq 10 \,\text{kN}$ |
| 7 | Kinetic Energy | $KE = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$ | Joules (J) |
| 8 | Momentum | $p = m v$ | kg·m/s |
| 9 | Coefficient of Restitution | $e = \dfrac{v_2' - v_1'}{v_1 - v_2}$ | $0 \leq e \leq 1$ |
| 10 | Crush Energy (Janssen) | $E = \int_0^{\delta} F(\delta)\,d\delta \approx A \cdot \delta_{\text{avg}} \cdot L_{\text{eff}}$ | $A =$ mean force |
| 11 | ECE R94 Impact Velocity | $v_{test} = 56 - \frac{2}{3}\cdot m_{dolly}$ (correction factor) | km/h |
| 12 | Euro NCAP Side MDB Mass | $m_{MDB} = 1400 \,\text{kg}$, $v = 50 \,\text{km/h}$ |  |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for HIC:** "**H**ead **I**njury **C**riterion — area under the acceleration curve, raised to the 2.5 power, multiplied by the time window." The exponent **2.5** comes from the **Wayne State Tolerance Curve** (Lissner/Patrick) and is *non-negotiable* in KTU derivations.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Crash Compatibility Engineering** is the single most important design driver for V2V safety in modern SUVs. Engineers tune the *front-end stiffness* so a heavy vehicle does not "over-ride" a light one.
- **Side-Impact Beam** design has migrated from round tubes to **hot-formed, tailor-rolled, B-pillar-reinforced extrusions** to keep thoracic deflection within Euro NCAP limits.
- The **HIC formula** is the basis of the *Abbreviated Injury Scale (AIS)* mapping for head injuries and is used in the *AIS ≥ 3* injury-risk curves worldwide.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Head Injury Criterion (HIC)

The HIC is derived from the **Wayne State Concussion Tolerance Curve**, which is an empirically fitted relationship between the **head's translational acceleration** $a(t)$ and the **time** for which it is sustained, before concussive brain injury occurs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 1 — Define the Effective Average Acceleration**
> Given a head acceleration time-history $a(t)$ (in $g$ or m/s²), select a sliding time window $[t_1, t_2]$ (the most common being 15 ms or 36 ms). The effective average acceleration over that window is:

$$
\bar{a} = \frac{1}{t_2 - t_1}\int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\,dt
$$

> **Plain Explanation:** This step computes the *average* head acceleration over a small slice of the crash, smoothing out spikes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 2 — Apply the Power-Law (Lissner) Relationship**
> The Wayne State Curve experimentally established that the brain injury threshold follows a **power law** of index 2.5. So the HIC is proportional to the effective acceleration raised to the 2.5 power, multiplied by the duration of the pulse:

$$
HIC = \left[\bar{a}\right]^{2.5} (t_2 - t_1)
$$

> **Plain Explanation:** If the average acceleration doubles, the injury risk grows by $2^{2.5} \approx 5.66$ times, illustrating why head accelerations are aggressively limited in vehicle design.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 3 — Combine the Two Expressions**
> Substituting $\bar{a}$ from Step 1 into Step 2 gives the *full HIC equation*:

$$
HIC = \left[\frac{1}{t_2 - t_1}\int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\,dt\right]^{2.5}(t_2 - t_1)
$$

> **Plain Explanation:** This is the equation KTU examiners expect. The **sliding window** is then optimized — the test engineer searches all valid $[t_1, t_2]$ pairs and reports the **maximum** value as $HIC_{15}$ or $HIC_{36}$.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (HIC Computation)

**Given:** A dummy's head acceleration time-history is approximately:

$$
a(t) = \begin{cases} 40 \cdot \sin(\pi t / 0.015) & 0 \leq t \leq 0.015 \,\text{s} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

Assume the entire pulse is captured in a 15 ms window. Compute $HIC_{15}$.

> **Step 1 — Compute the integral (area under the half-sine pulse):**

$$
\int_{0}^{0.015} a(t)\,dt = 40 \int_{0}^{0.015} \sin\!\left(\frac{\pi t}{0.015}\right) dt
$$

$$
= 40 \left[-\frac{0.015}{\pi}\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi t}{0.015}\right)\right]_{0}^{0.015}
$$

$$
= 40 \cdot \frac{0.015}{\pi} \cdot \left[-\cos(\pi) + \cos(0)\right] = 40 \cdot \frac{0.015}{\pi} \cdot 2
$$

$$
= \frac{1.2}{\pi} \approx 0.3820 \,\text{m/s}
$$

> **Step 2 — Compute the effective average acceleration over 15 ms:**

$$
\bar{a} = \frac{1}{0.015}\cdot 0.3820 = 25.47 \,\text{m/s}^2
$$

> **Step 3 — Apply the HIC formula:**

$$
HIC_{15} = (25.47)^{2.5} \cdot 0.015
$$

$$
25.47^{2} = 648.7
$$

$$
25.47^{0.5} = 5.047
$$

$$
25.47^{2.5} = 648.7 \cdot 5.047 \approx 3273.5
$$

$$
HIC_{15} = 3273.5 \cdot 0.015 = 49.10
$$

> **Step 4 — Interpret the result:**

$$
HIC_{15} = 49.1 \;\; \ll 700 \;\; \Longrightarrow \text{Pass (well below Euro NCAP limit)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trick Question Alert:** If the examiner asks "What is the AIS-equivalent risk at $HIC_{15}=700$?" — the answer is approximately **5% risk of severe (AIS 4) head injury** for an adult, and **~50% for a 6-year-old child**. The *head-injury tolerance* is **age-dependent**, which is why child dummies exist.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Crash Test Types at a Glance

| Test Type | Standard | Mass | Velocity | Direction | Primary Dummy | Primary Injury Metric |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Frontal Full-Width Rigid | FMVSS 208 | 100% vehicle | ~56 km/h | 0° | HIII 50th | HIC15, Chest Defl, Femur F |
| Frontal 40% Offset Deformable (ODB) | Euro NCAP / ECE R94 | 1400 kg dolly | 64 km/h | 0° | HIII 50th, HIII 5F, Q6, Q10 | HIC15, ThCC, NIC |
| Side MDB | Euro NCAP / FMVSS 214 / ECE R95 | 1400 kg | 50 km/h (Euro) / 62 km/h (IIHS) | 90° | WorldSID 50th, SID-IIs | HIC15, ThCC, Pelvis Acc, Pubic F |
| Side Pole | Euro NCAP | 1.2 t pole, rigid | 32 km/h | 75° | WorldSID 50th | ThCC, Abdominal F, HIC15 |
| Small Overlap Frontal | IIHS | 25% overlap rigid | 64 km/h | 0° | HIII 5F | HIC15, Foot/Ankle |
| Oblique / Small Overlap Side | Euro NCAP 2024 | 1500 kg trolley | 60 km/h | 75° | WorldSID 50th | Multi-axis chest loading |
| Vehicle-to-Vehicle (Car-to-Car) | RCAR / IIHS | 2 real cars | 110 km/h closing | 50% overlap | HIII 50th | Compatibility index |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — V2V and Side Impact Test Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vehicle Under Test] --> B[Crash Test Facility]
    B --> C{Test Type Selection}
    C -->|Frontal V2V / Offset| D[Acceleration Track + Tow Cable]
    C -->|Side MDB| E[Trolley Propulsion System]
    C -->|Side Pole| F[Winch + Rigid Pole Rig]

    D --> G[40 Percent ODB Honeycomb Face]
    E --> H[1400 kg MDB with Deformable Face]
    F --> I[28 cm Diameter Rigid Steel Pole]

    G --> J[Impact Event at 64 km per h]
    H --> K[Impact Event at 50 km per h]
    I --> L[Impact Event at 32 km per h at 75 deg]

    J --> M[High Speed Cameras and Accelerometers]
    K --> M
    L --> M

    M --> N[Onboard Dummy Data Acquisition]
    N --> O[Injury Criterion Calculation]
    O --> P[HIC and NIC and ThCC and Femur F]
    P --> Q[Euro NCAP Star Rating]
    P --> R[FMVSS Compliance Pass Fail]
    P --> S[ECE R94 R95 Compliance]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Side Impact Energy Dissipation Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Inbound MDB at 50 km per h] --> B[Side Door Outer Panel Crush]
    B --> C[Side Impact Beam Bending]
    C --> D[Inner Door Panel Reinforcement]
    D --> E[B Pillar Hot Stamped Section]
    E --> F[Roof Rail Load Path]
    F --> G[Vehicle Sill and Floor Pan]
    G --> H[Occupant Compartment]
    H --> I[Dummy Thorax Loading]
    I --> J[Side Airbag and Curtain Deployment]
    J --> K[ThCC and HIC and Pelvis Acceleration]
```

### 4.3 Functional Mapping — Crashworthiness Design Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Field Accident Data FARS GIDAS] --> B[Biomechanical Tolerance Limits]
    B --> C[Engineering Targets HIC less than 700]
    C --> D[CAE Simulation LS DYNA PAM CRASH]
    D --> E[Prototype Build]
    E --> F[Crash Test in Laboratory]
    F --> G{Dummy Values within Limits?}
    G -->|No| H[Design Iteration]
    H --> D
    G -->|Yes| I[Regulatory Certification]
    I --> J[Euro NCAP and IIHS Publication]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block represents a *physical subsystem* (test rig, body panel, restraint) or a *data flow* (CAE, data acquisition, injury criteria). Arrows indicate energy flow or information flow — distinguishing the two by the *arrow caption* (e.g., "Impact Event" = energy; "Injury Criterion Calculation" = information).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the Head Injury Criterion (HIC) and state its two standardised window durations used in automotive crash testing. What is the Euro NCAP limit for adult head protection?**

> **Model Answer:**
> The HIC is an empirical injury metric derived from the Wayne State Concussion Tolerance Curve that quantifies the severity of a head impact using the time-history of the translational acceleration $a(t)$ of the dummy's head centre of gravity.
>
> $$
> HIC = \left[\frac{1}{t_2 - t_1}\int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\,dt\right]^{2.5}(t_2 - t_1)
> $$
>
> The two standardised windows are **HIC15** (15 ms) and **HIC36** (36 ms). The Euro NCAP limit for adult head protection is **HIC15 ≤ 700** for the 50th-percentile Hybrid III dummy.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Why is a side impact more critical to occupant survival than a frontal impact at the same closing velocity?**

> **Model Answer:**
> A side impact is more critical because the *crush space* between the intruding barrier and the occupant is only the **door assembly thickness** (≈ 200 mm), compared with up to **1000 mm** of crumple zone in a frontal impact. Additionally:
>
> (a) There is **no engine bay** to absorb the kinetic energy; the door is the only deformable element.
> (b) The **thorax and pelvis** are directly loaded — they have a much lower tolerance than the lower extremities.
> (c) There is **no airbag pre-impact cushioning** on the inboard side of the door.
> (d) The **B-pillar and roof rail** are near the occupant's head, allowing direct lateral head contact.
> (e) Side airbags and curtain airbags must be deployed within **5–10 ms** of impact, demanding a *lateral* accelerometer-based trigger system.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:**
> When students write the HIC formula, they often **forget the power 2.5** or write $(t_2 - t_1)^2$ instead of $(t_2 - t_1)^1$. This alone causes a **3-mark loss**. Always derive the formula from the Wayne State Curve to avoid this error.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Moving Deformable Barrier (MDB) Side Impact Test** as per Euro NCAP. List the dummies used, their positioning, and the principal injury criteria measured. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A test vehicle is struck on the side by a **1400 kg MDB** at **50 km/h**. The dummy's thorax deflection time-history is tabulated below. Compute the maximum **ThCC (chest compression index)** and comment on the result against the Euro NCAP limit. **(7 Marks)**

| Time (ms) | 0 | 5 | 10 | 15 | 20 | 25 | 30 | 35 | 40 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Deflection (mm) | 0 | 8 | 19 | 28 | 35 | 38 | 32 | 22 | 10 |

---

> **Model Solution for (a):**
>
> **Block diagram (described):**
> 1. Test vehicle (VUT) is stationary on a level surface, with the engine running and a **WorldSID 50th** in the front struck-side seat, **SID-IIs 5th-percentile female** in the rear struck-side seat.
> 2. The **1400 kg MDB** is accelerated by a **propulsion system** (typically a linear hydraulic actuator or winch) to **50 km/h ± 0.5 km/h**.
> 3. The MDB strikes the driver-side door at **90°**, with the **barrier face centre aligned to the vehicle's R-point** (a point on the seat defined by the dummy's H-point and torso angle).
> 4. The MDB face is a **deformable honeycomb/foam** simulating the front structure of a striking vehicle.
> 5. **High-speed cameras** (≥ 1000 fps) record the impact.
> 6. **Onboard triaxial accelerometers** in the dummy's head, chest, and pelvis record loading.
> 7. **Post-test**, the injury criteria are computed and graded per the Euro NCAP protocol.
>
> **Injury Criteria Measured:**
>
> | Dummy | Region | Criterion | Limit |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | WorldSID 50th | Head | HIC15 | ≤ 700 |
> | WorldSID 50th | Thorax | ThCC (chest deflection) | ≤ 50 mm |
> | WorldSID 50th | Pelvis | Pubic symphysis force | ≤ 6 kN |
> | WorldSID 50th | Abdomen | Abdominal peak force | ≤ 2.5 kN (soft) |
> | SID-IIs 5F (rear) | Head | HIC15 | ≤ 700 |
> | SID-IIs 5F (rear) | Pelvis | Iliac wing load | ≤ 5 kN |
>
> **(7 Marks Awarded for: 2 M diagram + 2 M dummies + 2 M criteria + 1 M conclusion.)**

---

> **Model Solution for (b):**
>
> **Step 1 — Identify the maximum deflection from the table:**
>
> The peak deflection is **38 mm at t = 25 ms**.
>
> **Step 2 — Compute the ThCC (chest compression):**
>
> The ThCC is typically expressed as the *percentage compression* relative to the half-thorax depth $r_0$ (≈ 96 mm for HIII 50th, ≈ 96 mm for WorldSID):
>
> $$
> D = \frac{\delta_{\text{max}}}{r_0}\times 100 = \frac{38}{96}\times 100 = 39.6\%
> $$
>
> **Step 3 — Compare to Euro NCAP limit:**
>
> The Euro NCAP side-impact limit for **HIII 50th (and WorldSID) chest deflection is ≤ 50 mm** (≈ 52% compression).
>
> $$
> 38 \,\text{mm} \;\; < \;\; 50 \,\text{mm} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \text{Result: PASS (with margin of 12 mm).}
> $$
>
> **Step 4 — Compute the Viscous Criterion (V\*C) — bonus step:**
>
> The deflection rate at 25 ms is approximately:
>
> $$
> \dot{\delta}(25\,\text{ms}) = \frac{\delta(30) - \delta(20)}{10\,\text{ms}} = \frac{32 - 35}{0.010} = -300 \,\text{mm/s} = -0.3\,\text{m/s}
> $$
>
> $$
> C(25\,\text{ms}) = \frac{38}{96} = 0.396
> $$
>
> $$
> V\cdot C = 0.3 \cdot 0.396 \approx 0.119 \,\text{m/s}
> $$
>
> This is well below the V\*C limit of **1.0 m/s**, indicating acceptable viscous tolerance.
>
> **(7 Marks Awarded for: 2 M max deflection + 2 M ThCC formula + 2 M comparison + 1 M conclusion.)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Vehicle Crash Compatibility**. Discuss the *self-protection* and *partner-protection* design strategies with a neat block diagram. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Two cars — Car A (mass $m_A = 1500$ kg) and Car B (mass $m_B = 1000$ kg) — are involved in a **head-on full-width rigid barrier-equivalent test** at closing velocities of 50 km/h each, approaching each other. The coefficient of restitution is $e = 0$. Compute the **common post-impact velocity** of the combined mass and the **total energy dissipated** in the crash. **(7 Marks)**

---

> **Model Solution for (a):**
>
> **Vehicle Crash Compatibility** is the property of two vehicles in a collision to *mutually protect their occupants*, balancing the *intrusion resistance* of one vehicle with the *energy-absorption capability* of the other. It was introduced by **NHTSA and EEVC** in the late 1990s and is now central to Euro NCAP and IIHS protocols.
>
> **Self-Protection:** Designing a vehicle's *own* structure to manage its own kinetic energy — via crumple zones, restraint systems, and an efficient load path. This is achieved through:
> 1. A **progressive front structure** (low-stiffness crush tip, high-stiffness mid-rails) that absorbs energy in a controlled manner.
> 2. A **rigid occupant cell** that resists intrusion.
> 3. **Restraint systems** (airbags, pretensioners, load-limiters) that manage the *secondary* impact.
>
> **Partner-Protection:** Designing a vehicle so it does not impose excessive loads on the *other* vehicle in the crash. This requires:
> 1. **Geometric compatibility** — matching bumper heights, override/underride prevention, and front-end profiles.
> 2. **Stiffness compatibility** — a heavy vehicle's front structure must not be *excessively* stiff, otherwise it "punches through" the lighter partner's door.
> 3. **Mass distribution** — control of the *effective striking mass* through the structural load path.
>
> **Block Diagram:**
>
> ```mermaid
> flowchart TD
>     A[Crash Compatibility] --> B[Self Protection]
>     A --> C[Partner Protection]
>     B --> B1[Progressive Front Crush]
>     B --> B2[Rigid Occupant Cell]
>     B --> B3[Restraint System]
>     C --> C1[Geometric Match]
>     C --> C2[Stiffness Match]
>     C --> C3[Effective Mass Match]
>     B1 --> D[Occupant Safety Outcome]
>     B2 --> D
>     B3 --> D
>     C1 --> D
>     C2 --> D
>     C3 --> D
> ```
>
> **(7 Marks Awarded for: 2 M definition + 2 M self-protection + 2 M partner-protection + 1 M diagram.)**

---

> **Model Solution for (b):**
>
> **Step 1 — Define initial velocities (sign convention: +ve to the right):**
>
> $$
> v_{A,i} = +50 \,\text{km/h} = +13.89 \,\text{m/s}
> $$
>
> $$
> v_{B,i} = -50 \,\text{km/h} = -13.89 \,\text{m/s}
> $$
>
> **Step 2 — Apply conservation of linear momentum (perfectly inelastic assumption with $e=0$ implies the post-impact velocities are equal):**
>
> $$
> m_A v_{A,i} + m_B v_{B,i} = (m_A + m_B) v_f
> $$
>
> $$
> 1500 \cdot 13.89 + 1000 \cdot (-13.89) = 2500 \cdot v_f
> $$
>
> $$
> 20833.3 - 13888.9 = 2500 v_f
> $$
>
> $$
> 6944.4 = 2500 v_f
> $$
>
> $$
> v_f = 2.778 \,\text{m/s} = 10 \,\text{km/h} \;\;(\text{to the right})
> $$
>
> **Step 3 — Compute total initial kinetic energy:**
>
> $$
> KE_i = \frac{1}{2} m_A v_{A,i}^2 + \frac{1}{2} m_B v_{B,i}^2
> $$
>
> $$
> = \frac{1}{2}(1500)(13.89)^2 + \frac{1}{2}(1000)(13.89)^2
> $$
>
> $$
> = \frac{1}{2}(2500)(13.89)^2 = 1250 \cdot 192.93 \approx 241{,}163 \,\text{J}
> $$
>
> **Step 4 — Compute final kinetic energy (combined mass moving at $v_f$):**
>
> $$
> KE_f = \frac{1}{2}(m_A + m_B)v_f^2 = \frac{1}{2}(2500)(2.778)^2
> $$
>
> $$
> = 1250 \cdot 7.716 = 9{,}645 \,\text{J}
> $$
>
> **Step 5 — Compute total energy dissipated:**
>
> $$
> \Delta E = KE_i - KE_f = 241{,}163 - 9{,}645 = 231{,}518 \,\text{J} \approx 231.5 \,\text{kJ}
> $$
>
> This 231.5 kJ must be absorbed by the crumple zones of both vehicles.
>
> **Step 6 — Engineering interpretation:**
>
> The lighter Car B experiences a *higher specific energy absorption* per kg of vehicle mass, which is why lighter cars are intrinsically at a disadvantage in V2V crashes — a key *partner-protection* design challenge.
>
> $$
> \frac{\Delta E}{m_A} = \frac{231{,}518}{1500} = 154.3 \,\text{kJ/tonne}
> $$
>
> $$
> \frac{\Delta E}{m_B} = \frac{231{,}518}{1000} = 231.5 \,\text{kJ/tonne} \;\;\text{(50% more severe per tonne)}
> $$
>
> **(7 Marks Awarded for: 2 M setup + 2 M momentum solution + 2 M energy balance + 1 M interpretation.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2:**
> Students frequently **sign-mistake** the relative velocity in V2V problems. Always adopt a **single sign convention** at the start and label the equation. The coefficient-of-restitution formula $e = (v_2' - v_1')/(v_1 - v_2)$ is the most-skipped step in KTU papers; for the special case $e=0$ (perfectly plastic), the post-impact velocities of the *separately-treated masses* are *equal*, but the *combined-mass* approach is what KTU expects for V2V test analysis.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **HIC** uses a **sliding time window** of 15 ms (HIC15) or 36 ms (HIC36); the **maximum** value is reported.
- ✅ The exponent in HIC is **2.5** (Wayne State Concussion Tolerance Curve).
- ✅ The Euro NCAP side MDB weighs **1400 kg** and strikes at **50 km/h**.
- ✅ The Euro NCAP pole test runs at **32 km/h** at **75°**.
- ✅ **WorldSID 50th** and **SID-IIs 5F** are the dummies for side MDB; **Hybrid III 50th** dominates frontal.
- ✅ **HIC15 ≤ 700**, **ThCC ≤ 50 mm**, **Femur F ≤ 10 kN**, **NIC ≤ 15 m²/s²** — these are the four numbers to memorize.
- ✅ **Crash compatibility** = self-protection + partner-protection.
- ✅ A **lower crush space** in the side (≈ 200 mm) vs frontal (≈ 1000 mm) is *the* reason side impacts are more lethal per kJ.
- ✅ **Viscous Criterion (V\*C)** captures *rate* of chest deflection, not just magnitude.
- ✅ For V2V momentum problems, the **combined-mass approach** with $e=0$ is the standard KTU simplification.
- ✅ Always state *units* in every KTU derivation — marks are deducted for missing units.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Crash Test Sensors and Sensor Mounting Positions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMUT724 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Crash Test Sensors and Sensor Mounting Positions # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Definition of Crash Test Sensors A **Crash Test Sensor** is a precision electromechanical transducer deployed within a vehicle, crash test dummy, or structural component to measure, record, and quantify the physical parameters experienced during a controlled or simulated vehicle collision event. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OEMUT724)** framework, these sensors convert dynamic physical quantities such as acceleration, force, pressure, displacement, and angular rate into proportional electrical signals that can be sampled, stored, and post-processed to evaluate occupant safety and structural integrity. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 2):** > Crash test sensors are categorized as the *primary data acquisition interface* of any crash test event. Without these sensors, no regulatory compliance (FMVSS, ECE R94, R95, Euro NCAP, Bharat NCAP) can be objectively established.
