|---|---|
| Braking distance $s_b$ | **69.23 m** |
| Total stopping distance $s_{total}$ | **123.23 m** |
| Brake efficiency $\eta$ | **82.82 %** (Complies) |
| Safe following gap $d_{min}$ | **54 m** |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Units, units, units!** Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s in $v$ loses 1–2 marks instantly. Show the conversion $v = V_{km/h} \times \dfrac{5}{18}$ explicitly on the answer sheet.
> 2. **Confusing stopping distance with braking distance** — a 3-mark question that tests this distinction is a classic trap. Always write both definitions before using the formula.
> 3. **Using $g$ instead of $\mu g$ in the efficiency denominator** — this will give an efficiency > 100 % and the examiner will mark it as *physically meaningless*. Always write $\eta = a / (\mu g) \times 100\%$.
> 4. **Forgetting reaction time** when the problem says "stopping distance" — the examiner will deduct a full mark for the missing $v t_r$ term.
> 5. **Type-I and Type-II confusion** — Type-I is the *fade* test, Type-II is the *hot performance* test. Mnemonic: **"I is Inside (fade), II is Immediately after"**.
> 6. **Skid-mark formula direction** — accident reconstruction uses $v = \sqrt{2 \mu g s}$ (forward), while normal braking problems use $s = v^{2}/(2a)$ (backward). Mixing these up is a top-3 reason for losing marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Braking Distance Test**

- **Braking distance** = distance from brake application to stop = $\dfrac{v^{2}}{2a}$.
- **Stopping distance** = reaction distance + braking distance = $v t_r + \dfrac{v^{2}}{2a}$.
- **Brake efficiency** = $\dfrac{a_{actual}}{\mu g} \times 100\ \%$ — *always* use $\mu g$ as the denominator.
- **Rotational inertia** of wheels does **not** change $s_b$ — the factor $\delta$ cancels in the energy balance.
- **Forensic speed from skid** = $v = \sqrt{2 \mu g s}$ (assumes locked-wheel sliding).
- **ECE R13 standard minimum deceleration** for M1 passenger cars = **5.4 m/s²** at pedal force ≤ 500 N.
- **Type-0** → Cold performance; **Type-I** → Fade; **Type-II** → Hot performance; **Type-III** → Recovery.
- **Type-I fade test** = 15 snubs from 80 km/h at 0.3 g, no cooling allowed between stops.
- **Brake fade** is caused by (i) pad friction drop with temperature, and (ii) pad outgassing at high T.
- **Brake torque** $T_b = \mu_p F_c r_{eff}$; **braking force at tyre** $F_b = T_b / r_{wheel}$.
- The **maximum braking force** is capped by tyre-road friction: $F_{b,max} = \mu m g$ — beyond this, wheels lock and ABS activates.
- Always state the **boundary condition** in derivations: $v_f = 0$ for a "full stop" problem.
- **Effective brake radius** $r_{eff} = (r_{inner} + r_{outer}) / 2$ — a frequent KTU 3-mark sub-question.
- Conversion factor to memorise: $1 \ \text{km/h} = \dfrac{5}{18} \ \text{m/s}$, so $90 \ \text{km/h} = 25 \ \text{m/s}$.
- The **kinetic energy** dissipated by the brake is $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$ — all of this becomes heat in the disc/drum, which is why brake cooling is a design priority in heavy vehicles and race cars.
- For highway design (IRC:64), $t_r = 2.5$ s; for KTU exam problems, $t_r$ is given explicitly (usually 1.0 – 2.0 s).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wind Tunnel Test Requirements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT724 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Wind Tunnel Test Requirements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wind Tunnel Test Requirements** for an automotive vehicle refer to the set of design, geometric, kinematic, dynamic, and aerodynamic constraints that must be satisfied by both the facility (the wind tunnel itself) and the test article (the full-scale or scale model vehicle) in order to obtain **dimensionally and physically valid aerodynamic data** that can be confidently extrapolated to real-world road operation of the prototype.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, these requirements are categorized as:

1. **Geometric Similarity** — shape, contour, surface finish, and detail fidelity between the model and the prototype.
2. **Kinematic Similarity** — streamline patterns around the model must geometrically resemble those around the prototype.
3. **Dynamic Similarity** — the dimensionless groups governing the flow (Reynolds number, Mach number, etc.) must match between model and prototype.
4. **Facility / Operational Requirements** — test section dimensions, blockage ratio, turbulence intensity, flow angularity, and measurement window.
5. **Instrumentation & Calibration Requirements** — force balance accuracy, pressure tap density, boundary layer transition fixing, etc.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Goldfish Bowl Test:**
> Imagine you design a fish (a car) that is supposed to swim fast in the open ocean (real road at 120 km/h). You cannot throw your fish into the ocean every time you tweak its fin. Instead, you build a **smaller aquarium (wind tunnel)** that pumps water (air) past the fish at a controllable speed.
>
> But here's the catch: a goldfish swimming in a kitchen bowl will *not* behave like a tuna in the Pacific, because the water in the bowl is **viscous-dominated** (small Re), while the ocean is **inertia-dominated** (large Re). So your aquarium must be **large enough** and your pump must be **fast enough** so that the dimensionless "swim regime" matches the real ocean.

This is exactly the problem in automotive wind tunnel testing. The engineer must guarantee that:

- The **shape** of the test article = shape of the prototype.
- The **flow regime** (turbulent/laminar separation, Reynolds number) of the test = the real road.
- The **walls, floor, and supports** of the tunnel do not artificially inflate or suppress the measured drag.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Automotive Value |
|-----------|--------|--------------------------|
| Standard Air Density | $\rho$ | **1.225 kg/m³** (ISA, sea level) |
| Kinematic Viscosity of Air | $\nu$ | **1.46 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s** (at 15 °C) |
| Dynamic Viscosity of Air | $\mu$ | **1.789 × 10⁻⁵ N·s/m²** |
| Speed of Sound at 15 °C | $a$ | **340.3 m/s** |
| Test Velocity Range | $V$ | **40 – 250 km/h** (typical auto WT) |
| Target Drag Coefficient (Modern Sedan) | $C_D$ | **0.22 – 0.30** |
| Target Lift Coefficient (Front Axle) | $C_L$ | **0.05 – 0.20** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Geometric Similarity Requirements

The model must be a **scaled, geometrically faithful replica** of the prototype. For scale factor $\lambda = L_m / L_p$:

- All linear dimensions must scale by $\lambda$.
- All angular dimensions (windscreen rake, boat-tail angle, diffuser angle) must be **identical**, not scaled.
- Surface roughness and gap/edge details (mirror mounts, antennae, door handles, wiper blade lift) must be reproduced to within ±5 % of characteristic length to avoid bluff-body separation mismatches.
- Wheel rotation must be simulated (5-wheel belt system or moving ground plane) because rotating wheels generate a significant portion of total drag (≈ 10–15 %).

## 2.2 Dynamic Similarity Requirements

The dimensionless groups that govern external aerodynamic flow over a road vehicle are:

| Group | Definition | Target Match |
|-------|------------|--------------|
| Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu}$ | Re_model = Re_prototype |
| Mach Number | $M = \dfrac{V}{a}$ | $M \le 0.3$ to keep flow incompressible |
| Froude Number (for moving ground) | $Fr = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{gL}}$ | Re and Fr matched |
| Boundary Layer Parameter | $L / \delta$ | Matched via trip dots / transition fixing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For full-scale vehicles at **120 km/h**, $Re \approx 3.4 \times 10^6$ based on vehicle length. To match this on a 1/4-scale model, the wind tunnel must run the model at **≈ 480 km/h** OR use pressurization / cryogenic facilities. This is why most production wind tunnels use a **5/8 or 3/4 scale** with a moving ground plane.

## 2.3 Facility (Wind Tunnel) Requirements

### 2.3.1 Blockage Ratio

The blockage ratio is the ratio of the frontal area of the model $A_m$ to the test-section cross-sectional area $A_t$:

$$\text{BLOCKAGE} = \dfrac{A_m}{A_t} \times 100\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU / SAE standard: **Blockage < 5 % for closed test sections** and **< 7.5 % for open test sections**. Above this, the wall constrains the streamlines and **artificially increases** the measured $C_D$. Corrections (Maskell, Bacon, etc.) are applied above 5 %.

### 2.3.2 Turbulence Intensity

$$Tu = \dfrac{u'_{rms}}{U_\infty} \times 100\%$$

Modern automotive wind tunnels require **Tu < 0.1 %** in the empty test section. Higher turbulence prematurely trips the boundary layer and changes the pressure recovery on the rear glass.

### 2.3.3 Flow Angularity (Swirl and Yaw)

- **Yaw angle variation:** $\Delta \psi < \pm 0.25°$ across the test volume.
- **Pitch (upwash/downwash) variation:** $\Delta \theta < \pm 0.25°$.
- This is critical for $C_L$ (lift) and side-force $C_S$ measurements.

### 2.3.4 Moving Ground Plane (Boundary Layer Simulation)

A real road has a moving surface that suppresses the boundary layer underneath the car. Without a moving ground plane, the underside boundary layer is artificially thick, giving **false (high) underbody drag**. KTU-aligned best practice is a **5-belt system** (one for each wheel contact patch) or a **single wide moving belt** at floor level.

### 2.3.5 Test Section Size

Typical production wind tunnel for full-scale vehicles:

| Specification | Value |
|---------------|-------|
| Nozzle exit diameter / width | 7 – 11 m |
| Test section length | 10 – 22 m |
| Maximum wind speed | 250 – 300 km/h |
| Maximum power | 4 – 5 MW (with cooling) |

## 2.4 Instrumentation Requirements

1. **Force Balance (6-component)** — measures $F_D, F_L, F_S, M_{Roll}, M_{Pitch}, M_{Yaw}$ simultaneously. Accuracy: **< ±0.1 % of full scale**.
2. **Pressure Measurement** — multi-hole probes (7-hole or 5-hole) for spatial pressure mapping.
3. **PIV / LDV / Hot-wire Anemometry** — for velocity field visualization.
4. **Boundary Layer Rakes** — for ground plane verification.
5. **Wheel Torque Meters** — to separately capture wheel-rotational drag.
6. **Temperature and Barometric Compensation** — to normalize $\rho$ to ISA conditions.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` instead of `|` in all tables below — this is mandatory to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Purpose |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Dynamic Pressure | $q = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2$ | Pa (N/m²) | Reference pressure in $C_p$ definition |
| 2 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \dfrac{V L}{\nu}$ | dimensionless | Flow regime similarity |
| 3 | Mach Number | $M = V/a$ | dimensionless | Compressibility check |
| 4 | Drag Force | $F_D = C_D \, q \, A_f = C_D \, \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A_f$ | N | Direct measurement |
| 5 | Lift Force | $F_L = C_L \, q \, A_f = C_L \, \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A_f$ | N | Front/rear axle balance |
| 6 | Side Force | $F_S = C_S \, q \, A_f$ | N | Cross-wind sensitivity |
| 7 | Drag Coefficient | $C_D = F_D / (q A_f)$ | dimensionless | Performance metric |
| 8 | Pressure Coefficient | $C_p = (p - p_\infty)/q$ | dimensionless | Surface pressure mapping |
| 9 | Blockage Ratio | $\beta = A_m / A_t$ | dimensionless | Wall confinement check |
| 10 | Boundary Layer Thickness | $\delta \approx 0.37 \, x \, Re_x^{-1/5}$ | m | Turbulent BL estimate |
| 11 | Turbulence Intensity | $Tu = u'_{rms}/U_\infty$ | dimensionless | Flow quality metric |
| 12 | Froude Number | $Fr = V/\sqrt{gL}$ | dimensionless | Moving ground similarity |
| 13 | Specific Air Consumption (tunnel) | $N_s = P/(q V A)$ | dimensionless | Wind tunnel power efficiency |
| 14 | Blockage Correction (Maskell) | $\Delta C_D = -K \beta^2$ | dimensionless | Wall correction for $C_D$ |
| 15 | Energy Equivalent Speed | $EES = V_\infty \sqrt{\rho/\rho_0}$ | m/s | Density-normalized velocity |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Vehicle homologation & certification:** $C_D$ directly impacts **fuel economy (NEDC/WLTP cycles)** and **CO₂ fleet emissions** (every 0.01 drop in $C_D$ ≈ 0.5 g/km CO₂ reduction).
- **EV range optimization:** For an EV, every **0.01 drop in $C_D$** adds ≈ **5 km of range** on the WLTP cycle.
- **High-speed stability:** $C_L$ on the front and rear axles controls **steering feel and lift-induced wander** at 200+ km/h.
- **Cross-wind sensitivity:** $C_{S'}$ (side-force derivative w.r.t. yaw) determines **transient gust response**.
- **Cooling airflow:** Heat-exchanger exit conditions in the engine bay are validated in the WT with simulated ram-air.
- **Soiling / Water Management:** WT with rain rig for wiper effectiveness certification.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Reynolds Number Scaling Law for Wind Tunnel Testing

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

A full-scale prototype sedan has:
- Length $L_p = 4.5$ m
- Test velocity $V_p = 120$ km/h $= 33.33$ m/s
- Air properties: $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $\mu = 1.789 \times 10^{-5}$ N·s/m².

A 1/4-scale model is to be tested. **At what velocity $V_m$ must the model be run to maintain Reynolds similarity?**

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Compute prototype Reynolds number:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Re_p &= \frac{\rho \, V_p \, L_p}{\mu} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{(1.225)(33.33)(4.5)}{1.789 \times 10^{-5}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{183.71}{1.789 \times 10^{-5}} \\[4pt]
Re_p &\approx 1.027 \times 10^{7}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — State the similarity requirement:**

For Reynolds similarity, the model Reynolds number must equal the prototype Reynolds number:
$$Re_m = Re_p$$

**Step 3 — Substitute model variables using scale factor $\lambda$:**

Model length is $L_m = \lambda \, L_p = (1/4)(4.5) = 1.125$ m. So:
$$Re_m = \frac{\rho \, V_m \, (\lambda \, L_p)}{\mu}$$

**Step 4 — Equate $Re_m$ and $Re_p$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\rho \, V_m \, \lambda \, L_p}{\mu} &= \frac{\rho \, V_p \, L_p}{\mu} \\[4pt]
V_m \, \lambda &= V_p \\[4pt]
V_m &= \frac{V_p}{\lambda} = \frac{33.33}{0.25} \\[4pt]
V_m &\approx 133.33 \; \text{m/s} = 480 \; \text{km/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering conclusion:**

A 1/4-scale model must be tested at **480 km/h** to maintain $Re$ similarity — which exceeds the speed of sound for many tunnels. Hence, KTU-recommended practice is to use a **larger scale (3/4 or 5/8)**, **pressurize** the test section, or use a **cryogenic wind tunnel** to increase $\rho$ and reduce $\nu$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Dynamic Pressure & Drag Coefficient

**Step 1 — From dimensional analysis on a moving body in a fluid:**

For a body immersed in a uniform free-stream of density $\rho$ and velocity $V_\infty$, the drag force $F_D$ must be expressible as:
$$F_D = f(\rho, V_\infty, L, \mu)$$

Applying Buckingham Pi theorem with three primary dimensions (M, L, T), we get **two** dimensionless groups:
$$\Pi_1 = \frac{F_D}{\rho V_\infty^2 L^2}, \quad \Pi_2 = \frac{\rho V_\infty L}{\mu} = Re$$

**Step 2 — Functional relationship:**

$$\frac{F_D}{\rho V_\infty^2 L^2} = \phi(Re)$$

**Step 3 — Define the drag coefficient:**

Since $A_f \propto L^2$, define:
$$C_D = \frac{F_D}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2 A_f} = \frac{2 \Pi_1}{A_f/L^2}$$

So:
$$C_D = \phi(Re)$$

**Step 4 — Final form (the equation you use in the lab):**

$$
\boxed{\,F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\, V_\infty^{2}\, A_f\, C_D\,}
$$

where $A_f$ is the **frontal projected area** of the vehicle (NOT the planform area).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Blockage Correction (Maskell — Simplified)

When the model occupies too large a fraction of the test section, the streamlines are compressed, the local velocity around the model increases, and the measured drag is **artificially high**.

**Step 1 — Define the blockage ratio:**

$$\beta = \frac{A_m}{A_t}$$

**Step 2 — Maskell's first-order correction:**

$$\Delta C_D = -K \, \beta^{2}$$

where $K \approx 2.0$ for a streamlined automotive body and $K \approx 1.6$ for bluff bodies.

**Step 3 — Corrected drag coefficient:**

$$
C_{D,\text{corrected}} = C_{D,\text{measured}} - K \, \beta^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Worked example:**

Suppose $A_m = 2.1$ m², $A_t = 30$ m², measured $C_D = 0.305$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta &= \frac{2.1}{30} = 0.07 \;\; (\text{i.e., } 7\%) \\[4pt]
\Delta C_D &= -2.0 \times (0.07)^2 = -2.0 \times 0.0049 \\[4pt]
\Delta C_D &= -0.0098 \\[4pt]
C_{D,\text{corrected}} &= 0.305 - 0.0098 = 0.2952
\end{aligned}
$$

So the wall confinement was inflating the drag coefficient by about **3.2 %** — a non-trivial correction that directly affects the vehicle's declared fuel economy.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Energy Equivalent Speed (EES) for Density Normalization

Wind tunnel air density varies with temperature, pressure, and humidity. To compare runs done on different days, KTU/SAE mandates **Energy Equivalent Speed** normalization:

**Step 1 — Equate dynamic pressure at the two conditions:**

$$\tfrac{1}{2}\rho_1 V_1^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_0 V_{EES}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for EES:**

$$
\boxed{\,V_{EES} = V_1 \, \sqrt{\rho_1 / \rho_0}\,}
$$

where $\rho_0 = 1.225$ kg/m³ (ISA standard).

**Step 3 — Worked example:**

Measured velocity $V_1 = 140$ km/h at $\rho_1 = 1.180$ kg/m³ (hot summer day).

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{EES} &= 140 \, \sqrt{\frac{1.180}{1.225}} \\[4pt]
&= 140 \, \sqrt{0.9633} \\[4pt]
&= 140 \times 0.9815 \\[4pt]
V_{EES} &\approx 137.4 \text{ km/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

Reporting $V_{EES}$ in the test report makes the data directly comparable to ISO-standard runs.

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define blockage ratio. State its permissible limit for a closed test section wind tunnel used for automotive testing and explain why it must be controlled.**

**Model Answer:**

**Blockage Ratio** is defined as the ratio of the maximum frontal projected area of the test article $A_m$ to the cross-sectional area of the empty test section $A_t$:

$$\beta = \frac{A_m}{A_t}$$

For a **closed test section** wind tunnel used in automotive testing, the **KTU / SAE standard permissible limit is 5 %** ($\beta \le 0.05$). For open test sections, the limit is relaxed to **7.5 %**.

**Why it must be controlled:** When the model occupies a significant fraction of the test section, the surrounding walls constrain the streamlines, locally accelerate the flow, and **artificially increase** the measured drag, lift, and side-force coefficients. This phenomenon, called **solid blockage**, causes the apparent $C_D$ to be higher than the free-air value. To obtain meaningful aerodynamic data that can be extrapolated to free-road operation, the blockage must be kept within the standard limit or corrected mathematically (e.g., Maskell correction).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
**Explain why a moving ground plane is essential for accurate automotive wind tunnel testing.**

**Model Answer:**

A real road surface moves with the vehicle at the same speed as the free-stream air (i.e., it is in the vehicle's reference frame). In a **fixed-floor wind tunnel**, the floor is stationary relative to the tunnel walls, which creates an artificially thick boundary layer underneath the car. This exaggerates the underbody drag, alters the pressure distribution on the lower body, and changes the front-to-rear lift balance.

A **moving ground plane** (single belt or 5-belt system) simulates the relative motion between the vehicle and the road, suppressing the underbody boundary layer and providing realistic shear stress on the floor. Without it, the measured $C_D$ can be **0.02–0.05 higher** than the true free-road value, which is unacceptable for fuel-economy and EV-range certification. KTU mandates a moving ground plane for all **production-vehicle certification tests**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme ESE convention: Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **one full question** (either the OR alternative). The model solutions below are written in the same depth a topper would use.

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of neat sketches, explain the different **types of wind tunnels** used for automotive testing. Compare open-circuit and closed-circuit tunnels. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **drag coefficient** using dimensional analysis (Buckingham Pi theorem) and explain the **Reynolds similarity requirement** for matching prototype conditions in a scale-model test. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The principal types of wind tunnels used for automotive aerodynamic testing are:

**1. Open-Circuit (Blow-Down) Tunnel**
- Air is drawn from the room, accelerated through the contraction, passed over the test section, and exhausted back into the room.
- Simple construction, lower capital cost, but **flow quality is sensitive to room drafts** and turbulence intensity is higher.

**2. Closed-Circuit (Return-Type) Tunnel**
- Air recirculates continuously through the test section and back via corner vanes and a return duct.
- Better flow quality, lower turbulence intensity (Tu < 0.1 %), thermal stability, and higher energy efficiency — but **higher capital cost** and requires a larger facility footprint.

**3. Open-Jet (Free-Jet) Tunnel**
- The test section has no walls; the high-speed jet discharges into a large collector.
- Eliminates wall interference entirely (no blockage correction needed), but **requires a very large collector** to recover the flow.

**4. Closed-Return Full-Scale Tunnel**
- Industry standard for OEM certification — closed circuit with a 7–11 m test section and a moving ground plane.

| Feature | Open-Circuit | Closed-Circuit |
|---------|--------------|----------------|
| Capital cost | Low | High |
| Flow quality | Moderate | Excellent |
| Tu | 0.3 – 0.5 % | < 0.1 % |
| Energy efficiency | Low | High |
| Blockage limit | 7.5 % | 5 % |

A neat schematic showing inlet → screens → contraction → test section → diffuser → fan → return duct is expected.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Identify variables and primary dimensions:**

Drag force $F_D$ depends on fluid density $\rho$, free-stream velocity $V$, characteristic length $L$, and fluid viscosity $\mu$. So $F_D = f(\rho, V, L, \mu)$ — **5 variables, 3 dimensions**, giving **2 dimensionless groups**.

**Step 2 — Buckingham Pi construction:**

Choose repeating variables: $\rho, V, L$.

$$
\Pi_1 = \frac{F_D}{\rho V^2 L^2}, \quad \Pi_2 = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu} = Re
$$

**Step 3 — Functional relation:**

$$\frac{F_D}{\rho V^2 L^2} = \phi(Re)$$

**Step 4 — Define drag coefficient using frontal area $A_f \propto L^2$:**

$$
C_D = \frac{F_D}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A_f} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A_f C_D
$$

**Step 5 — Reynolds similarity:**

For a scale model of factor $\lambda = L_m / L_p$ to give data valid for the prototype, $Re_m = Re_p$. Hence $V_m = V_p / \lambda$ (at constant $\rho, \mu$). For $\lambda = 1/4$, $V_m = 4 V_p$, which is usually impractical — so **larger scales (3/4 or 5/8), pressurization, or cryogenic tunnels** are used in practice.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — OR Alternative `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** List and explain the **major facility requirements** (blockage ratio, turbulence intensity, flow angularity, moving ground plane) for an automotive wind tunnel. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 1/3-scale model of a sedan (prototype length 4.5 m) is tested in a wind tunnel with a test-section cross-section of 6 m × 4 m. The measured drag force at a model speed of 90 m/s is 95 N. The frontal area of the prototype is 2.25 m². Calculate: **(i)** the drag coefficient of the model, **(ii)** the prototype speed at which the test was simulated (Reynolds matched), and **(iii)** the prototype drag force. **Assume $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³ throughout.** **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

| Requirement | Formula | Typical Limit | Reason |
|-------------|---------|---------------|--------|
| Blockage Ratio | $\beta = A_m/A_t$ | < 5 % closed | Avoids wall-induced streamline compression |
| Turbulence Intensity | $Tu = u'_{rms}/U_\infty$ | < 0.1 % | Avoids premature boundary-layer transition |
| Flow Angularity | $\Delta\psi, \Delta\theta$ | < 0.25° each | Ensures accurate lift and side-force |
| Moving Ground Plane | Floor moves at $V_\infty$ | Required | Suppresses underbody boundary layer |
| Test Section Size | $D_t$ | ≥ 7 m for full-scale | Geometric similarity for full-scale cars |
| Force Balance Accuracy | — | < ±0.1 % FS | Required for $C_D$ resolution of 0.001 |

Each of these must be **measured, documented, and reported** in the test certification per KTU/SAE J1252 and J2071 standards.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- Scale factor $\lambda = 1/3$, so $L_m = 4.5/3 = 1.5$ m.
- Model frontal area: $A_{f,m} = \lambda^2 A_f = (1/3)^2 \times 2.25 = 0.25$ m².
- $V_m = 90$ m/s, $F_{D,m} = 95$ N, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³.

**Part (i) — Drag coefficient of the model:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{D,m} &= \frac{F_{D,m}}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_m^{2} A_{f,m}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{95}{0.5 \times 1.225 \times 90^{2} \times 0.25} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{95}{0.5 \times 1.225 \times 8100 \times 0.25} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{95}{1240.31} \\[4pt]
C_{D,m} &\approx 0.0766
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (ii) — Prototype speed for Reynolds match:**

$$
V_p = V_m \times \lambda = 90 \times \tfrac{1}{3} = 30 \text{ m/s} = 108 \text{ km/h}
$$

> Wait — re-derive carefully using $Re_m = Re_p$ with $\mu$ constant:
> $$\rho V_m L_m / \mu = \rho V_p L_p / \mu \;\Longrightarrow\; V_m L_m = V_p L_p$$
> $$V_p = V_m \times (L_m / L_p) = 90 \times (1/3) = 30 \text{ m/s} = 108 \text{ km/h}$$

**Part (iii) — Prototype drag force:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{D,p} &= \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_p^{2} A_f C_D \\[4pt]
&= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 30^{2} \times 2.25 \times 0.0766 \\[4pt]
&= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 900 \times 2.25 \times 0.0766 \\[4pt]
&= 95.05 \;\text{N (approx)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Insight:** At Reynolds similarity, the drag force at the matched prototype speed is the same as the measured model force (when frontal areas scale as $\lambda^2$). This is a useful sanity check for KTU exam problems.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks in Wind Tunnel Test Requirements questions:**
> 1. **Confusing frontal area $A_f$ with planform area** — $A_f$ is the **head-on shadow**, not the top-view area. Marks deducted for this in Q3(b)-type problems.
> 2. **Forgetting to square the scale factor for area** — when scaling $A_f$, remember $A_f \propto L^2$, so $A_{f,m} = \lambda^{2} A_{f,p}$. Several toppers lose a mark here.
> 3. **Mixing up Reynolds similarity rule** — $V_m L_m = V_p L_p$ (with constant fluid) — NOT $V_m = V_p / \lambda^2$. Wrong exponent costs full marks on the derivation.
> 4. **Not stating the 5 % blockage limit explicitly** — KTU examiner's keyword expectation: "blockage ratio < 5 %". Just writing "low blockage" is **insufficient** for full marks.
> 5. **Omitting moving ground plane discussion** in Q1 type — many students miss the **underbody boundary layer** argument, which is the actual physics reason. Marks lost on conceptual depth.
> 6. **Unit mismatch** — $C_D$ is dimensionless; writing "0.305 per unit" is incorrect and may cost a mark.
> 7. **Skipping the Reynolds number calculation step** in numerical problems — examiners award partial credit for showing each step; do not jump directly to the answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Wind Tunnel Test Requirements**

- [ ] **Three similarity pillars:** Geometric (shape), Kinematic (streamlines), Dynamic (Re, M).
- [ ] **Blockage ratio limit:** **5 % closed / 7.5 % open**. Above this, Maskell correction must be applied.
- [ ] **Turbulence intensity limit:** **Tu < 0.1 %** for production automotive WT.
- [ ] **Flow angularity:** Yaw and pitch variations **< 0.25°** each across the test volume.
- [ ] **Moving ground plane** is **mandatory** for realistic underbody flow simulation.
- [ ] **Reynolds scaling:** $V_m = V_p / \lambda$ at constant fluid properties. For full-scale Re ≈ 10⁷.
- [ ] **Froude number match** is required ONLY when moving ground plane is used (gravity matters for the moving belt).
- [ ] **Mach number** must be **< 0.3** for incompressible flow assumption to hold ($C_p$ validity).
- [ ] **Drag coefficient definition:** $C_D = F_D / (\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} A_f)$ — frontal projected area $A_f$.
- [ ] **Energy Equivalent Speed (EES):** $V_{EES} = V \sqrt{\rho / \rho_0}$ — used for density normalization across test sessions.
- [ ] **Boundary layer transition fixing:** trip dots or turbulator strips at 5 % chord from leading edge of windows/A-pillars to standardize transition location.
- [ ] **Force balance resolution:** Must resolve **0.001 in $C_D$** for meaningful fuel-economy claims.
- [ ] **KTU standard:** SAE J1252 (test methods), SAE J2071 (correction methods), ISO 12039 (tunnel-to-road correlation).
- [ ] **EV relevance:** 0.01 drop in $C_D$ ≈ 5 km extra WLTP range — strongest commercial justification for WT investment.
- [ ] **High-speed stability:** Front lift coefficient $C_{L,f}$ should be slightly negative for high-speed steering feel (downforce at front = 30 % of total).
- [ ] **Cross-wind sensitivity:** $C_{S'}$ derivative w.r.t. yaw angle (per degree) is measured in yaw sweep tests from ±10°.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ground Boundary Simulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT724 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ground Boundary Simulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Ground Boundary Simulation (GBS)** is the computational and mathematical modelling of the road–wheel contact interface used to replicate the dynamic interaction between a vehicle's tyres and the terrain in off-board numerical simulations, driving simulators, and Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL) test rigs. It is a *closed-loop co-simulation framework* in which:

1. A **road surface profile** (deterministic or stochastic) is generated as a function of distance, $h(x)$, $h(t)$.
2. The **vehicle dynamics solver** propagates tyre contact patch displacements and velocities.
3. A **boundary interaction model** computes reaction forces (normal, longitudinal, lateral) at the contact patch.
4. These forces are fed back to the **vehicle multibody model** to compute the next time-step response.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, GBS is positioned as the *third* of the four corner-stones of a full vehicle performance simulation pyramid: **(Vehicle Model) + (Tyre Model) + (Driver Model) + (Ground Boundary Model)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** GBS is *not* merely a road profile. It is the **integrated assembly of road excitation generator, contact patch kinematics solver, and the feedback force transfer algorithm** that links the road with the vehicle. KTU examiners expect you to describe all three subsystems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are blindfolded and asked to drive a remote-control car over an unknown surface. You cannot see the road, but you can:
- Send a **ping** (force) into the road.
- Measure the **return echo** (reaction).
- Guess the **shape of the surface** from the echo timing.

GBS works exactly backwards — the **road shape is already known** (pre-loaded), the wheel is the sensor, and the simulation asks:

> *"If I drive this wheel over THIS road shape, how much force will the road push back, and how will that force disturb the car?"*

A second analogy: think of GBS as a **virtual treadmill** for a vehicle. Just as a treadmill measures how a runner's foot pushes down on a moving belt, GBS mathematically "moves the road" under a stationary or moving virtual vehicle and measures the resulting tyre forces.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants in GBS

| Metric / Constant | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Reference spatial frequency | $n_0$ | **0.1 cycles/m** (ISO 8608) |
| Road waviness exponent | $w$ | **2** (typical) |
| Tyre vertical stiffness (passenger car) | $k_t$ | **200,000 – 300,000 N/m** |
| Tyre damping coefficient | $c_t$ | **1,500 – 3,500 Ns/m** |
| Contact patch length | $a_c$ | **0.10 – 0.15 m** |
| Time-step for HIL GBS | $\Delta t$ | **1 ms – 5 ms** |
| Standard simulation frequency | $f_s$ | **1 kHz** for ride, **100 Hz** for handling |
| Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard ISO 8608** classifies roads into **8 classes (A–H)** based on Power Spectral Density (PSD) magnitude. Class **A** is a smooth motorway; Class **H** is a severely damaged off-road track.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of GBS — Structured Logic

### Pillar I — Road Excitation Generator

- **Deterministic Inputs:** Bumps, steps, ramps, sine sweeps, Belgian paving. Used for **transient response** testing.
- **Stochastic Inputs:** Generated by **Inverse Fast Fourier Transform (IFFT)** of an ISO 8608 PSD function. Used for **ride comfort** and **fatigue** simulations.
- **Measured Inputs:** Real road profiles captured by laser profilometers, then resampled in time using vehicle speed.

### Pillar II — Contact Patch Kinematics Solver

- Determines the *instantaneous vertical penetration* of the tyre into the road: $\delta = r - (z_w - h(x))$
- Computes slip ratios ($\kappa$) and slip angles ($\alpha$) for longitudinal/lateral force generation.
- Captures the **enveloping effect** when tyre rolls over sharp obstacles shorter than the contact length.

### Pillar III — Reaction Force Feedback

- Vertical: spring-damper or non-linear (e.g., Dugoff, Fiala).
- Longitudinal & Lateral: Magic Formula (Pacejka), Brush, or Dugoff.
- Forces are then mapped back to the vehicle body solver.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | Why it Matters |
|---|---|
| Generate $h(x)$ stochastically | Real roads are random; deterministic inputs miss the broadband excitation needed for fatigue analysis. |
| Compute $\delta$ | This is the *geometric state variable* that drives the entire tyre model. |
| Apply Magic Formula | Captures the **non-linear saturation** of friction forces which linear models cannot. |
| Feed forces back to body | Closes the simulation loop; without this, the model is **open-loop** and unrealistic. |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** All units MUST accompany numerical answers. KTU examiners deduct **0.5 mark per missing unit**.

| # | Equation | Variables | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Phi(n) = \Phi(n_0) \left(\dfrac{n}{n_0}\right)^{-w}$ | $n$: spatial freq; $n_0$: 0.1 cyc/m; $w$: waviness | ISO 8608 PSD definition |
| 2 | $\Delta\Phi_i = \Phi(n_i) \cdot \Delta n$ | Power in frequency band | IFFT amplitude |
| 3 | $h(x) = \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N} \sqrt{2 \Delta\Phi_i} \cdot \cos(2\pi n_i x + \phi_i)$ | $h(x)$: road elevation | Stochastic road generation |
| 4 | $\delta = r_w - \left(z_w - h(x)\right)$ | $r_w$: unloaded radius; $z_w$: wheel centre | Tyre deflection |
| 5 | $F_z = k_t \delta + c_t \dot{\delta}$ | Linear vertical model | Normal force calc |
| 6 | $F_x = D \sin\!\left(C \arctan\!\left(B \kappa - E\!\left(B\kappa - \arctan(B\kappa)\right)\right)\right)$ | Magic Formula longitudinal | Pure longitudinal slip |
| 7 | $F_y = D \sin\!\left(C \arctan\!\left(B \alpha - E\!\left(B\alpha - \arctan(B\alpha)\right)\right)\right)$ | Magic Formula lateral | Pure slip angle |
| 8 | $\kappa = \dfrac{\omega_w r_w - v_x}{v_x}$ (braking); $\dfrac{\omega_w r_w - v_x}{\omega_w r_w}$ (driving) | $v_x$: body longitudinal vel. | Slip ratio |
| 9 | $\alpha = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{v_y}{v_x}\right) - \delta_{steer}$ | $\delta_{steer}$: steer angle | Slip angle |
| 10 | $m_s \ddot{z}_s + c_s(\dot{z}_s - \dot{z}_u) + k_s(z_s - z_u) = 0$ | Quarter-car ride model | Vertical dynamics |
| 11 | $m_u \ddot{z}_u + c_t(\dot{z}_u - \dot{h}) + k_t(z_u - h) - c_s(\dot{z}_s - \dot{z}_u) - k_s(z_s - z_u) = 0$ | Wheel-hop equation | Tyre vertical dynamics |
| 12 | $S_a = \dfrac{\langle a_{rms}^2 \rangle}{g^2}$ | Ride comfort index (VDV-related) | ISO 2631 evaluation |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **HIL Tyre Testing:** Major OEMs (Bosch, Continental, AVL) use GBS to validate Electronic Stability Programs (ESP) in the lab without driving the actual car.
- **Autonomous Vehicle Validation:** Simulating 10⁹ km of road exposure before deployment.
- **Race Engineering:** Pre-event tyre compound selection on virtual replicas of circuits like Suzuka or Nürburgring.
- **Active Suspension Development:** GBS provides the disturbance input that active dampers must reject.
- **Fatigue Life Prediction:** PSD road inputs drive duty cycles for chassis durability analysis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stochastic Road Profile from ISO 8608 PSD

**Goal:** Generate a 1-D road elevation profile $h(x)$ for a road of class **C** (medium road) over a length $L = 100$ m.

**Step 1 — Class selection and PSD reference value lookup**

ISO 8608 gives $\Phi(n_0)$ for each class at $n_0 = 0.1$ cycles/m.

Class C → $\Phi(n_0) = 256 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$ (geometric mean of B and D).

**Step 2 — Define spatial frequency range**

$$n_{\min} = \frac{1}{L} = \frac{1}{100} = 0.01 \text{ cycles/m}$$

$$n_{\max} = \frac{1}{0.05} = 20 \text{ cycles/m}$$

(0.05 m chosen as the smallest wavelength of physical interest, roughly twice a tyre-contact-patch length).

**Step 3 — Discretise frequency axis into $N$ bands**

For $N = 200$ bands:

$$\Delta n = \frac{n_{\max} - n_{\min}}{N} = \frac{20 - 0.01}{200} = 0.09995 \text{ cycles/m}$$

**Step 4 — PSD value at each band centre**

$$n_i = n_{\min} + \left(i - \tfrac{1}{2}\right)\Delta n, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, 200$$

$$\Phi(n_i) = \Phi(n_0) \left(\frac{n_i}{n_0}\right)^{-w} = 256 \times 10^{-6} \left(\frac{n_i}{0.1}\right)^{-2}$$

**Step 5 — Compute amplitude for each band**

The amplitude of the cosine component in band $i$:

$$A_i = \sqrt{2 \cdot \Phi(n_i) \cdot \Delta n}$$

The factor of **2** arises because PSD is a *two-sided* power representation but we are constructing a *one-sided* cosine sum (energy in $\pm f$ folded into positive $f$).

**Step 6 — Generate random phase for each band**

$\phi_i = 2\pi \cdot U(0,1)$, where $U$ is the uniform distribution on $[0,1]$.

**Step 7 — Assemble the road profile**

$$\boxed{\,h(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} A_i \cos\!\left(2\pi n_i x + \phi_i\right)\,}$$

For $x$ sampled at $\Delta x = 0.05$ m, we obtain a discrete road profile $h[k] = h(k\Delta x)$, $k = 0, 1, \ldots, 2000$.

**Step 8 — Validate by recomputing the PSD**

Using Welch's method, average periodogram of the generated $h(x)$ should recover the original $\Phi(n)$ within a 3 dB confidence band (this is the standard acceptance criterion).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Tyre Vertical Force in 7-DOF Vehicle Model

**Goal:** Derive the vertical dynamics of the wheel in a 7-DOF vehicle model (3 translations + 3 rotations + wheel rotation).

**Step 1 — Free body of the wheel**

Forces acting vertically on the wheel of mass $m_{w,i}$ (where $i \in \{FL, FR, RL, RR\}$):
1. Vertical spring force from suspension: $F_{s,i} = -k_s(z_{w,i} - z_{s,i}) - c_s(\dot{z}_{w,i} - \dot{z}_{s,i})$
2. Vertical tyre-ground contact force: $F_{t,i} = k_t (r_{w,i} - z_{w,i} + h_i) + c_t (\dot{r}_{w,i} - \dot{z}_{w,i} + \dot{h}_i)$

**Step 2 — Wheel mass treated as negligible** (standard simplification, $\approx 40$ kg vs. body $\approx 1500$ kg)

Quasi-static tyre force:

$$F_{t,i} = k_t \left(r_{w,i} - z_{w,i} + h_i\right) + c_t\left(\dot{r}_{w,i} - \dot{z}_{w,i} + \dot{h}_i\right)$$

**Step 3 — Body vertical, pitch, and roll dynamics**

Let $z_s$ = body CG vertical disp.; $\theta$ = pitch; $\phi$ = roll; $t_f, t_r$ = front/rear track half-widths.

For corner $i$, the body displacement at the contact point:

$$z_{s,i} = z_s + (-1)^{i_{lat}} t_i \sin\phi + (-1)^{i_{long}} l_i \sin\theta$$

$$\dot{z}_{s,i} = \dot{z}_s + (-1)^{i_{lat}} t_i \dot{\phi} \cos\phi + (-1)^{i_{long}} l_i \dot{\theta} \cos\theta$$

Where $l_i$ is the longitudinal distance from CG.

**Step 4 — Combine into body equations**

Vertical: $\displaystyle m_s \ddot{z}_s = -\sum_{i=1}^{4}\left[k_s(z_{s,i} - z_{w,i}) + c_s(\dot{z}_{s,i} - \dot{z}_{w,i})\right]$

Pitch: $\displaystyle I_y \ddot{\theta} = \sum_{i=1}^{4}(-1)^{i_{long}}\,l_i \left[k_s(z_{s,i} - z_{w,i}) + c_s(\dot{z}_{s,i} - \dot{z}_{w,i})\right]$

Roll: $\displaystyle I_x \ddot{\phi} = \sum_{i=1}^{4}(-1)^{i_{lat}}\,t_i \left[k_s(z_{s,i} - z_{w,i}) + c_s(\dot{z}_{s,i} - \dot{z}_{w,i})\right]$

**Step 5 — Total 7-DOF state vector**

$$\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} x & y & z_s & \phi & \theta & \psi & \omega_{w,FL} & \omega_{w,FR} & \omega_{w,RL} & \omega_{w,RR} \end{bmatrix}^\top$$

This 10-element state vector (4 wheel rotational speeds + 6 body DOF) is the canonical 7-DOF vehicle model used in commercial tools (CarSim, veDYNA, ASM).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Magic Formula Slip Force (Longitudinal)

**Goal:** Derive the longitudinal tyre force for a pure-braking condition.

**Step 1 — Define slip ratio**

$$\kappa = \frac{\omega_w r_w - v_x}{v_x} \quad \text{(braking, } v_x > 0\text{)}$$

**Step 2 — Identify Magic Formula constants from tyre data sheet**

- $B$ = **stiffness factor** (≈ 10)
- $C$ = **shape factor** (≈ 1.9)
- $D$ = **peak factor** (≈ $\mu \cdot F_z$)
- $E$ = **curvature factor** (≈ −0.8 for braking, +0.8 for driving)

**Step 3 — Compute normalised slip**

$$B\kappa = B \cdot \kappa$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the inner arctan**

$$\theta = B\kappa - E\bigl(B\kappa - \arctan(B\kappa)\bigr)$$

**Step 5 — Take outer arctan**

$$\Phi = C \cdot \arctan(\theta)$$

**Step 6 — Apply sine saturation**

$$\boxed{\,F_x = D \cdot \sin(\Phi)\,}$$

**Step 7 — Numerical example**

Take $\kappa = 0.1$ (10% slip), $B = 10$, $C = 1.9$, $D = 4000$ N, $E = -0.8$:

$$B\kappa = 10 \times 0.1 = 1.0$$

$$\arctan(1.0) = 0.7854 \text{ rad}$$

$$\theta = 1.0 - (-0.8)(1.0 - 0.7854) = 1.0 + 0.1717 = 1.1717$$

$$\Phi = 1.9 \times \arctan(1.1717) = 1.9 \times 0.8647 = 1.6430 \text{ rad}$$

$$F_x = 4000 \times \sin(1.6430) = 4000 \times 0.9979 = 3991.6 \text{ N}$$

So at 10% slip, the tyre delivers ≈ 99.8% of its peak friction force, confirming the rapid saturation behaviour of the Magic Formula.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — GBS Methods

| Method | Time to Setup | Fidelity | Real-Time Capable? | Cost | Used By |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Simple bump (deterministic) | Minutes | Low | Yes | Free | Pre-design |
| ISO PSD stochastic IFFT | Hours | Medium | Yes (with lookup) | Free | Ride & durability |
| Laser-measured profile replay | Hours | High | Yes | Free | Ride development |
| Brush / Fiala tyre model | 1 day | Medium | Yes | Free | Educational |
| Magic Formula (Pacejka) | 3 days | High | Yes (with HiL) | Free | Industry standard |
| FEM contact (Abaqus, ANSA) | 2 weeks | Very high | No | ₹₹₹ | Research, tyre design |
| GPU-accelerated multibody (Hummel3D) | 1 week | High | Yes | ₹₹ | Real-time simulator |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Full GBS Co-Simulation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Road Profile Generator] --> B[ISO 8608 PSD Engine]
    B --> C[IFFT Module]
    C --> D[Spatial Road h of x]
    D --> E[Vehicle Speed Map v of t]
    E --> F[Time-Domain Road h of t]
    F --> G[Contact Patch Solver]
    H[Vehicle Body Model 7-DOF] --> G
    G --> I[Tyre Deflection delta]
    I --> J[Magic Formula Tyre Model]
    J --> K[Reaction Forces Fx Fy Fz]
    K --> H
    H --> L[State Integrator RK4]
    L --> H
    J --> M[Telemetry Bus]
    M --> N[Dashboard Visualization]
    M --> O[Data Logger]
    H --> P[Driver Model in the Loop]
    P --> H
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — One Simulation Time-Step

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant DRV as Driver Model
    participant BDY as Vehicle Body 7-DOF
    participant WH as Wheel Node
    participant CP as Contact Patch
    participant TY as Tyre Force Model
    participant RD as Road Profile
    DRV->>BDY: Apply steer delta and T_engine
    BDY->>WH: Compute v_x, v_y, omega_w per wheel
    WH->>CP: Compute penetration delta
    CP->>RD: Sample h of x at contact point
    RD-->>CP: Return road height h
    CP->>TY: Send delta, delta_dot, kappa, alpha
    TY-->>WH: Return Fx, Fy, Fz, Mz
    WH-->>BDY: Apply forces at contact patches
    BDY->>BDY: Integrate state to t + dt
    BDY-->>DRV: Return new yaw rate, sideslip
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Subsystem Map of a Typical HIL GBS Rig

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph RealTime[Real-Time Simulation PC]
        RT1[Vehicle Dynamics Model]
        RT2[Tyre Model Magic Formula]
        RT3[Road Generator ISO PSD]
        RT1 --> RT2
        RT3 --> RT2
    end
    subgraph HiL[Hardware in the Loop]
        H1[Steering Robot]
        H2[Brake Pedal Robot]
        H3[Throttle Robot]
    end
    subgraph Sensors[Sensor Emulation]
        S1[Wheel Speed Encoder]
        S2[IMU 6 Axis]
        S3[Steering Angle Sensor]
    end
    RT2 --> S1
    RT2 --> S2
    RT2 --> S3
    H1 --> RT1
    H2 --> RT1
    H3 --> RT1
    S1 --> ECU[Vehicle ECU Under Test]
    S2 --> ECU
    S3 --> ECU
    ECU --> H1
    ECU --> H2
    ECU --> H3
```

## 4.4 Mermaid State Diagram — GBS Solver State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Initialization
    Initialization --> RoadGen: Load class A to H
    RoadGen --> PreRoll: h of x computed
    PreRoll --> SteadyState: Burn-in 2 seconds
    SteadyState --> Maneuver: Trigger by trigger flag
    Maneuver --> DataLog: Write state vectors
    DataLog --> Maneuver: Continue loop
    Maneuver --> Termination: End trigger
    Termination --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** Mermaid cannot render photorealistic 3-D scenes. The flow-charts above therefore depict the **functional architecture** of GBS — the exact content KTU 2024 examiners look for in 14-mark drawing questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Ground Boundary Simulation. List its three main subsystems.**

**Model Answer:**

Ground Boundary Simulation (GBS) is the mathematical and computational modelling of the road–wheel contact interface used to replicate the dynamic interaction between a vehicle's tyres and the terrain in numerical and Hardware-in-the-Loop simulations.

The three main subsystems are:
1. **Road Excitation Generator** — creates the elevation profile $h(x)$ as a function of distance.
2. **Contact Patch Kinematics Solver** — computes tyre deflection $\delta$ and slip states ($\kappa$, $\alpha$).
3. **Reaction Force Feedback Module** — applies tyre forces ($F_x$, $F_y$, $F_z$) back to the vehicle body.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for subsystems.)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the significance of ISO 8608 in Ground Boundary Simulation?**

**Model Answer:**

ISO 8608 is the international standard that classifies road surface roughness into eight classes (A through H) using a Power Spectral Density (PSD) function of the form:

$$\Phi(n) = \Phi(n_0)\left(\frac{n}{n_0}\right)^{-w}$$

where $n_0 = 0.1$ cycles/m and $w = 2$ typically. It is significant because it:
1. Provides a **standardised, repeatable** road excitation input for GBS.
2. Enables **comparison** of vehicle responses across different studies and OEMs.
3. Allows the use of **synthetic stochastic roads** generated by IFFT for repeatable ride and durability testing.

*(3 marks: 1 for equation, 1 for classification, 1 for significance.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently confuse **slip ratio** and **slip angle** definitions. The slip ratio $\kappa$ uses **longitudinal** wheel and body velocities; the slip angle $\alpha$ uses the **arctan** of lateral-over-longitudinal velocity. Mixing them costs 2–3 marks per question. Also, **do not forget to state the formula before substituting numbers** — a common 0.5-mark penalty.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) (7 marks)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a complete Ground Boundary Simulation system. Describe the role of each block.

**(b) (7 marks)** A passenger car of mass $m = 1500$ kg travels at constant speed $v = 20$ m/s over an ISO 8608 class C road. The PSD reference value is $\Phi(n_0) = 256 \times 10^{-6}$ m³, $n_0 = 0.1$ cycles/m, $w = 2$. Compute the road PSD at spatial frequencies $n = 0.5$ and $n = 2.0$ cycles/m, and explain the trend.

#### Model Solution to (a):

A GBS system has the following blocks (describe in the answer):

| Block | Role |
|---|---|
| Road Generator | Creates $h(x)$ from ISO PSD or measured data |
| Vehicle Speed Mapper | Converts $h(x)$ to $h(t) = h(vt)$ |
| Tyre Model | Computes $F_x, F_y, F_z, M_z$ from slip and load |
| Vehicle Body Model | 7-DOF multibody equations |
| Integrator | Advances state vector in time (RK4 typically) |
| Driver Model | Generates steer, brake, throttle inputs |
| HIL Interface | Real-time link to ECUs and actuators |

*(Full 7 marks for diagram + 1-line role for each block.)*

#### Model Solution to (b):

Using ISO 8608:

$$\Phi(n) = \Phi(n_0)\left(\frac{n}{n_0}\right)^{-w}$$

**At $n = 0.5$ cycles/m:**

$$\Phi(0.5) = 256 \times 10^{-6} \times \left(\frac{0.5}{0.1}\right)^{-2}$$

$$= 256 \times 10^{-6} \times (5)^{-2} = 256 \times 10^{-6} \times \frac{1}{25} = 10.24 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$$

**At $n = 2.0$ cycles/m:**

$$\Phi(2.0) = 256 \times 10^{-6} \times \left(\frac{2.0}{0.1}\right)^{-2}$$

$$= 256 \times 10^{-6} \times (20)^{-2} = 256 \times 10^{-6} \times \frac{1}{400} = 0.64 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$$

**Trend:** As spatial frequency $n$ increases (i.e., as we look at finer road features like small ripples), the PSD decreases as a power law. The road has more *energy* in long-wavelength features (hills, large undulations) than in short-wavelength features (rough texture). This is exactly the physical reality of natural roads.

*(7 marks: 2 for formula statement, 3 for two numerical evaluations, 1 for unit, 1 for trend explanation.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) (7 marks)** Explain the Magic Formula (Pacejka) tyre model. State the longitudinal force equation and identify the four magic parameters $B, C, D, E$. What does each parameter physically represent?

**(b) (7 marks)** For a quarter-car model with $m_s = 400$ kg, $m_u = 40$ kg, $k_s = 20{,}000$ N/m, $c_s = 1500$ Ns/m, $k_t = 200{,}000$ N/m, $c_t = 200$ Ns/m, derive the equations of motion. Compute the sprung-mass natural frequency.

#### Model Solution to (a):

The Magic Formula is an empirical model that captures the **non-linear saturation** of tyre friction forces with slip.

**Longitudinal force equation:**

$$F_x = D \sin\!\left[C \arctan\!\left(B\kappa - E\bigl(B\kappa - \arctan(B\kappa)\bigr)\right)\right]$$

**The four magic parameters:**

| Parameter | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|
| $B$ | Stiffness factor — controls initial slope $\partial F/\partial \kappa$ near origin |
| $C$ | Shape factor — controls the overall shape; $C \approx 1.9$ in most cases |
| $D$ | Peak factor — controls the maximum value of $F_x$ (usually $\approx \mu F_z$) |
| $E$ | Curvature factor — controls curvature near the peak; sign distinguishes braking ($E < 0$) vs driving ($E > 0$) |

**Physical significance:**
- $D$ is a **force** quantity, hence depends on vertical load $F_z$ and friction coefficient $\mu$.
- $B$ has units of **1/slip** (i.e., inverse of slip ratio).
- $C$ and $E$ are dimensionless.

*(7 marks: 2 for equation, 4 for parameter identification, 1 for physical meaning.)*

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Free body diagrams:**

Sprung mass: vertical forces = suspension spring $k_s$, suspension damper $c_s$, gravity $-m_s g$.

Unsprung mass: vertical forces = suspension reaction (Newton's third law), tyre spring $k_t$, tyre damper $c_t$, gravity $-m_u g$.

**Equations of motion:**

Sprung mass:

$$m_s \ddot{z}_s + c_s(\dot{z}_s - \dot{z}_u) + k_s(z_s - z_u) = 0$$

Unsprung mass:

$$m_u \ddot{z}_u + c_t(\dot{z}_u - \dot{h}) + k_t(z_u - h) - c_s(\dot{z}_s - \dot{z}_u) - k_s(z_s - z_u) = 0$$

**Sprung-mass natural frequency (decoupled approximation, ignoring $m_u$):**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k_s}{m_s}} = \sqrt{\frac{20{,}000}{400}} = \sqrt{50} = 7.07 \text{ rad/s}$$

Converted to Hz:

$$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{7.07}{2\pi} = 1.125 \text{ Hz}$$

This is the **typical sprung-mass bounce frequency** of a passenger car (1–1.5 Hz), which sits in the human-body comfort sensitivity range (4–8 Hz resonance of internal organs), hence why car suspensions are tuned near this value.

*(7 marks: 2 for sprung-mass EOM, 2 for unsprung-mass EOM, 2 for natural frequency calculation, 1 for Hz conversion and interpretation.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read 30 minutes before the exam:**

- [ ] **GBS = Road + Contact + Force Feedback.** Not just a road profile.
- [ ] **ISO 8608 PSD equation** uses $n_0 = 0.1$ cycles/m and $w = 2$.
- [ ] **Eight ISO classes** A (best) → H (worst). Class C/D typical for Indian state highways.
- [ ] **Slip ratio** $\kappa$ — uses longitudinal velocities, dimensionless, range $\pm 1$.
- [ ] **Slip angle** $\alpha$ — uses arctan of lateral over longitudinal velocity.
- [ ] **Magic Formula** uses **4 parameters** B, C, D, E — know each one's physical meaning.
- [ ] **Quarter-car model** has **2 DOF** (sprung + unsprung). Full car is 7-DOF.
- [ ] **Sprung-mass natural frequency** for a passenger car ≈ 1–1.5 Hz.
- [ ] **Unsprung-mass (wheel-hop) natural frequency** ≈ 10–15 Hz.
- [ ] **Tyre vertical stiffness** is much higher than suspension stiffness ($k_t \gg k_s$).
- [ ] **Always write the formula before substituting** — KTU valuation key demands it.
- [ ] **Include units in the final numerical answer** — otherwise lose 0.5 marks.
- [ ] **IFFT is the standard method** for generating stochastic roads in GBS.
- [ ] **HIL GBS** is used for **ECU testing** without driving the actual vehicle.
- [ ] **Driver-in-the-loop** simulators (e.g., DiL, Vi-Grade) are the *most realistic* form of GBS.
- [ ] **Welch's method** is the standard PSD validation technique after road generation.
- [ ] **Envelope effect** of tyres causes them to "ride over" sharp bumps smaller than contact patch length.
- [ ] **Fiala and Brush models** are alternatives to Magic Formula for educational use.
- [ ] **Co-simulation** between two solvers (e.g., CarSim + Simulink) is the standard industrial GBS practice.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wind Tunnel Selection and Reynolds Number Capability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT724 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Wind Tunnel Selection and Reynolds Number Capability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Wind Tunnel Selection — Formal Definition

**Wind Tunnel Selection** is the systematic engineering process of identifying the most appropriate aerodynamic test facility whose geometric, kinematic, and thermodynamic capabilities (test section size, flow velocity range, turbulence intensity, and Reynolds number envelope) match the simulation objectives of a specific vehicle or component under test.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, the selection is governed by four primary design drivers:
1. **Mach number requirement** of the test article
2. **Reynolds number capability** (boundary layer similarity)
3. **Geometric blockage ratio** ($\varepsilon_b \leq 5\%$, typically)
4. **Mission profile** of the vehicle (road car, race car, aircraft, missile, etc.)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 of *Vehicle Performance and Testing* explicitly links wind tunnel selection to **Reynolds Number Capability**, as this single non-dimensional parameter governs whether the boundary layer, wake structure, and pressure distribution measured in the model will faithfully represent the full-scale vehicle.

## 1.2 Reynolds Number Capability — Formal Definition

The **Reynolds Number Capability** of a wind tunnel is the maximum product of free-stream velocity ($V_\infty$), characteristic length ($L$), and inverse of kinematic viscosity ($1/\nu$) that the facility can sustain in its test section, expressed as:

$$Re_L = \frac{\rho V_\infty L}{\mu} = \frac{V_\infty L}{\nu}$$

Where:
- $\rho$ = fluid density (**kg/m³**)
- $V_\infty$ = free-stream velocity (**m/s**)
- $L$ = characteristic length (chord, wheelbase, body length) (**m**)
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity (**N·s/m²**)
- $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity (**m²/s**)

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "River and the Pebble"

Imagine a wide, slow river (low $Re$) and a narrow, fast stream (high $Re$). Drop the same pebble in both:
- In the **slow river**, the water flows smoothly around the pebble — laminar conditions.
- In the **fast stream**, the water tumbles chaotically downstream — turbulent conditions.

A **wind tunnel** is the engineer’s controlled "river," and **Reynolds number capability** tells you exactly how turbulent or smooth a stream you can generate. Just as you wouldn't measure a whirlpool in a bathtub to predict rapids in the Amazon, you wouldn't test a sports car at a low $Re$ and expect race-track fidelity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Takeaway:** Wind tunnel selection = matching the "size of your controlled river" to the "real river" your vehicle will actually experience. Reynolds number is the yardstick that compares them.

## 1.4 Why Reynolds Number Capability Dictates Selection

The aerodynamic coefficients ($C_D$, $C_L$, $C_M$) of bluff bodies like cars depend on the **boundary layer state**. A model at $Re = 2 \times 10^5$ (laminar separation) will produce a **completely different** drag coefficient than the same model at $Re = 5 \times 10^6$ (turbulent reattachment). Thus, achieving **Reynolds number similarity** between model and prototype is non-negotiable for credible vehicle testing.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Wind Tunnels by Reynolds Number Capability

Wind tunnels are classified primarily by their **maximum achievable Reynolds number per unit length**, often quoted as $Re/m$:

| Tunnel Class | Typical $Re/m$ Range | Test Section Mach | Application |
|:-------------|:---------------------|:------------------|:------------|
| Low-Speed Subsonic (Automotive) | $1 \times 10^5$ to $5 \times 10^6$ | $M \leq 0.3$ | Road cars, trucks, EV aero |
| High-Speed Subsonic | $5 \times 10^6$ to $5 \times 10^7$ | $0.3 < M \leq 0.7$ | Aircraft components, race cars |
| Transonic | $1 \times 10^7$ to $5 \times 10^7$ | $0.7 < M \leq 1.4$ | Aircraft wings, F1 front wings |
| Supersonic | $1 \times 10^6$ to $1 \times 10^7$ | $1.4 < M \leq 5$ | Missiles, jet intakes |
| Hypersonic | $1 \times 10^5$ to $1 \times 10^7$ | $M > 5$ | Re-entry vehicles |

> [!NOTE]
> For **automotive testing**, the relevant class is **Low-Speed Subsonic** ($M \leq 0.3$), where compressibility effects are negligible and $Re_L$ matching is the dominant similarity parameter.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|:-:|:--------|:------------|:--------------|
| 1 | $Re_L = \dfrac{\rho V_\infty L}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number (fundamental) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $Re_L = \dfrac{V_\infty L}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number (kinematic form) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $C_D = \dfrac{F_D}{\frac{1}{2} \rho V_\infty^2 A}$ | Drag coefficient definition | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\varepsilon_b = \dfrac{A_m}{A_{ts}}$ | Blockage ratio (model area / test-section area) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $C_{D,\text{corr}} = C_{D,\text{meas}} (1 - k \varepsilon_b^2)$ | Blockage correction (Maskell form) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $P_{\text{req}} = \dfrac{1}{2} \dot{m} V_\infty^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho A V_\infty^3$ | Wind tunnel drive power | **W (Watts)** |
| 7 | $Re_{L,\text{model}} = Re_{L,\text{full-scale}}$ (similarity) | Reynolds number matching criterion | dimensionless |
| 8 | $L_m = L_{fs} \cdot \beta$ | Geometric scale factor $\beta$ | **m** |
| 9 | $V_{m,\text{req}} = V_{fs} \cdot \beta \cdot \dfrac{\nu_{m}}{\nu_{fs}}$ | Model velocity for $Re$ matching | **m/s** |
| 10 | $T_u = \dfrac{\sqrt{\overline{u'^2}}}{U_\infty} \times 100\%$ | Turbulence intensity | **%** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Equation 9:** In wind tunnel testing, the model is *always* smaller than the prototype ($0.1 \leq \beta \leq 0.5$ typical). To keep $Re$ matched, the model velocity must be **higher** than full-scale by the inverse of the scale factor, OR the test medium must be denser (cryogenic/wind tunnel pressurization).

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Selection Logic

### Step 1: Define the Test Article
- Identify $L_{\text{char}}$ (chord, wheelbase, body length).
- Identify cruise/operating velocity $V_{\text{op}}$.

### Step 2: Compute Target Reynolds Number
- Calculate $Re_{L,\text{full-scale}}$ for the prototype at the design point.

### Step 3: Apply Scale Factor
- Choose a feasible geometric scale $\beta = L_m / L_{fs}$ (typically 0.25 to 0.4 for full-vehicle models, 0.4 to 0.6 for component tests).

### Step 4: Compute Required Test-Section Velocity
- Solve for $V_{m,\text{req}}$ using Equation 9.

### Step 5: Validate Against Tunnel Capability
- Check if $V_{m,\text{req}} \leq V_{\text{tunnel,max}}$ and blockage ratio $\varepsilon_b \leq 5\%$.

### Step 6: Verify Auxiliary Parameters
- Turbulence intensity ($T_u \leq 0.1\%$ for high-fidelity aero testing).
- Wall boundary layer correction devices in use.
- Pressure and temperature control (for cryogenic tunnels to boost $Re$).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **Formula 1**, the teams use 60% scale wind tunnel models at $V = 50$ m/s, achieving $Re$ matching for a 5 m F1 car at 80 m/s. The **Rolls-Royce Moving Floor Wind Tunnel** at Derby uses a 4.8 m test section with $T_u < 0.05\%$ for automotive-grade Reynolds numbers up to $Re = 4.5 \times 10^6$ per metre.

For **road car testing**, the MIRA Full-Scale Wind Tunnel (UK) operates at 150 mph with full-size vehicles — eliminating the need for scaling and offering direct $Re$ fidelity for $L \approx 4.5$ m cars ($Re \approx 5.4 \times 10^6$ at 100 km/h).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Reynolds Number Matching Condition for a Scaled Model

### Problem Setup
A full-scale passenger car has:
- Length $L_{fs} = 4.5$ m
- Operating speed $V_{fs} = 30$ m/s (≈ 108 km/h)
- Air at standard conditions: $\nu_{fs} = 1.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s

The test facility uses a geometric scale $\beta = 0.4$, and the test-section air is heated to reduce kinematic viscosity to $\nu_m = 1.0 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s.

**Find:** The required test-section velocity $V_m$ for Reynolds number similarity.

### Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Write the Reynolds number for the full-scale vehicle at the design point.

$$Re_{L,fs} = \frac{V_{fs} \cdot L_{fs}}{\nu_{fs}}$$

**Step 2:** Write the Reynolds number for the wind tunnel model.

$$Re_{L,m} = \frac{V_m \cdot L_m}{\nu_m}$$

**Step 3:** Express the model length in terms of the scale factor.

$$L_m = \beta \cdot L_{fs}$$

**Step 4:** Apply the similarity requirement $Re_{L,m} = Re_{L,fs}$ (the fundamental principle of dynamic similarity).

$$\frac{V_m \cdot (\beta \cdot L_{fs})}{\nu_m} = \frac{V_{fs} \cdot L_{fs}}{\nu_{fs}}$$

**Step 5:** Cancel $L_{fs}$ from both sides (since $L_{fs} \neq 0$).

$$\frac{V_m \cdot \beta}{\nu_m} = \frac{V_{fs}}{\nu_{fs}}$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $V_m$ algebraically.

$$V_m = \frac{V_{fs} \cdot \nu_m}{\beta \cdot \nu_{fs}}$$

**Step 7:** Substitute numerical values.

$$V_m = \frac{30 \times (1.0 \times 10^{-5})}{0.4 \times (1.5 \times 10^{-5})}$$

**Step 8:** Simplify the numerator.

$$\text{Numerator} = 30 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-5} = 3.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 9:** Simplify the denominator.

$$\text{Denominator} = 0.4 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-5} = 6.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 10:** Perform the final division.

$$V_m = \frac{3.0 \times 10^{-4}}{6.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 50 \text{ m/s}$$

**Conclusion:** The test section must be capable of **50 m/s** to achieve Reynolds number similarity. Since this is well within the typical automotive wind tunnel envelope ($M \leq 0.15$), the test is feasible.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Drive Power Required for a Subsonic Wind Tunnel

### Problem Setup
A closed-circuit subsonic wind tunnel has:
- Test section area $A_{ts} = 12$ m²
- Test section velocity $V_\infty = 50$ m/s
- Air density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³
- Cumulative loss coefficient (contraction, bends, screens) $K_{\text{total}} = 1.8$

**Find:** Total drive power required (ignoring fan efficiency for simplicity).

### Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Compute the mass flow rate through the test section.

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A_{ts} \cdot V_\infty$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values.

$$\dot{m} = 1.225 \times 12 \times 50 = 735 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the ideal (kinetic energy) power delivered to the airstream.

$$P_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{1}{2} \dot{m} V_\infty^2$$

**Step 4:** Substitute values.

$$P_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{1}{2} \times 735 \times (50)^2 = 0.5 \times 735 \times 2500$$

**Step 5:** Carry out multiplication.

$$P_{\text{ideal}} = 918{,}750 \text{ W} \approx 918.75 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6:** Compute total power accounting for circuit losses.

$$P_{\text{req}} = \frac{1}{2} \dot{m} V_\infty^2 \cdot (1 + K_{\text{total}})$$

**Step 7:** Substitute $K_{\text{total}} = 1.8$.

$$P_{\text{req}} = 918.75 \times (1 + 1.8) = 918.75 \times 2.8$$

**Step 8:** Final result.

$$P_{\text{req}} \approx 2572.5 \text{ kW} \approx 2.57 \text{ MW}$$

**Conclusion:** Approximately **2.57 MW** of electrical power is required to drive the tunnel, demonstrating why large Reynolds-number automotive tunnels (e.g., the MIRA facility with 5 MW drives) demand significant infrastructure.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Tunnel Selection Trade-Off

| Selection Criterion | Low-Speed (Open Circuit) | Closed Circuit | Cryogenic / Pressurized |
|:--------------------|:-------------------------|:---------------|:------------------------|
| **$Re$ Capability per metre** | $1 \times 10^5$–$10^6$ | $5 \times 10^6$–$2 \times 10^7$ | $10^7$–$10^9$ |
| **Cost (relative)** | Low (1×) | Medium (3–5×) | Very High (10–20×) |
| **Power Consumption** | Low | Medium | High |
| **Flow Quality ($T_u$)** | Moderate (0.3%) | Excellent (0.05%) | Excellent (0.02%) |
| **Test Article Size** | Small to medium | Large (full vehicle) | Component only |
| **Operating Cost** | Low | Medium | Very High (LN₂ or compressors) |
| **Best Suited For** | Student labs, motorcycles | F1, road car OEM testing | Aircraft model certification |

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Blockage Ratio Verification

### Problem Setup
A 1:5 scale vehicle model with frontal area $A_m = 0.4$ m² is to be tested in a wind tunnel with test-section cross-section $2.5 \text{ m} \times 4 \text{ m}$.

**Find:** The blockage ratio, and decide whether correction is required.

### Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Compute test-section area.

$$A_{ts} = 2.5 \times 4 = 10 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2:** Apply the blockage ratio formula.

$$\varepsilon_b = \frac{A_m}{A_{ts}}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values.

$$\varepsilon_b = \frac{0.4}{10} = 0.04 = 4\%$$

**Step 4:** Apply the standard KTU/SAE criterion: blockage ratio must be $\leq 5\%$.

$$\varepsilon_b = 4\% \leq 5\% \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5:** Conclusion: No blockage correction strictly required, but **Maskell's correction** is recommended for high-fidelity measurements:

$$C_{D,\text{corr}} = C_{D,\text{meas}} (1 - 3 \cdot \varepsilon_b)$$

**Step 6:** Compute correction factor.

$$(1 - 3 \times 0.04) = 1 - 0.12 = 0.88$$

Thus the corrected $C_D$ is 12% lower than the measured value.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Wind Tunnel Classification Flowchart (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Vehicle Wind Tunnel Selection] --> B0{Operating Mach Range}
    B0 -->|M less than 0.3| C0[Subsonic Automotive Class]
    B0 -->|M between 0.3 and 0.7| C1[High Speed Subsonic]
    B0 -->|M between 0.7 and 1.4| C2[Transonic]
    B0 -->|M greater than 1.4| C3[Supersonic Hypersonic]

    C0 --> D0{Re Circuit Type}
    D0 -->|Open Return| E0[Open Circuit Tunnel]
    D0 -->|Closed Loop| E1[Closed Circuit Tunnel]

    E0 --> F0[Low Re Capability]
    E0 --> F1[Low Cost]
    E0 --> F2[Educational Use]

    E1 --> G0[Boundary Layer Suction]
    E1 --> G1[Moving Floor Belt]
    E1 --> G2[Full Vehicle Testing]
    E1 --> G3[High Re Capability]

    G0 --> H0[Fidelity Enhancement]
    G1 --> H0
    G2 --> H0
    G3 --> H0

    H0 --> I0[Selected Tunnel for Vehicle Test]

    C1 --> J0[Aircraft Race Car Component]
    C2 --> J1[Aircraft Wing Transonic Tests]
    C3 --> J2[Missile Reentry Vehicles]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The flowchart demonstrates the **decision tree** a KTU 2024 Scheme student would apply when selecting a wind tunnel. Note the bifurcation at $M = 0.3$, which is the practical upper limit for automotive testing where compressibility remains negligible.

---

## 4.2 Reynolds Number Capability — Layered Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Prototype Vehicle] --> P2[Full Scale Re Calculation]
    P2 --> P3[Geometric Scale Beta Selection]
    P3 --> P4[Required Model Velocity Calculation]

    subgraph TEST_SECTION_BLOCK ["Test Section Physics"]
        T1[Test Section Velocity V]
        T2[Test Section Pressure P]
        T3[Test Section Temperature T]
        T4[Characteristic Length L]
        T1 --> R1[Re = rho V L / mu]
        T2 --> R1
        T3 --> R1
        T4 --> R1
    end

    P4 --> T1
    R1 --> V1[Re Capability Check]
    V1 --> MATCH{Re Match with Prototype}
    MATCH -->|Yes| OK[Selection Validated]
    MATCH -->|No| ADJ[Adjust V or P or T]
    ADJ --> R1
```

---

## 4.3 Selection Decision Block — Module 3 Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M0[Module 3 Wind Tunnel Selection] --> M1[Step 1 Define Test Article]
    M1 --> M2[Step 2 Compute Re Full Scale]
    M2 --> M3[Step 3 Choose Scale Factor Beta]
    M3 --> M4[Step 4 Calculate Required V Model]
    M4 --> M5[Step 5 Check Blockage Ratio]
    M5 --> M6[Step 6 Verify Turbulence Intensity]
    M6 --> M7[Step 7 Verify Drive Power]
    M7 --> M8[Step 8 Confirm Cost and Schedule]
    M8 --> FINAL[Final Tunnel Selection Decision]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Reynolds Number Capability of a wind tunnel. Why is it the most important selection criterion for automotive testing?
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Reynolds number capability** of a wind tunnel is the maximum non-dimensional ratio $Re_L = \rho V L / \mu$ that the facility can achieve for a model of characteristic length $L$ at free-stream velocity $V$ in air of density $\rho$ and dynamic viscosity $\mu$. It is the dominant selection criterion for automotive testing because bluff-body flows (cars, trucks) are highly sensitive to boundary layer transition and separation, which are governed by $Re$. A mismatch in $Re$ between model and full-scale vehicle leads to incorrect predictions of $C_D$, wake structure, and lift, making the test results non-representative of on-road performance. (3 marks)

### Question 2: List any three factors that govern wind tunnel selection for a vehicle testing programme.
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Reynolds number capability** — to ensure dynamic similarity of the boundary layer with the full-scale vehicle.
2. **Test section size / blockage ratio** — to accommodate the scaled model within a blockage ratio of $\leq 5\%$.
3. **Turbulence intensity ($T_u$)** — low $T_u$ ($\leq 0.1\%$) is essential for clean separation and reattachment studies.
(Any 3 of: Mach range, drive power, moving floor capability, pressure/temperature control, cost, type — open/closed circuit. Full 3 marks for any three valid points.)

---

## 5.2 Part B Question with Internal Choice (14 Marks)

### Question A: Wind Tunnel Selection and Reynolds Number Matching
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** A 1:4 scale model of a passenger car ($L_{fs} = 4.0$ m, $V_{op} = 25$ m/s) is to be tested in a closed-circuit subsonic wind tunnel. The kinematic viscosity of air at standard conditions is $\nu = 1.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s. Calculate:
1. The full-scale Reynolds number.
2. The required model test-section velocity for Reynolds number similarity (assuming same fluid).
3. The corresponding Mach number and verify if compressibility effects are negligible.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the criteria used for selecting a wind tunnel for automotive aerodynamic testing. Compare open-circuit and closed-circuit tunnels in terms of Reynolds number capability, cost, and flow quality.

---

### Complete Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks Step-by-Step Solution

**Given:**
- Scale factor $\beta = 1/4 = 0.25$
- $L_{fs} = 4.0$ m
- $V_{op} = V_{fs} = 25$ m/s
- $\nu = 1.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s (same in model and full-scale)

**Step 1: Compute full-scale Reynolds number (2 marks).**

$$Re_{fs} = \frac{V_{fs} \cdot L_{fs}}{\nu}$$

$$Re_{fs} = \frac{25 \times 4.0}{1.5 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{100}{1.5 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$Re_{fs} = 6.667 \times 10^6$$

**Step 2: Required model test-section velocity (3 marks).**

Using the matching condition $Re_{L,m} = Re_{L,fs}$:

$$\frac{V_m \cdot L_m}{\nu} = \frac{V_{fs} \cdot L_{fs}}{\nu}$$

$$V_m = V_{fs} \cdot \frac{L_{fs}}{L_m} = V_{fs} \cdot \frac{1}{\beta}$$

$$V_m = 25 \times \frac{1}{0.25} = 25 \times 4 = 100 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3: Mach number and compressibility check (2 marks).**

$$M = \frac{V_m}{a} = \frac{100}{343} = 0.291$$

Since $M < 0.3$, **compressibility effects are negligible**, and the test is valid in a low-speed subsonic tunnel. Density variation is less than 2%, so the incompressible assumption holds.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Wind Tunnel Selection Criteria (3.5 marks):**

1. **Reynolds number capability** matching with the full-scale vehicle for boundary layer similarity.
2. **Test section size** to maintain blockage ratio $\varepsilon_b \leq 5\%$.
3. **Flow quality** — low turbulence intensity ($T_u \leq 0.1\%$) and uniform flow.
4. **Moving floor or boundary layer suction** for ground-vehicle simulation.
5. **Drive power and operating cost** — feasibility for the testing programme.
6. **Instrumentation** — force balance, pressure scanners, PIV availability.

**Open-Circuit vs. Closed-Circuit Comparison (3.5 marks):**

| Criterion | Open-Circuit | Closed-Circuit |
|:----------|:-------------|:---------------|
| $Re$ capability | Lower ($Re/m \leq 10^6$) | Higher ($Re/m$ up to $2 \times 10^7$) |
| Cost | Lower (1×) | Higher (3–5×) |
| Power consumption | Lower | Higher |
| Flow quality ($T_u$) | 0.3–0.5% | 0.05–0.1% |
| Temperature stability | Variable (ambient) | Stable (closed loop) |
| Best for | Educational, motorcycles | OEM road car, F1, motorsport |

---

### Question B: Alternative Choice (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of Reynolds number capability of a wind tunnel. A subsonic wind tunnel has a test section of $1.5$ m $\times 1.5$ m. Air enters at $1.013 \times 10^5$ Pa and $300$ K. Calculate the maximum Reynolds number per metre if the maximum velocity achievable is $80$ m/s. Use $\mu = 1.846 \times 10^{-5}$ N·s/m² at 300 K.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 30% scale model of a truck ($L_{fs} = 12$ m, $V_{op} = 22$ m/s) is to be tested. If the wind tunnel can deliver a maximum of $90$ m/s, determine whether Reynolds number matching can be achieved. Assume $\nu = 1.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s for both model and full-scale.

---

### Complete Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Definition (2 marks):** Reynolds number capability is the maximum $Re$ achievable in a test section, defined as $Re_L = \rho V L / \mu$, where $L$ is the characteristic length (chord, body length) of the test article. It indicates the highest Reynolds number for which the tunnel can faithfully simulate the boundary layer behavior of the prototype.

**Given:**
- Test section: $1.5 \text{ m} \times 1.5 \text{ m}$
- $P = 1.013 \times 10^5$ Pa, $T = 300$ K
- $V_{\max} = 80$ m/s
- $\mu = 1.846 \times 10^{-5}$ N·s/m²

**Step 1: Compute air density using ideal gas law (2 marks).**

$$\rho = \frac{P}{R T} = \frac{1.013 \times 10^5}{287 \times 300}$$

$$\rho = \frac{1.013 \times 10^5}{86100} = 1.176 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 2: Compute Reynolds number per unit length (3 marks).**

$$\frac{Re}{L} = \frac{\rho V_{\max}}{\mu} = \frac{1.176 \times 80}{1.846 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$Re/L = \frac{94.08}{1.846 \times 10^{-5}} = 5.097 \times 10^6 \text{ per metre}$$

**Conclusion:** The maximum Reynolds number capability is approximately **$5.1 \times 10^6$ per metre**, suitable for automotive component and sub-scale full-vehicle testing.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- $\beta = 0.30$
- $L_{fs} = 12$ m
- $V_{fs} = 22$ m/s
- $V_{\max,\text{tunnel}} = 90$ m/s
- $\nu = 1.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s

**Step 1: Required model velocity for $Re$ matching (3 marks).**

$$V_{m,\text{req}} = \frac{V_{fs}}{\beta} = \frac{22}{0.30} = 73.33 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Compare with tunnel capability (2 marks).**

$$V_{m,\text{req}} = 73.33 \text{ m/s} < V_{\max} = 90 \text{ m/s} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 3: Validate the model (2 marks).**

Since the required velocity is within the tunnel envelope, **Reynolds number matching is achievable**. However, the test must be conducted at exactly $73.33$ m/s (or a $Re$-equivalent combination if temperature/pressure are adjusted). If $V_{m,\text{req}}$ had exceeded $90$ m/s, a cryogenic or pressurized tunnel would be needed, or a different $\beta$ would have to be chosen.
