**Conclusion:** The test is feasible in the available tunnel at the calculated $Re$ match condition.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — km/h to m/s conversion errors cost 1 mark typically. Always convert $V$ to **m/s** and $L$ to **m** before substituting in $Re$ formulas.
> 2. **Confusing $V_m = V_{fs} / \beta$ with $V_m = V_{fs} \times \beta$** — the model velocity is INVERSELY proportional to scale, not directly proportional. This is the most common 2-mark error.
> 3. **Ignoring the blockage ratio** — examiners frequently award 1 mark for stating that $\varepsilon_b \leq 5\%$ must be verified.
> 4. **Forgetting to verify the Mach number** — even in subsonic tunnels, $M < 0.3$ must be confirmed to justify the incompressible assumption.
> 5. **Using the wrong $\mu$ or $\nu$** — always state the temperature at which the property is evaluated; examiners deduct marks for not specifying the test condition.
> 6. **Not justifying tunnel type** — Part (b) answers that just list features without comparing open vs. closed circuits lose 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Reynolds number capability** is the single most important parameter governing wind tunnel selection for vehicle aerodynamic testing.
- The Reynolds number is defined as $Re_L = \rho V L / \mu = V L / \nu$ and must be **matched** between the model and the full-scale prototype for dynamic similarity.
- For a scaled model at the **same fluid**, the required model velocity is $V_m = V_{fs} / \beta$, where $\beta = L_m / L_{fs}$ is the geometric scale factor.
- For automotive testing, the relevant Mach range is **$M \leq 0.3$** (incompressible flow), making closed-circuit low-speed subsonic tunnels the industry standard.
- **Blockage ratio** $\varepsilon_b = A_m / A_{ts}$ must be **$\leq 5\%$**; otherwise, Maskell's correction $C_{D,\text{corr}} = C_{D,\text{meas}} (1 - k\varepsilon_b^2)$ must be applied.
- **Turbulence intensity** $T_u \leq 0.1\%$ is desirable for high-fidelity bluff-body testing; achieved using honeycombs and screens in the contraction.
- **Drive power** scales as $V^3$ — doubling test-section velocity requires **8× the power**, making high-$Re$ testing capital-intensive.
- **Closed-circuit tunnels** offer higher $Re$ capability, better flow quality, and temperature stability, but at 3–5× the capital cost of open-circuit tunnels.
- **Moving floor belts** and **boundary layer suction** are mandatory for ground-vehicle simulation to prevent unrealistically thick boundary layer growth under the model.
- **Cryogenic and pressurized tunnels** boost $\rho/\mu$ ratio, achieving $Re/m > 10^7$ for aircraft certification but are uneconomical for road car testing.
- Always verify three constraints before approving a test: (1) $V_m \leq V_{\text{tunnel,max}}$, (2) $\varepsilon_b \leq 5\%$, and (3) $M < 0.3$ for incompressibility.
- KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to **derive, calculate, and justify** — pure memorization of formulas is insufficient for full marks in Part B.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Model Requirements, Details, and Mounting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT724 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Model Requirements, Details, and Mounting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Vehicle Performance and Testing**, the term **"Model"** refers to a geometrically and dynamically scaled physical or mathematical replica of a full-scale vehicle (or a sub-system such as a body, chassis, engine, or wheel) that is used in laboratory or controlled environments to predict the performance characteristics of the prototype without subjecting the actual vehicle to expensive or destructive tests.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A **Vehicle Test Model** is a scaled representation of the prototype, designed to satisfy the three classical laws of **Similitude**:
> 1. **Geometric Similitude** (shape similarity)
> 2. **Kinematic Similitude** (motion similarity)
> 3. **Dynamic Similitude** (force similarity)

### 1.1.1 Sub-Categories of Models
| Model Type | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Aerodynamic Model** | Wind tunnel drag/lift testing | 1:4 scale car body in a wind tunnel |
| **Engine Test Model** | Dynamometer benchmarking | Single-cylinder research engine |
| **Suspension / Chassis Model** | Ride & handling studies | Quarter-car / half-car test rig |
| **Full Vehicle Scale Model** | Crash / NVH testing | Buck for frontal impact |
| **Mathematical (Computer) Model** | Simulation | MATLAB/Simulink vehicle dynamics |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Toy Car Principle"

Imagine you are a toy manufacturer wanting to predict how a real Ferrari will behave at **300 km/h** before mass-producing it. You obviously cannot crash-test the actual car 50 times. So you build a **small, accurately shaped, properly weighted, and correctly mounted Ferrari** in a wind tunnel. The airflow over this small replica must be **exactly proportional** to the airflow over the real car. This is the entire philosophy of **Model Requirements, Details, and Mounting**.

> [!NOTE]
> Just as a doll house must have the same proportions as the real house (sofas smaller but in the same relative position), a vehicle test model must obey the **laws of similitude** so that measurements on the model can be confidently scaled up to the real vehicle.

## 1.3 Why Model Mounting is a Critical "Hidden Variable"

Mounting is **not** just placing the model on a stand. The mount:
- Provides **strain isolation** (so the support doesn't disturb the airflow/load).
- Houses **force balances** (measuring drag, lift, side force, moments).
- Replicates **boundary conditions** (wheel rotation, ground plane simulation, suspension compliance).

> [!WARNING]
> A poorly mounted model introduces **blockage effects** and **support interference**, which are the two most common sources of error in KTU practical exam scenarios.

---

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard Atmospheric Conditions (ISA):** $P = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$, $T = 288.15 \text{ K}$, $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Dynamic Viscosity of Air:** $\mu = 1.789 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/(m·s)}$
- **Acceleration due to Gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- **Blockage Ratio Limit (Wind Tunnel):** $\varepsilon \le 0.10$ (10% rule)
- **Model Scale Ratios Used in Industry:** typically **1:4**, **1:5**, **1:1** (full scale)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Similitude

### Pillar 1 — Geometric Similitude
Every linear dimension of the model ($L_m$) is a constant multiple ($k_L$) of the prototype ($L_p$):

$$\frac{L_m}{L_p} = k_L = \text{constant}$$

This means **all** angles are preserved, and the **shape** is identical. The model is a *true* scaled copy.

> **Engineering "Why"**: Without geometric similarity, the streamlines around the model will not represent the streamlines around the prototype, making drag/lift data meaningless.

### Pillar 2 — Kinematic Similitude
The ratios of all velocities at corresponding points must be equal:

$$\frac{V_m}{V_p} = k_V = \text{constant}$$

This also implies that **time scales** consistently:

$$k_t = \frac{L_m / V_m}{L_p / V_p} = \frac{k_L}{k_V}$$

### Pillar 3 — Dynamic Similitude
The ratios of all forces (inertial, viscous, gravitational, elastic) acting at corresponding points must be equal:

$$\frac{F_{m,i}}{F_{p,i}} = \frac{F_{m,v}}{F_{p,v}} = \frac{F_{m,g}}{F_{p,g}} = \text{constant}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Dynamic similarity is achieved when the **dimensionless numbers** of the model match those of the prototype. The most important dimensionless groups for vehicle testing are listed in the Formula Sheet below.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Dimensionless Number | Formula | Physical Meaning | Required When |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Reynolds Number (Re)** | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu}$ | Inertial force / Viscous force | Aerodynamic drag testing |
| 2 | **Froude Number (Fr)** | $Fr = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{gL}}$ | Inertial force / Gravitational force | Hydrodynamic / wave-making tests |
| 3 | **Mach Number (Ma)** | $Ma = \dfrac{V}{c}$ | Vehicle speed / Local speed of sound | High-speed (>0.3 Ma) testing |
| 4 | **Strouhal Number (St)** | $St = \dfrac{f L}{V}$ | Unsteadiness frequency scaling | Vortex shedding, aero-acoustics |
| 5 | **Euler Number (Eu)** | $Eu = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\rho V^{2}}$ | Pressure force / Inertial force | Pressure distribution studies |
| 6 | **Weber Number (We)** | $We = \dfrac{\rho V^{2} L}{\sigma}$ | Inertial / Surface tension | Spray / fuel atomization tests |
| 7 | **Cauchy Number (Ca)** | $Ca = \dfrac{\rho V^{2}}{E}$ | Inertial / Elastic | Structural vibration testing |
| 8 | **Blockage Ratio** | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{A_m}{A_t}$ | Model area / Test-section area | Wind tunnel correction |
| 9 | **Drag Coefficient** | $C_D = \dfrac{2 F_D}{\rho V^{2} A}$ | Normalized drag | Final performance output |
| 10 | **Lift Coefficient** | $C_L = \dfrac{2 F_L}{\rho V^{2} A}$ | Normalized lift | Aero stability studies |

> **NOTE on Table Construction:** Vertical bars (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) have been intentionally avoided using $\vert$ LaTeX form to preserve markdown integrity. Use $\lvert x \rvert$ for absolute values.

---

## 2.3 Model Mounting — The Four Canonical Methods

The KTU syllabus recognizes **four** mounting philosophies, each suited to a different test objective:

### Method A — **Sting Mounting** (Tail-Sting)
- Model is supported from the **rear** by a slender strut ("sting").
- The sting is attached to a multi-component **force balance** behind the model.
- **Used for**: Aerodynamic force and moment measurements.

### Method B — **Strut Mounting** (Yawed-Strut / Side-Strut)
- Model is held by a strut from the **side, top, or bottom** of the test section.
- **Used for**: Pressure surveys, flow visualization, and yawed-flow studies.

### Method C — **Boundary-Layer Suction / Ground-Plane Mounting**
- Model sits on a **flat plate** simulating a moving road.
- Belt-drive moving ground planes (e.g., full-scale Pininfarina tunnel).
- **Used for**: Underbody flow, tire-road interaction, and ride-quality.

### Method D — **Dynamometer / Bed Mounting** (Engine & Driveline)
- Engine or vehicle is bolted to a test bed, with **dynamometer** absorbing power.
- **Used for**: Engine performance maps, BSFC, emissions, transmission tests.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Use of Vehicle Models |
|---|---|
| Formula 1 / Motorsport | 60% scale wind-tunnel models; CFD correlation |
| Passenger Cars (OEM) | Full-vehicle aerodynamic and NVH tests |
| Heavy Trucks / Buses | Drag reduction and fuel economy certification |
| Defense Vehicles | Blast, ballistics, and rough-terrain modeling |
| Two-Wheelers | Side-force stability and lean-angle testing |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Scaling Laws from Newton's Second Law

We begin with the inertial force on a fluid element:

$$F_i = m \cdot a = \rho L^{3} \cdot \frac{V^{2}}{L} = \rho V^{2} L^{2}$$

The viscous force per unit area is the **shear stress** $\tau = \mu \dfrac{V}{L}$ acting over area $L^{2}$:

$$F_v = \tau \cdot L^{2} = \mu \frac{V}{L} \cdot L^{2} = \mu V L$$

For dynamic similarity, the **ratio** $F_i / F_v$ must be equal for model and prototype:

$$\frac{F_i}{F_v} = \frac{\rho V^{2} L^{2}}{\mu V L} = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu}$$

**Textual Step-by-Step Explanation of the Logic:**

1. We started from Newton's second law: Force = mass × acceleration.
2. We substituted mass as density × volume: $m = \rho L^{3}$.
3. We substituted acceleration as velocity / length: $a = V/L$.
4. This gives us **inertial force** proportional to $\rho V^{2} L^{2}$.
5. For viscous force, we used Newton's law of viscosity: $\tau = \mu (du/dy) \approx \mu (V/L)$.
6. Multiplying stress by area $L^{2}$ gives viscous force $\propto \mu V L$.
7. Taking the ratio of these two forces yields the **Reynolds Number**:

$$\boxed{Re = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \text{same for model and prototype}}$$

8. Therefore, when we build a 1:4 scale model, we must adjust the wind-tunnel velocity such that:

$$\frac{V_m}{V_p} = \frac{1}{k_L} \cdot \frac{\mu_m / \rho_m}{\mu_p / \rho_p} = \frac{\nu_p}{k_L \, \nu_m}$$

where $\nu = \mu / \rho$ is the **kinematic viscosity**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Velocity Scaling for Geometrically Similar Aerodynamic Models

**Given:**
- Scale ratio $k_L = L_m / L_p = 1/4$
- Both at standard sea-level conditions (so $\nu_m = \nu_p$)
- Prototype speed $V_p = 30 \text{ m/s}$ (≈ 108 km/h)

**Find:** Required wind-tunnel test speed $V_m$.

**Step 1.** For Reynolds number matching:

$$Re_m = Re_p \implies \frac{V_m L_m}{\nu_m} = \frac{V_p L_p}{\nu_p}$$

**Step 2.** Rearrange to solve for $V_m$:

$$V_m = V_p \cdot \frac{L_p}{L_m} \cdot \frac{\nu_m}{\nu_p}$$

**Step 3.** Substitute $\nu_m = \nu_p$ (same air):

$$V_m = V_p \cdot \frac{L_p}{L_m} = V_p \cdot \frac{1}{k_L}$$

**Step 4.** Plug numerical values:

$$V_m = 30 \cdot \frac{1}{1/4} = 30 \times 4 = 120 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5.** Convert to km/h:

$$V_m = 120 \times 3.6 = 432 \text{ km/h}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When the wind tunnel cannot physically achieve the required velocity (e.g., $V_m > V_{tunnel,max}$), the test is conducted at **choked conditions** or **pressurized/refrigerated tunnels** — an examiner's favourite follow-up question.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Drag Force Scaling

The non-dimensional drag coefficient is conserved:

$$C_D = \frac{2 F_D}{\rho V^{2} A} = \text{constant}$$

Therefore, the prototype drag is:

$$F_{D,p} = F_{D,m} \cdot \left(\frac{\rho_p}{\rho_m}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{V_p}{V_m}\right)^{2} \cdot \left(\frac{A_p}{A_m}\right)$$

**Numerical Worked Example:**
A 1:5 model in a wind tunnel measures a drag of **$F_{D,m} = 8 \text{ N}$** at $V_m = 75 \text{ m/s}$. Find the drag on the prototype at $V_p = 25 \text{ m/s}$ (same air density, geometric similarity preserved).

**Step 1.** Compute the area scale: $A_p / A_m = k_L^{2} = (1/5)^{2} = 1/25$.

**Step 2.** Compute the velocity scale: $V_p / V_m = 25/75 = 1/3$.

**Step 3.** Compute the density ratio: $\rho_p / \rho_m = 1$.

**Step 4.** Plug into the drag equation:

$$F_{D,p} = 8 \cdot 1 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^{2} \cdot \frac{1}{25} = 8 \cdot \frac{1}{9} \cdot \frac{1}{25}$$

**Step 5.** Carry out multiplication:

$$F_{D,p} = \frac{8}{225} = 0.0356 \text{ N}$$

**Step 6.** Convert to more useful units:

$$F_{D,p} = 0.0356 \text{ N} \approx 35.6 \text{ mN}$$

> This small force illustrates why even **tiny surface imperfections** on the model (dust, scratches) can dominate the measured data — a major KTU exam pitfall.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Blockage Correction (Maskell Formula Simplified)

For a model whose frontal area is not negligible compared to the test section:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{A_m}{A_t} \quad \text{(blockage ratio)}$$

The **corrected drag coefficient** is:

$$C_{D,\text{corr}} = C_{D,\text{meas}} \cdot \left(1 - k_s \, \varepsilon\right)$$

where $k_s \approx 1.6$ to $2.0$ is the **solid-blockage constant** (for bluff bodies like cars).

**Example:** $\varepsilon = 0.08$, $k_s = 1.8$, $C_{D,\text{meas}} = 0.32$.

$$C_{D,\text{corr}} = 0.32 \cdot (1 - 1.8 \times 0.08) = 0.32 \cdot (1 - 0.144) = 0.32 \times 0.856$$

$$C_{D,\text{corr}} = 0.274$$

> This ~14% correction is precisely why KTU examiners emphasize blockage corrections in 14-mark questions.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Selection Guide for the Right Model & Mount

| Test Objective | Similitude Parameter | Recommended Model | Recommended Mount |
|---|---|---|---|
| Drag / Lift (low speed) | $Re$ | 1:4 to 1:5 hard model | Sting (tail) or top-strut |
| Cross-wind stability | $Re$, $St$ | Yaw-capable rig | Yawed-strut |
| High-speed aerodynamics | $Ma$ | Pressure-tolerant model | Sting with balance |
| Ride & Handling | $Fr$ equivalent | Quarter/half-car rig | Hydraulic shaker mount |
| Engine performance | Power/torque scale | Single-cyl. engine | Dynamometer bed |
| Crashworthiness | $Ca$, mass scale | Buck (full body) | Crash barrier / drop tower |
| Tire-road interaction | $Re$, slip ratio | Single-tire model | Flat-plate / moving belt |
| NVH & Vibration | $St$, $Ca$ | Modal-test body | Soft-bungee mount |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete Vehicle Test Model Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Prototype Vehicle] --> B{Define Test Objective}
    B --> C[Aerodynamic Test]
    B --> D[Engine Test]
    B --> E[Chassis / Ride Test]
    B --> F[Crash / Safety Test]
    C --> G[Scale Model 1:4]
    D --> H[Engine on Test Bed]
    E --> I[Half Car Rig]
    F --> J[Crash Buck]
    G --> K[Wind Tunnel Mounting]
    H --> L[Dynamometer Mounting]
    I --> M[Shaker Mounting]
    J --> N[Impact Barrier Mounting]
    K --> O[Sting or Strut]
    L --> P[Water Brake or Eddy Current Dyno]
    M --> Q[Hydraulic Actuator]
    N --> R[Accelerator Cart]
    O --> S[Force Balance]
    P --> T[Torque and Speed Sensors]
    Q --> U[Displacement and Force Transducers]
    R --> V[High Speed Cameras and Load Cells]
    S --> W[Data Acquisition System]
    T --> W
    U --> W
    V --> W
    W --> X[Similitude Check: Re Fr Ma St]
    X --> Y[Scale Up to Prototype Performance]
    Y --> Z[Final Vehicle Performance Report]
```

## 4.2 Schematic — Sting-Mounted Wind Tunnel Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Test Section Walls] --> B[Boundary Layer Suction Plate]
    B --> C[Scaled Vehicle Model]
    C --> D[Tail Sting Strut]
    D --> E[Five Component Force Balance]
    E --> F[Tunnel Turntable]
    F --> G[Yaw Angle Adjustment Mechanism]
    G --> H[Slip Rings and Signal Cabling]
    H --> I[Data Acquisition Unit]
    I --> J[Performance Coefficient Calculator]
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Dynamometer Engine Mounting

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Test Engine] --> B[Engine Mounting Cradle]
    B --> C[Thrust Bearing Block]
    C --> D[Coupling Flange]
    D --> E[Dynamometer Rotor]
    E --> F[Water Brake or Eddy Current Absorber]
    F --> G[Load Cell Assembly]
    G --> H[Tachometer and Speed Sensor]
    H --> I[Fuel Flow Meter]
    I --> J[Emissions Analyzer]
    J --> K[Engine Control Unit ECU]
    K --> L[Throttle and Ignition Actuators]
    L --> M[Central Data Logger]
    M --> N[Performance Map Generator]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Model Mounting Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Select Test Type] --> B{Aerodynamic?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Wall Interference Acceptable?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Use Sting Mount]
    C -- No --> E[Use Side Strut or Top Strut]
    B -- No --> F{Engine Test?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Dynamometer Mounting]
    F -- No --> H{Ride Comfort Test?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Shaker / Hydraulic Mount]
    H -- No --> J{Safety Crash Test?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Barrier or Sled Mounting]
    J -- No --> L[Re-evaluate Test Objective]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid block diagrams?** Complex physical drawings of wind-tunnel interiors, dynamometer rigs, and crash sleds are nearly impossible to render reliably in Mermaid. The block-functional architecture above preserves the **operational chain** (input → mounting → measurement → data → scaled output) which is the actual examinable content under KTU 2024 OBE patterns.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List the three laws of similitude that a vehicle test model must satisfy. State the dimensionless parameter associated with aerodynamic drag testing.**

**Model Answer:**

The three laws of similitude are:
1. **Geometric Similitude** — the model and prototype are identical in shape but different in size; all linear dimensions share a common scale factor $k_L$.
2. **Kinematic Similitude** — the ratios of velocities (and time) at corresponding points are equal; $V_m / V_p = k_V$.
3. **Dynamic Similitude** — the ratios of all forces (inertial, viscous, gravitational) at corresponding points are equal; governed by dimensionless numbers.

The dimensionless parameter for **aerodynamic drag testing** is the **Reynolds Number**:

$$Re = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu}$$

For the model to correctly represent the prototype, the test must be conducted at the **same Reynolds number** as the actual vehicle in service.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Sting Mounting and Strut Mounting of a vehicle model in a wind tunnel. Give one specific application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | **Sting Mounting** | **Strut Mounting** |
|---|---|---|
| Support location | Rear of the model | Side, top, or bottom of model |
| Interference | Minimal wake interference | Higher interference in flow field |
| Balance | Direct attachment to multi-axis balance | Requires external balance |
| Yaw range | Limited by sting length | Easy yaw adjustment |
| Application | Force and moment measurement of complete car | Pressure tap surveys, flow visualization |

**Application 1 (Sting):** Full-car aerodynamic drag and lift measurement at $0^{\circ}$ yaw.
**Application 2 (Strut):** Yawed-flow crosswind stability study at $\pm 15^{\circ}$ yaw.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Question Paper]**

> **(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **four mounting methods** used for vehicle test models in laboratories. List **one advantage** and **one disadvantage** of each. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** A 1:4 scale model of a car is tested in a wind tunnel at a speed of $V_m = 90 \text{ m/s}$. The drag force on the model is measured as $F_{D,m} = 22 \text{ N}$. If the prototype car travels at $V_p = 30 \text{ m/s}$ in still air, determine the drag force on the prototype. Assume the air density and viscosity are the same in both cases and the flow is Reynolds-number-similar. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

The four mounting methods are:

**1. Sting Mounting (Tail-Sting)**
- The model is supported by a slender sting attached to its rear.
- The sting connects to a force balance behind the model.
- *Advantage:* Very low wake interference; high accuracy for drag and pitching-moment measurement.
- *Disadvantage:* Sting limits the maximum yaw angle (typically $\pm 10^{\circ}$) due to structural clearance.

**2. Strut Mounting (Side / Top / Bottom)**
- A rigid strut supports the model from the side, ceiling, or floor of the test section.
- *Advantage:* Allows yaw sweeps up to $\pm 25^{\circ}$ and is mechanically simple.
- *Disadvantage:* The strut itself creates **support interference** that must be calibrated out.

**3. Ground-Plane / Moving-Belt Mounting**
- The model sits on a flat plate (or moving belt) that simulates the road.
- *Advantage:* Reproduces real underbody and tire-road flow.
- *Disadvantage:* Requires a moving-belt system (expensive) and boundary-layer suction to prevent flow separation at the leading edge.

**4. Dynamometer / Test-Bed Mounting**
- The engine (or whole vehicle) is mounted on a cradle coupled to an absorber.
- *Advantage:* Direct measurement of torque, BSFC, emissions at any speed/load.
- *Disadvantage:* Heat rejection and crankcase ventilation can affect the measurements.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the area scaling:**

$$\frac{A_p}{A_m} = k_L^{2} = \left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^{2} = \frac{1}{16}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the velocity scaling:**

$$\frac{V_p}{V_m} = \frac{30}{90} = \frac{1}{3}$$

**Step 3 — Density ratio:** $\rho_p / \rho_m = 1$ (same air).

**Step 4 — Apply the drag-scaling law:**

$$F_{D,p} = F_{D,m} \cdot \left(\frac{\rho_p}{\rho_m}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{V_p}{V_m}\right)^{2} \cdot \left(\frac{A_p}{A_m}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Substitute numbers:**

$$F_{D,p} = 22 \cdot 1 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^{2} \cdot \frac{1}{16}$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate:**

$$F_{D,p} = 22 \cdot \frac{1}{9} \cdot \frac{1}{16} = \frac{22}{144} = 0.1528 \text{ N}$$

**Step 7 — Express in N with three significant figures:**

$$\boxed{F_{D,p} \approx 0.153 \text{ N}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper]**

> **(a)** Explain the **requirements** that must be satisfied when designing a scaled vehicle model for **wind-tunnel testing**. Discuss **geometric, kinematic, and dynamic similarity** with relevant dimensionless numbers. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** A passenger-car prototype has a frontal area of $A_p = 2.2 \text{ m}^{2}$ and travels at $V_p = 90 \text{ km/h}$ through air of density $\rho = 1.22 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$. A 1:5 scale model is built for a wind tunnel with test-section area $A_t = 6.0 \text{ m}^{2}$. Compute: (i) the required wind-tunnel speed for Reynolds-number matching, (ii) the blockage ratio, and (iii) the corrected drag coefficient if the measured $C_{D,\text{meas}} = 0.305$ (assume $k_s = 1.8$). **[7 Marks]**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

The **model design requirements** fall into three categories:

**A. Geometric Requirements:**
- Constant linear scale ratio $k_L = L_m / L_p$.
- Faithful reproduction of all major body features (wheels, mirrors, underbody).
- Smooth surface finish (typically $R_a < 0.0005 \times L_m$).
- **Boundary-layer transition** fixed by **turbulator dots** or **wires** at $5\%$ chord from the leading edge.

**B. Kinematic Requirements:**
- Velocity scale $k_V$ chosen such that the relevant dimensionless number (Re, Ma) is matched.
- Rotating components (wheels) must rotate at the correct **slip-free** condition:

$$\frac{\omega_m R_m}{V_m} = \frac{\omega_p R_p}{V_p}$$

- Flow angularity (yaw) must be reproducible.

**C. Dynamic Requirements:**
- All force ratios must be equal. Practically, this is achieved by matching the dominant dimensionless group:
  - **Reynolds Number** ($Re$) for low-speed incompressible flow.
  - **Mach Number** ($Ma$) for high-speed compressible flow.
  - **Froude Number** ($Fr$) only when free-surface effects matter (rare for cars).

For a typical car in the speed range 30–180 km/h, the Mach number is below 0.15, so **incompressible Re-matching** is sufficient.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Given:**
- $A_p = 2.2 \text{ m}^{2}$, $V_p = 90 \text{ km/h} = 25 \text{ m/s}$, $\rho = 1.22 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$
- Scale $k_L = 1/5$, $A_t = 6.0 \text{ m}^{2}$, $C_{D,\text{meas}} = 0.305$, $k_s = 1.8$

**(i) Required tunnel speed $V_m$:**

For Reynolds-number matching with same air ($\nu_m = \nu_p$):

$$V_m = V_p \cdot \frac{1}{k_L} = 25 \cdot 5 = 125 \text{ m/s}$$

$$V_m = 125 \times 3.6 = \boxed{450 \text{ km/h}}$$

> *This is why most low-speed automotive tunnels use pressurized air or larger scales to bring $V_m$ down to realistic 60–80 m/s.*

**(ii) Blockage ratio $\varepsilon$:**

The model frontal area is:

$$A_m = A_p \cdot k_L^{2} = 2.2 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{5}\right)^{2} = \frac{2.2}{25} = 0.088 \text{ m}^{2}$$

$$\varepsilon = \frac{A_m}{A_t} = \frac{0.088}{6.0} = 0.01467$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon \approx 1.47\%}$$

> Since $\varepsilon < 10\%$, the **10% blockage rule** is satisfied; no major correction is mandated, but a small correction is still applied for accuracy.

**(iii) Corrected drag coefficient:**

$$C_{D,\text{corr}} = C_{D,\text{meas}} \cdot (1 - k_s \cdot \varepsilon)$$

$$C_{D,\text{corr}} = 0.305 \cdot (1 - 1.8 \times 0.01467)$$

$$C_{D,\text{corr}} = 0.305 \cdot (1 - 0.0264) = 0.305 \times 0.9736$$

$$\boxed{C_{D,\text{corr}} \approx 0.297}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Forgetting to convert $V_p$ from **km/h to m/s** before substitution. Examiners will deduct **1 mark per wrong unit** in numerical problems.
> 2. **Confusing $k_L$ with $L_m/L_p$:** Some students compute $L_p/L_m$ instead of $L_m/L_p$, leading to a **factor-of-25 error** in drag calculations.
> 3. **Ignoring Blockage Correction:** Reporting $C_{D,\text{meas}}$ as the final value. Always apply the **Maskell** or **standard wall-correction** for $\varepsilon > 0.01$.
> 4. **Forgetting Transition Fixing:** In 7-mark theory questions, failing to mention **turbulator dots/wires** for boundary-layer transition.
> 5. **Mixing Strut and Sting Mounting:** In 7-mark descriptive answers, students often swap the **advantages** of the two; remember — *sting* minimizes interference, *strut* maximizes yaw.
> 6. **Skipping the $C_D$ Formula Derivation:** A 14-mark question on "model details" almost always includes a $C_D$ sub-part; memorize the formula **before** entering the exam hall.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **Vehicle Test Model** is a scaled replica satisfying **Geometric, Kinematic, and Dynamic** similitude.
- The **Reynolds Number** is the dominant dimensionless group for subsonic vehicle aerodynamics.
- The **blockage ratio** must remain **below 10%** for reliable wind-tunnel data; use the **Maskell correction** for $\varepsilon > 0.01$.
- **Sting mounting** is preferred for force/moment accuracy; **strut mounting** is preferred for yaw sweeps.
- **Transition fixing** (turbulators) is mandatory for all wind-tunnel vehicle models to avoid laminar-separation mismatch with the turbulent prototype boundary layer.
- **Rotating wheels** must obey the slip-free condition $\omega R = V$.
- **Drag force scaling** law: $F_{D,p} = F_{D,m} \cdot (\rho_p / \rho_m) \cdot (V_p / V_m)^{2} \cdot (A_p / A_m)$.
- **Engine test beds** require **dynamometer mounting** with proper thrust-bearing alignment to avoid parasitic losses.
- **Mathematical models** (Simulink, GT-Suite, AVL CRUISE) supplement physical models and are examinable under the "mathematical model" sub-category of the KTU 2024 syllabus.
- The **10% rule**, **Re-matching**, and **$C_D$ formula** are the **top three high-frequency KTU questions** on this topic.
- Always **state the constants** (e.g., $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$, $\mu = 1.789 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/ms}$) explicitly in numerical answers for **half-mark grace** in valuation.
- For 14-mark problems, **structured step-by-step solutions with bolded final answers** consistently fetch **12–14 marks** under KTU's "step-marking" valuation policy.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Test Procedure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT724 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Test Procedure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Test Procedure (Vehicle Performance & Testing):** A *Test Procedure* is a rigorously standardized, sequential, and documented series of measurements, observations, and operational cycles executed on a vehicle (or its sub-assemblies) under prescribed ambient conditions, instrumentation, and operator protocols, in order to objectively quantify its performance, durability, emissions, fuel economy, and safety characteristics as per a governing code (e.g., AIS, IS, ISO, SAE, ECE).

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context (OEMUT724 — Module 3), the *Test Procedure* topic encompasses the **complete methodological framework** for conducting:
- Road tests (field trials on proving grounds or public roads)
- Chassis dynamometer tests (laboratory simulations)
- Type-approval and homologation tests
- Endurance / durability tests
- Emission, fuel-consumption, and brake test sequences

Every test procedure is governed by a **pyramid of standards** — International (ISO/ECE/SAE) → National (AIS/BIS) → OEM-specific — and is dictated by the fundamental requirement of **reproducibility**, *i.e.*, the same test performed on the same vehicle by two different laboratories must yield the same result within a defined tolerance band.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Recipe Book" of Vehicle Testing**

Think of a *Test Procedure* exactly like a **bakery recipe**:

- If a bakery in Kochi and a bakery in London both bake a "sponge cake," the recipe (procedure) is *identical* — same oven temperature (**standard test temperature: 25 °C ambient, unless otherwise specified**), same ingredient ratios, same mixing time.
- The "ingredients" are the **vehicle parameters** (speed, load, fuel, gradient).
- The "oven" is the **test track or chassis dynamometer**.
- The "stopwatch and weighing scale" are the **data acquisition system (DAQ) and calibrated instruments**.

Without the recipe (procedure), one bakery might bake at 180 °C for 25 min while another uses 200 °C for 30 min — and the cakes would be different. *Test procedures are the recipes that make vehicle performance measurements globally comparable.*

> **Key principle:** A measurement that is not repeatable is not a measurement — it is a *gu*.

### 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Proving Ground** | A dedicated, instrumented test track facility (e.g., NATRIP) used for repeatable vehicle testing. |
| **Type Approval** | Certification that a vehicle type meets all regulatory standards before mass production. |
| **Homologation** | The legal process of certifying that a vehicle conforms to the prescribed standards. |
| **Chassis Dynamometer (Dyno)** | A roller-based bench that simulates road load on a stationary vehicle. |
| **Conditioning Cycle** | A preparatory running cycle to bring tires, brakes, and fluids to operational temperature. |
| **Coasting Test** | A test where the vehicle runs in neutral (or with clutch depressed) to measure rolling resistance and driveline friction. |
| **Break-in / Running-in** | Initial running of a new vehicle (typically 1,000–3,000 km) before official tests. |
| **AIS** | Automotive Industry Standards (India) — issued by ARAI under CMVR. |
| **CMVR** | Central Motor Vehicle Rules — the legal backbone of Indian automotive testing. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Test Conditions (CMVR / AIS-008 / AIS-009 ambient):** Ambient temperature = **20 °C to 30 °C**, Atmospheric pressure = **91 kPa to 104 kPa**, Relative humidity = **30 % to 70 %**, Wind speed < **3 m/s**, Track gradient for straight runs ≤ **0.5 %**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Vehicle Test Procedures

Test procedures are organized as a strict hierarchy. Understanding this hierarchy is **essential** for the KTU module — it directly maps to questions on "classify the test procedure for X."

**Level 1 — By Test Venue**

1. **Road (Field) Tests**
   - Conducted on a proving ground or designated highway.
   - Examples: Maximum speed, fuel-consumption road test, gradeability, brake stopping-distance, coast-down.
   - Pros: Real-world load conditions, including wind, gradient, road roughness.
   - Cons: Weather-dependent, lower repeatability.

2. **Laboratory (Bench) Tests**
   - Conducted on a **chassis dynamometer** or component test rigs.
   - Examples: Engine bench, transmission rig, brake inertia dynamometer, emission CVS (Constant Volume Sampling).
   - Pros: Highest repeatability, controlled ambient, year-round testing.
   - Cons: Cannot perfectly replicate road aerodynamic and gradient loads.

**Level 2 — By Purpose (Functional Domain)**

| Functional Domain | Typical Procedure Reference | KTU-relevant Tests |
|---|---|---|
| **Performance** | AIS-008, SAE J1346 | Max. speed, acceleration 0–100 km/h, hill-climb |
| **Fuel Economy** | AIS-009 / ISO 6460 / IDC (Indian Driving Cycle) | Multi-cycle fuel-consumption test |
| **Emissions** | AIS-008 (Bharat Stage VI), ECE R83, R49 | THC, CO, NOx, PM, PN (particulate number) |
| **Braking** | AIS-090, ECE R13, ECE R78 | Type-0, Type-I, Type-II, Type-III brake tests |
| **Durability / Endurance** | OEM-specific, ECE R84 | Sustained high-speed, rough-road durability |
| **Safety / Crash** | AIS-098, ECE R94, R95 | Frontal impact, side impact |
| **NVH** | ISO 362, ISO 5130 | Drive-by noise, interior noise |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The four most-frequently asked procedure classifications in OEMUT724 are — (i) Performance, (ii) Fuel Economy, (iii) Emission, and (iv) Brake. Memorize the **AIS code** for each.

**Level 3 — By Vehicle State**

- **Cold-start tests** — Engine and lubricants at ambient temperature (no warm-up).
- **Hot-start tests** — After a defined warm-up phase.
- **Loaded vs. Un-loaded** — Laden mass (max. permissible) vs. Kerb mass.
- **Single-event vs. Cycle** — One measurement (e.g., max. speed) vs. a sequence (e.g., IDC, NEDC, WLTC).

### 2.2 The Master Sequence of a Generic Test Procedure

Every test procedure — whether a road test or a dyno test — follows the same **5-phase master sequence**. This is the "skeleton" the examiner expects you to know.

1. **Pre-Test Conditioning (Vehicle Preparation)**
   - Vehicle mass: laden or kerb, as specified.
   - Tyre pressure: cold inflation pressure recommended by OEM, **± 10 kPa** tolerance.
   - Fuel: reference fuel (e.g., IS 2796 grade) or commercial fuel as per procedure.
   - Fluids: filled to maximum marks.
   - Running-in: at least **1,500 km** for a new vehicle.
   - Driver / test mass: **75 kg ± 5 kg** driver plus instrumentation.

2. **Environmental Conditioning (Site Preparation)**
   - Ambient temperature, pressure, humidity, wind — all logged at **≥ 30 min** before test.
   - Track condition: dry, clean, free of standing water.
   - Photographic / data-logger calibration.

3. **Vehicle Warm-up**
   - Idle 60 s, then steady 60 km/h for 10 min, OR a 15-min engine warm-up at ½ rated speed.
   - Tire temperature typically 25 °C–40 °C, oil sump ≥ 80 °C.

4. **Measurement Run**
   - The actual data collection event(s).
   - DAQ sample rate: typically **10 Hz to 100 Hz** depending on signal.

5. **Post-Test Data Reduction & Reporting**
   - Correct measurements to **standard conditions** (STP: 25 °C, 101.325 kPa, dry air).
   - Compute derived quantities (mean, standard deviation, confidence interval).
   - Prepare a signed test report.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the **core computational building blocks** of any test procedure. They are the only formulae KTU examiners consistently test for Module 3.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Power absorbed by dynamometer** | $P_a = F \cdot v$ | W | $F$ = roller force, $v$ = roller peripheral speed |
| 2 | **Power correction to STP** | $P_{STP} = P_{meas} \cdot \dfrac{101.325}{p} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{T_{ref}}{T_{meas}}}$ | W | $p$ in kPa, $T$ in K |
| 3 | **Road-load horsepower (dyne equation)** | $P = \dfrac{(C_d A\,\rho\,v^3 + C_{rr}\,m\,g\,v + (m + m_{rot})\,a\cdot v)}{1000\,\eta_t}$ | kW | Three-term vehicle dynamics equation |
| 4 | **Coasting deceleration** | $a_c = \dfrac{v_i - v_f}{t}$ | m/s² | For rolling-resistance determination |
| 5 | **Rolling-resistance coefficient** | $C_{rr} = \dfrac{a_c}{g\cdot \cos\theta} - \dfrac{\rho\,C_d A\,v^2}{2 m g \cos\theta}$ | — | From coast-down test |
| 6 | **Air-density correction** | $\rho_a = \dfrac{p}{R_s\,T} \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{0.378\,p_v}{p}\right)$ | kg/m³ | $R_s = 287.05$ J/(kg·K) |
| 7 | **Equivalent inertial mass (dyno simulation)** | $I_{eq} = m_{vehicle} \cdot r_{roller}^2 + I_{roller}$ | kg·m² | For chassis dyno |
| 8 | **Stopping distance from energy** | $s = \dfrac{v^2}{2\,(a + g\,\sin\theta)}$ | m | Cold/hot brake test |
| 9 | **Mean effective brake deceleration (MEBD)** | $MEBD = \dfrac{v^2}{2\,s_{avg}}$ | m/s² | For ECE R78 / AIS-090 |
| 10 | **Fuel consumption (volume method)** | $FC = \dfrac{V_{fuel}}{d}\cdot 100$ | L/100 km | $V_{fuel}$ in mL, $d$ in km |
| 11 | **Fuel consumption (mass method)** | $FC = \dfrac{m_{fuel}}{\rho_{fuel} \cdot d}\cdot 100$ | L/100 km | More accurate, gravimetric |
| 12 | **Fuel consumption (carbon balance)** | $FC = \dfrac{0.866\,[\text{CO}] + 0.429\,[\text{CO}_2] + 0.273\,[\text{HC}]}{\rho_f \cdot d} \cdot 100$ | L/100 km | Used when no fuel measurement is possible |
| 13 | **Cycle-weighted fuel consumption** | $FC_{cycle} = \dfrac{\sum w_i \, FC_i}{\sum w_i}$ | L/100 km | For IDC / NEDC / WLTC |
| 14 | **Specific emission (g/km)** | $e = \dfrac{m_{pollutant}}{d}$ | g/km | $m_{pollutant}}$ from CVS bag |
| 15 | **CVS dilution mass** | $\dot{m}_{exh} = \rho_{air} \cdot \dot{V}_{CVS}$ | kg/s | For emission measurement |

> [!WARNING]
> **Absolute-value pitfall in tables:** When you write $\vert a \cdot v \vert$ or $\vert x \vert$ inside a markdown table row, **never use the literal `|` character** — it will break the table parser. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead. (e.g., $\vert a \cdot v \vert$, not $|a \cdot v|$.)

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why test procedures matter in industry:**
> - They form the **legal basis** for type-approval (a vehicle cannot be sold in India without passing the AIS-008 emission + AIS-009 fuel-economy + AIS-090 brake test bundle).
> - They enable **objective OEM-vs-OEM comparison** — a customer can compare the *IDC fuel economy* of two cars in confidence.
> - They support **warranty arbitration** — a disputed driveline failure can be resolved by re-running the prescribed durability cycle and checking the part-life target.
> - They drive **R&D closed-loop calibration** — engine and EV controllers are validated against standard cycles (WLTC, IDC) before sign-off.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Road-Load Power Equation (Three-Term)

This is the single most important derivation in Module 3. It underpins **every** performance and fuel-economy test procedure.

**Starting principle:** The instantaneous power required at the wheels of a vehicle moving at velocity $v$ on a flat road is the sum of three terms — aerodynamic drag, rolling resistance, and inertia.

**Step 1 — Aerodynamic drag force.**
Newtonian fluid resistance: $F_a = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_d\,A\,v^2$.

**Step 2 — Rolling resistance force.**
Coulomb-friction model on tires: $F_{rr} = C_{rr}\,m\,g\,\cos\theta$. (For small $\theta$, $\cos\theta \approx 1$.)

**Step 3 — Inertial force during acceleration.**
$F_i = (m + m_{rot})\,a$, where $m_{rot}$ is the equivalent rotational mass of rotating driveline parts. Typical value: $m_{rot} \approx 0.05\,m$ for ICE passenger cars, $\approx 0.10\,m$ for EVs.

**Step 4 — Hill-climb force** (on a road of slope $\theta$).
$F_{hc} = m\,g\,\sin\theta$.

**Step 5 — Total tractive force at the wheels.**
$$F_t = F_a + F_{rr} + F_i + F_{hc} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2 + C_{rr} m g \cos\theta + (m + m_{rot})a + m g \sin\theta$$

**Step 6 — Power at the wheels** is force times velocity:
$$P_{wheel} = F_t \cdot v$$

**Step 7 — Engine output power**, accounting for drivetrain efficiency $\eta_t$:
$$P_{engine} = \dfrac{P_{wheel}}{\eta_t} = \dfrac{1}{\eta_t}\left[\tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^3 + C_{rr}\,m\,g\,v\,\cos\theta + (m + m_{rot})\,a\,v + m\,g\,v\,\sin\theta\right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The $v^3$ dependence of the aerodynamic term is *why* fuel economy degrades so sharply above 80 km/h — power demand grows cubically with speed, and fuel-flow scales roughly linearly with power.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Determination of $C_d A$ and $C_{rr}$ from a Coast-Down Test

The coast-down test is a *passive* test: the vehicle is allowed to roll in neutral, and its deceleration $a_c(v)$ is measured as a function of speed.

**Step 1 — Free-body in coasting (no engine torque, no brake).**
$$0 = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2 + C_{rr} m g + m a_c$$
(assuming flat road, $\theta = 0$)

**Step 2 — Solve for $a_c$ as a function of $v$:**
$$a_c(v) = -\dfrac{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A}{m}\,v^2 - C_{rr}\,g$$

**Step 3 — Recognize this as a quadratic polynomial in $v$:**
$$a_c(v) = A_2 v^2 + A_0$$
where $A_2 = -\dfrac{\rho\,C_d\,A}{2\,m}$ and $A_0 = -C_{rr}\,g$.

**Step 4 — Experimental procedure.**
Drive the vehicle to a speed higher than the maximum test speed (e.g., 130 km/h), put it in neutral, and record the time–speed trace. Fit a 2nd-order polynomial to $v(t)$ or to $a_c(v)$ data over the range of interest.

**Step 5 — Extract the parameters:**
$$C_d A = -\dfrac{2\,m\,A_2}{\rho}, \qquad C_{rr} = -\dfrac{A_0}{g}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trap:** Many students forget the **negative sign** when extracting $C_d A$ and $C_{rr}$. Because $A_2$ and $A_0$ are both negative, the formulae are correctly signed only when the *negative* sign is preserved.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Chassis-Dynamometer Road-Load Setting

The chassis dyno must electronically simulate the road load, since the vehicle is stationary.

**Step 1 — On the road, the absorbing power is:**
$$P_{road} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^3 + C_{rr}\,m\,g\,v$$

**Step 2 — The dyno absorbs a force $F_d$ on a roller of radius $r$, spinning at peripheral speed $v = \omega\,r$.**
$$P_{dyno} = F_d\,v = F_d\,\omega\,r$$

**Step 3 — Equate for the same vehicle and speed:**
$$F_d = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho\,C_d\,A\,v^2 + C_{rr}\,m\,g$$

**Step 4 — This is a quadratic in $v$; modern dynos use a 3-coefficient "coefficient of drag" expression:**
$$F_d = A + B\,v + C\,v^2$$
where $A$ captures tire + driveline friction, $B$ captures speed-proportional friction, and $C$ captures aerodynamic drag.

**Step 5 — Coefficients are set by **running a coast-down on the dyno** (matching the road coast-down) at three speeds and solving a linear system.**

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Procedure → Measured Quantity → Governing Equation → AIS Reference

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is your **rapid-revision weapon** for the 14-mark questions. Memorize at least 4 of these for the ESE.

| Procedure | What is Measured | Governing Equation | AIS / Standard |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Coast-down** | $C_d A$ and $C_{rr}$ | $a_c(v) = -\frac{\rho C_d A}{2m}v^2 - C_{rr}g$ | AIS-008 (Annex) |
| **Max. speed (sustained)** | $v_{max}$ where $P_{avail} = P_{road}$ | $\frac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v_{max}^3 + C_{rr} m g v_{max} = P_{rated} \eta_t$ | OEM / AIS-008 |
| **Acceleration time** | $t$ from $v_1$ to $v_2$ | $\int_{v_1}^{v_2}\dfrac{(m+m_{rot})\,\delta v}{\tfrac{P_{eng}\eta_t}{v} - \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2 - C_{rr}mg - mg\sin\theta}$ | OEM / SAE J1346 |
| **Brake stopping distance** | $s$ from $v_0$ to $0$ | $s = \dfrac{v_0^2}{2\,(a+g\sin\theta)}$ | AIS-090 / ECE R13 |
| **Gradeability (sustained)** | max $\theta$ in $1^{st}$ gear at constant $v$ | $\sin\theta_{max} = \dfrac{\tfrac{P\eta_t}{v} - \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2 - C_{rr}mg}{mg}$ | OEM |
| **Fuel economy (cycle)** | $FC$ over IDC / WLTC | $FC = \dfrac{m_{fuel}}{\rho_{fuel}\,d} \times 100$ | AIS-009 / IS 14681 |
| **Emission (g/km)** | Mass of CO/HC/NO$_x$/PM | $e_i = \dfrac{m_i}{d}$ from CVS | AIS-008 / BS-VI |
| **Nvh — drive-by noise** | $L_{A,max}$ at 7.5 m | $L_{Aeq}$ over pass-by | AIS-018 / ISO 362 |

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (typical KTU style)

> **Q:** A 1,200 kg passenger car with $C_d A = 0.7$ m², $C_{rr} = 0.012$, $\eta_t = 0.92$, on a flat road, is tested for maximum speed. The engine delivers 75 kW at rated power. Air density $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³. Find $v_{max}$.

**Solution:**

Equate available power to road-load power:
$$\frac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v_{max}^3 + C_{rr} m g v_{max} = P_{rated}\,\eta_t$$

Substitute:
$$\frac{1}{2}(1.2)(0.7)v^3 + (0.012)(1200)(9.81)v = 75{,}000 \times 0.92$$

$$0.42\,v^3 + 141.264\,v = 69{,}000$$

Solve by iteration / Newton-Raphson. Trial $v = 50$ m/s (180 km/h):

- $0.42(125{,}000) + 141.264(50) = 52{,}500 + 7{,}063 = 59{,}563$ W (too low)
- Trial $v = 53$ m/s (190.8 km/h):
  - $0.42(148{,}877) + 141.264(53) = 62{,}528 + 7{,}487 = 70{,}015$ W ≈ 69,000 W ✓

$$\boxed{v_{max} \approx 53\ \text{m/s} \approx 191\ \text{km/h}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in the exam:** The examiner may give you any 4 of the 6 parameters and ask you to solve for the remaining 2. Practice solving this cubic — you may have to use the trial-and-error / Newton-Raphson method since the equation is not analytically invertible in closed form.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Master Test Procedure Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Define Test Objective] --> A1[Identify Governing Standard e.g. AIS 008 / 009 / 090]
    A1 --> A2[Select Test Venue Proving Ground vs Chassis Dyno]
    A2 --> A3[Pre Test Conditioning Vehicle mass Tyre pressure Fluids Running in]
    A3 --> A4[Environmental Conditioning Ambient T P RH Wind]
    A4 --> A5[Driver and DAQ Calibration Driver mass 75 kg plus 5 kg DAQ sample rate 10 to 100 Hz]
    A5 --> A6[Vehicle Warm up Idle 60 s then 60 km per h for 10 min]
    A6 --> A7[Measurement Run Apply procedure per AIS code]
    A7 --> A8[Post Test Data Reduction Correct to STP 25 deg C 101.325 kPa]
    A8 --> A9{Result within AIS Tolerance}
    A9 -- Yes --> A10[Pass Issue Test Report]
    A9 -- No --> A11[Fail Root Cause Analysis Iterate]
    A11 --> A3
    A10 --> A12[End Test]
    A11 --> A12
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Chassis-Dynamometer Test Cell

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VEH[Vehicle Under Test] --> ROLL[Roller Assembly Diameter 1.22 m or 1.5 m]
    ROLL --> TRAN[Torque Transducer Strain Gauge Type]
    TRAN --> BRK[Eddy Current or AC Eddy Current Absorber]
    BRK --> CTRL[Cell Controller with Road Load Equation]
    CTRL --> DAQ[Data Acquisition 10 Hz to 100 Hz]
    CTRL --> FAN[Speed Follower Fan for Vehicle Cooling]
    CTRL --> EXH[Exhaust Extraction CVS Tunnel]
    DAQ --> PROC[Data Processing and STP Correction]
    PROC --> REP[Automated Test Report Generation]
    EXH --> EMS[Emission Analyser CO HC NOx PM PN]
    EMS --> PROC
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Procedure-Classification Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[What is the question asking] --> S1{Is the vehicle stationary or moving}
    S1 -- Stationary --> S2[Chassis Dyno Tests]
    S2 --> S3{Which quantity}
    S3 -- Power and Torque --> S4[Engine Bench or Hub Dyno]
    S3 -- Emissions --> S5[CVS Emission Test BS VI]
    S3 -- Fuel Map --> S6[Engine Map Test 5000 cells]
    S1 -- Moving --> S7[Road Tests]
    S7 --> S8{Which quantity}
    S8 -- Aerodynamic and Rolling --> S9[Coast Down Test]
    S8 -- Braking --> S10[Stopping Distance Test AIS 090]
    S8 -- Top Speed --> S11[Maximum Speed Test]
    S8 -- Acceleration --> S12[0 to 100 km per h and Elapsed Time Test]
    S8 -- Economy --> S13[Fuel Consumption Test IDC or WLTC]
    S8 -- Hill Climb --> S14[Gradeability Test]
    S8 -- Noise --> S15[Drive By Noise Test AIS 018]
```

### 4.4 Schematic — Measurement Chain for a Brake Stopping-Distance Test

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DRV[Driver] --> BRKP[Brake Pedal Force Transducer]
    DRV --> STR[Steering Angle Sensor]
    VEH2[Test Vehicle laden mass] --> WHEEL[Wheel Speed Sensor and GPS Speed]
    BRKP --> DAQ2[Central DAQ 100 Hz]
    STR --> DAQ2
    WHEEL --> DAQ2
    DAQ2 --> COMP[Computation of Stopping Distance and Mean Fully Developed Deceleration]
    COMP --> CMP[Compare with AIS 090 Limit Type 0 less than 50 km per h]
    COMP --> HMP[Compare with AIS 090 Limit Type I above 50 km per h]
    CMP --> DEC[Pass or Fail Decision]
    HMP --> DEC
```

### 4.5 Process Flow — Emission Test (CMVR / AIS-008 Simplified)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Start] --> P1[Place Vehicle on Chassis Dyno]
    P1 --> P2[Set Road Load Coefficients A B C from Coast Down]
    P2 --> P3[Connect CVS Dilution Tunnel]
    P3 --> P4[Soak Vehicle 12 h at 20 to 30 deg C]
    P4 --> P5[Start Engine Sample Background]
    P5 --> P6[Run Test Cycle IDC India Driving Cycle or WLTC]
    P6 --> P7[Bag 1 and Bag 2 Sampling CVS]
    P7 --> P8[Analyse Bags CO HC NOx PM PN by FID NDIR chemiluminescence]
    P8 --> P9[Compute g per km and Compare to BS VI Limit]
    P9 --> P10{Pass}
    P10 -- Yes --> P11[Issue Type Approval Certificate]
    P10 -- No --> P12[Reject Vehicle Submit Revised Calibration]
    P12 --> P1
    P11 --> P13[End]
    P12 --> P13
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** *Define "Test Procedure" as applied to vehicle performance evaluation. List any four AIS (Automotive Industry Standards) codes relevant to Indian vehicle testing.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A *Test Procedure* is a standardized, sequential, and documented methodology for measuring the performance, fuel economy, emissions, or durability of a vehicle under prescribed conditions, instruments, and protocols, ensuring reproducibility of results across laboratories.

Four relevant AIS codes (½ mark each, 2 marks):
- **AIS-008** — Type Approval for emission of L, M, N category vehicles (BS-VI)
- **AIS-009** — Method of measuring fuel consumption
- **AIS-090** — Brake test procedure for M1 and N1 category vehicles
- **AIS-018** — Measurement of noise emitted by motor vehicles

*(Award the 3rd mark for the definition.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** *State the standard ambient test conditions prescribed under CMVR / AIS-008 for vehicle performance and emission tests.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Standard ambient test conditions (1 mark each, partial marks awarded for 3 of 4):
- **Ambient temperature:** 20 °C to 30 °C
- **Atmospheric pressure:** 91 kPa to 104 kPa
- **Relative humidity:** 30 % to 70 %
- **Wind speed:** Less than 3 m/s at the test site

(Plus 1 mark for the supporting statement: track shall be level within ± 0.5 % and dry.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]:**
>
> **(a)** [7 marks] Describe in detail the standard **road-test procedure** for the determination of the maximum speed of a passenger car as per AIS-008. Include site, vehicle, and environmental pre-conditions, the measurement method, and the data-reduction steps.
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] A 1,400 kg car with $C_d A = 0.65$ m², $C_{rr} = 0.010$, $\eta_t = 0.90$, on a level road ($\theta = 0$), is tested. The rated engine power is 80 kW. Air density is 1.18 kg/m³. Determine the theoretical maximum speed and the corresponding road-load power.

**Model Solution (Question A):**

**(a) Standard Procedure for Maximum-Speed Road Test [7 marks]:**

1. **Site (1 mark):** Flat, level, straight road of at least 1.5 km clear length. Track gradient ≤ 0.5 %. Surface shall be dry, clean, of uniform bituminous or concrete finish.

2. **Vehicle Preparation (2 marks):**
   - Running-in completed (≥ 1,500 km).
   - Tyre pressure set to OEM cold recommended value ± 10 kPa.
   - Vehicle mass: kerb + driver (75 kg ± 5 kg) + instrumentation.
   - All fluids topped to maximum marks.
   - Engine and lubricants warmed to operating temperature: oil sump ≥ 80 °C.

3. **Environmental Conditions (1 mark):** Ambient 20–30 °C, pressure 91–104 kPa, humidity 30–70 %, wind ≤ 3 m/s. Conditions logged at start of test and every 30 min thereafter.

4. **Measurement Method (2 marks):**
   - Vehicle accelerated to maximum speed, then held in top gear for at least 1 km of stabilized run.
   - Speed recorded via calibrated fifth wheel / GPS / non-contact speed sensor (≥ 1 Hz sampling).
   - Run repeated in opposite direction to nullify wind and gradient effect.
   - Direction-averaged maximum speed = arithmetic mean of two runs.

5. **Data Reduction (1 mark):** Correct the measured speed to standard conditions using STP correction; report as the highest speed sustained for ≥ 1 km.

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 marks]:**

Road-load power balance:
$$\tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A\,v^3 + C_{rr}\,m\,g\,v = P_{engine}\,\eta_t$$

Substitute:
$$\tfrac{1}{2}(1.18)(0.65)v^3 + (0.010)(1400)(9.81)v = 80{,}000 \times 0.90$$

$$0.3835\,v^3 + 137.34\,v = 72{,}000$$

Iterative solution:

| Trial $v$ (m/s) | $0.3835 v^3$ | $137.34 v$ | Sum (W) | vs. 72,000 W |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 50 | 47,938 | 6,867 | 54,805 | Low |
| 55 | 63,839 | 7,554 | 71,393 | Low (Δ = 607) |
| 56 | 67,330 | 7,691 | 75,021 | High (Δ = 3,021) |

Linear interpolation between 55 and 56 m/s:
$$v_{max} \approx 55 + \dfrac{72{,}000 - 71{,}393}{75{,}021 - 71{,}393} \times 1 \approx 55.17\ \text{m/s}$$

$$\boxed{v_{max} \approx 55.2\ \text{m/s} \approx 198.6\ \text{km/h}}$$

Corresponding road-load power:
$$P_{road} = 0.3835(55.17)^3 + 137.34(55.17) \approx 64{,}366 + 7{,}578 \approx 71{,}944\ \text{W} \approx 72\ \text{kW}$$

(Equivalent to rated 80 kW × 0.90 = 72 kW — sanity check passes.)

---

> **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — alternative choice]:**
>
> **(a)** [7 marks] With a neat flowchart, explain the **chassis-dynamometer test procedure** for the measurement of fuel consumption as per AIS-009. State the formula used and the units of the final result.
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] A vehicle consumes 1,825 mL of petrol (density 0.745 g/mL) over a distance of 58.4 km on a chassis dynamometer. Compute the fuel consumption in (i) L/100 km, (ii) km/L, and (iii) g/km.

**Model Solution (Question B):**

**(a) Chassis-Dyno Fuel-Consumption Test Procedure [7 marks]:**

- Step 1 — Vehicle prepared per AIS-009: 12-h soak at 20–30 °C, tyre pressure checked, fuel line connected to gravimetric fuel meter (1 mark).
- Step 2 — Vehicle placed on chassis dyno; road-load coefficients $A, B, C$ set from a coast-down match to within ± 5 % of the road coast-down (1 mark).
- Step 3 — Speed follower fan positioned 1 m behind vehicle, airflow matched to road speed within ± 5 km/h (1 mark).
- Step 4 — Test cycle run: Indian Driving Cycle (IDC) or WLTC; cycle comprises cold-start + transient phases (1 mark).
- Step 5 — Fuel consumption measured gravimetrically: $m_{fuel}$ in grams over distance $d$ in km (1 mark).
- Step 6 — Computed: $FC = \dfrac{m_{fuel}}{\rho_{fuel}\,d}\times 100$ in L/100 km (1 mark).
- Step 7 — Report also computes CO$_2$ mass using carbon-balance formula and emissions per km (1 mark).

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 marks]:**

Given:
- $V_{fuel} = 1{,}825$ mL
- $\rho_{fuel} = 0.745$ g/mL
- $d = 58.4$ km

Step 1 — Mass of fuel consumed:
$$m_{fuel} = V \cdot \rho = 1{,}825 \times 0.745 = 1{,}359.6\ \text{g}$$

Step 2 — (i) L/100 km:
$$FC = \dfrac{1.825}{58.4} \times 100 = 3.125\ \text{L/100 km}$$

Step 3 — (ii) km/L:
$$km/L = \dfrac{58.4}{1.825} = 32.0\ \text{km/L}$$

Step 4 — (iii) g/km:
$$g/km = \dfrac{1{,}359.6}{58.4} = 23.28\ \text{g/km}$$

$$\boxed{FC = 3.125\ \text{L/100 km} = 32.0\ \text{km/L} = 23.28\ \text{g/km}}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mark-Loss Traps in OEMUT724 Module 3 — Test Procedure:**
> 1. **Forgetting the STP correction** — power and emission results *must* be corrected to 25 °C, 101.325 kPa; the examiner specifically looks for the $\sqrt{T_{ref}/T_{meas}}$ factor.
> 2. **Mixing up AIS codes** — AIS-008 is **emissions**, AIS-009 is **fuel economy**, AIS-090 is **brakes**. Confusing any two costs at least 3 marks.
> 3. **Directional coast-down** — the *average* of two coast-downs in opposite directions is required; many students report only one run.
> 4. **Sign errors in $C_{rr}$ extraction** — the polynomial coefficients are negative; the formula is $C_{rr} = -A_0 / g$, not $+A_0 / g$.
> 5. **Standard ambient values** — quoting "25 °C" alone (instead of the *range* 20–30 °C) costs the third mark. Always quote the full allowable band.
> 6. **Drivetrain efficiency** — when asked for *engine* power, students often forget to divide wheel-power by $\eta_t$ (or multiply by it, depending on direction). State which one explicitly.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Test Procedure = Standardized, Reproducible, Documented.** Not optional. Not informal.
- **Standard ambient test conditions** (CMVR / AIS-008): T = 20–30 °C, p = 91–104 kPa, RH = 30–70 %, wind < 3 m/s, gradient ≤ 0.5 %.
- **Five-phase master sequence:** Conditioning → Environmental Prep → Warm-up → Measurement Run → Data Reduction & Reporting.
- **Three-term road-load equation** is the bedrock:
  $$P = \tfrac{1}{\eta_t}\left[\tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^3 + C_{rr}\,m\,g\,v + (m + m_{rot})\,a\,v + m\,g\,v\,\sin\theta\right]$$
- **Coast-down test** → extract $C_d A$ and $C_{rr}$ from a quadratic fit of $a_c(v)$.
- **Chassis dyno** must replicate the *road* coast-down via coefficients $A + Bv + Cv^2$.
- **AIS-008** (Emissions), **AIS-009** (Fuel Economy), **AIS-090** (Brakes), **AIS-018** (Noise) — memorize the purpose of each.
- **Fuel-consumption formulae:** volume, mass (gravimetric), and carbon balance — know all three.
- **Mean Fully Developed Deceleration (MFBD)** is the key metric for the brake test (AIS-090 / ECE R13).
- **Speed measurement:** non-contact / GPS / fifth wheel; DAQ at **10–100 Hz**.
- **STP correction** for power: $P_{STP} = P_{meas} \cdot (101.325/p) \cdot \sqrt{T_{ref}/T_{meas}}$.
- **Air-density correction** uses $R_s = 287.05$ J/(kg·K) and the psychrometric humidity factor.
- **Equivalent inertial mass** for an EV: $m_{rot} \approx 0.10\,m$; for an ICE car: $m_{rot} \approx 0.05\,m$.
- **Type-approval test** sequence for BS-VI: 12-h soak → cold-start → IDC/WLTC → CVS bag → analyser → g/km.
- **Always quote tolerances**, not just nominal values, in any test report.
- **KTU favourite 14-marker:** the *road-load cubic* — practice solving it iteratively to ± 1 % of the true root.
- **Units, units, units:** L/100 km, g/km, m/s², kW, °C, kPa — write them on every line.
- **Mark-7-question signature:** the sub-parts are *always* (a) 7 marks = procedure / flowchart + (b) 7 marks = numerical / derivation. Plan your 14-marker accordingly.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Automotive Noise Criteria
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMUT724 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Automotive Noise Criteria ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition of Automotive Noise **Automotive Noise** is defined as any unwanted sound or acoustic pressure fluctuation generated by a vehicle or its sub-systems (power-train, tire-road interface, aerodynamic flow, exhaust, and auxiliary components) that propagates through air, structure, or fluid media and is perceived by passengers, pedestrians, or the surrounding environment. In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3, **Automotive Noise Criteria** refer to the standardized set of: - **Quantitative limits** (maximum permissible sound levels) - **Measurement methodologies** (test conditions, instrumentation, positions) - **Evaluation metrics** (frequency-weighted levels, psychoacoustic descriptors) - **Frequency-shape templates** (Noise Criterion curves, Octave-band spectra) that are used to assess whether a vehicle complies with regulatory, comfort, and quality requirements. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Automotive Noise Criteria is the gateway topic that connects NVH (Noise, Vibration, Harshness) engineering with regulatory compliance. Mastery of decibel arithmetic and weighting filters is essential before tackling pass-by noise and interior acoustics. ### 1.2 Sound, Noise, and Vibration — The Triad | Term | Definition | Vehicle Example | |------|------------|-----------------| | **Sound** | A mechanical wave (pressure oscillation) within the audible frequency band (**20 Hz – 20 kHz**) | Engine note inside cabin | | **Noise** | Undesirable sound, judged subjectively by the listener | Clatter of a worn gearbox | | **Vibration** | Oscillatory motion of a structure, generally below 100 Hz | Steering column shake at idle | ### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Water Bucket" Model > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conceptual Analogy — Imagine a Bucket of Water:** > - The **water level** in the bucket = the Sound Pressure Level (SPL) in decibels. > - The **width of the bucket** = the bandwidth of frequencies we listen to. > - **Throwing a small pebble** (a single frequency like tire whine) creates a small ripple, adding only a little water (≈ +1 dB). > - **Throwing a boulder** (a loud exhaust pop) creates a huge splash, adding a lot of water (≈ +10 dB). > - **Pouring a second identical bucket of water** (two equal sources) does NOT double the level — it only adds **+3 dB** because decibels are logarithmic, not linear. This logarithmic nature is precisely why automotive noise is measured in **decibels (dB)** and not in pascals directly. ### 1.4 The Standard Acoustic Quantities The three fundamental measurable quantities in automotive acoustics are: 1. **Sound Pressure (p)** — Instantaneous local pressure deviation from atmospheric pressure. * **Unit:** Pascal (Pa) * **Reference:** $p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa} = 20 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{Pa}$ (threshold of human hearing at 1 kHz) 2. **Sound Intensity (I)** — Acoustic power transmitted per unit area in a specified direction. * **Unit:** Watts per square meter (W/m²) * **Reference:** $I_0 = 10^{-12}\ \text{W/m}^2$ 3. **Sound Power (W)** — Total acoustic energy radiated per unit time by a source. * **Unit:** Watt (W) * **Reference:** $W_0 = 10^{-12}\ \text{W}$ > [!NOTE] > **Why decibels?** The human ear responds to acoustic pressure over a range of roughly $10^6$ (from whisper to jet engine). The decibel compresses this enormous dynamic range into a manageable 0–140 dB scale. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Decibel — Foundation of Noise Metrics The decibel (dB) is a **logarithmic ratio** that compares a measured acoustic quantity against a fixed reference value. Three decibel formulations are used in automotive testing. #### 2.1.1 Sound Pressure Level (SPL or $L_p$) The most commonly measured parameter in vehicle testing. SPL is obtained using a microphone that converts acoustic pressure into an electrical signal. $$L_p = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_0}\right)\ \text{dB}$$ Where: * $p_{\text{rms}}$ = root-mean-square acoustic pressure (Pa) * $p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$ = reference pressure * The factor **20** comes from $20 = 10 \times 2$ (squared pressure ratio, then $\log_{10}$). #### 2.1.2 Sound Intensity Level ($L_I$) $$L_I = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right)\ \text{dB}$$ Where $I_0 = 10^{-12}\ \text{W/m}^2$. #### 2.1.3 Sound Power Level ($L_W$ or SWL) $$L_W = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{W}{W_0}\right)\ \text{dB}$$ Where $W_0 = 10^{-12}\ \text{W}$. #### 2.1.4 Conversion Between $L_p$ and $L_W$ (Free Field) For a source radiating into a free (hemispherical) field: $$L_W = L_p + 10 \log_{10}(S)\ \text{dB}$$ Where $S$ = measurement surface area in m². For a hemisphere of radius $r$: $$S = 2\pi r^2$$ ### 2.2 Decibel Arithmetic — The Board-Exam Essentials Because decibels are logarithmic, **you cannot simply add or subtract them arithmetically**. The KTU examiner expects mastery of the following operations. #### 2.2.1 Addition of Two Uncorrelated Sources $$L_{\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}\left(10^{L_1/10} + 10^{L_2/10}\right)\ \text{dB}$$ **Quick Reference Rules** (for direct application in exams): * Identical levels ($L_1 = L_2$): add **+3 dB** * Difference 1–2 dB: add **+3 dB** * Difference 3 dB: add **+1.8 dB** (≈ +2 dB) * Difference 10 dB or more: the **lower level is negligible** #### 2.2.2 Subtraction (Background Noise Correction) When a measurement includes background noise, the **true source level** is: $$L_{\text{source}} = 10 \log_{10}\left(10^{L_{\text{total}}/10} - 10^{L_{\text{bg}}/10}\right)\ \text{dB}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "3 dB Rule of Thumb":** Doubling the acoustic power increases the level by 3 dB. A 10 dB increase corresponds to a perceived **doubling of loudness** — this is the cornerstone of every regulatory noise test. ### 2.3 Frequency Weighting Filters — Matching Human Hearing The human ear is not equally sensitive to all frequencies. **A-weighting (dBA)** models the 40-phon Fletcher-Munson contour and is the **mandatory filter** for nearly every automotive noise standard. #### 2.3.1 A-Weighting Correction Table | Frequency (Hz) | A-Weighting (dB) | Frequency (Hz) | A-Weighting (dB) | |---------------:|-----------------:|---------------:|-----------------:| | 31.5 | −39.4 | 500 | −3.2 | | 63 | −26.2 | 1000 | 0.0 | | 125 | −16.1 | 2000 | +1.2 | | 250 | −8.6 | 4000 | +1.0 | | **50–5000 Hz** band: A-weighting heavily attenuates low frequencies, reflecting the ear's insensitivity to bass. | The A-weighted sound level is: $$L_A = L_p + \Delta A(f)\ \text{dB}$$ Where $\Delta A(f)$ is the A-weighting correction at frequency $f$. #### 2.3.2 B and C Weightings * **B-weighting (dBB):** Intermediate, rarely used; designed for moderate SPL. * **C-weighting (dBC):** Nearly flat across 50–5000 Hz; used for **peak sound pressure** measurements and engine exhaust noise certification. > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistake:** Writing "dB" without specifying the weighting in numerical problems. Always state **dBA** (or dBB, dBC) — KTU examiners deduct ½ to 1 mark if the weighting is omitted. ### 2.4 Noise Criterion (NC) and Preferred Noise Criterion (PNC) Curves The **NC curves** are a family of templates (NC-15, NC-20, NC-25, …, NC-70) used to evaluate **interior cabin noise** across octave bands (63 Hz – 8 kHz). The procedure is: 1. Measure the SPL in each octave band. 2. Plot the octave-band spectrum on a graph with NC curves overlaid. 3. The **highest NC curve touched (but not exceeded)** at any band defines the **NC rating** of the space. **Typical NC Targets for Vehicles:** * Passenger car cabin (cruising): **NC 40 – NC 50** * Luxury sedan cabin: **NC 30 – NC 40** * Heavy truck cabin: **NC 50 – NC 60** * Electric vehicle cabin: **NC 25 – NC 35** (no engine masking noise) > [!IMPORTANT] > **PNC Curves (Beranek, 1971):** Add a low-frequency specification to prevent excessive rumble. PNC is widely used in modern automotive interior evaluations. ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Reference | Key Constants | |---|----------|---------|-------------------|---------------| | 1 | Sound Pressure Level | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p_{\text{rms}}/p_0)$ | dB, re $p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$ | — | | 2 | Sound Power Level | $L_W = 10 \log_{10}(W/W_0)$ | dB, re $W_0 = 10^{-12}$ W | — | | 3 | Sound Intensity Level | $L_I = 10 \log_{10}(I/I_0)$ | dB, re $I_0 = 10^{-12}$ W/m² | — | | 4 | Field Conversion | $L_W = L_p + 10 \log_{10}(S)$ | S in m², free field | $S = 2\pi r^2$ | | 5 | Power Ratio | $\Delta L = 10 \log_{10}(W_1/W_2)$ | dB | Doubling ⇒ +3 dB | | 6 | Pressure Ratio | $\Delta L = 20 \log_{10}(p_1/p_2)$ | dB | Doubling ⇒ +6 dB | | 7 | Sum of Levels | $L_{\text{tot}} = 10 \log_{10}(\sum 10^{L_i/10})$ | dB | Equal levels ⇒ +3 dB | | 8 | Background Correction | $L_{\text{src}} = 10 \log_{10}(10^{L_{\text{tot}}/10} - 10^{L_{\text{bg}}/10})$ | dB | — | | 9 | A-Weighted Level | $L_A = L_p + \Delta A(f)$ | dB(A) | Table values per IEC 61672 | | 10 | Distance Attenuation (Free Field) | $L_{p2} = L_{p1} - 20 \log_{10}(r_2/r_1)$ | dB | Doubling distance ⇒ −6 dB | | 11 | Inverse-Square Intensity | $I \propto 1/r^2$ | W/m² | Doubling distance ⇒ $L_I$ drops 6 dB | | 12 | Loudness (Stevens) | $S = k \cdot (L_N - 40)^{0.6}$ | sones | 1 sone = 40-phon pure tone at 1 kHz | ### 2.6 Real-World Utility * **Pass-by Noise Certification:** ECE R51, ISO 362, SAE J1470 — all use **dBA** maximum SPL measured 7.5 m from the vehicle centerline. * **Passenger Comfort:** NC/PNC curves set cabin acoustic targets during the design phase of HVAC, body sealing, and powertrain mounts. * **NVH Refinement:** Psychoacoustic metrics (loudness, sharpness) drive subjective-objective correlation for "sound quality" of engines, door closures, and exhaust notes. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation — From Acoustic Pressure to Sound Pressure Level We start with the definition of the **bel** (named after Alexander Graham Bell), the logarithmic unit of which the decibel is one-tenth: $$L\ [\text{bels}] = \log_{10}\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_0}\right)$$ To convert bels to decibels, multiply by 10: $$L_p\ [\text{dB}] = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_0}\right)$$ However, the standard Sound Pressure Level uses the **square** of the pressure ratio because **acoustic intensity is proportional to $p^2$** (since $I = p^2 / (\rho c)$ for plane waves, where $\rho c$ is the characteristic acoustic impedance of air). To express SPL as a power-equivalent logarithmic measure: $$L_p = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}^2}{p_0^2}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_0}\right)\ \text{dB}$$ > **Physical Interpretation:** The factor **20** (not 10) arises because $10 \times 2 = 20$ when applying the log rule $\log(a^2) = 2 \log(a)$. ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem 1 — A-Weighted Cabin SPL **Problem:** A microphone inside a vehicle cabin records an octave-band sound pressure spectrum as follows. Compute the **overall A-weighted SPL (dBA)** using octave-band addition. | Octave Band (Hz) | $L_p$ (dB) | $\Delta A$ (dB) | $L_A$ (dBA) | |------------------|-----------:|----------------:|------------:| | 125 | 78 | −16.1 | 61.9 | | 250 | 76 | −8.6 | 67.4 | | 500 | 74 | −3.2 | 70.8 | | 1000 | 72 | 0.0 | 72.0 | | 2000 | 70 | +1.2 | 71.2 | | 4000 | 66 | +1.0 | 67.0 | **Step 1 — Apply A-Weighting:** Subtract the corrections from the measured SPL to obtain $L_A$ for each band. For the 125 Hz band: $L_A = 78 + (-16.1) = 61.9$ dBA For the 250 Hz band: $L_A = 76 + (-8.6) = 67.4$ dBA (and so on for the remaining bands as tabulated above). **Step 2 — Sum the Band Levels using the energy-addition formula:** $$ \begin{aligned} L_{A,\text{total}} &= 10 \log_{10}\left(10^{61.9/10} + 10^{67.4/10} + 10^{70.8/10} + 10^{72.0/10} + 10^{71.2/10} + 10^{67.0/10}\right) \\ &= 10 \log_{10}\left(1.549 \times 10^{6} + 5.495 \times 10^{6} + 1.202 \times 10^{7} + 1.585 \times 10^{7} + 1.318 \times 10^{7} + 5.012 \times 10^{6}\right) \\ &= 10 \log_{10}\left(5.315 \times 10^{7}\right) \\ L_{A,\text{total}} &\approx 77.26\ \text{dBA} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Interpret:** The cabin overall SPL is **77.3 dBA**. For a luxury sedan at highway cruise this is high (target ≤ 65 dBA), indicating excessive road-noise transmission. ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem 2 — Pass-By Noise & Distance Correction **Problem:** During an ECE R51 pass-by test, the microphone is positioned **7.5 m** from the vehicle's centerline. A reading of **74 dBA** is recorded. What would the SPL be at **3 m** (a typical urban-measurement point)? **Step 1 — Apply inverse-square law for a free field (point source):** $$ L_{p2} = L_{p1} - 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right) $$ **Step 2 — Substitute values:** $L_{p1} = 74$ dBA, $r_1 = 7.5$ m, $r_2 = 3$ m. $$ \begin{aligned} L_{p2} &= 74 - 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{3}{7.5}\right) \\ &= 74 - 20 \log_{10}(0.4) \\ &= 74 - 20 \times (-0.3979) \\ &= 74 - (-7.96) \\ L_{p2} &= 81.96\ \text{dBA} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Verify the sign convention:** Moving from 7.5 m to 3 m brings the receiver **closer** to the source, so the level should **rise** — and indeed, +8 dB is obtained. This matches the intuition: at 3 m we hear the exhaust note much more loudly than at 7.5 m. ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem 3 — Two Identical Engines Running vs One **Problem:** A single engine produces **85 dBA** at the operator's ear. What is the level when **two identical engines** run simultaneously (assume uncorrelated)? **Step 1 — Identify the rule:** Two identical, uncorrelated sources add **+3 dB** to the level. $$ L_{\text{total}} = L_1 + 3\ \text{dB} = 85 + 3 = 88\ \text{dBA} $$ **Step 2 — Verify with the rigorous formula:** $$ \begin{aligned} L_{\text{total}} &= 10 \log_{10}\left(10^{85/10} + 10^{85/10}\right) \\ &= 10 \log_{10}\left(2 \times 10^{8.5}\right) \\ &= 10 \log_{10}(2) + 10 \log_{10}\left(10^{8.5}\right) \\ &= 3.01 + 85 \\ &= 88.01\ \text{dBA} \end{aligned} $$ > **Important Result:** Two equal sources yield +3 dB, not +6 dB. To achieve +6 dB (a doubling of *perceived* loudness), you need **four** identical sources. ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Decoding Regulatory Test Scenarios | Test Type | Standard | Microphone Position | Speed Condition | Weighting | Typical Limit (dBA) | |-----------|----------|---------------------|-----------------|-----------|--------------------:| | Pass-by (M1 category) | ECE R51 / ISO 362 | 7.5 m lateral, 1.2 m height | 50 km/h (specified gear) | A | **72 – 74** | | Stationary noise | ECE R51 | 0.5 m from exhaust outlet | Engine at rated speed | A | **95 – 103** | | Interior cabin (cruise) | ISO 5128 | Driver's ear position | 120 km/h | A | **≤ 65 (luxury) / ≤ 70 (economy)** | | Tire-road noise (coast-by) | ISO 11819 | 7.5 m lateral | 80 km/h, engine off | A | **70 – 78** | | EV AVAS (Acoustic Vehicle Alert) | ECE R138 | Multiple, 1–2 m | 0–20 km/h | A | **56 – 75** (frequency-modulated) | ### 3.6 Derivation — Loudness in Sones from Phons The **loudness** $S$ in sones is related to **loudness level** $L_N$ in phons by Stevens' power law: $$S = 2^{(L_N - 40)/10}$$ Equivalently, in compact form: $$S = k \cdot \left(\frac{L_N}{40}\right)^{0.6}\quad\text{with } k = 1\ \text{sone at } L_N = 40\ \text{phons}$$ **Sample calculation:** A sound is judged by listeners to be **60 phons** loud. Find its loudness in sones. $$ \begin{aligned} S &= 2^{(60 - 40)/10} \\ &= 2^{2.0} \\ &= 4\ \text{sones} \end{aligned} $$ > **Result:** A 60-phon sound is perceived as **four times** as loud as the 40-phon reference, illustrating that a 20-phon (i.e., 20-dB SPL at 1 kHz) increase yields a four-fold loudness jump — a critical insight for sound-quality engineering in EV interior tuning. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Automotive Noise Measurement Chain ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Noise Source
Engine / Tyre / Aero] --> B[Sound Propagation
Path] B --> C[Microphone
1/2 inch Free-field] C --> D[Preamplifier
+ Gain] D --> E[Band-pass Filter
Octave / Third-octave] E --> F[Weighting Network
A / B / C] F --> G[Data Acquisition
DAQ / Analyzer] G --> H[Display / Recording
dBA or SPL] H --> I[Compliance Check
vs ECE R51 / ISO 362] subgraph Conditioning Chain D E F end subgraph Evaluation H I end style A fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#000 style B fill:#fff4d6,stroke:#a16207,color:#000 style C fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#000 style D fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,color:#000 style E fill:#ede9fe,stroke:#6d28d9,color:#000 style F fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#be185d,color:#000 style G fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#0369a1,color:#000 style H fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#000 style I fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram — Pass-By Noise Test Layout (ISO 362 / ECE R51) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Test_Track direction LR L1[Microphone PP'
7.5 m line left] --- CC[Vehicle Path
CC' Line] --- L2[Microphone PP
7.5 m line right] AA[Line AA
50 m before PP] --- BB[Line BB
10 m before PP] --- CC2[Line CC
at PP] --- DD[Line DD
10 m after PP] --- EE[Line EE
50 m after PP] end subgraph Setup V[Vehicle under test
