gear engaged] --> P[Accelerate between AA and BB] P --> R[Run-out between CC and EE] end subgraph Output S1[SPL_max at PP] --> Aweight[dBA weighted] Aweight --> Limit{Within limit?} Limit -- Yes --> PASS[Pass] Limit -- No --> FAIL[Fail - redesign] end R --> S1 style V fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#000 style Aweight fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,color:#000 style PASS fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,color:#000 style FAIL fill:#fecaca,stroke:#991b1b,color:#000 ``` ### 4.3 Block Diagram — Frequency Weighting Filter Response ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Input X[Wideband SPL Lp
in dB linear] end subgraph Filters direction TB Fa[A-weighting
IEC 61672
peaks near 2-4 kHz] Fb[B-weighting
intermediate
rarely used] Fc[C-weighting
nearly flat
for peak SPL] end subgraph Outputs Oa[dBA
occupational / vehicle] Ob[dBB
legacy] Oc[dBC
peak / exhaust] end X --> Fa --> Oa X --> Fb --> Ob X --> Fc --> Oc style Fa fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#000 style Fb fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#000 style Fc fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,color:#000 style Oa fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#000 style Oc fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,color:#000 ``` ### 4.4 Block Diagram — NC / PNC Curve Evaluation Procedure ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Record Octave-band SPL
63 Hz to 8 kHz] --> B[Plot Spectrum
on NC Chart] B --> C{Does any band
touch or exceed
an NC curve?} C -- No --> D[NC rating less than
lowest curve shown] C -- Yes --> E[Identify highest
NC curve touched] E --> F[NC rating of
the space] F --> G[Compare with
target e.g. NC 40] G --> H{Compliant?} H -- Yes --> I[Accept - comfort target met] H -- No --> J[Apply PNC check
low-frequency rumble] J --> K[Refine NVH treatment] style A fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#000 style F fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,color:#000 style H fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#000 style I fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,color:#000 style K fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,color:#000 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Set A]** **Q1. Define Sound Pressure Level. Why is A-weighting used in automotive noise measurement instead of linear (unweighted) SPL?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Sound Pressure Level (SPL) is defined as the logarithmic ratio of the rms sound pressure to a reference pressure of 20 μPa, expressed in decibels: $$L_p = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_0}\right)\ \text{dB}$$ (1 mark for the definition and reference pressure) A-weighting is used because the human ear is **not equally sensitive** to all frequencies. The ear is most sensitive around 1–4 kHz and rolls off at low and very high frequencies. The A-weighting filter mathematically emulates the 40-phon equal-loudness contour, so that the measured dBA value correlates well with the **perceived loudness** of the sound. (1 mark for explaining frequency response of the ear, 1 mark for the A-weighting purpose) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Q2. List any three major sources of automotive noise. For each, state the dominant frequency range and a typical engineering countermeasure.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Source | Dominant Frequency | Countermeasure | |--------|--------------------|----------------| | Engine (combustion + mechanical) | 50 – 1000 Hz (combustion) and 1 – 8 kHz (gear whine) | Engine mounts, balance shafts, NVH-tuned muffler | | Tire-road interaction | 200 – 2000 Hz (texture) and 800 – 1500 Hz (cavity) | Low-noise tread pattern, acoustic foam in wheel well | | Aerodynamic | 500 – 5000 Hz (wind rush, mirrors) | Streamlined A-pillars, side-glass shape, underbody panels | (1 mark per correct row × 3) ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) with Internal Choice > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Module 3, Question Module 3-A]** #### OR #### Question A (14 Marks) **(a)** Derive the relationship between Sound Pressure Level ($L_p$), Sound Power Level ($L_W$), and the measurement surface area in a free field. State the assumption clearly. (7 marks) **(b)** A vehicle cabin exhibits the following octave-band SPL spectrum at 120 km/h cruise. Compute: * (i) the **overall A-weighted SPL (dBA)**, and * (ii) the **NC rating** of the cabin. * (iii) Comment on whether the cabin meets the luxury-sedan target of **NC 40**. (7 marks) | Octave Band (Hz) | SPL (dB) | A-Weighting (dB) | |------------------|---------:|-----------------:| | 63 | 80 | −26.2 | | 125 | 78 | −16.1 | | 250 | 75 | −8.6 | | 500 | 72 | −3.2 | | 1000| 70 | 0.0 | | 2000| 68 | +1.2 | | 4000| 64 | +1.0 | | 8000| 58 | −1.1 | #### Model Solution to Question A **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):** We begin with the **sound power** of a source radiating into a free hemispherical field. For an omnidirectional source: $$W = I_{\text{avg}} \times S$$ where $I_{\text{avg}}$ is the time-averaged intensity on the hemisphere of radius $r$, and $S = 2\pi r^2$ is the hemisphere area. For a free (far) field, the intensity is related to the rms pressure by: $$I = \frac{p_{\text{rms}}^2}{\rho c}$$ where $\rho c \approx 413\ \text{N·s/m}^3$ is the **characteristic acoustic impedance of air at 20 °C**. Substituting: $$W = \frac{p_{\text{rms}}^2}{\rho c} \cdot 2\pi r^2$$ Now express both $W$ and $p_{\text{rms}}$ in decibels: $$ \begin{aligned} L_W &= 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{W}{W_0}\right) \\ L_p &= 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_0}\right) \end{aligned} $$ Convert W to dB form: $$ \begin{aligned} L_W &= 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}^2 \cdot 2\pi r^2}{\rho c \cdot W_0}\right) \\ &= 10 \log_{10}\left[\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_0}\right)^2 \cdot \frac{p_0^2 \cdot 2\pi r^2}{\rho c \cdot W_0}\right] \\ &= 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_0}\right) + 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{p_0^2 \cdot 2\pi r^2}{\rho c \cdot W_0}\right) \\ L_W &= L_p + 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{p_0^2 \cdot 2\pi r^2}{\rho c \cdot W_0}\right) \end{aligned} $$ Using $p_0 = 20 \times 10^{-6}$ Pa and $W_0 = 10^{-12}$ W: $$\frac{p_0^2}{\rho c \cdot W_0} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-10}}{413 \times 10^{-12}} = 0.9685$$ So $10 \log_{10}(0.9685) \approx -0.14$ dB, which for most engineering applications is negligible, giving the standard textbook form: $$\boxed{L_W \approx L_p + 10 \log_{10}(S)\ \text{dB}}\quad\text{where } S = 2\pi r^2\ \text{m}^2$$ **Assumption:** The source is **omnidirectional**, the field is **free** (no reflective surfaces), and the hemisphere is in the **far field** ($r \gg$ source dimensions, $r \gg \lambda$). (7 marks: 3 for derivation steps, 2 for area relationship, 1 for assumption, 1 for the impedance simplification) **Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):** **Step 1 — A-weighted octave-band levels:** | Band (Hz) | $L_p$ (dB) | $\Delta A$ (dB) | $L_A$ (dBA) | $10^{L_A/10}$ | |-----------|-----------:|----------------:|------------:|--------------:| | 63 | 80 | −26.2 | 53.8 | $2.398 \times 10^{5}$ | | 125 | 78 | −16.1 | 61.9 | $1.549 \times 10^{6}$ | | 250 | 75 | −8.6 | 66.4 | $4.365 \times 10^{6}$ | | 500 | 72 | −3.2 | 68.8 | $7.586 \times 10^{6}$ | | 1000 | 70 | 0.0 | 70.0 | $1.000 \times 10^{7}$ | | 2000 | 68 | +1.2 | 69.2 | $8.318 \times 10^{6}$ | | 4000 | 64 | +1.0 | 65.0 | $3.162 \times 10^{6}$ | | 8000 | 58 | −1.1 | 56.9 | $4.898 \times 10^{5}$ | **Step 2 — Sum the band energies:** $$ \begin{aligned} \sum 10^{L_A/10} &= 2.398\times 10^5 + 1.549\times 10^6 + 4.365\times 10^6 + 7.586\times 10^6 \\ &\quad + 1.000\times 10^7 + 8.318\times 10^6 + 3.162\times 10^6 + 4.898\times 10^5 \\ &= 3.566 \times 10^7 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Overall A-weighted SPL:** $$L_{A,\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}(3.566 \times 10^7) \approx \mathbf{75.5\ \text{dBA}}$$ **Step 4 — NC rating:** Comparing each octave-band $L_A$ value against the standard NC curves, the 1000 Hz band (70 dBA) touches the **NC 55** curve, and the 500 Hz band (68.8 dBA) is just below it. Hence the **NC rating is approximately 55**. **Step 5 — Compliance comment:** The luxury-sedan target is NC 40, but the measured rating is NC 55 — a **15-dB excess** that will be perceived as roughly **4× louder** than the target. This indicates inadequate body sealing and/or excessive road-noise transmission, requiring further NVH treatment. (7 marks: 2 for A-weighting, 2 for energy summation, 1 for final value, 1 for NC rating, 1 for commentary) --- #### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* **(a)** Explain the concept of Noise Criterion (NC) and Preferred Noise Criterion (PNC) curves. How are they used to evaluate vehicle cabin noise? (7 marks) **(b)** A truck cabin has a measured SPL of **76 dBA**. A noise-control treatment reduces the SPL to **70 dBA**. * (i) What is the **percentage reduction in sound power** achieved by the treatment? * (ii) By how many sones does the perceived loudness drop if the original loudness was 8 sones? (Assume Stevens' law: $S = 2^{(L_N-40)/10}$ and 1 sone = 40 phons.) (7 marks) #### Model Solution to Question B **Part (a) — NC and PNC Curves (7 marks):** **Noise Criterion (NC) Curves:** * Developed by Beranek (1957) and standardised in ANSI/ASA S12.2. * A family of equal-loudness contour-like templates, plotted on a graph of SPL (y-axis) vs. octave-band centre frequency (x-axis: 63, 125, 250, 500, 1000, 2000, 4000, 8000 Hz). * Each NC curve is labelled NC-15, NC-20, …, NC-70 in 5-dB steps. * The **NC rating** of a space is the highest NC curve touched by the measured octave-band spectrum. **Preferred Noise Criterion (PNC) Curves:** * Revised by Beranek in 1971 to address low-frequency rumble inadequately handled by NC curves. * PNC curves are similar to NC curves but have a steeper low-frequency slope and add a **low-frequency specification** (e.g., the PNC-40 curve requires < 75 dB at 63 Hz). **Application to Vehicle Cabin:** 1. Measure octave-band SPL inside the cabin at the driver's ear position. 2. Plot the spectrum. 3. Determine the NC (or PNC) rating. 4. Compare with target (NC 30–40 for luxury; NC 50–60 for trucks). 5. If rating exceeds target, identify the dominant bands and apply targeted treatments (sealing, damping, absorption). (7 marks: 2 NC definition, 2 PNC definition, 1 application procedure, 1 typical targets, 1 example application) **Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):** **(i) Sound Power Reduction (4 marks):** Given $L_{p1} = 76$ dBA and $L_{p2} = 70$ dBA. The level change is: $$ \Delta L = L_{p2} - L_{p1} = 70 - 76 = -6\ \text{dB} $$ The power ratio is: $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{W_2}{W_1} &= 10^{\Delta L / 10} = 10^{-6/10} = 10^{-0.6} = 0.2512 \\ \text{Percentage retained} &= 25.12\% \\ \text{Percentage reduction} &= (1 - 0.2512) \times 100\% = \mathbf{74.88\%} \end{aligned} $$ **Engineering Interpretation:** A 6-dB reduction corresponds to halving the rms sound pressure and a 75 % reduction in sound power. This is a substantial NVH improvement typically requiring a combination of absorption (foam), damping (constrained layer), and isolation (mounts). **(ii) Loudness Reduction in Sones (3 marks):** Given $S_1 = 8$ sones. Compute the corresponding loudness level in phons using: $$ \begin{aligned} L_{N1} &= 40 + 10 \log_2(S_1) = 40 + 10 \log_2(8) = 40 + 10 \times 3 = 70\ \text{phons} \end{aligned} $$ A 6-dB SPL drop corresponds approximately to a **6-phon** drop in loudness level (since the SPL change of 6 dB at 1 kHz equals the phon change for moderate levels): $$ L_{N2} = L_{N1} - 6 = 70 - 6 = 64\ \text{phons} $$ Now compute the new loudness in sones: $$ S_2 = 2^{(L_{N2}-40)/10} = 2^{(64-40)/10} = 2^{2.4} \approx 5.28\ \text{sones} $$ The reduction in perceived loudness is: $$ \Delta S = S_1 - S_2 = 8 - 5.28 = \mathbf{2.72\ \text{sones}} $$ **Interpretation:** The 6-dB SPL reduction is perceived as a 34 % drop in loudness — a meaningful subjective improvement for cabin comfort. (7 marks: 4 for part (i), 3 for part (ii)) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Mark-Loss Hot Spots:** > * **Forgetting the factor 20 in SPL:** Students often use the intensity formula (factor 10) for SPL — *always* use 20 log for pressure ratios. > * **Linear addition of decibels:** A common error is to write $L_{\text{total}} = L_1 + L_2$. This is **wrong** — decibels are logarithmic; use the energy sum. > * **Unit confusion for dB, dBA, dBC:** Always specify the weighting suffix when answering automotive noise problems. > * **NC vs PNC mix-up:** The examiner may explicitly ask for "PNC" — do not write NC by reflex. > * **Rounding errors:** Round SPL values to one decimal place at most; rounding to whole numbers in intermediate steps causes 1–2 dB errors that cascade. ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Automotive Noise Criteria** * ✅ Sound Pressure Level uses **$L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p/p_0)$** with $p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$ — remember the **factor 20**. * ✅ Sound Power Level uses **$L_W = 10 \log_{10}(W/W_0)$** with $W_0 = 10^{-12}$ W — remember the **factor 10**. * ✅ Free-field relation: **$L_W = L_p + 10 \log_{10}(S)$** where $S = 2\pi r^2$ (hemisphere). * ✅ Two equal sources ⇒ **+3 dB**; ten-fold increase ⇒ **+10 dB**; halving the distance ⇒ **+6 dB** (free field). * ✅ **A-weighting** emulates the 40-phon equal-loudness contour — heavily penalises low frequencies. * ✅ **dBC** is used for **peak SPL** and exhaust certification; **dBA** is the universal vehicle standard. * ✅ **NC curves** (63 Hz – 8 kHz) rate interior cabin comfort; **PNC curves** add a low-frequency rumble spec. * ✅ **ECE R51** = pass-by noise at 7.5 m, A-weighted; **ECE R138** = EV acoustic alert (AVAS). * ✅ **Stevens' Law** links sone to phon: $S = 2^{(L_N - 40)/10}$ — a 10-phon rise doubles the perceived loudness. * ✅ Vehicle noise sources: **power-train**, **tire-road**, **aerodynamic**, **exhaust**, **auxiliaries** (HVAC, wipers). * ✅ Always state units as **dB(A)**, **dB(C)** — never just "dB" in vehicle test answers. * ✅ Sound Power (W) is **independent of distance**; Sound Pressure ($p$) drops with $1/r$, SPL drops with $1/r$ in dB as $20 \log_{10}(r)$.

topics.title: Standard Noise Measurement Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMUT724 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Standard Noise Measurement Methods ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is Vehicle Noise Measurement? **Noise measurement** in the context of vehicle performance refers to the standardized, repeatable quantification of acoustic energy radiated by a road vehicle under controlled test conditions. The goal is to produce a single number (expressed in **decibels, dB**) that represents the perceived loudness of a vehicle as heard by a roadside observer or a stationary listener. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition:** *Standard Noise Measurement* is the internationally accepted procedure (governed by standards such as **ISO 362-1:2022**, **ECE Regulation No. 51**, and **SAE J1470**) for measuring the sound pressure level (SPL) emitted by a road vehicle during defined operating conditions (acceleration, constant speed, stationary) using a precision sound level meter, calibrated microphone, and a defined test environment. ### 1.2 The Three Acoustic Quantities To grasp noise measurement, the student must clearly distinguish between three physical quantities that are often confused: | Quantity | Symbol | Unit | What it really measures | |----------|--------|------|--------------------------| | Sound Pressure | $p$ | Pascal (Pa) | The local compression/rarefaction of air at the microphone | | Sound Intensity | $I$ | W/m² | The acoustic energy flowing *through* a unit area per second | | Sound Power | $W$ | Watt (W) | The total acoustic energy radiated by the source per second | > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy (The Lamp Analogy):** > Imagine a glowing light bulb in a room. > - **Sound Power (Watt)** = total light energy radiated by the bulb (intrinsic to the source). > - **Sound Intensity (W/m²)** = the light falling on a small patch of the wall. > - **Sound Pressure (Pa)** = how brightly a particular spot on the wall is *illuminated* (depends on distance, reflections, and the room's acoustics). > > The **sound level meter** actually measures *pressure*, but from it we infer the radiated *power* of the vehicle. This is the central challenge of noise measurement — you measure a "wall brightness" and try to deduce the "bulb wattage." ### 1.3 Why We Use Decibels (dB) The human ear responds to sound pressure **logarithmically**, not linearly. The ratio between the faintest audible sound and the threshold of pain is approximately **1 : 10,000,000** (1 Pa vs 20 µPa). To compress this enormous range into manageable numbers, we use the **decibel (dB)** scale. The standard reference pressure is $p_0 = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa} = 20 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{Pa}$ (the average human hearing threshold at 1 kHz). ### 1.4 Frequency Weighting — The "A" Filter The human ear is not equally sensitive to all frequencies. We hear mid-range sounds (1–4 kHz) much better than very low or very high frequencies. To make a sound level meter mimic human hearing, an electronic filter called the **A-weighting network** is applied. > [!IMPORTANT] > **A-weighted sound level (dB(A))** is the de facto global standard for vehicle noise measurement, occupational noise, and environmental noise. It is what you will see reported in every vehicle brochure and homologation certificate. Other weightings (B, C, D) exist: - **C-weighting** — nearly flat, used for peak sound pressure measurements. - **D-weighting** — specifically designed for aircraft noise perception (rarely used for cars). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Hierarchy of a Noise Measurement Chain The measurement is not a single reading; it is a chain of decisions. Each link must be correct for the result to be valid: 1. **Choose the standard** → ISO 362 (pass-by), ECE R51 (European homologation), SAE J1470 (US stationary), SAE J986 (exterior). 2. **Choose the microphone position** → depends on the standard (e.g., 7.5 m lateral for ISO 362). 3. **Choose the operating condition** → full-throttle acceleration, constant 50 km/h, or engine at rated speed (stationary). 4. **Choose the weighting** → almost always A-weighting. 5. **Choose the response time** → Fast (125 ms) or Slow (1 s). 6. **Record maximum SPL** during the run. 7. **Apply background noise correction** if ambient > 10 dB below source. 8. **Apply temperature/altitude correction** (only for ISO 362 in some versions). ### 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **All formulas below are essential for KTU ESE numerical problems. Memorize the variables, units, and reference values.** | # | Formula | Description | |---|---------|-------------| | 1 | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_0}\right) \text{ dB}$ | Sound Pressure Level, where $p_0 = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$ | | 2 | $L_W = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{W}{W_0}\right) \text{ dB}$ | Sound Power Level, where $W_0 = 10^{-12}$ W | | 3 | $I = \dfrac{p_{\text{rms}}^2}{\rho \, c}$ | Sound Intensity, where $\rho c \approx 408$ rayl (N·s/m³) in air at 20°C | | 4 | $L_I = L_p - 10\log_{10}(\rho c / 10^{-12}) \approx L_p$ for free field | Conversion between pressure level and intensity level | | 5 | $L_{\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} 10^{L_i/10}\right)$ | Adding *uncorrelated* sound levels | | 6 | $L_{\text{sum}} \approx L_{\text{max}} + 10 \log_{10}(n)$ | Quick estimate for $n$ *equal* uncorrelated sources | | 7 | $\Delta L = -10 \log_{10}\!\left(1 - 10^{-\Delta L_m / 10}\right)$ | Background noise correction, where $\Delta L_m = L_{\text{signal+bg}} - L_{\text{bg}}$ | | 8 | $L_{p2} = L_{p1} - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{r_2}{r_1}\right)$ | Inverse-square law (point source, free field) | | 9 | $L_{p2} = L_{p1} - 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{r_2}{r_1}\right)$ | Line source (e.g., traffic stream) inverse-distance law | | 10 | $K_2 = K_1 \cdot \left(\dfrac{T_2}{T_1}\right)^{1/2}$ | Sound level meter calibration check (frequency response shift) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rule of Thumb (6 dB and 3 dB Laws):** > - **Doubling of distance** from a point source → **−6 dB** drop. > - **Doubling of acoustic power** (two identical cars) → **+3 dB** rise. > - **10 dB rise** → subjectively *about* "twice as loud" to the human ear. > - **+10 dB** → **×10** sound power, **×3.16** sound pressure (rms). ### 2.3 Sound Level Meter (SLM) — The Instrument A modern integrating sound level meter is essentially a precision ac voltmeter wrapped around a calibrated microphone. Its key specifications, per **IEC 61672-1**, are: - **Type 1 (Precision)** — laboratory and field use, $\pm 0.7$ dB tolerance, used in vehicle homologation. - **Type 2 (General Purpose)** — $\pm 1.5$ dB tolerance, suitable for survey work. - **Type 0** — laboratory reference standard. The meter must be **calibrated** before and after each test session with a reference sound source (94 dB or 114 dB pistonphone / acoustic calibrator). A drift of more than **0.5 dB** invalidates the test. ### 2.4 Time Weightings | Response | Time constant | Use case | |----------|--------------|----------| | **Fast** | 125 ms | Most vehicle pass-by measurements (ISO 362 uses Fast) | | **Slow** | 1 s | Steady-state readings, fluctuations smoothed | | **Impulse** | 35 ms rise, 1.5 s decay | Sudden sharp noises (rarely used for vehicles) | | **Peak** | < 100 µs | Maximum instantaneous pressure (C-weighted) | ### 2.5 Engineering Utility Beyond regulatory compliance, standard noise measurement is critical for: - **NVH (Noise, Vibration, Harshness) development** in vehicle R&D. - **Passenger car marketing** (cabin quietness, refinement). - **EV acoustic alerting** (since 2019, EU requires AVAS — Acoustic Vehicle Alerting System for EVs at low speed, measured under UN R138). - **Component benchmarking** — comparing exhaust systems, tires, and intake silencers. - **Tire-road noise** is now the dominant pass-by source at constant speed for EVs and modern ICE cars. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Sound Pressure Level from a Measured Pressure **Given:** A microphone reads an rms sound pressure of $p_{\text{rms}} = 0.2$ Pa. The reference pressure is $p_0 = 20 \times 10^{-6}$ Pa. **Find:** The A-weighted sound pressure level in dB(A). **Step 1 — State the governing equation:** $$L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_0}\right) \text{ dB}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values:** $$L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.2}{20 \times 10^{-6}}\right)$$ **Step 3 — Simplify the ratio inside the logarithm:** $$\frac{0.2}{20 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{0.2}{0.00002} = 10{,}000 = 10^{4}$$ **Step 4 — Evaluate the logarithm:** $$L_p = 20 \times \log_{10}(10^{4}) = 20 \times 4 = 80 \text{ dB}$$ **Step 5 — Apply A-weighting at the dominant frequency:** If the source is broadband with most energy near 1 kHz, the A-weighting correction is **0 dB**, so $L_A = 80$ dB(A). > [!NOTE] > **Result:** A pressure of 0.2 Pa corresponds to 80 dB. Notice that a **100-fold increase** in pressure (0.002 Pa → 0.2 Pa) gives only a **40 dB** rise — this is the compression effect of the logarithmic scale. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Adding Two Sound Sources (e.g., Two Cars Passing) **Given:** Two identical cars each produce 70 dB(A) at a measurement point. **Find:** The combined sound level. **Step 1 — Convert each level from dB to linear power ratio:** For each car: $L_1 = L_2 = 70$ dB $$\frac{I_1}{I_0} = 10^{L_1/10} = 10^{70/10} = 10^{7}$$ **Step 2 — Add the two linear power ratios (sound powers are uncorrelated):** $$\frac{I_{\text{total}}}{I_0} = 10^{7} + 10^{7} = 2 \times 10^{7}$$ **Step 3 — Convert the sum back to dB:** $$L_{\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}(2 \times 10^{7})$$ $$L_{\text{total}} = 10 \left[\log_{10}(2) + \log_{10}(10^{7})\right]$$ $$L_{\text{total}} = 10 \left[0.3010 + 7\right] = 10 \times 7.3010 = 73.01 \text{ dB(A)}$$ **Step 4 — Verify with the quick estimate formula:** For $n = 2$ equal sources: $L_{\text{sum}} \approx L_{\text{max}} + 10 \log_{10}(n) = 70 + 10 \log_{10}(2) = 70 + 3.01 = 73.01$ dB(A). ✓ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** Two equally loud cars only give **+3 dB**, not +6 dB. Ten cars would give +10 dB. This is why traffic noise does not simply scale with the number of vehicles. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Background Noise Correction **Problem:** During a vehicle pass-by, the meter reads 78 dB(A). When the vehicle is not running, the ambient (background) reads 74 dB(A). Is the reading valid, and what is the *true* vehicle contribution? **Step 1 — Compute the difference $\Delta L_m$:** $$\Delta L_m = L_{\text{signal+bg}} - L_{\text{bg}} = 78 - 74 = 4 \text{ dB}$$ **Step 2 — Check the validity criterion:** Per ISO 362 / IEC guidelines, the difference should be **at least 10 dB** for the measurement to be considered valid without correction. Here, $\Delta L_m = 4$ dB < 10 dB, so a **correction is mandatory**, and the result is also of low confidence. **Step 3 — Apply the background noise correction formula:** $$\Delta L = -10 \log_{10}\!\left(1 - 10^{-\Delta L_m/10}\right)$$ **Step 4 — Substitute $\Delta L_m = 4$:** $$10^{-\Delta L_m/10} = 10^{-4/10} = 10^{-0.4} = 0.3981$$ **Step 5 — Evaluate the bracket:** $$1 - 0.3981 = 0.6019$$ **Step 6 — Compute the log term:** $$\log_{10}(0.6019) = -0.2205$$ **Step 7 — Final correction:** $$\Delta L = -10 \times (-0.2205) = +2.205 \text{ dB}$$ **Step 8 — Apply correction to get the *true* vehicle-only level:** $$L_{\text{vehicle}} = L_{\text{signal+bg}} + \Delta L = 78 - 2.205 \approx 75.8 \text{ dB(A)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** Because background noise was high, the meter overstated the vehicle's contribution by about 2.2 dB. The corrected true level is 75.8 dB(A). --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4: Distance Correction (Inverse-Square Law) **Given:** A vehicle pass-by measures 82 dB(A) at 7.5 m. Estimate the level at 15 m (free field, point source approximation). **Step 1 — Apply the inverse-square pressure equation:** $$L_{p2} = L_{p1} - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)$$ **Step 2 — Substitute:** $$L_{p2} = 82 - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{15}{7.5}\right) = 82 - 20 \log_{10}(2)$$ **Step 3 — Evaluate:** $$L_{p2} = 82 - 20 \times 0.3010 = 82 - 6.02 \approx 75.98 \text{ dB(A)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering meaning:** Doubling the distance from a vehicle in the free field costs you **6 dB** of perceived loudness. This is the basis for buffer-zone planning near highways. --- ### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Key Standards at a Glance | Parameter | ISO 362-1:2022 | ECE R51 (Rev.3) | SAE J1470 | SAE J986 | |-----------|----------------|------------------|-----------|----------| | Test type | Pass-by (moving) | Pass-by + stationary | Stationary (engine) | Stationary (exterior) | | Microphone distance | 7.5 m lateral, 1.2 m height | 7.5 m, 1.2 m | 0.5 m / 1.0 m from source | 15 m or 7.6 m | | Microphone orientation | Free-field, pointing up | Free-field | Random / grazing | Free-field | | Vehicle speed | 50 km/h constant, full-throttle accel | 50 / 40 km/h | Engine at rated speed | Engine at rated speed | | Weighting | A | A | A | A | | Time weighting | Fast | Fast | Slow | Slow | | Reported value | Max SPL during pass-by | Max SPL | Average of stable readings | Average SPL | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram of a Sound Level Meter ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Sound Field] --> B[Microphone
Capsule] B --> C[Preamplifier] C --> D[Frequency Weighting
A B C Z] D --> E[Time Weighting
Fast Slow Impulse] E --> F[RMS Detector] F --> G[Logarithmic
Converter] G --> H[Digital Display
dB] I[Acoustic Calibrator
94 dB at 1 kHz] -.->|Calibration check| B J[Reference Source
10 V or 94 dB] -.->|Electrical cal| E ``` **Description of the signal path:** 1. Acoustic pressure impinges the diaphragm of the **microphone**, converting pressure to an analog voltage. 2. The **preamplifier** boosts the millivolt-level signal and provides impedance matching. 3. The **frequency weighting filter** (A, B, C, or Z = linear) shapes the frequency response to mimic human perception or to provide unweighted measurement. 4. The **time weighting** (Fast/Slow/Impulse) sets the dynamic response of the RMS detector. 5. The **RMS detector** computes the true root-mean-square value. 6. The **log converter** transforms the linear RMS voltage into a decibel readout. --- ### 4.2 Test Track Layout for Pass-by Noise (ISO 362 / ECE R51) ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph TestTrack[ISO 362 Test Track Layout] direction LR A1[Line AA
Vehicle entry] --> A2[Line PP
Microphone at 7.5 m lateral
1.2 m height] A2 --> A3[Line BB
Vehicle exit] end subgraph MicrophoneZone[Microphone Zone - Free Field] M1[Mic P1
1.2 m height] M2[Mic P2
1.2 m height
10 m behind P1 optional] end A2 -.-> M1 subgraph RunUp[Run-up section] R1[20 m run-up before PP] --> R2[Acceleration zone] end R2 --> A1 ``` **Layout notes:** - The test track must be a flat, open, paved surface, free of reflecting obstacles (buildings, walls) within **50 m** of the line PP. - A **reference microphone (P2)** placed 10 m behind the main microphone (line PP) is used as a check against reflections and instrumentation errors. - The microphone must be a **free-field type** oriented with its diaphragm **vertical**, reference axis pointing **upward**. --- ### 4.3 A-Weighting Frequency Response (Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart TD F1[10 Hz] -->|Correction: -70.4 dB| A1 F2[31.5 Hz] -->|Correction: -39.4 dB| A1 F3[100 Hz] -->|Correction: -19.1 dB| A1 F4[500 Hz] -->|Correction: -3.2 dB| A1 F5[1000 Hz] -->|Correction: 0 dB REFERENCE| A1 F6[2000 Hz] -->|Correction: +1.2 dB| A1 F7[5000 Hz] -->|Correction: +0.5 dB| A1 F8[10000 Hz] -->|Correction: -2.5 dB| A1 A1[Output: dB A] A1 --> R[Rolloff below 1 kHz and above 5 kHz
Maximum sensitivity near 1 to 4 kHz] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **What the student must remember:** The A-weighting curve has **0 dB** at 1 kHz by definition. It heavily attenuates low frequencies (a 100 Hz tone needs about +19 dB more sound power to sound as loud as a 1 kHz tone). High frequencies roll off above 5 kHz. This is why a noisy diesel at 80 Hz may measure 75 dB(A), but to the ear it sounds quieter than a 75 dB(A) whining gear at 4 kHz. --- ### 4.4 Process Flow of a Standardized Vehicle Noise Test ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Step 1 - Vehicle Preparation] --> P2[Step 2 - Microphone Calibration at 94 dB] P2 --> P3[Step 3 - Background Noise Measurement] P3 --> P4{Background OK?
at least 10 dB below expected source} P4 -- No --> P5[Delay test, reduce noise] P4 -- Yes --> P6[Step 4 - Run vehicle per standard procedure] P6 --> P7[Step 5 - Record maximum SPL A-weighted Fast] P7 --> P8[Step 6 - Repeat for required gears / conditions] P8 --> P9[Step 7 - Background noise recheck] P9 --> P10[Step 8 - Apply corrections and report] P10 --> P11[Final Reported Value in dB A] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] *What is A-weighted sound level? Why is it used for vehicle noise measurement instead of unweighted SPL?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A-weighted sound level, denoted **dB(A)**, is the sound pressure level measured after passing the signal through an electronic filter (A-weighting network) that approximates the **frequency response of the human ear** at moderate loudness (40-phon Fletcher-Munson curve). (1 mark) The filter has 0 dB gain at 1 kHz, attenuates low frequencies heavily (e.g., −19 dB at 100 Hz) and high frequencies moderately, peaking around 2–5 kHz. (1 mark) It is used for vehicle noise because (i) it correlates better with human annoyance and perceived loudness, (ii) it is the legally mandated metric in ISO 362, ECE R51, and most national regulations, and (iii) it makes measurements across different vehicle types comparable on a perceptually meaningful scale. (1 mark) --- **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024] *Define a Type 1 Sound Level Meter. Where is it used in vehicle testing?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **Type 1 Sound Level Meter (Precision Grade)** is an instrument conforming to **IEC 61672-1** with an absolute tolerance of **±0.7 dB** under reference environmental conditions and field tolerance of **±1.5 dB**. (1.5 marks) It features A, C, and Z (linear) frequency weightings, Fast/Slow/Impulse time weightings, and must be supplied with a traceable calibration certificate. (1 mark) In vehicle testing, Type 1 meters are **mandatory** for type approval / homologation tests (ISO 362, ECE R51) and for any regulatory pass-by or stationary noise certification. Type 2 meters are acceptable only for general surveys, not for legal reporting. (0.5 mark) --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024]** *(a)* Describe with a neat sketch the standard **ISO 362 pass-by noise measurement** procedure for road vehicles. List the microphone position, vehicle speed, and weighting to be used. (7 marks) *(b)* Two sound pressure levels of **74 dB(A)** and **78 dB(A)** are measured simultaneously at a point by two independent sources. Calculate the **combined sound pressure level**. If a third identical source of 78 dB(A) is added, what will be the new combined level? (7 marks) --- **Model Solution:** **(a) ISO 362 Pass-by Noise Procedure (7 marks):** - **Test environment (1.5 marks):** Open, flat paved test track. No large reflecting surfaces (buildings, walls, embankments) within 50 m of the microphone line. Wind speed < 5 m/s. Air temperature between 5 °C and 40 °C. Background noise at least 10 dB below expected vehicle level. - **Microphone position (1.5 marks):** A free-field microphone is mounted at a **lateral distance of 7.5 m** from the vehicle's centerline (line CC′) and at a **height of 1.2 m** above the ground. A second reference microphone P2 is placed 10 m behind P1 along the track centerline. - **Vehicle operating condition (2 marks):** Two conditions are tested — (i) **constant speed 50 km/h** approaching line PP, and (ii) **wide-open-throttle acceleration** from a defined run-up speed, with the gear selected so the engine speed at line PP is between 75% and 85% of rated speed. The reference acceleration is achieved by placing a "target line" or using a method that ensures the engine load is full. - **Measurement (1.5 marks):** A-weighting, **Fast** time response. The **maximum A-weighted SPL** during the vehicle's passage through the test zone is recorded. The test is run on both sides of the vehicle, and the higher reading is reported. - **Corrections (0.5 mark):** Background noise correction applied if $\Delta L_m < 10$ dB; temperature correction only for some older versions; mean of valid runs reported to one decimal place in dB(A). **(b) Numerical — Combining Sound Levels (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Convert each level to linear power ratio (2 marks):** $$L_1 = 74 \text{ dB} \Rightarrow 10^{74/10} = 10^{7.4} = 2.512 \times 10^{7}$$ $$L_2 = 78 \text{ dB} \Rightarrow 10^{78/10} = 10^{7.8} = 6.310 \times 10^{7}$$ **Step 2 — Sum the linear ratios (1 mark):** $$I_{\text{total}} / I_0 = 2.512 \times 10^{7} + 6.310 \times 10^{7} = 8.822 \times 10^{7}$$ **Step 3 — Convert back to dB (1 mark):** $$L_{\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}(8.822 \times 10^{7}) = 10 (0.9455 + 7) = 10 \times 7.9455 = 79.46 \text{ dB(A)}$$ > Or use the "add a smaller value to a larger value" shortcut: difference is 4 dB → add 1.5 dB → 78 + 1.5 ≈ 79.5 dB(A). ✓ **Step 4 — Add the third identical 78 dB(A) source (3 marks):** $$L_{\text{new,linear}} = 8.822 \times 10^{7} + 6.310 \times 10^{7} = 15.132 \times 10^{7} = 1.5132 \times 10^{8}$$ $$L_{\text{new}} = 10 \log_{10}(1.5132 \times 10^{8}) = 10 (0.1798 + 8) = 81.80 \text{ dB(A)}$$ > **Final answers:** (i) Two-source combined level = **79.46 dB(A)**, (ii) Three-source combined level = **81.80 dB(A)**. --- **Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE** **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024]** *(a)* With the help of a block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of an integrating sound level meter**. List any four specifications per **IEC 61672-1**. (7 marks) *(b)* During a pass-by test, the measured SPL is **82 dB(A)** and the background noise level is **76 dB(A)**. Determine (i) whether the measurement is valid, (ii) the corrected vehicle-only sound level, and (iii) the level at a distance of **15 m** assuming free-field point-source propagation. (7 marks) --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Sound Level Meter Architecture (7 marks):** Refer to the block diagram in Section 4.1. **Block-by-block description (5 marks):** 1. **Microphone** — ½-inch free-field condenser type, polarised, frequency response flat from 20 Hz to 20 kHz. 2. **Preamplifier** — Provides high input impedance and low noise; mounted close to the mic capsule to avoid cable loss. 3. **Frequency weighting network** — Switchable A, C, Z filters per IEC 61672-1. 4. **Time weighting circuit** — Fast (125 ms), Slow (1 s), Impulse per standard time constants. 5. **RMS detector + log converter + digital display** — Computes true RMS, applies logarithm, displays in dB. **Four key specifications per IEC 61672-1 (2 marks — any four):** - Type classification: Type 0 / 1 / 2 with corresponding tolerance. - Linear operating range: typically 22–122 dB(A) for Type 1. - Frequency range: 20 Hz to 20 kHz. - Directional response: per clause 5.4 of the standard. - Self-generated noise: < 17 dB(A) for Type 1. - Calibration: 94 dB at 1 kHz reference source. **(b) Numerical (7 marks):** **(i) Validity check (2 marks):** $$\Delta L_m = 82 - 76 = 6 \text{ dB}$$ This is **less than 10 dB** → **measurement is not fully valid**; background noise correction is **mandatory**, and the result is of reduced confidence. **(ii) Background noise correction (3 marks):** $$\Delta L = -10 \log_{10}\!\left(1 - 10^{-6/10}\right)$$ $$10^{-0.6} = 0.2512$$ $$\Delta L = -10 \log_{10}(1 - 0.2512) = -10 \log_{10}(0.7488) = -10 \times (-0.1257) = +1.257 \text{ dB}$$ $$L_{\text{vehicle}} = 82 - 1.26 \approx 80.74 \text{ dB(A)}$$ **(iii) Distance correction (2 marks):** From 7.5 m to 15 m is a doubling of distance: $$L_{15} = 82 - 20 \log_{10}(15/7.5) = 82 - 6.02 \approx 75.98 \text{ dB(A)}$$ Or using the corrected value: $L_{15, \text{corr}} = 80.74 - 6.02 = 74.72$ dB(A). > **Final answers:** (i) **Not valid** as $\Delta L_m = 6$ dB < 10 dB, correction required. (ii) **Corrected level ≈ 80.7 dB(A)**. (iii) **Level at 15 m ≈ 75.98 dB(A)** (uncorrected) or **74.72 dB(A)** (with both corrections). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — How students LOSE marks:** > 1. **Confusing "sound pressure" with "sound power"** — they are different quantities with different reference values. Pressure uses $p_0 = 20 \, \mu$Pa, Power uses $W_0 = 10^{-12}$ W. (Loses 1–2 marks) > 2. **Forgetting the 10 vs 20 log factor** — Pressure levels use **20 log**, power/intensity levels use **10 log**. Mixing them up yields wrong numerical answers. > 3. **Linear addition inside a log** — students often write $L_{\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}(L_1 + L_2)$. This is **wrong**. You must convert to linear first, sum, then convert back. > 4. **Ignoring the validity criterion** — the 10 dB background-noise rule. Always check it. > 5. **Forgetting units on the inverse-square law** — distance ratio is dimensionless; result is still in dB. > 6. **Mixing A-weighting with C-weighting** — almost all vehicle regulations require A. If the question says "the SPL is 80 dB C-weighted," you cannot call it dB(A) without conversion. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] **Sound Pressure Level** is defined as $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p_{\text{rms}} / 20 \, \mu\text{Pa})$ dB. - [x] **A-weighting (dB(A))** is the universal metric for vehicle noise — it mimics the **40-phon** equal-loudness contour of the human ear. - [x] **Reference pressure** $p_0 = 20 \, \mu$Pa; **reference power** $W_0 = 10^{-12}$ W. - [x] **Type 1 SLM (IEC 61672-1, ±0.7 dB)** is mandatory for vehicle homologation; **Type 2 (±1.5 dB)** is for surveys only. - [x] **Doubling distance** from a point source in free field → **−6 dB**. - [x] **Doubling equal sound sources** → **+3 dB**; ten equal sources → **+10 dB**. - [x] **Adding levels** uses $L_{\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}(\sum 10^{L_i/10})$; never add dB values directly. - [x] **Background noise correction** is mandatory when $\Delta L_m < 10$ dB; formula: $\Delta L = -10 \log_{10}(1 - 10^{-\Delta L_m/10})$. - [x] **ISO 362-1:2022** — pass-by at 7.5 m lateral, 1.2 m height, A-weighted, Fast response, max SPL. - [x] **ECE R51** — European homologation, pass-by + stationary noise; tests both vehicle sides. - [x] **SAE J1470** — stationary engine noise at rated speed, microphone 0.5 m from exhaust outlet. - [x] **SAE J986** — exterior stationary noise at 15 m (or 7.6 m), engine at rated speed. - [x] **Calibration** before and after every test session with **94 dB / 1 kHz** calibrator; drift > 0.5 dB invalidates the run. - [x] **AVAS (UN R138)** — mandatory for electric and hybrid vehicles below ~20 km/h to alert pedestrians. - [x] **Time weightings:** Fast = 125 ms, Slow = 1 s, Impulse = 35 ms rise / 1.5 s decay. - [x] **Test environment must be open** — no reflecting walls within 50 m; wind < 5 m/s; background > 10 dB below source. - [x] **Tire-road noise** dominates constant-speed pass-by noise for modern EVs and ICE cars above ~60 km/h.

topics.title: Noise Inside and Outside the Vehicle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT724 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Noise Inside and Outside the Vehicle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Vehicle Noise

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vehicle Noise (KTU 2024 Definition):** Any unwanted acoustic pressure fluctuation generated by the vehicle's propulsion system, structure, tires, aerodynamic flow, and auxiliary components, propagating either as **structure-borne** (through mechanical paths) or **air-borne** (through the cabin air mass) sound waves, and measured typically in the frequency range of **20 Hz to 20,000 Hz**.

**Interior Noise** refers to the acoustic pressure field **inside the passenger compartment** at the occupant's ear position, usually evaluated at the driver's head location using a binaural or artificial head measurement system.

**Exterior Noise** refers to the acoustic radiation emitted by the vehicle into the surrounding environment, primarily measured as **pass-by noise** using a microphone placed **7.5 m** perpendicular to the centerline of the vehicle's path of motion, as per **ISO 362-1:2022** and **ECE Regulation No. 51**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric:** Sound Pressure Level (SPL) is the fundamental quantity used to express noise magnitude. It is measured in **decibels (dB)** referenced to the threshold of human hearing: **p₀ = 20 µPa (2 × 10⁻⁵ Pa)**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: "The Orchestra of Mechanical Chaos"

Imagine a vehicle in motion as a **roving orchestra**, where every component is a musician playing its own note at its own volume:
- The **engine** is a **brass section** — producing low-frequency rumble, combustion knock, and exhaust roars.
- The **tires** act like **percussionists** — striking the road surface thousands of times per second.
- The **wind** is a **flute** — whistling past A-pillars, mirrors, and windshield seals.
- The **transmission and driveline** are **string instruments** — whining and gear-meshing harmonics.

The **interior noise** problem is like trying to have a quiet conversation inside a moving concert hall, while the **exterior noise** problem is like being a neighbor trying to sleep while that orchestra passes by. Vehicle engineers are essentially **acoustic architects** who must dampen, block, and absorb these sounds.

## 1.3 Classification of Vehicle Noise

### By Source Origin:
| Category | Primary Sources | Frequency Range |
|----------|----------------|-----------------|
| **Powertrain Noise** | Engine combustion, exhaust, intake | 20 – 500 Hz (dom) |
| **Tire/Road Noise** | Tire tread impact, road texture | 500 – 4000 Hz |
| **Aerodynamic Noise** | Wind flow, turbulence, vortex shedding | 1000 – 10000 Hz |
| **Driveline Noise** | Gear mesh, bearings, CV joints | 200 – 2000 Hz |
| **Auxiliary Noise** | HVAC, wipers, accessories | 100 – 2000 Hz |

### By Transmission Path:
- **Air-borne Noise:** Travels through the cabin air via openings, glass panels, or thin walls.
- **Structure-borne Noise:** Travels through the chassis, firewall, and floor pan, eventually radiating into the cabin.

## 1.4 Human Hearing & Weighting

> [!NOTE]
> The human ear does not respond equally to all frequencies. The **A-weighting curve (dB(A))** approximates this response and is the **mandatory metric** for all vehicle noise regulations worldwide. Pure tones at low frequencies (e.g., 31 Hz) feel far less loud than equal-SPL tones at 2–4 kHz.

The A-weighting correction is given by:

$$ A(f) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left[\frac{R_A(f)}{R_A(1000)}\right] \text{ dB} $$

where $R_A(f)$ is the standardized frequency response function of the A-weighting filter defined in **IEC 61672-1**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Vehicle Acoustics

The study of vehicle noise is rooted in three fundamental physical quantities:

1. **Sound Pressure (p)** — Instantaneous deviation from atmospheric pressure, in Pascals (Pa).
2. **Sound Intensity (I)** — Acoustic power transmitted per unit area, in W/m².
3. **Sound Power (W)** — Total acoustic energy radiated per unit time, in Watts (W).

These are related through the **acoustic impedance of air** ($Z_0 \approx 413$ rayls at 20 °C):

$$ I = \frac{p^2}{Z_0} = \frac{p^2}{\rho_0 c} $$

where $\rho_0$ is the density of air ($\approx 1.204$ kg/m³ at 20 °C) and $c$ is the speed of sound ($\approx 343$ m/s).

## 2.2 The Decibel Scale — A Logarithmic Necessity

The human ear responds to sound **logarithmically**, not linearly. The ratio between the loudest tolerable sound (~20 Pa) and the threshold of hearing (~20 µPa) is **1,000,000:1**. Linear scales are impractical, so the **Bel** (and its tenth, the **decibel**) was adopted.

### Derivation Logic:
- Define reference quantities: $p_0 = 20\,\mu\text{Pa}$ and $I_0 = 10^{-12}\,\text{W/m}^2$.
- Take the base-10 logarithm of the squared ratio (to handle RMS pressure).
- Multiply by 10 to convert Bels to decibels.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Sound Pressure Level | $L_p = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p}{p_0}\right)$ | dB | $p_0 = 20\,\mu\text{Pa}$ |
| 2 | Sound Intensity Level | $L_I = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right)$ | dB | $I_0 = 10^{-12}\,\text{W/m}^2$ |
| 3 | Sound Power Level | $L_W = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{W}{W_0}\right)$ | dB | $W_0 = 10^{-12}\,\text{W}$ |
| 4 | Addition of SPLs | $L_{p,\text{total}} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\sum 10^{L_i/10}\right)$ | dB | Energy addition |
| 5 | Subtraction of SPLs | $L_{p,\text{ambient}} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(10^{L_T/10} - 10^{L_B/10}\right)$ | dB | When background $L_B$ known |
| 6 | A-Weighted SPL | $L_A = L_p + A(f)$ | dB(A) | $A(f)$ from IEC 61672-1 |
| 7 | Transmission Loss (TL) | $TL = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_t}{p_i}\right)$ | dB | Ratio of incident to transmitted pressure |
| 8 | Mass Law (Single Wall) | $TL \approx 20\log_{10}(m \cdot f) - 47$ | dB | $m$ in kg/m², $f$ in Hz |
| 9 | Sound Absorption (Sabine) | $\alpha = 1 - \left(\frac{n-1}{n}\right)^2$ | — | $n$ = intensity ratio |
| 10 | Reverb Time (Sabine) | $T_{60} = 0.161\,\frac{V}{A}$ | s | $V$ in m³, $A = \sum S_i\alpha_i$ |
| 11 | Coincidence Frequency | $f_c = \frac{c^2}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{m}{D}}$ | Hz | $D$ = bending stiffness |
| 12 | Critical Frequency (Stiffness) | $f_c = \frac{c^2}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{12(1-\nu^2)\rho_s}{Eh^2}}$ | Hz | For plates |
| 13 | Wavelength | $\lambda = c/f$ | m | Speed of sound $c \approx 343$ m/s |
| 14 | Octave Band Center | $f_c = \sqrt{f_L \cdot f_H}$ | Hz | Geometric mean |
| 15 | Air Density Impedance | $Z_0 = \rho_0 c \approx 413$ | rayls | At 20 °C, sea level |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy vs. Pressure Addition Rule:** A common KTU exam trap is treating dB values as additive. Remember — **doubling the sound energy adds 3 dB**, **doubling the number of equal sources adds 3 dB**, and **increasing SPL by 10 dB corresponds to a 10× increase in acoustic power**.

## 2.4 Interior vs. Exterior Noise — Engineering Significance

### Interior Noise:
- **Regulatory Limit (India):** AIS-020 sets the cabin noise at the driver's ear at **≤ 80 dB(A)** for passenger cars at 60 km/h on a smooth road.
- **Comfort target** for premium vehicles: **≤ 55 dB(A)** at 120 km/h cruise.
- Influences driver fatigue, speech intelligibility, NVH ratings.

### Exterior Noise:
- **ECE R51 / ISO 362-1:** Pass-by noise limit for passenger cars is **74 dB(A)** under the new ASEP procedure (2023+).
- Affects urban noise pollution, pedestrian health, and is mandatory for vehicle type approval.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles in this module are directly applied in:
1. **Cabin NVH Tuning** — Material selection (Damplifier, mass-loaded vinyl, constrained-layer damping).
2. **Exhaust System Design** — Reactive and absorptive mufflers, Helmholtz resonators, quarter-wave tubes.
3. **Tire Design** — Tread pattern optimization, rubber compound selection for low rolling noise.
4. **Aeroacoustics** — Side mirror shaping, A-pillar vortex control, underbody paneling.
5. **Active Noise Control (ANC)** — Phase-inverted speaker output to cancel cabin boom.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Sound Pressure Level (SPL)

The Sound Pressure Level quantifies the logarithmic ratio of the **RMS sound pressure** $p_{\text{rms}}$ to a reference pressure $p_0$.

### Step 1 — Define the acoustic intensity in terms of pressure
The instantaneous acoustic intensity is:

$$ I = \frac{p^2}{\rho_0 \, c} $$

**Conversion logic:** Using the acoustic impedance of air, $Z_0 = \rho_0 c$, intensity is the ratio of squared pressure to impedance. This links pressure-squared directly to energy flux.

### Step 2 — Define the reference intensity
The standard reference intensity corresponds to the threshold of human hearing at 1 kHz:

$$ I_0 = 10^{-12}\;\text{W/m}^2 $$

**Conversion logic:** This corresponds to an RMS pressure of $p_0 = 20\,\mu\text{Pa}$.

### Step 3 — Form the intensity ratio

$$ \frac{I}{I_0} = \frac{p^2 / Z_0}{p_0^2 / Z_0} = \left(\frac{p}{p_0}\right)^2 $$

**Conversion logic:** Since $Z_0$ cancels, the intensity ratio reduces to the squared pressure ratio.

### Step 4 — Apply base-10 logarithm

$$ \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = 2 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p}{p_0}\right) $$

**Conversion logic:** Logarithm of a square = 2 × log of base.

### Step 5 — Convert Bels to decibels (multiply by 10)

$$ L_p = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p}{p_0}\right) \text{ dB} $$

**Final Result:**

$$ \boxed{L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_0}\right) \quad \text{(dB, with } p_0 = 20\,\mu\text{Pa)} } $$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Mass Law for Sound Transmission Loss

The Mass Law is the foundational design equation for **single-panel sound barriers** such as vehicle firewall, floor pan, and door panels.

### Step 1 — Consider a thin, limp panel of surface density $m$ (kg/m²)
The panel has mass per unit area $m$ and is exposed to an incident sound wave of frequency $f$.

### Step 2 — Apply Newton's second law to panel motion
For a pressure difference $\Delta p$ across the panel, the panel acceleration is:

$$ a = \frac{\Delta p}{m} $$

**Conversion logic:** The mass resists motion, so heavier panels accelerate less for the same pressure.

### Step 3 — At low frequencies (mass-controlled region), the panel acts as a rigid wall
The transmitted pressure $p_t$ is approximated by the panel's velocity response. The transmission coefficient $\tau$ (ratio of transmitted to incident power) becomes:

$$ \tau = \frac{1}{\left(1 + \omega m / 2\rho_0 c\right)^2} $$

**Conversion logic:** As $m$ or $f$ ($\omega = 2\pi f$) increases, the denominator grows, so $\tau$ shrinks.

### Step 4 — For $m \cdot f \gg 1$ (typical vehicle panels: $m \approx 5$–$15$ kg/m², $f > 200$ Hz), approximate:

$$ \tau \approx \left(\frac{2\rho_0 c}{\omega m}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{\rho_0 c}{\pi m f}\right)^2 $$

### Step 5 — Define Transmission Loss as $TL = 10\log_{10}(1/\tau)$:

$$ TL = 10 \log_{10}\!\left[\left(\frac{\pi m f}{\rho_0 c}\right)^2\right] = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\pi m f}{\rho_0 c}\right) $$

### Step 6 — Evaluate the constant term
Substitute $\rho_0 c = 413$ rayls and $1/\pi \approx 0.318$:

$$ TL = 20 \log_{10}(m f) + 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\pi}{413}\right) $$

$$ TL = 20 \log_{10}(m f) - 42.4 $$

The **standard engineering form** uses the empirical constant $-47$ to account for edge effects and near-field corrections:

$$ \boxed{TL \approx 20 \log_{10}(m f) - 47 \quad \text{(dB; } m \text{ in kg/m}^2, f \text{ in Hz)} } $$

**Engineering Insight:** **Doubling the panel mass adds only ~3 dB** of TL. **Doubling the frequency adds 6 dB.** This is why high-frequency tire noise is easier to block than low-frequency engine rumble.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A vehicle cabin SPL is measured as **78 dB(A)** at the driver's ear at 80 km/h on asphalt. If the engine is switched off and only tire/road noise remains, the SPL drops to **72 dB(A)**. Calculate the **engine contribution alone** in dB(A).

**Solution:**

Convert dB to intensity ratios using the inverse formula:

$$ 10^{L/10} = \text{intensity ratio} $$

Step 1 — Total intensity (engine + tire):

$$ I_T = 10^{78/10} = 10^{7.8} = 6.31 \times 10^{7} $$

Step 2 — Tire noise intensity alone:

$$ I_{\text{tire}} = 10^{72/10} = 10^{7.2} = 1.585 \times 10^{7} $$

Step 3 — Engine intensity (subtraction in energy domain):

$$ I_{\text{eng}} = I_T - I_{\text{tire}} = 6.31 \times 10^{7} - 1.585 \times 10^{7} $$

$$ I_{\text{eng}} = 4.725 \times 10^{7} $$

Step 4 — Convert back to dB:

$$ L_{\text{eng}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(4.725 \times 10^{7}\right) $$

$$ L_{\text{eng}} = 10 \times (7 + \log_{10}\!\left(4.725\right)) $$

$$ L_{\text{eng}} = 10 \times (7 + 0.6745) $$

$$ L_{\text{eng}} = 10 \times 7.6745 $$

$$ \boxed{L_{\text{eng}} \approx 76.7 \;\text{dB(A)} } $$

**Note:** Even though the engine dominates, the total is only 1.3 dB higher than the engine alone — a perfect illustration of why the **dB scale is logarithmic and non-intuitive**.

---

## 3.4 Derivations: Reverberation Time (Sabine's Formula)

Used to predict cabin acoustics during **interior noise testing in semi-anechoic and reverberant chambers**.

### Step 1 — Energy density at time $t$
When a sound source is cut off, energy decays exponentially:

$$ E(t) = E_0 \, e^{-kt} $$

where $k$ is the decay constant.

### Step 2 — Decay constant depends on absorption
$$ k = \frac{c \, A}{4 V} $$

with $A = \sum S_i \alpha_i$ (total absorption in sabins), $V$ = volume.

### Step 3 — Reverberation time is when energy drops by $10^{-6}$ (i.e., 60 dB)

$$ \frac{E(t)}{E_0} = 10^{-6} = e^{-k T_{60}} $$

$$ T_{60} = \frac{6 \ln(10)}{k} = \frac{6 \times 2.3026}{c A / 4V} $$

$$ T_{60} = \frac{55.26 \, V}{c \, A} $$

### Step 4 — With $c = 343$ m/s:

$$ \boxed{T_{60} = 0.161 \,\frac{V}{A} \quad \text{(seconds)} } $$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics
