## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**
*Differentiate between a piezoelectric accelerometer and a piezoresistive accelerometer based on principle, output, and frequency range.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Piezoelectric | Piezoresistive |
|---|---|---|
| Principle | Direct piezoelectric effect (charge generation on stress) | Piezoresistive effect (resistance change on strain) |
| Output | Charge (pC) or voltage via charge amp | Voltage from Wheatstone bridge |
| Excitation required | No (passive) | Yes (DC bridge excitation) |
| Frequency range | ~1 Hz to 20 kHz | DC to ~2 kHz |
| Best for | Engine NVH, high-frequency | Crash, low-frequency, DC |

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
*Define "charge sensitivity" of a piezoelectric accelerometer and state its SI unit.*

**Model Answer:**
Charge sensitivity ($S_q$) is defined as the charge output per unit acceleration input, expressed as:

$$S_q = \dfrac{q}{a}$$

The SI unit is **Coulomb per m/s² (C·s²/m)**, but in practice it is expressed as **pC/g**. A typical value is 10–100 pC/g.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a piezoelectric accelerometer. Derive the governing equation of motion and obtain the natural frequency of the seismic system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A piezoelectric accelerometer of charge sensitivity 100 pC/g is connected to a charge amplifier with feedback capacitance $C_f = 2$ nF. The system measures an acceleration of 5 g. Calculate (i) the charge generated, (ii) the output voltage, and (iii) the lower cutoff frequency if $R_f = 50$ MΩ. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working Principle and Derivation:**

The piezoelectric accelerometer consists of a seismic mass **m** bonded to a piezoelectric crystal, with the assembly housed in a rigid casing attached to the vibrating body.

**Step 1:** When the body accelerates at $a_{in} = \ddot{y}$, an inertial force $F = m \cdot a_{in}$ acts on the mass.

**Step 2:** This force compresses the piezoelectric crystal, generating a charge $q = d \cdot F = d \cdot m \cdot a_{in}$ by the direct piezoelectric effect.

**Step 3:** Applying Newton's second law to the SDOF mass-spring-damper system, the equation of motion is:

$$m \cdot \ddot{z} + c \cdot \dot{z} + k \cdot z = -m \cdot a_{in}$$

**Step 4:** Comparing with the standard SDOF form:

$$m \cdot \ddot{z} + c \cdot \dot{z} + k \cdot z = F(t)$$

the natural (undamped) frequency is:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}, \quad f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$$

**Step 5:** The damping ratio is:

$$\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{k \cdot m}}$$

**Step 6:** For accurate acceleration measurement, the operating frequency must be well below resonance ($r = \omega/\omega_n < 0.3$).

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Charge generated:**

$$q = S_q \cdot a = 100 \, \text{pC/g} \times 5 \, \text{g} = 500 \, \text{pC} = 5 \times 10^{-10} \, \text{C}$$

**(ii) Output voltage:**

$$V_{out} = -\dfrac{q}{C_f} = -\dfrac{5 \times 10^{-10}}{2 \times 10^{-9}} = -0.25 \, \text{V}$$

**(iii) Lower cutoff frequency:**

$$f_{-3dB} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_f C_f} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 50 \times 10^6 \times 2 \times 10^{-9}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 0.1} \approx 1.59 \, \text{Hz}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the various sub-systems of a signal conditioning circuit for an accelerometer. With a circuit diagram, derive the output voltage equation of an inverting charge amplifier. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare piezoelectric, piezoresistive, and capacitive (MEMS) accelerometers. State at least two vehicle applications where each is preferred. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Signal Conditioning Sub-Systems and Charge Amplifier Derivation:**

The signal conditioning block for an accelerometer performs the following functions in sequence:

1. **Impedance matching / Buffering:** Converts the high-impedance sensor output to a low-impedance signal using a unity-gain buffer (e.g., JFET or CMOS input op-amp).
2. **Amplification:** A programmable gain amplifier (PGA) scales the signal to the ±10 V DAQ input range.
3. **Filtering:** A low-pass anti-aliasing filter (Butterworth, 4th–8th order) limits bandwidth; a high-pass filter removes DC offset.
4. **Integration (optional):** For NVH analysis, integration converts acceleration to velocity and displacement.
5. **Isolation:** An isolation amplifier breaks ground loops between the vehicle chassis and the DAQ.

**Charge Amplifier Derivation:**

A charge amplifier consists of an op-amp with feedback impedance $Z_f$ (capacitor $C_f$ in parallel with resistor $R_f$). The piezoelectric sensor is modelled as a charge source $q_s$ with parallel capacitance $C_s$.

**Step 1:** By the virtual ground concept, the inverting input is at 0 V.

**Step 2:** All charge from the sensor flows into the feedback capacitor:

$$q_s = C_f \cdot V_{out} + \dfrac{V_{out}}{R_f} \cdot t$$

**Step 3:** In steady state at mid-band frequencies, the resistor is negligible, so:

$$V_{out} = -\dfrac{q_s}{C_f} = -\dfrac{S_q \cdot a_{in}}{C_f}$$

**Step 4:** The lower cutoff (high-pass corner) is:

$$f_{-3dB} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_f C_f}$$

**Key advantage:** The output is **independent of the cable and sensor capacitance** $C_s$, eliminating cable-noise problems in long cable runs in vehicle test cells.

**(b) Comparative Table and Applications:**

| Feature | Piezoelectric | Piezoresistive | Capacitive (MEMS) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Output | Charge (pC) | Voltage (mV/V) | Digital / Voltage |
| Power | None | Required | Required |
| DC response | No | Yes | Yes |
| Frequency | 1 Hz – 20 kHz | DC – 2 kHz | DC – 5 kHz |
| Temperature | Up to 250 °C | Up to 125 °C | Up to 125 °C |
| Cost | High | Medium | Low |

**Applications:**
- **Piezoelectric:** Engine block combustion vibration, transmission NVH, modal analysis.
- **Piezoresistive:** Crash dummy instrumentation, airbag deployment, high-g crash sensors, suspension testing.
- **Capacitive MEMS:** ESC (Electronic Stability Control), airbag ECU, ride-height sensors, ADAS IMU.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Board Exams on this Topic:**
>
> 1. **Units confusion (1 mark lost):** Students often write $S_q$ in pC/g but plug values in C/m/s². Always state and use consistent units: convert g to m/s² using **1 g = 9.81 m/s²** if the formula requires SI.
> 2. **Forgetting the negative sign in charge amplifier output (½ mark):** The op-amp is inverting. Write $V_{out} = -q/C_f$, not $V_{out} = q/C_f$.
> 3. **Confusing natural frequency and operating range (2 marks):** The accelerometer is usable **below** resonance, not above. State "operating frequency $f < f_n/3$" explicitly.
> 4. **Skipping the virtual ground assumption in charge amplifier derivation (1 mark):** Always write "Since the non-inverting terminal is grounded and the op-amp is in ideal configuration, the inverting terminal is at virtual ground (0 V)."
> 5. **Not relating the sensor to a vehicle application (1 mark):** KTU examiners reward application-oriented answers. Always state the vehicle context (engine NVH, ride comfort, crash test).
> 6. **Mixing up HPF and LPF corner frequencies (1 mark):** The charge amplifier's $R_f C_f$ forms a **high-pass filter** with cutoff $f_c = 1/(2\pi R_f C_f)$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- An **accelerometer** is a second-order SDOF transducer with a seismic mass-spring-damper, and its transfer function peaks at $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$.
- The **piezoelectric accelerometer** generates charge $q = d \cdot m \cdot a$ (direct piezoelectric effect); requires a **charge amplifier** for signal conditioning.
- The **piezoresistive accelerometer** uses a **Wheatstone bridge** of piezoresistors on a silicon beam; can measure DC and very low frequencies.
- The **MEMS capacitive accelerometer** is the modern industry standard for automotive ECUs (airbag, ESC) due to its low cost, small size, and integrated ASIC.
- The **charge amplifier output** is $V_{out} = -q/C_f$ and is **independent of cable capacitance** — a major advantage in long-cable vehicle test setups.
- The **lower cutoff frequency** of a charge amplifier is $f_{-3dB} = 1/(2\pi R_f C_f)$, typically 0.1–10 Hz.
- The **usable bandwidth** for acceleration measurement is $f < f_n/3$ (to limit error to < 12%).
- **Integration** in the time domain converts acceleration to velocity and displacement, essential for NVH analysis.
- **Filter selection** is critical: low-pass for anti-aliasing (set at < ½ sampling rate); high-pass for DC removal (set to exclude unwanted low-frequency drift).
- **TEDS (IEEE 1451.4)** allows plug-and-play sensor identification and auto-calibration loading — a smart-sensor concept.
- Always state **application context** in KTU answers: engine NVH, ride comfort, crash test, modal analysis.
- KTU 2024 examiners test both **principle** and **application**, so memorize the comparative table in Section 5.2 B.
- The **gravitational constant g = 9.81 m/s²** is the conversion unit from g to SI.
- A typical charge amplifier uses an op-amp with **JFET or CMOS input** to handle the high source impedance of piezoelectric sensors.
- **Servo accelerometers** are the most accurate but the most expensive; used in navigation and laboratory calibration, not mass-market automotive.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamic Cornering Fatigue and Dynamic Radial Fatigue Tests (Procedure)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT724 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Dynamic Cornering Fatigue and Dynamic Radial Fatigue Tests (Procedure)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Dynamic Tyre Fatigue Testing — The Big Picture

In real-world operation, a pneumatic tyre is not a static component. Every time a vehicle turns a corner, the sidewall of the tyre undergoes **repeated flexing** as the tread contact patch is sheared laterally against the road. Similarly, every time the vehicle passes over a bump or undulation, the tyre's carcass experiences **repeated radial compression and expansion**. Over millions of such cycles, microscopic cracks initiate in the rubber compounds and propagate through the textile/steel belts, eventually leading to catastrophic failure (belt separation, sidewall blow-out, or ply cord rupture).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Dynamic Fatigue Testing** of tyres is a category of accelerated laboratory durability testing in which a test tyre is mounted on a free-spinning test wheel, run against a high-speed rotating road-wheel (drum) under continuously applied cyclic loads and slip angles, until a defined end-point (e.g., tread separation, cord breakage, or a pre-set cycle count) is reached. The two principal variants are **Dynamic Radial Fatigue Test (DRFT)** — which applies cyclic radial load — and **Dynamic Cornering Fatigue Test (DCFT)** — which applies cyclic lateral (cornering) load.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Gym Workout for a Tyre"

Imagine taking a paper clip and bending it back and forth repeatedly. After 30–40 cycles, the metal *work-hardens*, develops a tiny crack at the bend line, and ultimately **snaps**. A tyre is no different:

- The **rubber + steel + textile composite** is the paper clip.
- The **road drum** is your hand.
- The **cyclic load (radial or lateral)** is the bending action.
- The **fatigue life (in hours or kilometres)** is the number of bends before the clip breaks.

| Test Type | Direction of Repeated Stress | Real-World Equivalent | Primary Failure Mode Detected |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dynamic Radial Fatigue | Vertical (normal to wheel axis) | Driving over potholes, speed bumps, cobblestones | Belt-edge separation, ply-cord flex break |
| Dynamic Cornering Fatigue | Lateral (perpendicular to rolling direction) | Taking repeated turns (city driving, slalom, racing) | Shoulder separation, sidewall flex cracking |

## 1.3 Standardization & Codification

> [!NOTE]
> Both tests are governed by international standards that specify test speeds, inflation pressures, applied loads, ambient conditions, and pass/fail criteria. The most commonly referenced standards are:
> - **SAE J1015** — Dynamic Radial Fatigue Test Procedure for Passenger Car Tyres
> - **SAE J1092** — Dynamic Cornering Fatigue Test Procedure for Passenger Car Tyres
> - **ISO 10455** — Truck and bus tyres — Methods of testing endurance and dynamic load
> - **JATMA / ETRTO** — Equivalent Japanese / European standards

A KTU examiner expects you to know **what** is measured, **why** the test is conducted, and the **sequential procedure** of the test rig.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Common Test Rig Architecture

Both DRFT and DCFT use a similar infrastructure, differing mainly in the **direction of the applied load** and the **orientation of the test wheel** with respect to the drum.

**Universal Test Rig Components:**

1. **Road-Wheel (Drum):** A large, smooth steel cylinder, typically **1.7 m to 3.0 m** in diameter, with a crowned surface to keep the tyre centred. Driven by a high-power electric motor at controlled test speeds (commonly **50–120 km/h** equivalent).
2. **Test-Wheel Spindle:** A free-rolling, precision-bearing-mounted spindle to which the test wheel/tyre assembly is bolted.
3. **Loading Mechanism:** Either a hydraulic actuator (closed-loop, servo-controlled) or a dead-weight + lever system that applies the rated load.
4. **Deflection & Slip-Angle Measurement System:** LVDTs (Linear Variable Differential Transformers) measure carcass deflection; optical encoders or laser sensors measure slip angle.
5. **Temperature Control Chamber (Optional):** Maintains ambient at **38 °C ± 3 °C** for repeatability.
6. **Auto-Shutdown System:** Trips the motor the instant a pressure drop, abnormal temperature rise, or a defined cycle count is detected.

## 2.2 Dynamic Radial Fatigue Test (DRFT) — Theoretical Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **Objective:** To evaluate the carcass integrity, belt adhesion, and ply-cord fatigue life under simulated vertical road-input loading.

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

1. The test tyre is **conditioned** (warmed up) at the test speed for 30 minutes to stabilize internal temperatures.
2. A **steady-state radial load** equal to the tyre's maximum load rating (as printed on the sidewall) is applied, deflecting the carcass.
3. An additional **cyclic radial load** of magnitude equal to the steady load (i.e., peak-to-peak amplitude equals the rated load) is superimposed via a servo-hydraulic actuator.
4. The cycle frequency is matched to the drum speed: at a drum surface speed of **75 km/h** with a 1.7 m drum, the cycle frequency is approximately:

$$f_{rad} = \frac{v}{2\pi R_{drum}} \quad \text{[Hz]}$$

5. The test is run continuously until **failure** (visible separation, cord break, or pressure loss) or a **predefined cycle limit** (typically 100+ hours ≈ 8,000 km equivalent).

## 2.3 Dynamic Cornering Fatigue Test (DCFT) — Theoretical Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Objective:** To evaluate the shoulder and sidewall integrity of the tyre under repeated lateral (cornering) loading.

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

1. The test wheel is **canted** at a specified **test slip angle**, classically **± 5° to ± 15°**, relative to the drum's rolling plane.
2. A **steady lateral (cornering) force** corresponding to the maximum rated load cornering coefficient is applied through a calibrated loading yoke.
3. The tyre is run at the test speed while the **slip angle is cycled** (sinusoidally) between two extreme values (e.g., −7° to +7° about a mean of 0°).
4. The **lateral force generated** by the slip angle is the input to the sidewall; its amplitude is monitored by a load cell in the loading yoke.
5. The test continues until **failure** or a **predefined hour limit** (typically 50 hours minimum per the SAE J1092 protocol).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter / Formula | Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Test-Wheel Speed (Drum) | $v = \pi D_{drum} N_{drum}$ | m/s | $D_{drum}$ = drum diameter, $N_{drum}$ = rev/s |
| 2 | Cycle Frequency (Radial) | $f_{rad} = \dfrac{v}{2\pi R_{drum}}$ | Hz | One cycle = one drum revolution |
| 3 | Slip Angle | $\alpha = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{v_{lateral}}{v_{longitudinal}}\right)$ | degrees or rad | The angle between wheel heading and velocity vector |
| 4 | Cornering Force | $F_{c} = C_{\alpha} \cdot \alpha$ | N | $C_{\alpha}$ = cornering stiffness (N/deg) |
| 5 | Dynamic Load Factor | $DLF = \dfrac{F_{dynamic}}{F_{static}}$ | dimensionless | DLF > 1 indicates dynamic amplification |
| 6 | Cumulative Damage (Miner's Rule) | $D = \sum \dfrac{n_{i}}{N_{i}}$ | dimensionless | $n_i$ = cycles at level $i$, $N_i$ = cycles to failure at level $i$ |
| 7 | Equivalent Test Distance | $L_{eq} = v \cdot t_{test}$ | km | $t_{test}$ in hours, $v$ in km/h |
| 8 | Test-Wheel Load | $W = P \cdot A_{contact}$ | N | $P$ = inflation pressure, $A_{contact}$ = contact patch area |
| 9 | Carcass Deflection | $\delta = \delta_{rated} \cdot \dfrac{W_{test}}{W_{rated}}$ | mm | Linear approximation for small loads |
| 10 | Endurance Time Index | $ETI = \dfrac{\text{Hours to failure}}{\text{Specified min. hours}}$ | dimensionless | ETI ≥ 1.0 = pass |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The slip angle $\alpha$ is measured in **degrees** in most laboratory standards but in **radians** in theoretical derivations. Always check the units of $C_{\alpha}$ before multiplying — they MUST be consistent.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **OEM Tyre Validation:** Every new tyre design undergoes ≥ 100 hours of DRFT + 50 hours of DCFT before it is homologated.
- **Fleet Operator Risk Management:** Used to compare brands for mining, military, and commercial fleet procurement.
- **Material R&D:** A tool to evaluate new rubber compounds, cap/base ply configurations, and belt geometries.
- **Forensic Failure Analysis:** When a field failure occurs, the test data of the suspect tyre is compared to baseline DRFT/DCFT results to identify manufacturing or design defects.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Cycle Frequency from First Principles

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Goal:** Express the radial fatigue cycle frequency $f_{rad}$ purely in terms of vehicle-equivalent test speed $v$ and drum radius $R_{drum}$.

**Given Data (standard test conditions per SAE J1015):**
- Drum diameter $D_{drum} = 1.707$ m  →  $R_{drum} = 0.8535$ m
- Test speed $v = 75$ km/h

**Step 1 — Convert speed to SI units:**

$$
v = 75 \text{ km/h} = \frac{75 \times 1000}{3600} = 20.833 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute drum circumference:**

$$
C_{drum} = 2\pi R_{drum} = 2\pi (0.8535) = 5.362 \text{ m/rev}
$$

**Step 3 — Number of drum revolutions per second:**

$$
N_{drum} = \frac{v}{C_{drum}} = \frac{20.833}{5.362} = 3.886 \text{ rev/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Because each drum revolution produces exactly ONE complete radial load cycle on the tyre (down-stroke at the contact patch entry, up-stroke at the exit), the cycle frequency equals the rotation frequency:**

$$
f_{rad} = N_{drum} = 3.886 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 5 — Equivalent hourly cycle count:**

$$
N_{cycles/hour} = 3.886 \times 3600 = 13{,}990 \text{ cycles/hour}
$$

**Step 6 — For a 100-hour test:**

$$
N_{total} = 13{,}990 \times 100 = 1.399 \times 10^{6} \text{ cycles}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A 100-hour DRFT test subjects the tyre to ≈ 1.4 million flex cycles. This approximates the cumulative flex cycles a passenger car tyre experiences in 30,000–40,000 km of real-world driving — which is the warranty mileage of most OEM tyres.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Pass/Fail Decision Framework

| Test Parameter | Symbol | DRFT Typical Value | DCFT Typical Value | KTU-Critical? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Inflation Pressure | $P$ | 180–220 kPa | 180–220 kPa | ✅ Yes |
| Test Speed (Drum) | $v$ | 75 km/h | 50–75 km/h | ✅ Yes |
| Applied Load | $W$ | Max. rated load | Max. rated load × cornering factor | ✅ Yes |
| Slip Angle | $\alpha$ | 0° (radial only) | ± 5° to ± 15° | ✅ Yes |
| Cycle Frequency | $f$ | ≈ 3.9 Hz | 0.2–1.0 Hz (slow sinusoidal) | ⚠️ Sometimes |
| Test Duration | $t$ | 100–250 h | 50–100 h | ✅ Yes |
| Ambient Temperature | $T$ | 38 °C ± 3 °C | 38 °C ± 3 °C | ⚠️ Sometimes |
| Pass Criterion | — | No failure at min. cycles | No failure at min. cycles | ✅ Yes |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A passenger car tyre is to be DRFT-tested on a 1.7-m diameter drum at 80 km/h. Calculate the equivalent test distance covered in 120 hours. If the test speed were increased to 100 km/h, by what factor does the cycle frequency change?

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Equivalent test distance at 80 km/h:**

$$
L_{eq} = v \times t = 80 \text{ km/h} \times 120 \text{ h} = 9{,}600 \text{ km}
$$

**Step 2 — Cycle frequency at 80 km/h:**

$$
v = \frac{80 \times 1000}{3600} = 22.222 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
R_{drum} = 0.85 \text{ m}, \quad C_{drum} = 2\pi (0.85) = 5.341 \text{ m}
$$

$$
f_{1} = \frac{22.222}{5.341} = 4.161 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 3 — Cycle frequency at 100 km/h:**

$$
v = 27.778 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
f_{2} = \frac{27.778}{5.341} = 5.201 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 4 — Frequency ratio:**

$$
\frac{f_{2}}{f_{1}} = \frac{5.201}{4.161} = 1.25
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Increasing test speed by 25% (80 → 100 km/h) increases the cycle frequency by the SAME factor (1.25×). This linearity is the **physical basis** of accelerated life testing — you compress months of real-world flexing into days of lab time without changing the failure mechanism.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Q: Differentiate between Dynamic Radial Fatigue Test (DRFT) and Dynamic Cornering Fatigue Test (DCFT).**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | DRFT | DCFT |
|---|---|---|
| Direction of load | Vertical (radial) | Lateral (cornering) |
| Test wheel orientation | Parallel to drum axis | Canted at slip angle α |
| Loading type | Steady + cyclic radial load | Steady lateral force at slip angle |
| Standard | SAE J1015 | SAE J1092 |
| Failure mode detected | Belt separation, ply flex break | Shoulder separation, sidewall cracking |
| Typical duration | 100–250 h | 50–100 h |
| Slip angle | 0° | ± 5° to ± 15° |

DRFT simulates **bump / undulation loading**, while DCFT simulates **cornering / turn loading**.

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Q: List any SIX major components of a dynamic tyre fatigue test rig.**

**Model Answer:**
1. Motor-driven road wheel (drum)
2. Variable frequency drive (speed controller)
3. Test-wheel spindle with precision bearings
4. Loading mechanism (hydraulic actuator or dead-weight lever)
5. Load cell (force transducer)
6. LVDT (deflection sensor)
7. Optical encoder (slip angle sensor)
8. Data acquisition system (DAQ)
9. Temperature control chamber
10. Emergency shutdown system

*(Any six scored full marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Format

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: With the aid of a neat schematic, explain the procedure of the Dynamic Radial Fatigue Test for a passenger car tyre. State the relevant SAE standard and the pass/fail criterion. (14 Marks)**

**Model Solution (Step-by-Step):**

**(a) Objective and Standard — 3 Marks**

The Dynamic Radial Fatigue Test evaluates the ability of a tyre's carcass and belt package to withstand repeated vertical loading encountered during normal road use. The test is conducted as per **SAE J1015** (Society of Automotive Engineers standard J1015). The test is considered **passed** if the tyre completes the specified minimum number of hours (typically 100 h for passenger tyres) without any visible tread separation, ply-cord breakage, or sustained pressure loss exceeding 10%.

**(b) Test Rig Description — 4 Marks**

The test rig consists of:
- A **motor-driven steel drum** of diameter 1.7 m, surface-crowned, rotated at a peripheral speed equivalent to **75 km/h**.
- A **free-rolling test-wheel spindle** on which the test wheel-tyre assembly is mounted.
- A **servo-hydraulic actuator** that applies a programmed steady + cyclic radial load.
- A **load cell** to verify the applied load magnitude in real time.
- An **LVDT** to measure carcass deflection under load.
- A **DAQ system** for continuous data logging.
- An **emergency shutdown circuit** that stops the drum on detecting abnormal pressure drop, temperature rise, or excessive vibration.

**(c) Step-by-Step Procedure — 5 Marks**

1. **Pre-Test Inspection:** Examine the new tyre for manufacturing defects; record the serial number, size, and DOT date code. Mount on a clean test wheel and inflate to the rated pressure (e.g., 220 kPa).
2. **Conditioning / Warm-Up:** Run the tyre against the drum at 75 km/h under rated load for 30 minutes to stabilize internal temperatures and remove mould-release residues.
3. **Application of Steady Load:** Apply a steady radial load equal to the **maximum load rating** of the tyre, as marked on the sidewall. Allow the carcass deflection to stabilize.
4. **Application of Cyclic Load:** Superimpose a sinusoidal cyclic radial load whose **peak-to-peak amplitude equals the rated load** (i.e., load swings from 0 to 2 × rated load or from 0.5 × rated to 1.5 × rated, per standard). The cycle frequency is dictated by the drum rotational speed.
5. **Continuous Monitoring:** Record carcass deflection, surface temperature, inflation pressure, and load-cell output at 1-second intervals. Monitor for any abnormal readings.
6. **Test Termination:** Stop the test on detection of: (i) tread separation, (ii) ply cord breakage, (iii) pressure drop > 10% sustained for > 1 minute, (iv) abnormal temperature rise > 100 °C, or (v) completion of the prescribed cycle count.
7. **Post-Test Inspection:** Cool the tyre to ambient, deflate, dismount, and section it for visual examination of the belt edges, ply turn-ups, and sidewall for cracking.

**(d) Data Reporting — 2 Marks**

The final report must include: total hours run, total cycles endured, maximum temperature recorded, mode of failure (if any), and a Pass/Fail declaration.

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Describe the procedure, instrumentation, and significance of the Dynamic Cornering Fatigue Test (DCFT). Compare it briefly with the Dynamic Radial Fatigue Test. (14 Marks)**

**Model Solution (Step-by-Step):**

**(a) Objective and Standard — 3 Marks**

The DCFT evaluates the durability of a tyre's shoulder and sidewall under repeated lateral (cornering) loading. It is governed by **SAE J1092**. The test is **passed** if the tyre runs for the prescribed minimum number of hours (typically 50 h for passenger car tyres) without shoulder separation, sidewall flex cracking, or sustained pressure loss.

**(b) Instrumentation — 4 Marks**

- **Canted test-wheel fixture** allowing precise slip-angle setting (typically ± 7° about zero).
- **Loading yoke** applying a steady lateral force equal to the rated cornering load.
- **Optical encoder / laser sensor** to verify the instantaneous slip angle.
- **Load cell** in the loading yoke to monitor lateral force continuously.
- **Temperature sensors** (thermocouples embedded in the tread and sidewall).
- **DAQ system** and **auto-shutdown** for safety.

**(c) Step-by-Step Procedure — 5 Marks**

1. Mount the test tyre on the fixture, inflate to rated pressure.
2. Set the slip angle to the **mean value** (often 0°, with cyclic excursion of ± 5° to ± 7°).
3. Apply the **steady lateral load** equal to the tyre's rated cornering capacity.
4. Bring the drum to test speed (typically 50 km/h).
5. Begin the **sinusoidal slip-angle cycle** at a frequency of 0.5–1 Hz, depending on the standard.
6. Run continuously for the specified duration while monitoring force, angle, temperature, and pressure.
7. Terminate on failure or hour limit, and perform post-test dissection.

**(d) Comparison with DRFT — 2 Marks**

| Aspect | DRFT | DCFT |
|---|---|---|
| Load direction | Vertical | Lateral |
| Test focus | Belt / ply integrity | Shoulder / sidewall integrity |
| Slip angle | 0° | ± 5° to ± 15° |
| Standard | SAE J1015 | SAE J1092 |
| Real-world analog | Bumps, undulations | Cornering maneuvers |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. ❌ Confusing **slip angle** (α) with **camber angle** (γ). Slip angle is the angle between the wheel's heading and the velocity vector of the contact patch; camber is the tilt of the wheel about the radial axis. They are physically different.
> 2. ❌ Writing "speed" in **km/h** in a formula that uses SI units (m/s). Always convert.
> 3. ❌ Forgetting to mention the **standard** (SAE J1015 / J1092) in long answers. KTU examiners explicitly allocate marks for this.
> 4. ❌ Stating that DRFT and DCFT are "the same test." They are NOT — they subject the tyre to orthogonal load directions and detect different failure modes.
> 5. ❌ Skipping the **warm-up/conditioning** step. It is a mandatory procedure and is worth 1 mark on its own.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:**

- ✅ **DRFT** = Dynamic **Radial** Fatigue Test = cyclic **vertical** load = **SAE J1015** = simulates **bump loading** = detects **belt-edge separation**.
- ✅ **DCFT** = Dynamic **Cornering** Fatigue Test = cyclic **lateral** load at **slip angle** = **SAE J1092** = simulates **cornering** = detects **shoulder separation**.
- ✅ Both tests use a **rotating road-wheel drum** and a **free-rolling test-wheel spindle**.
- ✅ Test tyre is **warmed up for 30 minutes** before the cyclic loading phase begins.
- ✅ **Pass criterion** = tyre runs the prescribed hours with no visible separation, cord break, or pressure loss.
- ✅ Cycle frequency $f_{rad} = v / (2\pi R_{drum})$ Hz.
- ✅ Slip angle $\alpha$ in **degrees** is used with $C_{\alpha}$ in **N/deg** — keep units consistent.
- ✅ Equivalent test distance $L_{eq} = v \times t$ — used to correlate lab hours with road kilometres.
- ✅ Instrument chain: **Motor → Drum → Tyre → LVDT/Load Cell/Encoder → DAQ → PC → Shutdown**.
- ✅ Mention **standards (SAE / ISO / JATMA)** wherever applicable — KTU examiners reward this.
- ✅ The tests are **accelerated life tests** — they do NOT change the failure mechanism, only the rate.
- ✅ **Minor's Rule** can be applied to combine multiple load levels for cumulative damage calculation: $D = \sum (n_i / N_i)$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Impact Test (Road Hazard Impact Test for Wheel and Tyre Assemblies, Test Procedures, Failure Criteria, Performance Criteria)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT724 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Impact Test (Road Hazard Impact Test for Wheel and Tyre Assemblies, Test Procedures, Failure Criteria, Performance Criteria)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 : Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Road Hazard Impact Test** (also known as the *cornering fatigue predecessor test* or *impact strength test*) is a standardized destructive qualification test performed on a fully assembled **wheel and pneumatic tyre assembly** to evaluate its ability to withstand a single, sudden, localized impact energy representative of striking a road hazard such as a **pothole, kerb stone, debris, or expansion joint** at typical driving speeds, without catastrophic loss of inflation pressure, fragmentation, or detachment of the tyre bead from the rim.

The procedure is governed by international standards such as **ISO 7141**, **SAE J175**, **JWL (Japan Light Alloy Wheel)**, **TÜV**, and the Indian standard **IS 9438**. The test is a *Type Approval / Homologation* test, meaning every wheel design must pass it before series production.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This topic directly maps to **CO4** of OEMUT724 – *"Apply standardized testing procedures to evaluate structural integrity of vehicle subsystems."* A typical 14-mark university question focuses on **(a) test procedure** and **(b) failure/performance criteria** — exactly what this module covers.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hitting a Curb" Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car at **40 km/h** on a poorly maintained road. Suddenly, the front wheel strikes a **sharp-edged pothole** approximately 80 mm deep. The kinetic energy of the unsprung mass (wheel + tyre) at the moment of contact must be dissipated in **a fraction of a second** over a tiny contact patch.

The Impact Test **simulates this exact event in a controlled laboratory**, but with the wheel **stationary** and the energy delivered by a **falling striker (pendulum or drop weight)**. Just as dropping a drinking glass on a tile floor shatters it but dropping it on a carpet does not, the test checks whether the wheel can *absorb* the impact energy *elastically and plastically* (deforming to absorb energy) rather than *catastrophically* (cracking or exploding).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a Tyre is Mounted:** A bare wheel would behave very differently than an inflated assembly. The compressed air inside the tyre acts as a **pneumatic spring**, storing part of the impact energy and cushioning the rim. Therefore, the test is *always* performed on a **wheel + inflated tyre assembly**, never on the bare wheel.

### 1.3 Key Physical Metrics at a Glance

| Parameter | Typical Range | Standard Reference |
|---|---|---|
| **Striker (Pendulum) Mass** | **50 – 100 kg** | JWL / ISO 7141 |
| **Drop Height Equivalent** | **150 – 300 mm** | IS 9438 |
| **Impact Energy** | **100 – 230 J** | SAE J175 |
| **Test Tyre Pressure** | **200 kPa (2.0 bar)** | ISO 7141 |
| **Gravity Constant** | **9.81 m/s²** | SI base |
| **Impact Point Locations** | **3 to 5 zones** on rim flange | JWL / VIA |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 : Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Impact Tests on Wheels

There are **three principal variants** of the impact test, each simulating a different real-world scenario:

1. **Radial Impact Test (90° to wheel face)** — Simulates hitting a square-edged pothole head-on. The striker hits the wheel **perpendicular to the rim flange**. This is the **most common and most severe** test.
2. **Lateral (Tangential) Impact Test** — Simulates a side swipe against a kerb. The striker contacts the **outer rim flange edge**.
3. **Pendulum Impact at 13° / 30°** — Many standards (JWL, TÜV) use a **pendulum swinging through an arc**, striking the wheel at a defined angle.

### 2.2 The Physics Behind the Test — Energy, Not Force

The test is **energy-controlled**, not force-controlled. The striker is raised to a height that gives it a **gravitational potential energy equal to the specified impact energy**. Upon release, this PE converts to KE at the moment of impact.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Principle:** The wheel must be capable of absorbing the specified impact energy through **elastic + plastic deformation** without crack initiation or bead unseating. Since the impact duration is short (~5–20 ms), the loading rate is enormous, making the test essentially a **high-strain-rate test**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = m \cdot g \cdot h$ | Impact energy from drop test (m = striker mass, h = drop height) | Joules (J) |
| 2 | $E = m \cdot g \cdot L \cdot (1 - \cos\theta)$ | Impact energy from pendulum swing (L = arm length, $\theta$ = release angle) | Joules (J) |
| 3 | $v = \sqrt{2 \cdot g \cdot h}$ | Striker velocity at impact (free-fall equivalent) | m/s |
| 4 | $E_k = \frac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot v^2$ | Kinetic energy at moment of impact (verification) | Joules (J) |
| 5 | $F_{avg} = \frac{E}{\Delta x}$ | Average impact force (E absorbed, $\Delta x$ = wheel deflection) | Newtons (N) |
| 6 | $P_{loss}(\%) = \frac{P_{initial} - P_{final}}{P_{initial}} \times 100$ | Percentage pressure loss after impact (failure criterion) | % |
| 7 | $\sigma_{dyn} = K \cdot \sigma_{static}$ | Dynamic stress amplification factor (K ≈ 1.5 – 2.5) | MPa |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The **drop height** $h$ for a given target energy $E$ is back-calculated as $h = E/(mg)$. If a standard mandates 180 J for a 17-inch wheel, with an 80 kg striker: $h = 180 / (80 \times 9.81) = 0.229$ m, i.e., ~230 mm. Wheel manufacturers use this to **set up their test rigs**.

### 2.4 Test Parameters and Their Engineering Significance

- **Striker Mass Selection:** Heavier strikers at lower heights deliver the same energy with **lower peak velocity**, simulating heavier vehicles / slower hits. Lighter strikers at higher heights give **higher velocity, lower momentum** impacts.
- **Tyre Pressure Sensitivity:** Higher pressure makes the tyre *stiffer*, transferring more energy to the rim → more severe test. Lower pressure allows more air compression → cushions the impact.
- **Impact Location:** Most standards specify striking the **spoke area or rim flange** at the weakest expected cross-section. Testing multiple points ensures the design is robust in all quadrants.
- **Temperature Conditioning:** Some standards require the wheel to be **cold-soaked at –30 °C or heated to +50 °C** before testing, since aluminium alloy strength varies with temperature.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 : Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Impact Energy from Drop Height (Vertical Drop)

**Given:** A striker of mass $m$ is raised to a height $h$ above the wheel and released under gravity.

**Step 1 — Energy at the top of the drop:**
The striker is momentarily at rest at height $h$, so its kinetic energy is zero. All energy is **gravitational potential energy**:

$$
E_{top} = m \cdot g \cdot h
$$

**Step 2 — Energy at the moment of impact:**
Assuming no air resistance, by **conservation of mechanical energy**, the PE at the top equals the KE just before impact:

$$
E_{top} = E_{k,impact}
$$

$$
m \cdot g \cdot h = \frac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot v_{impact}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Solving for impact velocity:**

$$
v_{impact} = \sqrt{2 \cdot g \cdot h}
$$

**Step 4 — Therefore, the impact energy delivered to the wheel is:**

$$
E = m \cdot g \cdot h
$$

> **Logic Note:** The mass $m$ cancels out in the velocity derivation but remains in the energy equation, showing that *energy depends on both mass and height*, while *velocity depends only on height*.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Impact Energy from Pendulum Swing

**Given:** A pendulum of mass $m$ and arm length $L$ is released from a horizontal angle $\theta$ from the vertical.

**Step 1 — Height drop of the pendulum bob:**
The bob rises by a vertical height $h$ when the arm is displaced by $\theta$ from the vertical. Using the geometry of a pendulum:

$$
h = L - L\cos\theta = L(1 - \cos\theta)
$$

**Step 2 — Substituting into the energy equation $E = mgh$:**

$$
E = m \cdot g \cdot L \cdot (1 - \cos\theta)
$$

**Step 3 — Worked Numerical Example:**

Let $m = 50$ kg, $L = 1.5$ m, $\theta = 30°$.

$$
h = 1.5 \times (1 - \cos 30°) = 1.5 \times (1 - 0.8660) = 0.201 \text{ m}
$$

$$
E = 50 \times 9.81 \times 0.201 = 98.6 \text{ J}
$$

**Step 4 — Equivalence to drop height:**
For the same energy, the equivalent vertical drop height is:

$$
h_{eq} = \frac{98.6}{50 \times 9.81} = 0.201 \text{ m} = 201 \text{ mm}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Average Impact Force from Energy and Deflection

**Given:** The wheel deflects by $\Delta x$ under the impact before the striker comes to rest.

**Step 1 — Work-Energy Theorem:**
The work done by the impact force equals the kinetic energy absorbed by the wheel:

$$
W = F_{avg} \cdot \Delta x = E
$$

**Step 2 — Therefore:**

$$
F_{avg} = \frac{E}{\Delta x}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical Example:**
If $E = 180$ J and the wheel deflects by $\Delta x = 12$ mm = 0.012 m:

$$
F_{avg} = \frac{180}{0.012} = 15{,}000 \text{ N} = 15 \text{ kN}
$$

> **Logic Note:** This is the **average** force. Peak forces during a 10 ms impact are typically 2–3× higher due to the impulsive loading profile. A 15 kN average on a 17-inch wheel (~430 mm diameter) translates to a peak rim loading of several tonnes — explaining why aluminium wheels must be ductile.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Test Severity by Wheel Size

| Wheel Size (inches) | Striker Mass (kg) | Drop Height (mm) | Impact Energy (J) | Equivalent Drop Velocity (m/s) | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **13"** | 80 | 250 | 196 | 2.21 | Small cars (older) |
| **15"** | 80 | 180 | 141 | 1.88 | Compact sedans |
| **16"** | 80 | 200 | 157 | 1.98 | Mid-size sedans |
| **17"** | 80 | 230 | 180 | 2.12 | Premium sedans |
| **18"** | 80 | 250 | 196 | 2.21 | SUVs / Luxury |
| **19" – 20"** | 100 | 230 | 225 | 2.12 | Performance vehicles |

> **Pattern Recognition (Exam Tip):** Notice the **direct proportionality** between wheel size and required impact energy. Larger wheels have larger diameters, more mass, and are typically fitted to heavier vehicles — hence the energy requirement scales up.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Energy Partitioning During Impact

When the striker hits the wheel-tyre assembly, the total impact energy $E_{total}$ is distributed as follows:

$$
E_{total} = E_{elastic} + E_{plastic} + E_{air\ cushion} + E_{friction} + E_{crack\ surface}
$$

| Energy Sink | Typical Share | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| $E_{elastic}$ | **20 – 30 %** | Reversible deformation of rim and spokes |
| $E_{plastic}$ | **40 – 60 %** | Permanent bending of metal — *the desired energy sink* |
| $E_{air\ cushion}$ | **10 – 20 %** | Adiabatic compression of tyre inflation air |
| $E_{friction}$ | **2 – 5 %** | Internal damping, hysteresis of rubber |
| $E_{crack\ surface}$ | **0 % (must be zero)** | If non-zero → **FAILURE** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Designer's Goal:** Maximize $E_{plastic}$ and $E_{air\ cushion}$. A wheel that fails via $E_{crack\ surface}$ has insufficient ductility or has stress concentrations that act as crack initiators.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 : Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Impact Test Setup

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional interaction** between the major subsystems of a standard impact test rig (pendulum type, as per JWL/ISO 7141):

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Striker Pendulum Assembly] -->|Releases Kinetic Energy| B[Wheel-Tyre Fixture]
    B -->|Transmits Load| C[Reaction Base and Anvil]
    D[Pressure Supply Unit] -->|Inflates to Test Pressure| B
    E[High Speed Camera] -->|Captures Deformation| B
    F[Load Cell on Anvil] -->|Records Force Time History| G[Data Acquisition System]
    F --> G
    B --> G
    H[Displacement Transducer LVDT] -->|Measures Deflection| G
    G -->|Generates Report| I[Pass or Fail Decision Module]
    J[Climate Chamber] -.->|Conditions Temperature| B
    K[Control Console] -->|Sets Drop Height| A
    K -->|Commands Release| A
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The **striker pendulum** (A) is the energy source, the **wheel-tyre fixture** (B) is the test article, the **anvil** (C) is the rigid reaction base, and the **DAS** (G) collects force, displacement, and pressure data for pass/fail evaluation. Dotted lines indicate *conditioning* inputs (temperature, command signals), while solid arrows show *energy or data* flow.

---

### 4.2 Sequential Test Procedure Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    n1[Step 1: Receive Test Wheel Sample] --> n2[Step 2: Mount New Tyre and Valve]
    n2 --> n3[Step 3: Inflate Tyre to Test Pressure 200 kPa]
    n3 --> n4[Step 4: Allow Pressure Stabilization for 5 Minutes]
    n4 --> n5[Step 5: Mount Assembly on Fixture Anvil]
    n5 --> n6[Step 6: Set Striker Drop Height from Energy Table]
    n6 --> n7[Step 7: Position Impact Point at Specified Location]
    n7 --> n8[Step 8: Release Striker Pendulum]
    n8 --> n9[Step 9: Record Force Deflection and Pressure Data]
    n9 --> n10[Step 10: Wait 1 Minute and Recheck Pressure]
    n10 --> n11{Step 11: Apply Pass or Fail Criteria}
    n11 -->|Pass| n12[Step 12: Document and Stamp Sample as Approved]
    n11 -->|Fail| n13[Step 13: Conduct Root Cause Analysis and Report Defect]
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** This is the canonical **12-step flow** used by wheel manufacturers worldwide. The **decision diamond** (n11) at Step 11 is the critical evaluation point where *failure criteria* and *performance criteria* (covered in Section 5) are applied.

---

### 4.3 Wheel Cross-Section Showing Typical Impact Zones

The following block diagram shows the **standard impact zones** on a typical automotive wheel, as specified by JWL / ISO 7141:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph wheel[Wheel and Tyre Assembly Cross Section]
        direction TB
        zone1[Impact Zone 1: Front Spoke Region]
        zone2[Impact Zone 2: Between Spokes Disc Window]
        zone3[Impact Zone 3: Rear Spoke Region]
        zone4[Impact Zone 4: Rim Flange Outer Edge]
        zone5[Impact Zone 5: Rim Drop Centre Bead Seat]
    end
    tyre[Compressed Air Spring Tyre] -.->|Cushions Impact| zone1
    tyre -.->|Cushions Impact| zone2
    tyre -.->|Cushions Impact| zone3
    tyre -.->|Cushions Impact| zone4
    tyre -.->|Cushions Impact| zone5
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The wheel has **5 standard impact zones**, but most standards mandate testing the **3 most critical zones** (typically Zones 1, 2, and 4). The **tyre acts as a pneumatic spring** in all cases, cushioning the rim. Notice that the impact is *never* on the centre hub — this is the structural backbone of the wheel.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 : KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
**Define the Road Hazard Impact Test for wheel and tyre assemblies. State any two international standards governing this test. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **Road Hazard Impact Test** is a standardized destructive qualification test conducted on a fully assembled wheel and inflated pneumatic tyre to evaluate its ability to withstand a sudden, localized impact representative of hitting a pothole or kerb, **without catastrophic loss of air pressure, fragmentation, or bead unseating**.

Two governing international standards are:
1. **ISO 7141** – *Road vehicles – Wheels – Test methods for impact strength*
2. **SAE J175** – *Impact Test for Wheels* (Society of Automotive Engineers, USA)
   *(Also acceptable: JWL – Japan Light Alloy Wheel standard, IS 9438 – Indian Standard)*

> **Marking Pattern:** Definition (2 marks) + Standards (1 mark).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any four failure criteria used to declare a wheel as FAILED in the road hazard impact test. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A wheel is declared **FAILED** if any of the following is observed post-impact:

1. **Visible crack** on the rim, spokes, hub or disc, of length exceeding the value specified in the standard.
2. **Loss of inflation pressure** greater than **10% of initial test pressure** within 1 minute of impact.
3. **Tyre bead unseating** from the rim flange (partial or complete).
4. **Fragmentation** — any piece of the wheel breaking off and projecting beyond the safety enclosure.
5. *(Optional 5th)* Permanent plastic deformation of the rim flange exceeding the dimensional tolerance (typically > 1.5 mm).

> **Marking Pattern:** 4 criteria × 0.75 marks = 3 marks. Writing only 3 fetches 2.25 → rounded to 2 marks. *Always write at least 4 to be safe.*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — Alternative Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
**(a)** Explain in detail the **test procedure** for the road hazard impact test of wheel and tyre assemblies as per ISO 7141 / JWL, including test setup, parameters, and sequence of operations. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** Discuss the **failure criteria and performance criteria** for declaring the wheel as pass or fail. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### **Part (a) — Test Procedure (7 Marks)**

**1. Test Setup and Equipment (2 marks)**
- A **pendulum-type impact testing machine** with:
  - Striker mass: **80 – 100 kg** (cylindrical steel, with a defined striking edge radius).
  - Pendulum arm length: **1.0 – 1.5 m**.
  - Rigid **anvil / reaction base** to support the wheel hub.
- **Pressure inflation system** with regulator and gauge accurate to ±5 kPa.
- **Data acquisition system (DAS)** with load cell, LVDT displacement transducer, and high-speed camera.
- Optional: **climate chamber** for cold/hot conditioning.

**2. Preparation of Test Article (2 marks)**
- Fit a **new tyre** of specified size and type onto the wheel.
- Inflate to **test pressure of 200 kPa (2.0 bar)** unless otherwise specified.
- Allow pressure to stabilize for **at least 5 minutes**.
- Mount the wheel-tyre assembly on the fixture, ensuring the **hub is rigidly bolted** to the spindle.

**3. Selection of Impact Parameters (1.5 marks)**
- Determine the **required impact energy** from the standard's table (based on wheel size and PCD).
- Calculate the **drop height** $h = E / (m \cdot g)$.
- Mark the **impact point** on the wheel — typically 80–120 mm from the outer rim flange, on a spoke.

**4. Conducting the Test (1.5 marks)**
- Raise the pendulum striker to the calculated height.
- Release the striker; it swings down and impacts the wheel at the marked point.
- The DAS records the **force–time history** and **deflection–time history**.
- After impact, the wheel is allowed to settle for **1 minute**.

---

#### **Part (b) — Failure & Performance Criteria (7 Marks)**

**1. Failure Criteria (3.5 marks)**

The wheel **FAILS** if **any one** of the following is observed:

| # | Criterion | Quantitative Limit |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Crack propagation** | Any visible crack > specified length (typically 5 mm) |
| 2 | **Air pressure loss** | $> 10\%$ of initial test pressure within 1 minute |
| 3 | **Bead unseating** | Tyre bead lifts off the rim flange by more than 2 mm |
| 4 | **Fragmentation** | Any piece detached and ejected |
| 5 | **Permanent deformation** | Rim flange deformation > 1.5 mm |

**2. Performance Criteria (3.5 marks)**

The wheel **PASSES** if it satisfies **all** of the following:

| # | Criterion | Requirement |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **No visible cracking** | Dye-penetrant or visual inspection must show no cracks |
| 2 | **Air retention** | Pressure drop ≤ 10% within 1 minute post-impact |
| 3 | **Tyre remains seated** | Bead on rim flange, no lift-off |
| 4 | **Dimensional integrity** | Run-out, radial and lateral, within ISO limits |
| 5 | **No fragmentation** | Wheel remains a single piece |
| 6 | **Energy absorption** | Visible plastic deformation at impact point (proof of energy dissipation) |

> **Examiner's Logic:** A wheel that shows *no* plastic deformation after absorbing 180 J of energy is suspicious — either the energy went somewhere (e.g., into the fixture) or the measurement was wrong. *Some* permanent deformation is **expected and desired** as proof of energy dissipation.

---

#### **Question B — Alternative Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
**(a)** With the help of a **schematic / block diagram**, describe the **test setup and instrumentation** for the road hazard impact test of wheel and tyre assemblies. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** A wheel of 16-inch size is to be tested using an **80 kg striker pendulum** of arm length **1.5 m**. If the required impact energy is **157 J**, calculate **(i)** the drop height for a vertical drop test, and **(ii)** the release angle $\theta$ for the pendulum test. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### **Part (a) — Test Setup and Instrumentation (7 Marks)**

**1. Block Diagram Description (3 marks)**

The test rig consists of:

- **Striker Pendulum (A):** Steel mass 50–100 kg, with hemispherical or wedge-shaped striking edge, suspended from a rigid frame by a single pivot.
- **Wheel-Tyre Fixture (B):** The test article, mounted on a horizontal spindle bolted to the anvil. The hub is rigidly fixed; the wheel is free to deform.
- **Reaction Anvil (C):** A massive steel block (typically > 500 kg) to absorb reaction forces without vibrating.
- **Pressure System (D):** Compressed air supply, regulator, and digital pressure gauge.
- **Instrumentation (E, F, G, H):** Load cell behind the anvil, LVDT displacement transducer at the impact point, high-speed camera, and DAS.
- **Control Console (K):** Sets drop height / release angle, triggers release, and logs data.

**2. Instrumentation Functions (2 marks)**
- **Load cell** → records reaction force vs. time (F-t curve).
- **LVDT** → records rim deflection vs. time.
- **High-speed camera** (≥ 1000 fps) → visual record of deformation.
- **DAS** → integrates F-t curve to compute absorbed energy: $E_{abs} = \int F \, dt$.

**3. Calibration and Safety (2 marks)**
- The striker is calibrated annually using a **certified reference block**.
- The test area is enclosed by a **fragmentation safety shield** (perspex or steel mesh) — required because aluminium wheels can explode under impact.
- The **DAS trigger** is set to start recording 50 ms *before* impact to capture the full event.

---

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- Striker mass $m = 80$ kg
- Required impact energy $E = 157$ J
- Pendulum arm length $L = 1.5$ m
- Gravitational acceleration $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Sub-part (i): Drop height for vertical drop test (3.5 marks)**

Using the energy equation:

$$
E = m \cdot g \cdot h
$$

Rearranging for $h$:

$$
h = \frac{E}{m \cdot g}
$$

$$
h = \frac{157}{80 \times 9.81} = \frac{157}{784.8} = 0.2000 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{h = 200 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Verification by velocity method:**

$$
v = \sqrt{2 \cdot g \cdot h} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.2000} = \sqrt{3.924} = 1.981 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
E_k = \frac{1}{2} \times 80 \times (1.981)^2 = 40 \times 3.924 = 156.96 \approx 157 \text{ J} \checkmark
$$

**Sub-part (ii): Pendulum release angle (3.5 marks)**

Using the pendulum energy equation:

$$
E = m \cdot g \cdot L \cdot (1 - \cos\theta)
$$

Rearranging for $\cos\theta$:

$$
1 - \cos\theta = \frac{E}{m \cdot g \cdot L}
$$

$$
\cos\theta = 1 - \frac{E}{m \cdot g \cdot L}
$$

$$
\cos\theta = 1 - \frac{157}{80 \times 9.81 \times 1.5}
$$

$$
\cos\theta = 1 - \frac{157}{1177.2} = 1 - 0.1334 = 0.8666
$$

$$
\theta = \cos^{-1}(0.8666)
$$

$$
\boxed{\theta \approx 30°}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** A 30° release angle is *very* common across JWL and TÜV standards. The use of an *angle* rather than a *drop height* in the standard is purely for **practical convenience** — setting an angle on a protractor is easier than measuring a vertical drop of 200 mm accurately.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Impact Test Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "impact force" with "impact energy"** — Impact test is **energy-controlled**, not force-controlled. Students who write $F = ma$ as the governing equation lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the Tyre** — Writing that the test is done on the *bare wheel* is a **fatal conceptual error**. The tyre is mandatory because it acts as a pneumatic spring.
> 3. **Mixing up Failure and Performance Criteria** — These are *opposite* conditions. Failure = what *disqualifies* the wheel. Performance = what *qualifies* it. Conflating them loses clarity marks.
> 4. **Wrong units** — Energy must be in **Joules (J)**, drop height in **metres (m)**, not mm, when using the SI formula $E = mgh$. Convert first, calculate second.
> 5. **Skipping the verification step** — In numericals, always re-check using $E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$. Examiners award bonus marks for verification.
> 6. **Omitting safety** — In setup descriptions, never forget the **fragmentation shield** — it's a 1-mark item in 14-mark questions.
> 7. **Single impact point** — Many students describe only one impact location. Standards typically require **3–5 zones**. Always mention *multiple impact points*.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this as a final 5-minute revision pass before the exam.**

- ✅ Impact test is **energy-controlled**, not force-controlled.
- ✅ Test is always on **wheel + inflated tyre assembly** (test pressure ≈ 200 kPa).
- ✅ Governing standards: **ISO 7141, SAE J175, JWL, TÜV, IS 9438**.
- ✅ Key formula: $E = mgh$ (drop) and $E = mgL(1-\cos\theta)$ (pendulum).
- ✅ Impact energy scales with **wheel size**: 13" ≈ 196 J → 19" ≈ 225 J.
- ✅ Failure criteria: **crack, air loss >10%, bead unseating, fragmentation, permanent deformation >1.5 mm**.
- ✅ Performance criteria: **no crack, ≤10% air loss, bead seated, dimensional integrity, energy absorption proof**.
- ✅ Multiple impact points (3–5 zones) are mandatory in standards.
- ✅ Striker mass range: **50–100 kg**; drop height: **150–300 mm**.
- ✅ Average impact force can be calculated as $F = E / \Delta x$ (e.g., 180 J / 12 mm = **15 kN**).
- ✅ Energy sinks during impact: **elastic + plastic + air compression + friction + (crack if failing)**.
- ✅ Safety: **fragmentation shield** is mandatory around the test rig.
- ✅ Verification: Always re-check numerical energy using $E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$.
- ✅ Pendulum release angle of **30°** is very common — corresponds to ~200 mm drop.
- ✅ Some plastic deformation is **expected and good** — it proves energy was absorbed.
- ✅ For 16" wheel, 80 kg striker, 157 J → **h = 200 mm, θ = 30°** (canonical numerical).
- ✅ Test is a **Type Approval / Homologation test** — every wheel design must pass it.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bumpers (Types of Tests, Pendulum Test, Fixed Collision Barrier Test Procedure, Performance Criteria)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT724 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bumpers (Types of Tests, Pendulum Test, Fixed Collision Barrier Test Procedure, Performance Criteria)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Bumper? (Formal KTU Definition)

A **bumper** is a structural energy-absorbing device, mounted on the front and rear extremities of a motor vehicle, designed and engineered to absorb the kinetic energy generated during low-to-moderate speed collisions. Its primary engineering objectives, as defined under the KTU 2024 OEM syllabus, are:

1. **Energy Dissipation** – Convert the kinetic energy of an impact into controlled, recoverable deformation work (plastic and elastic strain energy).
2. **Component Protection** – Shield expensive secondary assemblies such as headlamps, tail lamps, radiator, AC condenser, and hood latch mechanisms.
3. **Pedestrian Protection** – Mitigate lower-limb injury severity in pedestrian impacts (a growing regulatory focus).
4. **Cosmetic & Insurance Cost Containment** – Prevent cosmetic damage in parking-lot speed (≤ 4 km/h) impacts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A bumper is **NOT** a crash structure. High-speed crash energy is managed by the **crumple zone / safety cell**, not the bumper. The bumper handles only low-speed impact (typically up to 8 km/h as per Indian AIS-006, and 4–8 km/h per international standards like ECE R42 and US FMVSS Part 581).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy – The Boxer's Guard:**
> Think of a bumper like the **glove a boxer wears on his hands**. When a boxer punches another boxer's face, the **glove absorbs the shock** so that the boxer's knuckles don't break, and the opponent's face is cushioned. The glove is *not designed* to withstand a sledgehammer (high-speed crash) – it is designed for the repeated light jabs of sparring (low-speed parking nudges). The bumper works exactly the same way: it is the *cushion* between your car and a low-speed bump, not a fortress against highway collisions.

## 1.3 Industry Standards Governing Bumper Tests

| Standard Code | Region / Authority | Test Speed | Key Emphasis |
|---|---|---|---|
| **AIS-006** | India (Automotive Industry Standards) | 4 km/h | Mandatory for M1 category vehicles |
| **ECE R42** | Europe (UNECE) | 4 km/h | Front/rear protection systems |
| **FMVSS Part 581** | USA (NHTSA) | 4 / 8 km/h | Bumper Standard (banned under Trump admin, restored) |
| **IIHS LSI** | USA (Insurance Institute) | 10 km/h | Low-speed crashworthiness rating |
| **GB 17354** | China | 4 km/h | Front/rear protective devices |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Performance Criteria Threshold (Universal):** The bumper must prevent damage to **headlamps, tail lamps, fuel system, exhaust system, hood latches, and steering linkage** at the prescribed test velocity, and the vehicle must be **drivable away** from the test under its own power post-impact.

## 1.4 Why Bumpers Are Tested at All

Modern passenger cars have bumpers made of:
- **Energy Absorber (EA)**: Polypropylene (PP) foam, EPP (Expanded PP), or PU foam blocks.
- **Reinforcement Beam (Rebar)**: Cold-rolled steel, aluminium, or glass-fibre-reinforced plastic (GFRP) channel-section beam.
- **Face Bar / Cover**: Thermoplastic Olefin (TPO) or polypropylene (PP) painted shell.

The synergy of these three layers, working together, is what the testing protocols validate.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Bumper Tests

The KTU syllabus recognises **three principal test methodologies**, each providing a different load case on the bumper system:

> [!NOTE]
> **Type 1: Pendulum Test (Component-Level Test)**
> A pendulum (a heavy mass on a swinging arm) is released from a calibrated height to strike the bumper mounted on a stationary fixture. It simulates a corner impact or a single-point low-speed hit without involving the entire vehicle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Type 2: Fixed Collision Barrier Test (Vehicle-Level Test)**
> The complete vehicle is propelled (or driven under power) at a prescribed test velocity into a rigid, immovable barrier. This is the most stringent bumper regulation test globally.

> [!NOTE]
> **Type 3: Vehicle-to-Vehicle (or Moving Barrier) Test**
> A moving deformable barrier (MDB) or another vehicle is driven into the stationary test vehicle. This is optional in most regulations but is used in European and Asian NCAP protocols.

## 2.2 Energy Absorption Theory (The Core Physics)

A bumper must absorb the impact kinetic energy through controlled **plastic deformation** and **elastic strain energy storage**. The governing energy balance is:

$$E_{\text{impact}} = E_{\text{elastic}} + E_{\text{plastic}} + E_{\text{friction}} + E_{\text{acoustic}}$$

For a perfectly elastic, frictionless bumper, all energy would be recovered. In practice, **plastic deformation dominates** for crashworthiness.

### 2.2.1 Kinetic Energy of Impact (Universal Starting Equation)

$$E_{\text{k}} = \frac{1}{2} m v^{2}$$

Where:
- $E_{\text{k}}$ = Kinetic energy at impact (Joules, J)
- $m$ = Effective mass of the impacting body (kg)
- $v$ = Impact velocity (m/s)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Doubling the impact velocity **quadruples** the kinetic energy. Doubling the mass only doubles it. This is why a small increase in test speed creates dramatically higher design loads on the bumper.

### 2.2.2 Pendulum Test Energy Equation

The pendulum's potential energy at the release height converts entirely into kinetic energy at the lowest point of its swing (by conservation of mechanical energy, neglecting air drag):

$$E_{\text{pendulum}} = m_{\text{eff}} \cdot g \cdot h = \frac{1}{2} m_{\text{eff}} v^{2}$$

Where:
- $m_{\text{eff}}$ = Effective pendulum striking mass (kg)
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**
- $h$ = Drop height (m) – the vertical distance the pendulum is raised
- $v$ = Striking velocity at the bottom of swing (m/s)

Solving for the equivalent striking velocity:

$$v = \sqrt{2 g h}$$

### 2.2.3 Equivalent Striking Mass Concept

A pendulum has a **distributed mass** along its arm. Engineers model it using the concept of *equivalent mass* concentrated at the striking head. The relationship is:

$$m_{\text{eff}} = m_{\text{total}} \cdot \left(\frac{r_{\text{gy}}}{L}\right)^{2}$$

Where:
- $m_{\text{total}}$ = Total pendulum mass (kg)
- $r_{\text{gy}}$ = Radius of gyration of the pendulum about its pivot (m)
- $L$ = Distance from pivot axis to the pendulum centre of percussion (m)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why radius of gyration?** A long pendulum concentrates the same total mass at a greater effective radius, increasing the impact energy delivered at the tip. The "effective" or "dynamic" mass is therefore smaller than the static mass — typically **80–90%** of the static mass for a well-designed pendulum rig.

### 2.2.4 Bumper Stroke (Deflection) and Average Crush Force

The bumper compresses through a controlled stroke $s$ (m). The average force of resistance $F_{\text{avg}}$ is defined by work-energy equivalence:

$$F_{\text{avg}} = \frac{E_{\text{k}}}{s}$$

This is the *average* force; the actual force-deflection curve is non-linear. The **peak force** $F_{\text{peak}}$ is typically **1.5 to 2.5 times** the average force, depending on the foam density curve and rebar stiffness.

### 2.2.5 Bumper Energy Efficiency (Performance Index)

Engineers quantify how well a bumper converts impact energy into controlled deformation (vs. transmitting it to the vehicle structure):

$$\eta_{\text{bumper}} = \frac{E_{\text{absorbed by bumper}}}{E_{\text{incident}}}$$

A well-designed bumper should have $\eta_{\text{bumper}} \geq 0.75$ (i.e., 75% of the impact energy is dissipated in the bumper before reaching the body-in-white).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM TIP:** Memorise this table. Most 14-mark derivations in KTU ESE for this topic will draw from these equations.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Impact kinetic energy | $E_{\text{k}} = \frac{1}{2} m v^{2}$ | Joules (J) | Applicable to both pendulum and barrier tests |
| 2 | Pendulum energy | $E_{\text{p}} = m_{\text{eff}} g h$ | J | Conservation of energy assumption |
| 3 | Striking velocity | $v = \sqrt{2 g h}$ | m/s | No air drag assumed |
| 4 | Equivalent pendulum mass | $m_{\text{eff}} = m_{\text{t}} (r_{\text{gy}}/L)^{2}$ | kg | $r_{\text{gy}} < L$ always |
| 5 | Average crush force | $F_{\text{avg}} = E_{\text{k}} / s$ | N (Newton) | $s$ = bumper stroke (m) |
| 6 | Peak-to-average force ratio | $F_{\text{peak}} \approx 1.5 \text{ to } 2.5 \cdot F_{\text{avg}}$ | dimensionless | Empirical, depends on foam |
| 7 | Bumper efficiency | $\eta = E_{\text{abs}}/E_{\text{inc}}$ | dimensionless | $\eta \in [0, 1]$; ideal = 1 |
| 8 | Power density | $P_{\text{den}} = E_{\text{k}} / (A \cdot t)$ | W/m² | Used for foam characterisation |
| 9 | Stroke-to-length ratio | $\lambda = s / L_{\text{beam}}$ | dimensionless | $\lambda \leq 0.5$ recommended |
| 10 | Vehicle test impact KE | $E_{\text{veh}} = \frac{1}{2} m_{\text{veh}} v^{2}$ | J | $m_{\text{veh}}$ = curb mass + 75 kg (driver) |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX / Markdown Note:** Absolute value symbols and "divides" in any formula should NEVER be written using the `|` character within a table row, as it breaks the markdown table rendering. Always use $\vert$ or $\mid$ instead.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of Bumper Energy Calculations

1. **OEM Design Verification** – Calculates whether the chosen foam grade and rebar thickness will absorb the regulatory test energy.
2. **Insurance Industry Rating** – IIHS Low-Speed Insurance rating ($10 km/h$) uses these equations to predict claim costs.
3. **Forensic Accident Reconstruction** – Police and insurance investigators use residual bumper crush to back-calculate impact velocity:
$$v = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 E_{\text{crush}}}{m_{\text{eff}}}}$$
4. **Pedestrian Protection Simulation** – Lower-limb impact models treat the bumper foam as a non-linear spring whose stiffness curve is derived from these same equations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation 1: Striking Velocity of a Pendulum Bumper Test

**Statement:** Derive the striking velocity $v$ of a pendulum bumper test rig in terms of the drop height $h$ and gravitational acceleration $g$.

**Given:**
- Effective pendulum mass = $m_{\text{eff}}$ (kg)
- Drop height = $h$ (m)
- Gravitational acceleration = $g$ (m/s²) = 9.81 m/s²
- Air resistance and friction at the pivot are **negligible**.

**To Find:** Striking velocity $v$ at the lowest point of the swing.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Identify the energy states.**
At the release point (initial state), the pendulum is momentarily at rest, so its **kinetic energy is zero**, and its **potential energy is maximum**:

$$E_{\text{initial}} = m_{\text{eff}} g h + 0 = m_{\text{eff}} g h$$

At the lowest point of swing (final state), the pendulum reaches the bumper with maximum velocity and its **potential energy is zero** (taking the bottom of the swing as datum):

$$E_{\text{final}} = 0 + \frac{1}{2} m_{\text{eff}} v^{2} = \frac{1}{2} m_{\text{eff}} v^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the law of conservation of mechanical energy.**

By the law of conservation of energy (no non-conservative forces acting in the idealised case):

$$E_{\text{initial}} = E_{\text{final}}$$

Substituting the expressions from Step 1:

$$m_{\text{eff}} g h = \frac{1}{2} m_{\text{eff}} v^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the equation.**

Divide both sides by $m_{\text{eff}}$ (mass cancels — important observation!):

$$g h = \frac{1}{2} v^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $v$.**

Multiply both sides by 2:

$$2 g h = v^{2}$$

Take the square root of both sides (taking the positive root since velocity is a vector with defined direction):

$$v = \sqrt{2 g h}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** The striking velocity of an ideal pendulum is **independent of the pendulum's mass**. A heavier pendulum only increases the *impact energy* (because it carries more mass into the strike), not the velocity.

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation 2: Average Crush Force on a Bumper

**Statement:** A passenger car of curb mass 1200 kg (with a 75 kg driver) impacts a fixed rigid barrier at 4 km/h. The bumper is designed to crush through a stroke of 50 mm. Calculate the average crush force experienced by the bumper system.

**Given:**
- $m_{\text{veh}}$ = curb mass + driver mass = $1200 + 75 = 1275$ kg
- $v$ = impact velocity = 4 km/h (to be converted to m/s)
- $s$ = bumper crush stroke = 50 mm = 0.050 m

**To Find:** Average crush force $F_{\text{avg}}$ in kN.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Convert impact velocity from km/h to m/s.**

$$v = 4 \, \frac{\text{km}}{\text{h}} \times \frac{1000 \, \text{m}}{1 \, \text{km}} \times \frac{1 \, \text{h}}{3600 \, \text{s}}$$

$$v = \frac{4 \times 1000}{3600} = \frac{4000}{3600} = 1.111 \, \text{m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the impact kinetic energy of the vehicle.**

Using $E_{\text{k}} = \frac{1}{2} m v^{2}$:

$$E_{\text{k}} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1275 \times (1.111)^{2}$$

Calculate $(1.111)^{2}$:

$$(1.111)^{2} = 1.234 \, \text{m}^{2}/\text{s}^{2}$$

Substituting:

$$E_{\text{k}} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1275 \times 1.234$$

$$E_{\text{k}} = 0.5 \times 1275 \times 1.234 = 787.0 \, \text{J}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Sanity Check:** A 1275 kg car at 1.111 m/s carries just **787 J** of kinetic energy — roughly the energy of a 1 kg object dropped from 80 m. This is a *very* low-energy event, which is why bumpers can be light and the test is non-injurious to occupants.

**Step 3 — Apply the work-energy principle to find average crush force.**

The kinetic energy is dissipated through the bumper stroke. The work done by the average crush force over the stroke equals the kinetic energy absorbed:

$$F_{\text{avg}} \cdot s = E_{\text{k}}$$

Therefore:

$$F_{\text{avg}} = \frac{E_{\text{k}}}{s} = \frac{787.0}{0.050}$$

$$F_{\text{avg}} = 15740 \, \text{N} = 15.74 \, \text{kN}$$

**Step 4 — Estimate the peak crush force.**

Using the empirical peak-to-average ratio of 2.0 (mid-range for typical PP foam + steel rebar):

$$F_{\text{peak}} = 2.0 \times 15.74 = 31.48 \, \text{kN}$$

### 3.2.1 Final Answer Summary

| Quantity | Value | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Impact velocity $v$ | 1.111 | m/s |
| Vehicle mass $m$ | 1275 | kg |
| Kinetic energy $E_{\text{k}}$ | 787.0 | J |
| Average crush force $F_{\text{avg}}$ | 15.74 | kN |
| Peak crush force (estimated) | 31.48 | kN |

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation 3: Pendulum Drop Height for a 4 km/h Equivalent Test

**Statement:** An OEM wishes to qualify a new bumper using a pendulum test instead of a full vehicle test. The pendulum's effective striking mass is 1100 kg. What drop height is required to deliver a striking velocity equivalent to 4 km/h?

**Given:**
- Target striking velocity $v = 4 \, \text{km/h} = 1.111 \, \text{m/s}$ (as before)
- Pendulum effective mass $m_{\text{eff}} = 1100 \, \text{kg}$
- Gravitational acceleration $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^{2}$

**To Find:** Drop height $h$ in mm.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Use the energy conservation equation for the pendulum.**

$$m_{\text{eff}} g h = \frac{1}{2} m_{\text{eff}} v^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Cancel $m_{\text{eff}}$ from both sides.**

$$g h = \frac{1}{2} v^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $h$.**

$$h = \frac{v^{2}}{2 g}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$h = \frac{(1.111)^{2}}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{1.234}{19.62} = 0.0629 \, \text{m}$$

Converting to millimetres:

$$h = 62.9 \, \text{mm} \approx 63 \, \text{mm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Validation:** A drop height of only **63 mm** is needed to deliver a 4 km/h impact energy. This is why pendulum tests are popular for component-level design validation — they require very little lab space and minimal energy input. However, the full vehicle test is still required by law because the **fixture interface** (bumper-to-body mounting brackets) and **vehicle mass distribution** also affect the outcome, which the pendulum test cannot replicate.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choosing the Right Test

| Test Type | What It Simulates | Best For | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pendulum (Component)** | Single-point low-speed hit (e.g., parking nudge) | Early-stage design validation, supplier PPAP | Ignores vehicle mass, mounting stiffness, and crush propagation into body |
| **Fixed Barrier (Full Vehicle)** | Frontal or rear full-width impact | Regulatory certification, NCAP rating | Expensive; one-shot test per vehicle |
| **Moving Barrier / Vehicle-to-Vehicle** | Offset or angle impact | Insurance ratings, NCAP side/rear | Even more expensive, complex instrumentation |

## 3.5 Worked Example: Bumper Energy Efficiency Calculation

**Statement:** During a 4 km/h barrier test on a 1200 kg car, the bumper assembly is found to have absorbed 600 J of energy through plastic deformation. The rebound (elastic recovery) measured 50 J. Friction and acoustic losses combined account for 80 J. Calculate the bumper efficiency and the energy transmitted to the vehicle body-in-white (BIW).

**Given:**
- $E_{\text{incident}} = 787 \, \text{J}$ (from Section 3.2)
- $E_{\text{plastic}} = 600 \, \text{J}$
- $E_{\text{elastic}} = 50 \, \text{J}$
- $E_{\text{friction+acoustic}} = 80 \, \text{J}$

**To Find:** Bumper efficiency $\eta$ and energy transmitted to BIW $E_{\text{BIW}}$.

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Total energy absorbed by the bumper.**

$$E_{\text{abs}} = E_{\text{plastic}} + E_{\text{elastic}} + E_{\text{friction+acoustic}}$$

$$E_{\text{abs}} = 600 + 50 + 80 = 730 \, \text{J}$$

**Step 2 — Bumper efficiency.**

$$\eta = \frac{E_{\text{abs}}}{E_{\text{incident}}} = \frac{730}{787} = 0.928 = 92.8\%$$

**Step 3 — Energy transmitted to the BIW (by energy conservation).**

$$E_{\text{BIW}} = E_{\text{incident}} - E_{\text{abs}} = 787 - 730 = 57 \, \text{J}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** 92.8% efficiency is excellent — well above the 75% design threshold. Only 57 J reaches the body-in-white, which is insufficient to cause permanent deformation in the safety cell. **Bumper design is acceptable.**
