# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Bumper System

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the energy flow path from impact event to vehicle body, illustrating the role of each bumper subsystem:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Impact Event\nLow Speed 4 km/h] --> B[Face Bar / Cover\nTPO or PP]
    B --> C[Energy Absorber\nPP / EPP Foam]
    C --> D[Reinforcement Beam\nSteel or Aluminium]
    D --> E[Bumper Mounting Brackets\nLeft and Right]
    E --> F[Body-in-White / Chassis\nLongitudinal Rails]
    F --> G[Protected Components\nHeadlamps, Lamps, Latches]

    H[Crash Sensor\nOptional] --> D
    D --> I[Energy Dissipated\nPlastic Deformation]
    D --> J[Energy Returned\nElastic Rebound]
    D --> K[Energy Lost\nFriction and Acoustic]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Energy flows from `A` (impact) through a **series** of energy-absorbing layers (`B → C → D → E`) before reaching the protected components.
- The three right-side branches (`I`, `J`, `K`) represent the **three energy sinks** that the bumper exploits to protect the vehicle.
- The dashed-style path `H → D` indicates an optional active safety sensor that may trigger pyrotechnic pre-tensioners in advanced ADAS-equipped vehicles.

## 4.2 Bumper Cross-Section Schematic (Front View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BUMPER_ASSEMBLY[Bumper Sub-System]
        direction TB
        A1[Outer Cover / Face Bar\n2.5 to 3.5 mm TPO]
        A2[Energy Absorber Foam\n30 to 60 mm EPP]
        A3[Reinforcement Beam\n1.2 to 1.8 mm Steel Channel]
        A4[Side Bracket and Crush Tube\nStamping]
    end

    A1 -.gap.-> A2
    A2 -.adheres.-> A3
    A3 -.welded.-> A4
    A4 -.bolted.-> VEH[Vehicle Longitudinal Rail]

    style A1 fill:#FFE4B5
    style A2 fill:#FFA07A
    style A3 fill:#87CEEB
    style A4 fill:#98FB98
    style VEH fill:#D3D3D3
```

**Component Stack-Up (typical):**
- **A1** – The aesthetic, paint-matched shell that the customer sees.
- **A2** – The compressible foam that absorbs the bulk of the energy.
- **A3** – The structural beam that distributes the load across the two rails.
- **A4** – The mounting interface that controls the crush progression and prevents the beam from intruding into the wheel well.

## 4.3 Test Procedure Flowchart (Generic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Pre-Test Setup] --> P1[Vehicle Weighing and Instrumentation]
    P1 --> P2[Mounting of Bumper or Vehicle on Test Fixture]
    P2 --> P3[Calibration of Speed Measurement Systems]
    P3 --> P4[Run Test at Prescribed Velocity]
    P4 --> P5{Does the Bumper Meet\nPerformance Criteria?}
    P5 -- Yes --> P6[Pass - Issue Compliance Certificate]

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part A carries 3 marks per question, typically testing direct factual recall or a one-line derivation. Answers should be concise and technical.

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: List the three main energy-absorbing mechanisms of a vehicle bumper during a low-speed impact.**

**Model Answer (3 key points for 3 marks):**

1. **Plastic Deformation of the Reinforcement Beam and Energy Absorber Foam** — Irreversible work done in crushing the foam cells and bending the steel rebar accounts for the largest share (~70–80%) of energy dissipation.
2. **Elastic Strain Energy Storage and Rebound** — A small fraction (~5–10%) of the kinetic energy is temporarily stored as elastic strain and returned to the pendulum/barrier upon rebound.
3. **Friction and Acoustic Losses** — Inter-laminar friction between the cover, foam, beam, and brackets, along with the acoustic energy of the impact (a "bang"), accounts for the remaining 5–15% of the dissipation.

*(Examiner's note: Award 1 mark for each of the three mechanisms. Award full 3 marks only if all three are identified and briefly described.)*

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between the Pendulum Test and the Fixed Collision Barrier Test for bumpers.**

**Model Answer (Structured Table for 3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Pendulum Test | Fixed Collision Barrier Test |
|---|---|---|
| **Test Article** | Bumper sub-system mounted on a fixture | Complete vehicle in running condition |
| **Moving Body** | Pendulum (heavy mass on swinging arm) | The vehicle itself |
| **Target** | Stationary bumper on rig | Stationary rigid barrier (concrete wall) |
| **Cost** | Low (component-level) | High (vehicle-level) |
| **Regulatory Status** | Design validation tool (PPAP) | Compliance / homologation test |
| **Energy Equivalence** | Calculated from drop height | Calculated from vehicle mass and velocity |

*(Examiner's note: Any three valid points of difference earn 3 marks. Use of a table is recommended for clarity.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B questions carry 14 marks each, with internal choice (two alternative questions per slot). Each part question typically has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a) Explain with a neat sketch the procedure for conducting a Fixed Collision Barrier Test on a passenger car bumper. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **Fixed Collision Barrier Test (FCBT)** is the most widely used regulatory test for vehicle bumpers, mandated under AIS-006 (India), ECE R42 (Europe), and FMVSS Part 581 (USA). The procedure is as follows:

1. **Test Vehicle Preparation:**
   - The vehicle is prepared in its **kerb weight + 75 kg (driver) condition** (per AIS-006).
   - Fuel tank is filled to **90% of its capacity** with a non-flammable fluid (typically water or test fluid) to simulate fuel load without fire risk.
   - All movable masses (driver, anthropomorphic test devices, instrumentation) are securely fixed.

2. **Test Barrier Specifications:**
   - The barrier is a **rigid, non-deformable, vertical concrete wall** with a minimum mass such that its deformation under impact is negligible.
   - The barrier face is **flat, perpendicular to the line of impact, and at least 2 m wide** to cover the full vehicle width.
   - The barrier surface is finished smooth and vertical.

3. **Propulsion System:**
   - The vehicle is propelled into the barrier using a **winch, hydraulic actuator, or self-propulsion** (drive the vehicle into the barrier at the prescribed velocity).
   - The propulsion system is **disengaged at the moment of impact** to ensure the vehicle impacts the barrier under free, unpowered motion.

4. **Test Speeds:**
   - **Frontal test:** $4 \pm 0.1 \, \text{km/h}$ (AIS-006)
   - **Rear test:** $4 \pm 0.1 \, \text{km/h}$ (AIS-006)
   - The vehicle's speed is measured within **0.5 m** of the barrier face using a **speed trap** (light gates or Doppler radar).

5. **Instrumentation and Measurement:**
   - **High-speed cameras** record the impact from front, side, and oblique angles.
   - **Accelerometers** on the bumper beam and chassis record the deceleration pulse.
   - **Load cells** (where fitted) measure the force at the barrier interface.

6. **Post-Test Inspection:**
   - Visual inspection for damage to **headlamps, tail lamps, fuel system, exhaust, hood latches, doors, and steering**.
   - Measurement of any **fuel leakage** (must be ≤ prescribed threshold — typically 30 g/min for AIS-006).
   - **Drive-away test:** The vehicle must be capable of being driven away from the test under its own power, with all doors operable and the steering functional.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sketch — Free-Body Description (Verbal):**
> A simple side-view diagram should show: the test vehicle moving left-to-right, the rigid vertical barrier on the right, the speed measurement trap (light gates) in front of the barrier, and the bumper deformable region indicated between the front overhang of the vehicle and the barrier. (Examiner's note: 1 mark is reserved for the sketch; the remaining 6 marks are for procedural steps.)

**Examiner's Note on Mark Distribution:**
- Sketch: 1 mark
- Vehicle preparation: 1 mark
- Barrier specs: 1 mark
- Propulsion & speed: 2 marks
- Instrumentation: 1 mark
- Performance evaluation criteria: 1 mark

**Q: (b) State and explain the performance criteria that a bumper must satisfy to pass the Fixed Collision Barrier Test. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

To pass the FCBT, the bumper system must demonstrate compliance with the following **performance criteria** (as per AIS-006 / ECE R42):

1. **No Damage to Safety-Critical Lighting:**
   - The **headlamps, position lamps, tail lamps, and turn indicators** must remain **operationally intact and properly aligned** post-impact.
   - No part of the lighting assembly should be dislocated, broken, or shifted from its mounting.

2. **No Fuel System Leakage:**
   - The **fuel tank, fuel lines, and filler cap** must remain leak-tight.
   - Maximum permissible leakage is **30 g/min** under AIS-006, with **no continuous dripping** during the test.
   - The fuel filler cap must remain sealed.

3. **No Damage to Hood, Doors, and Latches:**
   - The **hood latch** must not release during impact (this is a critical safety criterion to prevent the hood from flying open and obstructing the driver's vision).
   - **Doors** must remain closed and operable.
   - The **tailgate / boot lid** (in rear tests) must not unlatch.

4. **No Intrusion into Cooling and Steering Systems:**
   - The **radiator, AC condenser, and steering linkage** must be free from impact damage.
   - The vehicle must not show any sharp protrusions or burrs that could pose a laceration risk to pedestrians.

5. **Drive-Away Capability:**
   - The vehicle must be **capable of being driven away from the test under its own engine power** post-impact, without requiring external assistance.
   - This validates that the steering, brakes, and driveline remain functional.

6. **Pedestrian Protection (Modern Regulations):**
   - The bumper must not have **sharp edges** that would increase the HIC (Head Injury Criterion) value for a struck pedestrian.
   - Some regulations now also require the bumper to fold or deform in a controlled manner to minimise femur and tibia loading.

7. **Cosmetic Damage Tolerance:**
   - Up to a **prescribed level of cosmetic damage** (scuffs, paint scratches) is permissible on the bumper cover itself, provided none of the above functional criteria are violated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Note:** Award 1 mark for each correctly stated and briefly explained criterion. The student is expected to list at least 5–6 criteria for full marks. Mere listing without explanation fetches partial credit (max 4 of 7 marks).

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a) Describe in detail the Pendulum Test for bumpers. Include the test setup, procedure, and energy calculations. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Test Setup:**
- A **pendulum test rig** consists of a heavy mass (typically 1000–1500 kg equivalent striking mass) suspended from a **pivoted arm** of length 3–5 m.
- The bumper to be tested is **rigidly mounted on a fixture** that simulates the vehicle's longitudinal rail stiffness.
- The pendulum is fitted with a **striking head** shaped to mimic a passenger car's bumper geometry (typically a flat or slightly curved face of width 200–400 mm).
- A **height scale** measures the drop height; **high-speed cameras** record the impact; **load cells** at the fixture base record the reaction force.

**Test Procedure:**
1. The pendulum is **raised to a pre-calculated drop height** $h$ such that the resulting striking velocity equals the regulatory test speed (4 km/h or as required).
2. The pendulum is **released from rest** and allowed to swing freely under gravity.
3. The pendulum strikes the **centre of the bumper** (or at specified off-centre locations) at the bottom of its swing.
4. The impact event is recorded by cameras and instrumentation.

**Energy Calculations:**

The striking velocity at the bottom of the swing is given by the energy conservation:

$$v = \sqrt{2 g h}$$

For a target test speed of $v = 4 \, \text{km/h} = 1.111 \, \text{m/s}$, the required drop height is:

$$h = \frac{v^{2}}{2g} = \frac{(1.111)^{2}}{2 \times 9.81} = 0.0629 \, \text{m} \approx 63 \, \text{mm}$$

The impact energy is:

$$E_{\text{impact}} = \frac{1}{2} m_{\text{eff}} v^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1100 \times 1.234 = 679 \, \text{J}$$

**Performance Evaluation:**
- **Peak force** on the bumper must be below the allowable threshold.
- **No detachment** of the bumper from the fixture.
- **Energy absorbed** by the bumper is measured by the work done in crushing, calculated from the force-deflection curve:

$$E_{\text{abs}} = \int_{0}^{s} F(x) \, dx$$

**Q: (b) A 1300 kg passenger car (with driver) impacts a rigid barrier at 4 km/h. The bumper has a stroke of 60 mm. Calculate: (i) the impact kinetic energy, (ii) the average crush force, (iii) the peak crush force (assume a peak-to-average ratio of 2.0). (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Convert test velocity to SI units.**

$$v = 4 \times \frac{1000}{3600} = 1.111 \, \text{m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the impact kinetic energy.**

$$E_{\text{k}} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1300 \times (1.111)^{2}$$

$$E_{\text{k}} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1300 \times 1.234 = 802.1 \, \text{J}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the average crush force.**

Using the work-energy principle:

$$F_{\text{avg}} = \frac{E_{\text{k}}}{s} = \frac{802.1}{0.060} = 13368 \, \text{N} = 13.37 \, \text{kN}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the peak crush force.**

$$F_{\text{peak}} = 2.0 \times F_{\text{avg}} = 2.0 \times 13.37 = 26.74 \, \text{kN}$$

**Final Answer Table:**

| Quantity | Value | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Impact velocity $v$ | 1.111 | m/s |
| Kinetic energy $E_{\text{k}}$ | 802.1 | J |
| Average crush force $F_{\text{avg}}$ | 13.37 | kN |
| Peak crush force $F_{\text{peak}}$ | 26.74 | kN |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Bumper Questions:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Failing to convert km/h to m/s in energy calculations is the **#1 cause of marks loss**. Always remember: $1 \, \text{km/h} = \frac{1000}{3600} = 0.2778 \, \text{m/s}$.
> 2. **Forgetting the Driver Mass:** In vehicle-level test calculations, the driver mass (75 kg per AIS-006) must be added to the curb weight. Omitting this loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing Pendulum Energy and Striking Velocity:** Students often write $v = \sqrt{2gh}$ correctly but then incorrectly claim that doubling the pendulum mass doubles the velocity. **The striking velocity is mass-independent.**
> 4. **Missing the "Drive-Away" Criterion:** The drive-away capability is a unique bumper performance criterion. Students often list only cosmetic criteria, missing this critical 1-mark item.
> 5. **No Sketch in Fixed Barrier Test Question:** Always draw a simple labelled sketch — the question explicitly says "with a neat sketch." A missing sketch costs **at least 1–2 marks**.
> 6. **Wrong Performance Criteria List:** Listing only *cosmetic* criteria (scratches, dents) without functional criteria (no fuel leak, hood latch intact) is a common shallow answer that caps marks at 3 of 7.
> 7. **Mixing Up Pendulum vs. Barrier Test Energies:** Students sometimes calculate the pendulum energy using the full static mass instead of the **effective mass** $m_{\text{eff}}$. Use $m_{\text{eff}} = m_{\text{total}} (r_{\text{gy}}/L)^{2}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section. It is your last-minute revision sheet.**

### Core Concepts
- [ ] **Bumper purpose:** Absorb low-speed impact energy; protect lights, fuel system, and latches.
- [ ] **Regulatory test speed:** **4 km/h** in India (AIS-006), Europe (ECE R42), and US (FMVSS 581).
- [ ] **Three test types:** Pendulum (component), Fixed Barrier (vehicle), Moving Barrier (vehicle-to-vehicle).
- [ ] **Three energy sinks:** Plastic deformation (~75%), Elastic rebound (~5%), Friction + Acoustic (~10–15%).

### Key Equations (Memorise!)
- [ ] Impact kinetic energy: $E_{\text{k}} = \frac{1}{2} m v^{2}$
- [ ] Striking velocity (pendulum): $v = \sqrt{2 g h}$
- [ ] Drop height: $h = v^{2} / (2g)$
- [ ] Average crush force: $F_{\text{avg}} = E_{\text{k}} / s$
- [ ] Bumper efficiency: $\eta = E_{\text{abs}} / E_{\text{inc}}$

### Performance Criteria (Mnemonic: **"No LIGHT-DL"**)
- [ ] **L**ighting intact (headlamps, tail lamps)
- [ ] **I**ntact fuel system (no leak)
- [ ] **G**love-box / general body integrity
- [ ] **H**ood latch (must not release)
- [ ] **T**ailgate/door latches (must not release)
- [ ] **D**rive-away capability
- [ ] **L**aceration-free edges (pedestrian safety)

### Units and Conversions
- [ ] $1 \, \text{km/h} = 0.2778 \, \text{m/s}$
- [ ] $1 \, \text{m/s} = 3.6 \, \text{km/h}$
- [ ] $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^{2}$
- [ ] $1 \, \text{kJ} = 1000 \, \text{J}$

### Quick Numerical Reference (for last-minute recall)
- [ ] At 4 km/h, a 1200 kg car carries only **~750 J** of kinetic energy.
- [ ] Bumper stroke: **30–60 mm** typical.
- [ ] Average crush force: **~15 kN** for a typical compact sedan at 4 km/h.
- [ ] Pendulum drop height for 4 km/h equivalent: **~63 mm**.
- [ ] Bumper efficiency target: **≥ 75%**.

### Common Exam Terminology
- [ ] **FMVSS** = Federal Motor Vehicle Safety Standard (USA)
- [ ] **ECE** = Economic Commission for Europe (UN regulation)
- [ ] **AIS** = Automotive Industry Standard (India)
- [ ] **NCAP** = New Car Assessment Programme
- [ ] **MDB** = Moving Deformable Barrier
- [ ] **BIW** = Body-in-White (the unpainted vehicle structure)

### Design Philosophy (One-liner for Essays)
- [ ] *"A bumper is engineered to fail gracefully: it absorbs energy through controlled, recoverable deformation, ensuring that the costly body structure and safety-critical components remain undamaged at regulatory test velocities."*
